topics.title: Water Quality: Impurities (Pollutants and Contaminants) in Water, Their Significance and Estimation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Quality: Impurities (Pollutants and Contaminants) in Water, Their Significance and Estimation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Environmental Engineering pedagogy, **water quality** refers to the chemical, physical, biological, and radiological characteristics of water relative to the requirements of one or more biotic species or to any human need or purpose. An **impurity** is any substance present in water other than the pure water molecule (H₂O) that alters its natural properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction - Pollutant vs. Contaminant:**
> - **Pollutant:** A substance that is introduced into the environment (especially water) and has harmful or poisonous effects. Pollutants are context-dependent; a substance is a pollutant only when it reaches a concentration that causes detrimental effects.
> - **Contaminant:** Any physical, chemical, biological, or radiological substance found in water that is not intentionally added. All pollutants are contaminants, but not all contaminants are pollutants (e.g., dissolved minerals in drinking water are contaminants but not pollutants at normal levels).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **glass of pure, distilled water** as a perfectly clean highway with only authorized vehicles (H₂O molecules). Now imagine rain falling into a forest, picking up leaves, soil, microbes, and dissolved minerals along the way — this is water in nature.

- **Contaminants** = ALL the extra "vehicles" on the highway (leaves, sticks, cars, bicycles, pedestrians). They are simply *there*.
- **Pollutants** = ONLY those extra vehicles that cause a traffic jam or accident — i.e., substances at concentrations that **degrade water quality** for intended use.

A teaspoon of iron in a lake is a contaminant; if it stains laundry and clogs pipes, it becomes a pollutant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric for Water Quality:**
> The standard metric used universally (and accepted by the Bureau of Indian Standards, BIS 10500:2012, and KTU syllabus) is **mg/L (milligrams per litre)**, which is numerically equivalent to **ppm (parts per million)** for dilute aqueous solutions where the density of water ≈ **1 kg/L = 1000 g/L = 10⁶ mg/L**.

### 1.3 Significance of Studying Water Impurities

Understanding impurities is critical for the following engineering applications:
1. **Drinking water supply design** — determining treatment train.
2. **Industrial process water** — preventing scale, corrosion, and fouling in boilers/cooling towers.
3. **Wastewater treatment** — sizing biological/chemical treatment units.
4. **Public health protection** — controlling waterborne diseases.
5. **Aquatic ecosystem preservation** — maintaining Dissolved Oxygen (DO) and limiting eutrophication.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Impurities in Water (KTU Syllabus-Defined Framework)

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes impurities into four broad families. The structured breakdown is given below.

#### A. Physical Impurities
- **Suspended Solids (SS):** Settleable, floatable, colloidal particles (silt, clay, organic debris).
- **Turbidity:** Optical property caused by suspended matter, measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.
- **Colour:** Dissolved humic substances, metallic ions (Fe, Mn), measured in **Hazen Units** or **Pt-Co Units**.
- **Temperature & Taste/Odour:** Affect palatability and ecological balance.

#### B. Chemical Impurities
- **Inorganic (Total Dissolved Solids - TDS):** Salts of Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻, NO₃⁻.
- **Organic:** Proteins, carbohydrates, oils, surfactants, pesticides.
- **Toxic Metals:** Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Chromium (Cr⁶⁺).
- **Gases:** Dissolved Oxygen (DO), CO₂, H₂S, NH₃.
- **Nutrients:** Nitrogen (N) and Phosphorus (P) compounds — primary drivers of eutrophication.

#### C. Biological Impurities
- **Pathogenic microorganisms:** Bacteria (*E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*), viruses (Rotavirus, Hepatitis A), protozoa (*Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*), helminths.
- **Indicator organisms:** Total Coliforms and Fecal Coliforms (most probable number test).
- **Algae and aquatic plants:** Cause taste, odour, and clogging.

#### D. Radiological Impurities
- Radium-226, Radon-222, Uranium isotopes — relevant only in groundwater drawn from specific geological formations.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Impurity — Significance for Treatment Design

- **Suspended Solids** → cause turbidity, shield pathogens from disinfectants, deposit as sludge in pipes → must be removed by sedimentation/filtration.
- **Dissolved Salts (TDS)** → impart hardness, scaling, salinity → treated by ion exchange, RO.
- **Organic Matter** → consumes oxygen, causes anaerobic conditions → measured by BOD/COD → removed by biological oxidation.
- **Pathogens** → cause waterborne disease outbreaks → removed by chlorination, UV, ozonation.
- **Toxic Metals** → bioaccumulate, cause cancer/neurological damage → removed by precipitation, adsorption, membrane processes.

### 2.3 Estimation Techniques — KTU Engineering Reference

| Impurity Parameter | Standard Estimation Method | Instrument / Reagent | Reporting Unit | Typical Acceptable Limit (BIS 10500) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turbidity | Nephelometric Method | Nephelometer / Turbidimeter | NTU | $\leq 1$ NTU (max 5 NTU) |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | Gravimetric (dried at 103–105 °C) | Oven, Desiccator, Gooch Crucible | mg/L | — |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | Gravimetric (dried at 180 °C) | Oven, Evaporating Dish | mg/L | $\leq 500$ mg/L |
| Acidity/Alkalinity | Titrimetric (H₂SO₄ / HCl vs Na₂CO₃ indicator) | Phenolphthalein, Methyl Orange | mg/L as CaCO₃ | 200–600 mg/L |
| Hardness (Total) | EDTA Complexometric Titration | EDTA solution, Eriochrome Black-T | mg/L as CaCO₃ | $\leq 200$ mg/L |
| Chloride | Argentometric (Mohr's Method) | AgNO₃ + K₂CrO₄ indicator | mg/L as Cl⁻ | $\leq 250$ mg/L |
| Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | Winkler's Iodometric Method | MnSO₄ + Alkali-Iodide-Azide, Starch | mg/L as O₂ | $\geq 5$ mg/L (aquatic life) |
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) | 5-day BOD at 20 °C, dilution method | BOD incubator, Winkler titration | mg/L as O₂ | $\leq 2$ mg/L (drinking) |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) | Open Reflux / Closed Reflux Dichromate | K₂Cr₂O₇ + H₂SO₄ + HgSO₄, FAS titration | mg/L as O₂ | $\leq 250$ mg/L (sewage discharge) |
| Nitrogen (Ammoniacal / Nitrate) | Distillation + Nesslerization / Phenol-Disulphonic Acid | Spectrophotometer | mg/L as N | $\leq 0.5$ mg/L (NH₃-N) |
| Phosphates | Ascorbic Acid Method | Spectrophotometer at 880 nm | mg/L as PO₄³⁻ | — |
| Heavy Metals | Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) / ICP | AAS / ICP-OES | mg/L | $\leq 0.01$–$0.05$ mg/L |
| Coliforms | Multiple Tube Fermentation (MTF) / Membrane Filtration (MF) | Lactose broth, EMB agar | MPN per 100 mL | 0 per 100 mL (drinking) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Conversion Note for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> Hardness, alkalinity, and acidity are conventionally reported "**as CaCO₃**". To convert a substance of equivalent weight $E$ to CaCO₃ equivalent, use the conversion factor:
> $$\text{Multiplier} = \frac{50}{E}$$
> where the equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq**.

### 2.4 High-Yield Concentration Conversion Formulas

| Purpose | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| mg/L to ppm (in dilute aqueous solution) | $\text{ppm} = \text{mg/L} \times 1$ | Since $\rho_{water} = 1$ kg/L |
| mg/L to % | $\% = \dfrac{\text{mg/L}}{10\,000}$ | Since $1\% = 10\,000$ mg/L |
| mg/L to g/m³ | $\text{g/m}^3 = \text{mg/L} \times 1$ | Numerically equal |
| meq/L to mg/L as CaCO₃ | $\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \text{meq/L} \times 50$ | KTU favourite |
| mg/L of ion to mg/L as CaCO₃ | $\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \text{mg/L of ion} \times \dfrac{50}{E}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight of the ion |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **municipal water treatment plants** in Kerala (e.g., Kerala Water Authority facilities), the BOD/COD ratio determines whether biological treatment (aerobic vs anaerobic) is feasible. A BOD/COD > 0.5 indicates **biodegradability** — a critical decision variable for KTU 2024 design problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hardness Expressed "as CaCO₃"

**Statement:** Any cation concentration can be expressed as CaCO₃ equivalent using the relation:
$$\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \text{mg/L of cation} \times \frac{50}{\text{Equivalent Weight of cation}}$$

**Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

Let $C$ = concentration of the cation in mg/L and $E$ = its equivalent weight in g/eq.

The milli-equivalent concentration is defined as:
$$\text{meq/L} = \frac{C}{E}$$

By definition, 1 meq/L of **any** species is chemically equivalent to 1 meq/L of CaCO₃. The CaCO₃ equivalent in mg/L is:
$$\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \text{meq/L} \times 50$$

Substituting the meq/L expression:
$$\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \frac{C}{E} \times 50$$

$$\boxed{\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = C \times \frac{50}{E}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — 5-Day BOD (BOD₅) Calculation

**Statement:** The 5-day BOD of a sample is given by:
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{D_1 - D_2 - \dfrac{(B_1 - B_2) \times (1 - f)}{f}}{P}$$

**Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

Let the following be defined for the BOD bottle:
- $D_1$ = Initial DO of diluted sample (mg/L)
- $D_2$ = Final DO of diluted sample after 5 days (mg/L)
- $B_1$ = Initial DO of dilution water blank (mg/L)
- $B_2$ = Final DO of dilution water blank after 5 days (mg/L)
- $P$ = Decimal fraction of sample in the dilution mixture (e.g., 0.05 for 5% dilution)
- $f$ = Fraction of dilution water in the bottle = $1 - P$

**Step 1:** The oxygen depletion in the **diluted sample** is:
$$\Delta D = D_1 - D_2$$

**Step 2:** The oxygen depletion in the **dilution water blank** represents correction for the water's own oxygen demand:
$$\Delta B = B_1 - B_2$$

**Step 3:** The oxygen depletion attributable **only to the sample's impurities** is the total depletion of the diluted mixture minus the depletion due to dilution water alone, weighted by the fraction of dilution water present:
$$\text{Net sample depletion (in diluted bottle, mg/L)} = (D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times \frac{f}{1} \times \frac{\text{Volume of dilution water}}{\text{Volume of dilution water}}$$

**Step 3 (Rewritten correctly):** Standard APHA formulation:
$$\text{Net depletion in bottle} = (D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)\left(\frac{1 - P}{P}\right) \times P$$

A more common engineering text form is:
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — BOD₅ Calculation

**Problem [KTU Model]:** A wastewater sample is diluted such that 5 mL of sample is added to 295 mL of dilution water. Initial DO of diluted sample = 8.0 mg/L; DO after 5 days = 4.0 mg/L. Initial DO of dilution water blank = 8.2 mg/L; final DO of blank = 8.0 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $P$, the volume fraction of sample:
$$P = \frac{5}{5 + 295} = \frac{5}{300} = 0.01667$$

**Step 2:** Compute depletion of diluted sample:
$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 4.0 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** Compute depletion of blank:
$$B_1 - B_2 = 8.2 - 8.0 = 0.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4:** Apply formula:
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.0 - (0.2)(1 - 0.01667)}{0.01667}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.0 - (0.2)(0.98333)}{0.01667}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.0 - 0.19667}{0.01667}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{3.80333}{0.01667} \approx 228.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Answer:** $\text{BOD}_5 \approx 228.2$ mg/L.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Hardness "as CaCO₃"

**Problem [KTU Model]:** A water sample contains 80 mg/L of Ca²⁺ and 30 mg/L of Mg²⁺. Calculate total hardness in mg/L as CaCO₃. (Equivalent weight of Ca²⁺ = 20; Mg²⁺ = 12.15)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Ca²⁺ contribution as CaCO₃:
$$\text{Ca}^{2+}\text{ as CaCO}_3 = 80 \times \frac{50}{20} = 80 \times 2.5 = 200 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2:** Mg²⁺ contribution as CaCO₃:
$$\text{Mg}^{2+}\text{ as CaCO}_3 = 30 \times \frac{50}{12.15} = 30 \times 4.115 = 123.46 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** Total hardness:
$$\text{TH} = 200 + 123.46 = 323.46 \approx 323.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: Exceeds BIS limit of 200 mg/L → water is classified as **very hard**; softening required.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Population Equivalent

**Population Equivalent (PE)** is the number of persons whose BOD contribution per day equals the BOD load of the wastewater source.

**Formula:**
$$\text{PE} = \frac{\text{Total BOD load (kg/day)}}{\text{Per capita BOD (g/person/day)}}$$

**Standard Assumed Value (KTU/CPHEEO):**
Per capita BOD contribution = **0.054 kg/person/day = 54 g/person/day** (Indian standard, moderate climate).

**Problem [KTU Model]:** A dairy discharges 1000 m³/day of wastewater with BOD = 1500 mg/L. Calculate the population equivalent.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute BOD load per day:
$$\text{BOD load} = Q \times C = 1000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 1500 \text{ mg/L}$$

Convert: $1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^6$ mg/L × m³ → mass = $1000 \times 10^3$ L/day × 1500 mg/L = $1.5 \times 10^9$ mg/day = **1500 kg/day**.

**Step 2:** Compute PE:
$$\text{PE} = \frac{1500 \text{ kg/day}}{0.054 \text{ kg/person/day}}$$

$$\text{PE} = 27\,778 \text{ persons (approximately)}$$

**Answer:** $\text{PE} \approx 27\,778$ persons.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Test

| Scenario | Preferred Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Municipal sewage monitoring | BOD₅ | Reflects biodegradable organic load |
| Industrial effluent with toxic compounds | COD | Toxic substances inhibit BOD bacteria; COD captures both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics |
| Quick routine assessment of organic load | COD (2–3 h test) | Faster than 5-day BOD |
| Establishing treatability of wastewater | BOD/COD ratio | If $> 0.5$, biological treatment is feasible |
| Hardness assessment for boiler feed water | EDTA titration | Direct, accurate, fast |
| Microbial safety of drinking water | MPN or MF coliform test | Regulatory standard (BIS, WHO) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Water Impurities (Mermaid Architecture Flow)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a pollutant and a contaminant in water with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
A **contaminant** is any foreign substance present in water irrespective of its concentration or effect, whereas a **pollutant** is a contaminant that is present in a concentration high enough to cause harm or degrade the water for its intended use.

**Example:** Dissolved calcium (Ca²⁺) at 40 mg/L is a **contaminant** but not a pollutant. The same calcium at 600 mg/L (causing scaling in pipes) is a **pollutant**.

> [!NOTE]
> (3 marks: 1 for definition of contaminant, 1 for definition of pollutant, 1 for example with reasoning)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the four major categories of impurities in water and give one estimation method for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Category | Example | Estimation Method |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | Turbidity | Nephelometry (NTU) |
| Chemical | Hardness | EDTA titration |
| Biological | Coliforms | MPN / Membrane Filtration |
| Radiological | Ra-226 | Scintillation counting |

(3 marks: 1 for each correctly matched pair)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify the impurities found in water. Discuss, with examples, the significance of each category in the design of water and wastewater treatment systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard 5-day BOD of a municipal sewage sample is to be determined. 3 mL of sewage is added to 297 mL of dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is 8.5 mg/L, and after 5 days of incubation at 20 °C, the DO is 3.5 mg/L. The initial DO of the dilution water blank is 8.6 mg/L, and the final DO of the blank is 8.2 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the sewage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

Impurities in water are classified into four categories:

1. **Physical Impurities** — Suspended solids, turbidity, colour, temperature, taste, odour.
   - *Significance:* Cause aesthetic problems, shield pathogens from disinfectants, and deposit in distribution mains. Removed by sedimentation, filtration, and aeration.

2. **Chemical Impurities** — Dissolved salts (TDS), organic matter, toxic metals, gases, nutrients.
   - *Significance:* Hardness causes scaling in boilers; organic matter consumes DO causing fish kills; nitrates cause methemoglobinemia; heavy metals are carcinogenic. Treated by ion exchange, RO, biological oxidation, chemical precipitation.

3. **Biological Impurities** — Pathogens (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths) and indicator organisms (coliforms).
   - *Significance:* Direct cause of waterborne diseases (typhoid, cholera, hepatitis, dysentery). Removed by chlorination, UV, ozonation, and membrane filtration.

4. **Radiological Impurities** — Radium-226, Radon-222, Uranium isotopes.
   - *Significance:* Long-term cancer risk. Removed by ion exchange and reverse osmosis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often lump "chemical" and "physical" together. Maintain the four-class classification strictly and link each to a *specific* treatment — generic statements lose marks.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Volume of sample = 3 mL
- Volume of dilution water = 297 mL
- Total volume = 300 mL
- $D_1 = 8.5$ mg/L, $D_2 = 3.5$ mg/L
- $B_1 = 8.6$ mg/L, $B_2 = 8.2$ mg/L

**Step 1:** Compute $P$:
$$P = \frac{3}{300} = 0.01$$

**Step 2:** Apply the BOD₅ formula:
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.5 - 3.5) - (8.6 - 8.2)(1 - 0.01)}{0.01}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{5.0 - (0.4)(0.99)}{0.01}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{5.0 - 0.396}{0.01}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.604}{0.01}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 460.4 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfall (BOD Calculations):**
> 1. Forgetting to subtract the **blank correction** $(B_1 - B_2)(1-P)$ — loses 2 marks.
> 2. Using $P = 1/300$ correctly but mistakenly omitting blank correction — loses 2 marks.
> 3. Writing the formula but not mentioning **units (mg/L)** in the final answer — loses 0.5 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than or equal to BOD for the same wastewater sample? Discuss the significance of the BOD/COD ratio in deciding the type of biological treatment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water sample analysis reports: Ca²⁺ = 60 mg/L, Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L, Na⁺ = 50 mg/L, HCO₃⁻ = 180 mg/L, SO₄²⁻ = 40 mg/L, Cl⁻ = 35 mg/L. Calculate:
  (i) Total Hardness as CaCO₃
  (ii) Total Alkalinity as CaCO₃
  (iii) Net Non-Carbonate Hardness, if any

Use equivalent weights: Ca²⁺ = 20, Mg²⁺ = 12.15, HCO₃⁻ = 61, CaCO₃ = 50. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** Measures the amount of **dissolved oxygen** consumed by **microorganisms** while biologically oxidizing the **biodegradable** organic matter in water under aerobic conditions at 20 °C over **5 days** (BOD₅). Unit: mg/L as O₂.

**COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** Measures the amount of oxygen equivalent consumed by **strong chemical oxidant** (K₂Cr₂O₇ in H₂SO₄) to oxidize **both biodegradable and non-biodegradable** organic matter. Test duration: 2–3 hours. Unit: mg/L as O₂.

**Why COD ≥ BOD:**
- BOD measures only **biodegradable** organics; COD measures **all organics** (biodegradable + non-biodegradable).
- Some compounds (e.g., lignin, cellulose, aromatic hydrocarbons) resist microbial attack but are oxidized by dichromate.
- Therefore: $\text{COD} \geq \text{BOD}$, with equality only when the wastewater is 100% biodegradable.

**Significance of BOD/COD Ratio:**
- If **BOD/COD > 0.5** → wastewater is **biodegradable** → conventional biological treatment (aerobic activated sludge, trickling filter) is feasible.
- If **BOD/COD between 0.3 and 0.5** → moderately biodegradable → may need acclimatized seed or two-stage treatment.
- If **BOD/COD < 0.3** → poorly biodegradable → physical-chemical treatment (coagulation, adsorption, advanced oxidation) preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "COD is always greater than BOD" without explaining **why** (i.e., dichromate oxidizes a wider range of organics). Always state the reason for the inequality.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Total Hardness (TH) as CaCO₃:**

TH is contributed by Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ only (Na⁺ does not cause hardness).
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 60 \times \frac{50}{20} = 60 \times 2.5 = 150 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 24 \times \frac{50}{12.15} = 24 \times 4.115 = 98.76 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TH} = 150 + 98.76 = 248.76 \approx 248.8 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**Step 2 — Total Alkalinity (TA) as CaCO₃:**

Only HCO₃⁻ is considered (assuming pH < 8.3, which is typical).
$$\text{HCO}_3^- \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 180 \times \frac{50}{61} = 180 \times 0.8197 = 147.54 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TA} = 147.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**Step 3 — Carbonate Hardness (CH), Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH):**

$$\text{CH} = \min(\text{TH}, \text{TA}) = \min(248.8, 147.5) = 147.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{NCH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH} = 248.8 - 147.5 = 101.3 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Net Non-Carbonate Hardness} = 101.3 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Water is very hard (TH > 200 mg/L). Carbonate hardness is 147.5 mg/L; the remaining 101.3 mg/L must be removed by **lime–soda softening** or ion exchange.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Hardness Problems):**
> 1. Including Na⁺ in total hardness — **Na⁺ does not cause hardness** (it is monovalent and forms soluble salts). Loses 1.5 marks.
> 2. Confusing alkalinity with hardness. Alkalinity is from HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻; hardness is from Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺.
> 3. Forgetting that Carbonate Hardness = min(TH, TA). Students often wrongly subtract TA from TH directly without considering which is smaller.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Water Impurities):**

- ✅ **Pollutant vs. Contaminant:** Contaminant = any foreign substance; Pollutant = contaminant at harmful concentration.
- ✅ **Four Categories:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Radiological.
- ✅ **mg/L ≈ ppm** for dilute aqueous solutions (density ≈ 1 kg/L).
- ✅ **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 g/eq** — the universal reference for hardness and alkalinity.
- ✅ **Hardness conversion formula:** $\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3 = C \times 50/E$.
- ✅ **BOD₅ formula:** $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$.
- ✅ **BOD/COD > 0.5** → biodegradable; < 0.3 → non-biodegradable.
- ✅ **Winkler's Method** → DO; **EDTA titration** → hardness; **Mohr's Method** → chloride; **Argentometric** → chloride; **Bichromate reflux** → COD.
- ✅ **Carbonate Hardness (CH) = min(TH, TA)**; **NCH = TH − CH**.
- ✅ **Indian standard per capita BOD = 54 g/person/day** (used for Population Equivalent problems).
- ✅ **BIS 10500:2012 limits to remember:** TDS ≤ 500 mg/L; Total Hardness ≤ 200 mg/L; Chloride ≤ 250 mg/L; Turbidity ≤ 1 NTU (max 5 NTU); Coliform = 0 per 100 mL.
- ✅ **Primary Drinking Water Standards** deal with **health** (toxic metals, pathogens, nitrates); **Secondary Standards** deal with **aesthetics** (colour, odour, taste, TDS).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waterborne Diseases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Waterborne Diseases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Waterborne Disease** is an infectious illness caused by pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or helminths) that are transmitted through the ingestion of contaminated water. According to the World Health Organization (WHO) classification, waterborne diseases constitute a subcategory of **Water-Related Diseases**, specifically those whose primary transmission route is the fecal–oral pathway through unsafe drinking water supplies.

Mathematically, the risk of infection from a waterborne pathogen can be expressed using the **dose-response relationship**:

$$P_{inf} = 1 - e^{-r \cdot N}$$

Where:
- $P_{inf}$ = Probability of infection per exposure event
- $r$ = Dose-response coefficient (pathogen-specific)
- $N$ = Ingested dose (number of viable organisms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PECET861 (Water and Air Quality Management) explicitly requires students to classify waterborne diseases by causative agent, identify transmission routes, and correlate disease incidence with water quality parameters (especially microbial indicators like **Total Coliforms** and **Fecal Coliforms**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your drinking water pipeline as a **highway for invisible passengers**. Most of the time, the water is clean — like a deserted highway. But if even a tiny amount of sewage leaks into this pipeline (similar to a contaminated truck spilling its load), thousands of microscopic "hitchhikers" (bacteria, viruses, parasites) travel along the highway straight into your glass. You cannot see, smell, or taste them. Once consumed, they set up "camps" inside your intestines, multiply rapidly, and cause illness.

This is precisely what happens during a waterborne disease outbreak — like the classic case of a town sourcing water downstream of a leaking septic tank, leading to a sudden spike in **cholera** or **typhoid** cases.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Water-Related Diseases (WHO)

The WHO classifies water-related illnesses into four distinct categories based on the **transmission pathway**. This is a **high-yield topic** for KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four WHO Categories of Water-Related Diseases:**
> 1. **Waterborne** — Caused by ingestion of water containing pathogens (e.g., Cholera, Typhoid)
> 2. **Water-washed (or Water-scarce)** — Caused by poor personal hygiene due to water shortage (e.g., Trachoma, Scabies)
> 3. **Water-based** — Caused by parasites living in water that penetrate the skin (e.g., Schistosomiasis, Dracunculiasis)
> 4. **Water-related insect vectors** — Spread by insects breeding in water (e.g., Malaria, Dengue, Yellow Fever)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **WHO Drinking Water Standard for E. coli / Fecal Coliforms: 0 CFU per 100 mL** (zero tolerance in any 100 mL sample)
- **Unit of measurement: CFU/100 mL** (Colony Forming Units per 100 milliliters)
- **Infectious dose for Vibrio cholerae: as low as $10^3$ to $10^6$ organisms**
- **Infectious dose for Giardia lamblia: as low as 10 to 100 cysts**
- **Incubation period for Typhoid: 7 to 14 days**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Waterborne Diseases by Causative Agent

Waterborne diseases are systematically classified into four major etiological groups. Each group exhibits distinct characteristics, water-survival behavior, and chlorine resistance — all of which are critical for designing water treatment systems.

### 2.1.1 Bacterial Waterborne Diseases
These are caused by pathogenic bacteria that proliferate in the human intestinal tract and are shed in feces. Water acts as the mechanical vector.

- **Cholera** (*Vibrio cholerae*) — Acute diarrheal infection; causes severe dehydration
- **Typhoid Fever** (*Salmonella typhi*) — Systemic infection; high fever, intestinal perforation risk
- **Bacillary Dysentery** (*Shigella* spp.) — Bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps
- **Gastroenteritis** (*E. coli*, *Campylobacter jejuni*) — Mild to severe diarrhea
- **Leptospirosis** (*Leptospira interrogans*) — Contact with water contaminated by rodent urine

### 2.1.2 Viral Waterborne Diseases
Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites and cannot replicate outside host cells. They are highly resistant to environmental degradation.

- **Hepatitis A & E** (HAV, HEV) — Liver inflammation; fecal-oral transmission
- **Rotavirus** — Leading cause of severe childhood diarrhea globally
- **Poliomyelitis** (*Poliovirus*) — Paralytic disease (now nearly eradicated)
- **Norwalk Virus / Norovirus** — Acute gastroenteritis outbreaks

### 2.1.3 Protozoan Waterborne Diseases
Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotes that form resistant **cysts** or **oocysts** enabling long-term survival in water. They are notoriously **chlorine-resistant**.

- **Giardiasis** (*Giardia lamblia / intestinalis*) — Cysts survive for months in cold water
- **Amoebic Dysentery** (*Entamoeba histolytica*) — Liver abscess complications
- **Cryptosporidiosis** (*Cryptosporidium parvum*) — Resists chlorination; 1993 Milwaukee outbreak
- **Cyclosporiasis** (*Cyclospora cayetanensis*)

### 2.1.4 Helminthic (Worm) Waterborne Diseases
Caused by parasitic worms transmitted via water containing larval or egg stages.

- **Dracunculiasis** (*Dracunculus medinensis*) — Guinea worm disease (now near-eradicated)
- **Schistosomiasis** — Larvae penetrate skin during water contact (technically water-based, not strictly waterborne)

## 2.2 The "Why and How" — The Fecal-Oral Transmission Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic of the fecal-oral transmission pathway:**
> - **Step 1 — Contamination:** Fecal matter from an infected host (human or animal) enters a water source
> - **Step 2 — Persistence:** The pathogen survives in the aquatic environment
> - **Step 3 — Ingestion:** A susceptible host consumes the contaminated water
> - **Step 4 — Colonization:** The pathogen establishes infection in the host's gastrointestinal tract
> - **Step 5 — Multiplication & Shedding:** Pathogen replicates and is shed back into the environment via feces, completing the cycle

This cycle is broken by proper sanitation, water treatment, and personal hygiene — a principle articulated in the **F-diagram** (Fluids, Fields, Flies, Fingers, Fomites) widely referenced in environmental sanitation engineering.

## 2.3 The F-Diagram Conceptual Model

The F-diagram is a critical KTU-concept that identifies the six principal routes by which fecal pathogens reach a new host:

| Symbol | Pathway | Engineering Intervention |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------------------|
| **F** Fluids | Contaminated water/wells | Protected water sources, filtration, chlorination |
| **F** Fields | Soil contamination with feces | Proper sanitation, latrines, sewage treatment |
| **F** Flies | Houseflies carrying pathogens | Insect control, screened latrines |
| **F** Fingers | Hand-to-mouth contact | Handwashing, hygiene education |
| **F** Fomites | Inanimate objects (clothes, utensils) | Disinfection, boiling |
| **F** Food | Contaminated food preparation | Safe food handling, washing produce |

> The diagram illustrates that there are multiple "barriers" (latrines, water treatment, handwashing) that can block transmission at each step. **The most effective single intervention is providing safe drinking water and adequate sanitation.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Unit / Standard | Application |
|:--------|:-------------------|:----------------|:------------|
| Probability of Infection | $P_{inf} = 1 - e^{-r \cdot N}$ | Dimensionless | Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA) |
| Disease Burden (DALY) | $DALY = YLL + YLD$ | Years | WHO global burden estimation |
| Most Probable Number (MPN) | Statistical estimate from tube dilution | MPN/100 mL | Fecal coliform enumeration |
| Chlorine Residual (CT concept) | $C \times T = k$ | mg/L × min | Disinfection efficacy |
| Coliform Removal | $\% R = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Treatment plant performance |
| Fecal Coliform Standard | $\leq 0$ CFU/100 mL | CFU/100 mL | WHO Drinking Water Guideline |
| Total Coliform Standard | $\leq 0$ CFU/100 mL (ideal) | CFU/100 mL | IS 10500:2012 (Indian Standard) |

> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding the dose-response relationship is essential for designing water treatment plants. Engineers must ensure that the residual pathogen concentration post-treatment is **below the threshold of acceptable risk** (typically $10^{-4}$, i.e., 1 infection per 10,000 consumers per year for drinking water).

## 2.5 Factors Influencing Waterborne Disease Outbreaks

**Why do outbreaks occur?** The convergence of multiple risk factors leads to epidemics:

- **Inadequate water treatment** — Inadequate filtration, suboptimal chlorination
- **Cross-connection contamination** — Sewage pipes leaking into water mains
- **Cyanobacterial blooms** — Algal toxins in surface water reservoirs
- **Natural disasters** — Floods overwhelm sanitation infrastructure
- **Climate change** — Increased temperatures favor pathogen growth
- **Population density** — Overcrowding accelerates fecal-oral spread
- **Lack of sanitation** — Open defecation contaminating water sources
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Classification Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Classification Matrix of Waterborne Diseases

Since this topic is descriptive-rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" takes the form of an exhaustive analytical classification matrix. Each step is explicitly justified for KTU examination purposes.

### Step 1: Define the Master Classification Framework

Waterborne diseases are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Causative Agent** (Bacterial / Viral / Protozoan / Helminthic)
2. **Symptom Profile** (Diarrheal / Systemic / Hepatic / Neurological)
3. **Transmission Mode** (Strictly waterborne / water-washed / water-based / vector-related)
4. **Persistence in Water** (Days / Weeks / Months / Years)

### Step 2: Apply Axis 1 — Causative Agent Mapping

Each disease is mapped to its taxonomic group with explicit clinical and microbiological justification.

### Step 3: Apply Axis 2 & 3 — Symptom and Transmission Cross-Tabulation

This cross-tabulation allows students to recognize diseases by clinical presentation.

### Step 4: Apply Axis 4 — Survival Kinetics

Pathogen persistence dictates treatment design. A protozoan cyst surviving for months requires far more rigorous filtration and disinfection than a fragile bacterium.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Master Table of Major Waterborne Diseases

| Disease | Causative Agent | Type | Incubation Period | Key Symptoms | Persistence in Water | Chlorine Resistance | Primary Transmission |
|:--------|:----------------|:-----|:------------------|:-------------|:---------------------|:--------------------|:---------------------|
| **Cholera** | *Vibrio cholerae* | Bacterium | 1–5 days | Profuse watery diarrhea ("rice-water stools"), severe dehydration, muscle cramps | Days to weeks | Low (easily killed) | Fecal-oral via water |
| **Typhoid Fever** | *Salmonella typhi* | Bacterium | 7–14 days | Sustained high fever, weakness, abdominal pain, rose spots | Days to weeks | Moderate | Fecal-oral via water/food |
| **Bacillary Dysentery** | *Shigella* spp. | Bacterium | 1–4 days | Bloody diarrhea, fever, tenesmus | Days | Low | Fecal-oral, person-to-person |
| **Hepatitis A** | Hepatitis A virus (HAV) | Virus | 15–50 days | Jaundice, fatigue, nausea, dark urine | Weeks | High | Fecal-oral via water/food |
| **Hepatitis E** | Hepatitis E virus (HEV) | Virus | 2–9 weeks | Jaundice, severe in pregnancy | Weeks | High | Fecal-oral via water |
| **Rotavirus Diarrhea** | Rotavirus | Virus | 1–3 days | Severe watery diarrhea in children, vomiting | Weeks to months | High | Fecal-oral, fomites |
| **Giardiasis** | *Giardia lamblia* | Protozoan | 7–14 days | Foul-smelling diarrhea, flatulence, weight loss | Months (cold water) | Very High (cyst) | Waterborne (cysts) |
| **Amoebic Dysentery** | *Entamoeba histolytica* | Protozoan | 2–4 weeks | Bloody diarrhea, liver abscess | Weeks to months | Moderate | Fecal-oral via water/food |
| **Cryptosporidiosis** | *Cryptosporidium parvum* | Protozoan | 2–10 days | Profuse watery diarrhea, immunocompromised risk | Years (oocysts) | Extremely High | Waterborne (oocysts) |
| **Poliomyelitis** | Poliovirus | Virus | 7–14 days | Paralysis, meningitis | Weeks | Moderate | Fecal-oral via water |
| **Leptospirosis** | *Leptospira interrogans* | Bacterium | 2–30 days | High fever, jaundice (Weil's disease), renal failure | Weeks | Low | Contact with urine-contaminated water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is chlorine resistance critical for engineers?** Because *Cryptosporidium* oocysts are **virtually immune to standard chlorination**, treatment plants must rely on **physical removal** (coagulation, flocculation, and filtration) rather than chemical disinfection alone. This is why dual-stage filtration is mandated for surface water sources in advanced treatment systems.

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — Microbial Risk Assessment

**Problem (typical KTU numerical):** A community draws drinking water from a river. The fecal coliform count in the raw water is $1 \times 10^6$ CFU/100 mL. After conventional treatment (coagulation, sedimentation, filtration, chlorination), the coliform count reduces to $10$ CFU/100 mL. Calculate the percentage removal achieved.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Given:
- $C_{in} = 1 \times 10^6$ CFU/100 mL
- $C_{out} = 10$ CFU/100 mL

Apply the percentage removal formula:

$$\% R = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$$

Substitute the values:

$$\% R = \frac{(1 \times 10^{6}) - (10)}{1 \times 10^{6}} \times 100$$

$$\% R = \frac{999{,}990}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\% R = 0.99999 \times 100$$

$$\% R = 99.999\%$$

**Conclusion:** The treatment plant achieves **5-log (99.999%) removal** of fecal coliforms. This represents an excellent level of treatment, well above the WHO benchmark of **3-log to 4-log removal (99.9% to 99.99%)** required for safe drinking water.

## 3.4 Analytical Hierarchy — Engineering Control Measures

The multi-barrier approach for controlling waterborne diseases operates on a **hierarchy of control**, from source to tap:

**Level 1 — Source Protection (Watershed Management):**
- Preventing contamination at origin through land-use controls, riparian buffers, restricted livestock access
- **Effectiveness:** Reduces pathogen load by 1–2 logs

**Level 2 — Pre-Treatment (Coagulation-Flocculation-Sedimentation):**
- Removes suspended particles carrying adsorbed pathogens
- **Effectiveness:** 1–2 log reduction for bacteria and viruses

**Level 3 — Filtration (Rapid Sand Filtration / Membrane Filtration):**
- Physically excludes *Cryptosporidium* oocysts (~4–6 μm) and *Giardia* cysts (~8–12 μm)
- **Effectiveness:** 2–3 log removal for protozoa

**Level 4 — Disinfection (Chlorination / UV / Ozonation):**
- Inactivates remaining bacterial and viral pathogens
- **Effectiveness:** 2–4 log inactivation depending on CT value

**Level 5 — Distribution System Integrity:**
- Maintains residual chlorine (≥ 0.2 mg/L at consumer end per IS 10500)
- Prevents recontamination through pipe maintenance
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WHO Classification of Water-Related Diseases (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WATER RELATED DISEASES"] --> B["WATERBORNE"]
    A --> C["WATER WASHED"]
    A --> D["WATER BASED"]
    A --> E["VECTOR RELATED"]

    B --> B1["Cholera"]
    B --> B2["Typhoid"]
    B --> B3["Hepatitis A and E"]
    B --> B4["Dysentery"]

    C --> C1["Trachoma"]
    C --> C2["Scabies"]
    C --> C3["Skin Infections"]

    D --> D1["Schistosomiasis"]
    D --> D2["Dracunculiasis"]
    D --> D3["Guinea Worm"]

    E --> E1["Malaria"]
    E --> E2["Dengue"]
    E --> E3["Yellow Fever"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cat1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef cat2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef cat3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef cat4 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 cat1
    class C,C1,C2,C3 cat2
    class D,D1,D2,D3 cat3
    class E,E1,E2,E3 cat4
```

## 4.2 Fecal-Oral Transmission Cycle (F-Diagram Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FAECES IN ENVIRONMENT"] --> B["CONTAMINATED WATER FLUIDS"]
    A --> C["CONTAMINATED FIELDS SOIL"]
    A --> D["FLIES"]
    A --> E["FINGERS HANDS"]
    A --> F["FOMITES OBJECTS"]
    A --> G["FOOD"]

    B --> H["NEW HOST INGESTION"]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I["INFECTION IN NEW HOST"]
    I --> A

    classDef source fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef pathway fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef host fill:#006400,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A source
    class B,C,D,E,F,G pathway
    class H,I host
```

## 4.3 Multi-Barrier Water Treatment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOURCE RAW WATER"] --> P1["LEVEL 1 WATERSHED PROTECTION"]
    P1 --> P2["LEVEL 2 COAGULATION FLOCCULATION"]
    P2 --> P3["LEVEL 3 SEDIMENTATION"]
    P3 --> P4["LEVEL 4 RAPID SAND FILTRATION"]
    P4 --> P5["LEVEL 5 DISINFECTION CHLORINATION UV"]
    P5 --> P6["LEVEL 6 DISTRIBUTION NETWORK"]
    P6 --> T["TREATED SAFE WATER AT CONSUMER TAP"]

    P1 -.->|"Removes 1 to 2 logs"| M1["Pathogen Load Reduction"]
    P2 -.-> M1
    P3 -.-> M1
    P4 -.-> M1
    P5 -.-> M1
    P6 -.-> M1

    classDef input fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#f5a623,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#7ed321,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef measure fill:#9013fe,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class S,T input
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 process
    class M1 output
```

## 4.4 Pathogen Size vs. Treatment Barrier Effectiveness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V["VIRUSES 0.02 to 0.3 micrometer"] --> R1["Requires Chemical Disinfection Chlorine UV Ozone"]
    B["BACTERIA 0.5 to 5 micrometer"] --> R2["Removed by Filtration plus Disinfection"]
    P["PROTOZOA CYSTS 4 to 15 micrometer"] --> R3["Removed by Filtration Cryptosporidium Chlorine Resistant"]
    H["HELMINTH EGGS 30 to 150 micrometer"] --> R4["Removed by Sedimentation and Filtration"]

    classDef virus fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bact fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef proto fill:#388e3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef helm fill:#1976d2,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class V,R1 virus
    class B,R2 bact
    class P,R3 proto
    class H,R4 helm
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define waterborne diseases. List any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Waterborne diseases are infectious illnesses caused by pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or helminths) that are transmitted primarily through the ingestion of contaminated water containing fecal matter from infected hosts.

**Four examples:**
1. Cholera (*Vibrio cholerae*)
2. Typhoid fever (*Salmonella typhi*)
3. Hepatitis A (HAV)
4. Giardiasis (*Giardia lamblia*)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between waterborne and water-based diseases with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Waterborne Diseases | Water-Based Diseases |
|:----------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| Transmission | Ingestion of contaminated water | Skin contact with infested water |
| Pathogen Location | Pathogen carried in water | Pathogen lives part of its life cycle in water |
| Route of Entry | Oral | Percutaneous (through skin) |
| Example | Cholera, Typhoid | Schistosomiasis, Dracunculiasis (Guinea worm) |

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) Classify waterborne diseases based on the causative agent. Discuss the symptoms, causative organism, and mode of transmission of Cholera and Typhoid.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Waterborne Diseases (4 categories):**

1. **Bacterial:** Cholera, Typhoid, Bacillary dysentery, Gastroenteritis, Leptospirosis
2. **Viral:** Hepatitis A and E, Rotavirus infection, Poliomyelitis, Norovirus gastroenteritis
3. **Protozoan:** Giardiasis, Amoebic dysentery, Cryptosporidiosis, Cyclosporiasis
4. **Helminthic:** Dracunculiasis (Guinea worm disease)

**Cholera — Detailed Analysis:**

- **Causative organism:** *Vibrio cholerae* (a comma-shaped, gram-negative bacterium)
- **Incubation period:** 1 to 5 days
- **Symptoms:** Profuse, painless watery diarrhea resembling "rice-water stools"; severe dehydration; electrolyte imbalance; muscle cramps; hypovolemic shock; potentially fatal within hours if untreated
- **Mode of transmission:** Strictly fecal-oral via contaminated water; also through contaminated food (especially raw shellfish, raw vegetables irrigated with sewage)

**Typhoid Fever — Detailed Analysis:**

- **Causative organism:** *Salmonella typhi* (a gram-negative rod)
- **Incubation period:** 7 to 14 days
- **Symptoms:** Sustained high fever (step-ladder pattern), headache, malaise, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, hepatosplenomegaly, rose-colored spots on trunk, intestinal hemorrhage or perforation in severe cases
- **Mode of transmission:** Fecal-oral route via contaminated water, milk, or food; chronic carriers can shed bacteria for years

**(b) Explain the F-diagram in detail. Describe the various engineering interventions to break the fecal-oral transmission chain.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The F-diagram is a conceptual model developed by the WHO that identifies the **six principal pathways** through which fecal pathogens from an infected host's excreta can reach a new susceptible host. Each letter "F" represents one pathway:

1. **Fluids** — Contaminated drinking water, polluted wells, rivers
2. **Fields** — Soil contaminated with feces (agricultural runoff, open defecation)
3. **Flies** — Houseflies that breed in feces and transfer pathogens to food
4. **Fingers** — Direct hand-to-mouth contact after defecation
5. **Fomites** — Inanimate objects (utensils, clothing, door handles) contaminated with pathogens
6. **Food** — Contaminated raw vegetables, fruits, milk, or improperly cooked food

**Engineering Interventions to Break the Transmission Chain:**

| Pathway | Primary Intervention | Supporting Intervention |
|:--------|:--------------------|:-----------------------|
| Fluids | Protected water sources (bore wells, covered reservoirs) | Filtration, chlorination, household water treatment |
| Fields | Constructed latrines, septic tanks, sewage treatment plants | Composting of feces, restricted livestock access |
| Flies | Screened latrines, proper refuse management | Insecticides, fly traps |
| Fingers | Handwashing stations with soap | Hygiene education campaigns |
| Fomites | Boiling of utensils, chlorination of surfaces | Laundering with hot water |
| Food | Proper washing and cooking, food vendor licensing | Refrigeration, safe storage |

> The **multi-barrier approach** is the cornerstone of public health engineering — no single intervention provides complete protection, but the **cumulative effect** of several barriers dramatically reduces disease transmission.

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Alternative]

**(a) Discuss the microbial indicators used to assess water quality. Why is E. coli considered the most reliable indicator of recent fecal contamination?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Microbial Indicators of Water Quality:**

Microbial indicators are surrogate organisms whose presence in water signals the likely presence of pathogenic microorganisms. The ideal indicator organism should satisfy the following criteria:
- Present in large numbers in the intestines of warm-blooded animals
- Detected easily and rapidly by simple laboratory methods
- Not pathogenic to humans
- Does not multiply in natural water
- More resistant to environmental stresses and disinfectants than pathogens

**Common Indicator Organisms:**

1. **Total Coliforms** — Gram-negative, lactose-fermenting, rod-shaped bacteria belonging to the Enterobacteriaceae family. Includes both fecal and environmental species.
2. **Fecal Coliforms (Thermotolerant Coliforms)** — Subset that ferments lactose at 44.5°C, indicating probable fecal origin.
3. **Escherichia coli (E. coli)** — The most specific fecal indicator; a primary member of the fecal coliform group.
4. **Fecal Streptococci / Enterococci** — Intestinal bacteria that supplement E. coli testing; more persistent in water.
5. **Clostridium perfringens spores** — Indicator of past or intermittent fecal contamination; extremely resistant spores survive for long periods.
6. **Coliphages** — Viruses that infect E. coli; used as viral indicators.
7. **Heterotrophic Plate Count (HPC)** — General measure of bacterial population.

**Why E. coli is the Most Reliable Indicator:**

- **High specificity:** E. coli is exclusively found in the intestines of warm-blooded animals; its detection is unambiguous evidence of **recent fecal contamination** (within hours to a few days).
- **High abundance:** Present in enormous numbers in human and animal feces (approximately $10^9$ per gram of feces).
- **Easy detection:** Modern techniques like defined substrate tests (Colilert, m-T7 agar) allow enumeration within 18–24 hours.
- **WHO recommendation:** The WHO Global Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality (2017) explicitly recommends **E. coli** as the primary indicator for routine monitoring.
- **Strong correlation:** Multiple epidemiological studies confirm strong statistical correlation between E. coli density in drinking water and the incidence of gastrointestinal illness in consumers.

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the transmission cycle of a waterborne disease. Explain the role of the multiple-barrier approach in preventing waterborne diseases.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Transmission Cycle of a Waterborne Disease (refer to F-diagram in Section 4.2):**

The transmission cycle involves four sequential stages:
1. **Contamination** of water source with fecal pathogens
2. **Persistence** of pathogens in the aquatic environment
3. **Ingestion** by a susceptible host
4. **Shedding** of pathogens by the newly infected host, continuing the cycle

**Multiple-Barrier Approach (refer to architecture in Section 4.3):**

The multiple-barrier approach is a **defense-in-depth strategy** used in modern water supply engineering. It recognizes that no single treatment process is 100% effective, and therefore layers multiple independent barriers so that failure of one is compensated by others.

**Key barriers (in sequence):**
1. **Source protection** — Watershed management, restricted access
2. **Pre-treatment** — Screening, microstraining
3. **Coagulation-Flocculation-Sedimentation** — Removes suspended and colloidal particles
4. **Filtration** — Removes *Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*, and most bacteria
5. **Disinfection** — Chlorination, UV, or ozonation inactivates bacteria and viruses
6. **Distribution system integrity** — Maintains residual chlorine, prevents cross-connections

**Why this approach works:** The total log-removal is **additive** across barriers. A 4-log removal by filtration plus a 3-log inactivation by chlorination yields a cumulative **7-log reduction** — sufficient to render even heavily contaminated source water safe for consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse "coliform" with "fecal coliform" — remember that total coliforms include environmental species, whereas fecal coliforms indicate **specifically warm-blooded fecal origin**.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Many students write "*E. coli is a pathogen*" — it is a **commensal indicator**, not a pathogen. The diarrheagenic *E. coli* (e.g., O157:H7) is a separate pathogenic strain.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Do not state that "chlorination kills all pathogens" — *Cryptosporidium* oocysts are **highly resistant to chlorine** and require physical removal. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> - **Pitfall 4:** When asked about Typhoid transmission, mention **Typhoid Mary (Mary Mallon)** as the famous chronic carrier who infected 51 people — examiners love such historical references.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Waterborne Diseases":**

- **Definition:** Diseases transmitted via ingestion of fecal-contaminated water
- **WHO 4-category framework:** Waterborne, Water-washed, Water-based, Water-related vector
- **Key bacterial diseases:** Cholera, Typhoid, Dysentery, Leptospirosis
- **Key viral diseases:** Hepatitis A & E, Rotavirus, Polio
- **Key protozoan diseases:** Giardiasis, Amoebiasis, Cryptosporidiosis
- **F-Diagram:** Fluids, Fields, Flies, Fingers, Fomites, Food
- **Fecal-oral cycle:** Contamination → Persistence → Ingestion → Shedding
- **Indicator organism hierarchy:** Total Coliforms → Fecal Coliforms → **E. coli (gold standard)**
- **WHO Standard:** 0 CFU/100 mL for E. coli in drinking water
- **Chlorine-resistant pathogens:** *Cryptosporidium* oocysts, *Giardia* cysts
- **Multi-barrier approach:** Source protection + Treatment + Disinfection + Distribution integrity
- **Dose-response formula:** $P_{inf} = 1 - e^{-rN}$
- **Log removal targets:** 3–4 log for bacteria, 4 log for viruses, 3 log for protozoa
- **Typhoid carrier:** Mary Mallon (Typhoid Mary)
- **Cryptosporidium outbreak:** Milwaukee, 1993 (≈ 400,000 cases)
- **Indian standard:** IS 10500:2012 for drinking water quality
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards of Potable Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standards of Potable Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Potable Water:** Potable water is water that is safe for human consumption, suitable for drinking, cooking, and personal hygiene, without posing any short-term or long-term health risks. The term "potable" originates from the Latin word *potare*, meaning "to drink."

A **Standard of Potable Water** is a regulatory specification that prescribes the maximum permissible concentration of physical, chemical, biological, and radiological parameters present in drinking water. These standards are established by national and international agencies to protect public health and ensure aesthetic acceptability of the water supplied to the public.

The two principal references governing potable water quality globally and nationally are:

1. **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (GDWQ)** – Internationally accepted benchmark (latest 4th edition incorporating addenda).
2. **IS 10500:2012 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** – The Indian national standard for drinking water, revised in 2012.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 10500:2012 – Drinking Water — Specification (Second Revision)** is the controlling standard in India and is enforced through the **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO)** under the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, and adopted by the **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a recipe for a perfectly safe glass of water — except this recipe is enforced by law. Just like a chef must ensure the dish has the right amount of salt (not too much, not too little), water agencies must ensure the water has the right balance of minerals and the right absence of harmful substances. If the "salt" (chloride) is too high, the water tastes salty. If the "iron" is too high, it stains clothes. If invisible "germs" (coliforms) are present, the water can cause cholera. Standards of potable water are essentially the **legal recipe card** for safe drinking water — a checklist of "must not exceed" and "should ideally be" limits for every measurable substance.

### 1.3 Categories of Water Quality Parameters

The standards typically classify parameters into the following categories:

- **Physical Parameters** – Turbidity, Colour, Odour, Taste, Total Dissolved Solids (TDS).
- **Chemical Parameters** – pH, Hardness, Chloride, Sulphate, Nitrate, Fluoride, Iron, Heavy Metals (Lead, Arsenic, Mercury, Cadmium, Chromium).
- **Bacteriological Parameters** – Total Coliforms, Faecal Coliforms, *E. coli*.
- **Radiological Parameters** – Gross alpha activity, Gross beta activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** IS 10500:2012 prescribes **two limits** for most parameters:
> - **Acceptable Limit (Requirement):** The concentration below which no adverse effect is observed. This is the target for routine operation.
> - **Permissible Limit (In the Absence of Alternate Source):** The maximum allowable concentration when no alternative source is available, provided the water does not pose an acute health hazard.

### 1.4 Key Drinking Water Standards Bodies

| Agency | Standard | Governing Region |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| **WHO** | Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (GDWQ) | Global / International |
| **BIS** | IS 10500:2012 | India |
| **US EPA** | National Primary Drinking Water Regulations (NPDWR) | United States |
| **EU** | Council Directive 98/83/EC | European Union |

Explicitly stated constants/metrics: **Acceptable Limit**, **Permissible Limit**, **TDS**, **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit)**, **CFU (Colony Forming Unit)**, **MPN (Most Probable Number)**, **µg/L (micrograms per litre)**, **mg/L (milligrams per litre)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Standards

A drinking water standard is not just a single number; it is a **multi-tiered specification** built on:

1. **Health-Based Targets:** Concentrations of contaminants that do not pose appreciable risk to health over a lifetime of consumption.
2. **Aesthetic Parameters:** Limits for parameters affecting the appearance, taste, or odour of water.
3. **Operational Monitoring Limits:** Real-time trigger values used by treatment plants.

### 2.2 Why Two Limits? (Acceptable vs Permissible)

- **Acceptable Limit:** The concentration at which water is considered safe and palatable for lifelong consumption with no known health effects.
- **Permissible Limit:** A relaxation provided when an alternative water source is unavailable, in which case treatment or alternate supply must be arranged within a stipulated period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Permissible Limit is NOT a long-term goal. It is a temporary emergency relaxation. Treatment is mandatory before water at the permissible limit reaches the consumer, OR a parallel safe source must be developed within a defined timeframe.**

### 2.3 IS 10500:2012 — Key Parameters and Limits

The following tables summarize the **essential, board-exam-favourite** parameters from IS 10500:2012.

#### 2.3.1 Physical Parameters

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit |
|------|-----------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Colour | Hazen units | 5 | 15 |
| 2 | Odour | — | Unobjectionable | — |
| 3 | Taste | — | Agreeable | — |
| 4 | Turbidity | NTU | 1 | 5 |
| 5 | Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | mg/L | 500 | 2000 |

#### 2.3.2 Chemical Parameters (General)

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit |
|------|-----------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | pH | — | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |
| 2 | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | mg/L | 200 | 600 |
| 3 | Calcium (as Ca) | mg/L | 75 | 200 |
| 4 | Magnesium (as Mg) | mg/L | 30 | 100 |
| 5 | Chloride (as Cl) | mg/L | 250 | 1000 |
| 6 | Sulphate (as SO₄) | mg/L | 200 | 400 |
| 7 | Nitrate (as NO₃) | mg/L | 45 | No relaxation |
| 8 | Fluoride (as F) | mg/L | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| 9 | Iron (as Fe) | mg/L | 0.3 | No relaxation |
| 10 | Total Alkalinity (as CaCO₃) | mg/L | 200 | 600 |

#### 2.3.3 Chemical Parameters (Heavy Metals & Toxic Substances)

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit |
|------|-----------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Lead (as Pb) | mg/L | 0.01 | No relaxation |
| 2 | Arsenic (as As) | mg/L | 0.01 | 0.05 |
| 3 | Mercury (as Hg) | mg/L | 0.001 | No relaxation |
| 4 | Cadmium (as Cd) | mg/L | 0.003 | No relaxation |
| 5 | Chromium (as Cr⁶⁺) | mg/L | 0.05 | No relaxation |
| 6 | Copper (as Cu) | mg/L | 0.05 | 1.5 |
| 7 | Manganese (as Mn) | mg/L | 0.1 | 0.3 |
| 8 | Zinc (as Zn) | mg/L | 5 | 15 |
| 9 | Cyanide (as CN) | mg/L | 0.05 | No relaxation |
| 10 | Phenolic Compounds (as C₆H₅OH) | mg/L | 0.001 | 0.002 |

#### 2.3.4 Bacteriological Parameters

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit |
|------|-----------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Total Coliforms | MPN per 100 mL (or CFU per 100 mL) | Shall not be detectable in any 100 mL sample | — |
| 2 | Faecal Coliforms | MPN per 100 mL | Shall not be detectable in any 100 mL sample | — |
| 3 | *E. coli* | MPN per 100 mL | Shall not be detectable in any 100 mL sample | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical interpretation:** "Shall not be detectable" in routine sampling means **zero** in 100 mL. The standard uses statistical sampling (out of a defined number of samples, the geometric mean must conform).

#### 2.3.5 Radiological Parameters (Optional under IS 10500)

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit |
|------|-----------|------|------------------|
| 1 | Gross Alpha activity | Bq/L | 0.1 |
| 2 | Gross Beta activity | Bq/L | 1.0 |

### 2.4 WHO Guidelines — Key Reference Values

| S.No | Parameter | Unit | Guideline Value |
|------|-----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | pH | — | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| 2 | Turbidity | NTU | < 1 (ideally < 0.1 for disinfection efficiency) |
| 3 | TDS | mg/L | 600 – 1000 (aesthetic, no health-based) |
| 4 | Nitrate (as NO₃⁻) | mg/L | 50 (health-based) |
| 5 | Fluoride | mg/L | 1.5 |
| 6 | Arsenic | mg/L | 0.01 |
| 7 | Lead | mg/L | 0.01 |
| 8 | Cadmium | mg/L | 0.003 |
| 9 | Mercury | mg/L | 0.006 |
| 10 | Chromium (hexavalent) | mg/L | 0.05 |
| 11 | Total Coliforms | per 100 mL | Must not be detectable |
| 12 | *E. coli* | per 100 mL | Must not be detectable |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Hardness (as CaCO₃ equivalent) | $ \text{Hardness} = 2.5 \cdot \lbrack\text{Ca}^{2+}\rbrack + 4.1 \cdot \lbrack\text{Mg}^{2+}\rbrack $ (mg/L) | Soft < 75; Moderately hard 75–150; Hard 150–300; Very hard > 300 | Designing softeners and predicting scaling in boilers/pipes |
| TDS (sum of ions) | $ \text{TDS} \approx 0.55 \cdot \text{EC} $ (in dS/m) | < 500 desirable | Indicating overall mineral content |
| MPN Index (statistical estimate) | $ \text{MPN/100 mL} $ from probability table | 0 for safe water | Quantifying coliform density |
| Bicarbonate alkalinity | $ \lbrack\text{HCO}_3^-\rbrack = 1.22 \cdot \text{phenolphthalein alkalinity} $ | 200 mg/L | Carbonate equilibrium in water treatment |
| Fluoride concentration vs dental health | $< 0.5$ → dental caries; 0.5–1.5 → optimum; > 1.5 → fluorosis | 0.5 – 1.5 mg/L | Defluoridation plant design |
| Nitrate–N vs Methemoglobinemia | $\lbrack\text{NO}_3^-\rbrack / 4.43 = \lbrack\text{NO}_3\text{-N}\rbrack$ | NO₃-N < 10 mg/L | Protecting infants from blue baby syndrome |

> [!NOTE]
> **No `|` symbol in tables:** Where the original expression would contain a vertical bar, we have used `\lbrack \rbrack` for square brackets to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Treatment Plant Design:** Standards dictate the design criteria for unit operations (e.g., turbidity ≤ 1 NTU requires sand filtration with coagulation).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Public water supplies must obtain consent from the Pollution Control Board (PCB) confirming compliance.
- **Health Surveillance:** Epidemiologists correlate disease incidence with deviations from standards.
- **Industrial Use:** Boiler-feed, pharmaceutical, and electronics manufacturing industries impose even stricter standards (e.g., Type-I ultrapure water at 18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting Hardness from Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ to CaCO₃ Equivalent

The hardness of water is reported as **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) equivalent** because CaCO₃ has a molecular weight of 100 g/mol, which gives a convenient numerical basis for comparison.

**Step 1:** Identify the molecular weights involved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Molecular weight of CaCO}_3 &= 40\,(\text{Ca}) + 12\,(\text{C}) + 3 \times 16\,(\text{O}) = 100\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molecular weight of Ca} &= 40\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molecular weight of Mg} &= 24\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Molecular weight of MgCO}_3 &= 84\,\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3 &= 100/2 = 50\,\text{g/eq} \\
\text{Equivalent weight of Ca}^{2+} &= 40/2 = 20\,\text{g/eq} \\
\text{Equivalent weight of Mg}^{2+} &= 24/2 = 12\,\text{g/eq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Formulate the conversion factors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Multiplier for Ca}^{2+} &= \frac{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3}{\text{Equivalent weight of Ca}^{2+}} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5 \\
\text{Multiplier for Mg}^{2+} &= \frac{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3}{\text{Equivalent weight of Mg}^{2+}} = \frac{50}{12} \approx 4.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compose the universal hardness equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = 2.5 \times \lbrack\text{Ca}^{2+}\rbrack_{\text{mg/L}} + 4.1 \times \lbrack\text{Mg}^{2+}\rbrack_{\text{mg/L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical demonstration.

Suppose a water sample contains Ca²⁺ = 60 mg/L and Mg²⁺ = 20 mg/L. Compute the total hardness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness} &= 2.5 \times 60 + 4.1 \times 20 \\
&= 150 + 82 \\
&= 232\,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Classification of the water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } 150 < 232 < 300 \text{ mg/L, the water is classified as \textbf{Hard}.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Converting TDS from Electrical Conductivity (EC)

Instruments commonly measure the electrical conductivity (EC) of water. TDS is then derived using an empirical multiplier.

**Step 1:** Define the proportional relationship.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS (mg/L)} = k \times \text{EC (dS/m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard empirical constant for most natural waters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &\approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the typical value of 0.64 (or commonly 0.55 as per KTU reference). For a sample with EC = 1.2 dS/m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS} &= 0.55 \times 1.2 \\
&= 0.66\,\text{g/L} \\
&= 660\,\text{mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with IS 10500 limit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
660\,\text{mg/L} &> 500\,\text{mg/L (acceptable)} \\
660\,\text{mg/L} &< 2000\,\text{mg/L (permissible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the water **fails the acceptable limit** but is within the **permissible limit**, requiring corrective action but acceptable in the short term.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Parameters by Source of Water

| Source | Critical Parameters of Concern | Reason for Concern |
|--------|--------------------------------|--------------------|
| Surface water (river, lake) | Turbidity, Total Coliforms, COD, BOD, Algae, Pesticides | Open exposure to runoff and sewage |
| Ground water (shallow wells) | Nitrate, Fluoride, Total Hardness, Salinity, Arsenic (in endemic belts) | Geological leaching and agricultural seepage |
| Ground water (deep bore wells) | Fluoride, Arsenic, Total Hardness, Fe, Mn | Long contact with mineral strata |
| Municipal treated supply | Residual Chlorine, Trihalomethanes, Microbial regrowth | Disinfection by-products |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Health Effects of Parameter Deviation

| Parameter | Effect of Excess | Effect of Deficiency |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Fluoride (F⁻) | Dental fluorosis (> 1.5 mg/L), skeletal fluorosis (> 4 mg/L) | Dental caries (< 0.5 mg/L) |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | Methemoglobinemia (blue baby syndrome) in infants | — |
| Lead (Pb) | Neurological damage, kidney damage, anaemia | — |
| Arsenic (As) | Hyperkeratosis, skin cancer, cardiovascular disease | — |
| Total Hardness | Scaling in pipes, soap scum, laxative effect in adapted consumers | Possible correlation with cardiovascular disease (debated) |
| Iron (Fe) | Staining of clothes, metallic taste, promotes iron bacteria growth | — |
| TDS | Salty/bitter taste, laxative effect | — |

### 3.5 Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Style)

> **Problem:** A water sample from a community bore well in a fluoride-endemic area is analysed and reports the following: Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L, Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L, F⁻ = 1.4 mg/L, NO₃⁻ = 50 mg/L, pH = 7.2, Fe = 0.4 mg/L. Compare each parameter against IS 10500:2012 and advise on potability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness} &= 2.5 \times 80 + 4.1 \times 24 \\
&= 200 + 98.4 \\
&= 298.4\,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parameter-wise evaluation:**

| Parameter | Observed | Acceptable | Permissible | Verdict |
|-----------|----------|------------|-------------|---------|
| Total Hardness | 298.4 mg/L | 200 | 600 | Within permissible, exceeds acceptable → hard water |
| Fluoride | 1.4 mg/L | 1.0 | 1.5 | Within permissible, exceeds acceptable → fluorosis risk |
| Nitrate | 50 mg/L | 45 | No relaxation | Exceeds acceptable; not permissible → unsafe for infants |
| pH | 7.2 | 6.5–8.5 | No relaxation | Within acceptable |
| Iron | 0.4 mg/L | 0.3 | No relaxation | Exceeds acceptable; no relaxation → unsafe |

**Conclusion:** The water is **NOT potable** in its raw form. It requires: (i) defluoridation (e.g., Nalgonda/activated alumina process), (ii) ion-exchange or reverse osmosis for nitrate reduction, (iii) aeration and sand filtration for iron removal, and (iv) lime-soda softening for hardness reduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Potable Water Standards Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Potable Water Standards] --> B[Physical Parameters]
    A --> C[Chemical Parameters]
    A --> D[Bacteriological Parameters]
    A --> E[Radiological Parameters]

    B --> B1[Colour]
    B --> B2[Turbidity]
    B --> B3[Odour]
    B --> B4[Taste]
    B --> B5[Total Dissolved Solids]

    C --> C1[General Chemicals]
    C --> C2[Heavy Metals and Toxins]
    C --> C3[Disinfection By Products]

    C1 --> C1a[pH]
    C1 --> C1b[Total Hardness]
    C1 --> C1c[Chloride]
    C1 --> C1d[Sulphate]
    C1 --> C1e[Nitrate]
    C1 --> C1f[Fluoride]
    C1 --> C1g[Iron and Manganese]

    C2 --> C2a[Lead]
    C2 --> C2b[Arsenic]
    C2 --> C2c[Mercury]
    C2 --> C2d[Cadmium]
    C2 --> C2e[Hexavalent Chromium]
    C2 --> C2f[Cyanide]

    C3 --> C3a[Trihalomethanes]
    C3 --> C3b[Residual Chlorine]

    D --> D1[Total Coliforms]
    D --> D2[Faecal Coliforms]
    D --> D3[E coli]

    E --> E1[Gross Alpha Activity]
    E --> E2[Gross Beta Activity]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — IS 10500:2012 Two-Tier Limit Framework

```mermaid
graph LR
    RAW[Raw Water Sample] --> LAB[Laboratory Analysis]
    LAB --> Q1{Result vs Acceptable Limit}
    Q1 -- Within Acceptable --> SAFE[Water is Potable No Treatment Issue]
    Q1 -- Between Acceptable and Permissible --> WARN[Water is Conditionally Potable Provide Treatment and Seek Alternate Source]
    Q1 -- Exceeds Permissible --> FAIL[Water is Non Potable Reject and Identify Alternate Safe Source]

    SAFE --> MON[Routine Monitoring]
    WARN --> TRT[Advanced Treatment Required]
    FAIL --> SRC[Source Replacement Mandatory]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Drinking Water Quality Compliance Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Parameter Identification] --> P2[Sampling Plan]
    P2 --> P3[Field and Lab Analysis]
    P3 --> P4[Comparison with IS 10500 and WHO]
    P4 --> P5{Compliant}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Distribution to Consumer]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Process Modification or Treatment]
    P7 --> P3
    P6 --> P8[Continuous Monitoring and Reporting to PCB]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Interrelationship Between Parameters and Health Effects

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph HEALTH_OUTCOMES
        H1[Dental Fluorosis]
        H2[Methemoglobinemia]
        H3[Neurological Damage]
        H4[Kidney Disease]
        H5[Cholera and Typhoid]
        H6[Cardiovascular Effects]
    end

    subgraph PARAMETERS
        Q1[High Fluoride]
        Q2[High Nitrate]
        Q3[Lead]
        Q4[Arsenic and Cadmium]
        Q5[Coliforms]
        Q6[Hardness and TDS]
    end

    Q1 --> H1
    Q2 --> H2
    Q3 --> H3
    Q4 --> H4
    Q5 --> H5
    Q6 --> H6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define potable water. List any four physical parameters specified in IS 10500:2012 along with their acceptable limits.

**Model Answer:**
Potable water is water that is safe for human consumption — free from pathogens, harmful chemicals, and aesthetically acceptable. Four physical parameters and their acceptable limits under IS 10500:2012 are:

1. Colour: ≤ **5 Hazen units**
2. Turbidity: ≤ **1 NTU**
3. Total Dissolved Solids (TDS): ≤ **500 mg/L**
4. Odour: **Unobjectionable**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Distinguish between "Acceptable Limit" and "Permissible Limit" as defined in IS 10500:2012. Why is the permissible limit not considered a long-term goal?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Concentration with no adverse effect on lifelong consumption | Concentration allowed in absence of an alternate source |
| Duration | Permanent, lifelong standard | Temporary, emergency relaxation |
| Health target | Zero appreciable risk | No acute hazard |
| Long-term use | Yes | No — corrective action mandatory |

The permissible limit is not a long-term goal because prolonged consumption at these levels may produce sub-clinical or chronic health effects; the standard mandates that authorities arrange a safe alternate source within a defined period.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** In questions on standards, students frequently (i) confuse **mg/L** with **µg/L** for heavy metals (this alone costs 1–2 marks), (ii) write the acceptable limit but forget the permissible limit, and (iii) give unit-less numbers without specifying "as CaCO₃" or "NTU" — which is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners. Always write units explicitly and quote both limits when applicable.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the drinking water standards prescribed by IS 10500:2012 for the following parameters, with their acceptable and permissible limits: pH, Total Hardness, Fluoride, Nitrate, Iron, Total Coliforms, Lead, Arsenic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A water sample has Ca²⁺ = 50 mg/L and Mg²⁺ = 30 mg/L. Calculate the total hardness as CaCO₃ equivalent. Classify the water and recommend a treatment method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IS 10500:2012 Standards — Parameter-wise (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit | Significance |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation | Affects disinfection, corrosion, taste |
| Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L | Scaling, soap consumption |
| Fluoride (as F) | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L | Dental/skeletal fluorosis prevention |
| Nitrate (as NO₃) | 45 mg/L | No relaxation | Infant methemoglobinemia |
| Iron (as Fe) | 0.3 mg/L | No relaxation | Staining, taste, iron bacteria |
| Total Coliforms | Not detectable per 100 mL | — | Indicator of faecal contamination |
| Lead (as Pb) | 0.01 mg/L | No relaxation | Neurotoxicity, especially in children |
| Arsenic (as As) | 0.01 mg/L | 0.05 mg/L | Carcinogen, skin lesions |

**Explanation:**

- **pH:** Within 6.5–8.5, water is non-corrosive to pipes and not so alkaline as to taste bitter. pH strongly influences chlorine disinfection efficiency (best at pH 6.5–7.5).
- **Total Hardness:** Below 200 mg/L the water is soft; above 600 mg/L it is very hard and unsuitable for boilers and laundries.
- **Fluoride:** At 1.0 mg/L it is optimal for dental health. Above 1.5 mg/L it causes fluorosis; in endemic belts (Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh) defluoridation is necessary.
- **Nitrate:** Above 45 mg/L, infants below 6 months can develop methemoglobinemia, hence no permissible relaxation.
- **Iron:** Beyond 0.3 mg/L, water turns reddish-brown, stains clothes, and supports iron bacteria that clog pipes.
- **Total Coliforms:** Their presence indicates possible faecal contamination and the potential presence of pathogens.
- **Lead:** Even at 0.01 mg/L it can accumulate to neurotoxic levels; leaching from old lead pipes is a major source.
- **Arsenic:** A known carcinogen; West Bengal and Bangladesh aquifer arsenic poisoning is the classic case study. IS 10500 allows the stricter 0.01 mg/L acceptable limit but permits 0.05 mg/L where no alternative exists.

---

**(b) Total Hardness Calculation (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness (as CaCO}_3) &= 2.5 \times \lbrack\text{Ca}^{2+}\rbrack + 4.1 \times \lbrack\text{Mg}^{2+}\rbrack \\
&= 2.5 \times 50 + 4.1 \times 30 \\
&= 125 + 123 \\
&= 248\,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (per Durfor and Becker scale):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
248\,\text{mg/L} &\Rightarrow \text{water is classified as \textbf{HARD}} \\
\text{Acceptable limit (IS 10500)} &= 200\,\text{mg/L} \\
\text{Permissible limit} &= 600\,\text{mg/L} \\
\therefore 200 < 248 < 600 &\Rightarrow \text{within permissible range}
\end{aligned}
$$
