<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 2, Part A, Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a **flash mixer** and a **flocculator** based on detention time and velocity gradient.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Flash Mixer | Flocculator |
|---|---|---|
| Detention time | 20–60 seconds | 20–40 minutes |
| Velocity gradient (G) | 700–1000 s⁻¹ | 10–75 s⁻¹ |
| G·t value | ~10⁴ | 10⁴–10⁵ |
| Purpose | Rapid dispersion of coagulant | Gentle growth of floc |
| Mixing intensity | Highly turbulent | Mild / transitional |

(2 marks for tabulated contrast, 1 mark for stating the purpose difference.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 2, Part A, Q2]**
> **Q2.** State **Camp and Stein's** equation for velocity gradient and explain the significance of the **G·t** parameter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$$

where $P$ is power input (W), $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²), and $V$ is basin volume (m³).

**G·t** is a dimensionless parameter (Camp's number) representing the total opportunity for particle collisions. For flocculation, $G \cdot t$ should lie between **10⁴ and 10⁵**; for flash mixing, values up to ~2 × 10⁴ are typical. (1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definition of G·t, 1 mark for stating the optimum range.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2, Part B, Q3A]**
> **Q3A (a)** Classify flash mixers with neat sketches. Explain in detail any **two types of mechanical flash mixers**. (7 marks)
>
> **Q3A (b)** Design a **mechanical flocculator** for a treatment plant with the following data: Flow = 0.1 m³/s, target G = 25 s⁻¹, detention time = 30 min, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Use a paddle-type flocculator with $C_D = 1.8$, paddle area $A = 10$ m², effective diameter $D = 2$ m. Determine (i) power input, (ii) rotational speed of the paddle, and (iii) G·t. Comment on the design. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q3A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification (2 marks):**
- **Hydraulic flash mixers** — use head loss as the energy source. Types: hydraulic jump, baffled basin, free-fall jet, in-line static mixer.
- **Mechanical flash mixers** — use motor-driven impellers. Types: propeller, paddle, turbine.

**Mechanical Flash Mixer Type 1 — Propeller (Axial-flow) Type (2.5 marks):**
A multi-blade propeller is mounted on a vertical shaft inside a cylindrical or square tank. The high rotational speed (typically 200–1500 rpm) produces a strong axial draft, drawing water from above and discharging it downward. Suited for small- to medium-capacity plants. Detention time 20–60 s.

**Mechanical Flash Mixer Type 2 — Turbine (Radial-flow) Type (2.5 marks):**
Uses a flat-blade or disk-type turbine (e.g., Rushton) mounted on a vertical shaft. Centrifugal action throws water radially outward, creating intense local shear. Best for very rapid dispersion in high-volume tanks. Higher power consumption than propellers.

---

**Model Solution for Q3A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Volume of flocculator:**
$$V = Q \cdot t = 0.1 \times (30 \times 60) = 0.1 \times 1800 = 180 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Power required for target G:**
$$P = G^{2} \, \mu \, V = (25)^{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 180$$
$$P = 625 \times 0.001 \times 180 = 112.5 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Relate P to rotational speed using the drag equation:**
$$P = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_D \, A \, \rho \, (\pi n D)^{3}$$

Solve for $n$:

$$(\pi n D)^{3} = \dfrac{2P}{C_D A \rho} = \dfrac{2 \times 112.5}{1.8 \times 10 \times 1000} = \dfrac{225}{18\,000} = 0.0125$$

$$\pi n D = (0.0125)^{1/3} = 0.2321$$

$$n = \dfrac{0.2321}{\pi \times 2} = \dfrac{0.2321}{6.2832} = 0.0369 \, \text{rev/s} \approx 2.22 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 — G·t check:**
$$G \cdot t = 25 \times (30 \times 60) = 25 \times 1800 = 45\,000 = 4.5 \times 10^{4}$$

This is within the **optimum 10⁴–10⁵** range. ✔
**Step 5 — Comment:** The design is energy-efficient (only 112.5 W for 180 m³). The low rpm (2.2) is consistent with a slow-mix flocculator and ensures no floc shearing.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2, Part B, Q3B]**
> **Q3B (a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of **(i) baffled channel flocculator** and **(ii) tapered flocculator**. Discuss the advantages of the latter. (7 marks)
>
> **Q3B (b)** A **hydraulic flash mixer** in the form of a hydraulic jump has a flow of 0.3 m³/s. The head loss across the jump is 0.5 m. The water temperature is 20 °C ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m², $\gamma = 9800$ N/m³). If the detention time in the jump zone is 5 seconds, calculate **(i) the power dissipated, (ii) the velocity gradient G, and (iii) the G·t value.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Q3B(a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Baffled Channel Flocculator (3.5 marks):**
A long rectangular channel is fitted with wooden or concrete baffles. Water flows either **around-the-end** (alternate baffles attached to opposite walls) or **over-and-under** (baffles alternately reach the water surface and the floor). Each turn of the flow causes local turbulence and gentle particle collisions. Head loss per turn is small but accumulates over many turns, providing uniform slow mixing throughout. G ≈ 10–50 s⁻¹. Disadvantage: head loss is not adjustable once constructed.

**(ii) Tapered Flocculator (3.5 marks):**
A modification of the baffled flocculator where the **spacing between baffles is progressively increased** from inlet to outlet. Near the inlet, baffles are closely spaced → high head loss → **high G (≈ 60 s⁻¹)**. Near the outlet, spacing is wide → low head loss → **low G (≈ 20 s⁻¹)**. This **tapering G** exactly matches the flocculation requirement (intense initial contact, gentle terminal growth). Saves 30–50 % energy compared to uniform G designs. Easily achieved in mechanical flocculators by **variable-speed drives**.

---

**Model Solution for Q3B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Power dissipated from head loss:**
$$P = \gamma \, Q \, h_L = 9800 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 = 1470 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of jump zone:**
$$V = Q \cdot t = 0.3 \times 5 = 1.5 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity gradient G:**
$$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1470}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.5}}$$

$$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{1470}{0.0015}} = \sqrt{980\,000} \approx 990 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — G·t check:**
$$G \cdot t = 990 \times 5 = 4950 \approx 4.95 \times 10^{3}$$

**Comment:** $G = 990 \, s^{-1}$ lies in the typical flash-mixer range (700–1000 s⁻¹) ✔. However, the G·t of ~5 × 10³ is on the **lower end**, indicating that either detention time should be increased (10–15 s) or additional baffles added downstream.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit confusion between $\mu$ (dynamic) and $\nu$ (kinematic) viscosity** — use $\mu$ in the $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu V)}$ equation, NOT $\nu$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the drag power equation** — the formula is $P = \frac{1}{2} C_D A \rho v_r^3$, not $C_D A \rho v_r^3$.
> 3. **Confusing $v_r$ (relative velocity of paddle w.r.t. water) with absolute water velocity** — relative velocity is what produces drag.
> 4. **Not checking the G·t value** — KTU examiners love a final comment on whether the design is acceptable ($10^4$–$10^5$). Marks are reserved for this.
> 5. **Stating detention time of 20–60 s for flocculators** — a classic trap. Flocculators need **20–40 minutes**, not seconds.
> 6. **Confusing the role of the basin volume** — in $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu V)}$, $V$ is the volume of water in the basin at any instant, not the flow rate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Flash Mixers and Flocculators**

- **Flash mixer** = rapid dispersion of coagulant; **G = 700–1000 s⁻¹**, t = 20–60 s.
- **Flocculator** = gentle floc growth; **G = 10–75 s⁻¹**, t = 20–40 min.
- **Camp–Stein equation:** $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu V)}$ — must be on your fingertip.
- **Optimum G·t (Camp's number):** $10^{4}$ to $10^{5}$ for flocculation.
- **Flash mixers are of TWO classes:** Hydraulic (head-loss based) and Mechanical (impeller based).
- **Flocculators are of TWO classes:** Hydraulic (baffled, heddle, tapered, solids-contact) and Mechanical (paddle, walking beam, turbine, floc blanket).
- **Paddle power equation:** $P = \frac{1}{2} C_D A \rho v_r^{3}$ with $v_r = \pi n D$.
- **Tapered flocculator** is the most energy-efficient modern design (high G → low G).
- **Hydraulic devices** have *no power cost*; the *head loss* is the only energy input.
- **Solids-contact flocculators** double as clarifiers and are common in softening plants.
- **At 20 °C**, $\mu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² — a default exam value.
- **Drag coefficient** $C_D$ for flat blades: **1.5–2.0** (use 1.8 if not specified).
- **Common units trap:** $G$ in s⁻¹, $P$ in W, $V$ in m³, $\mu$ in N·s/m² — mixing units is a frequent deduction point.
- **KTU favourite derivations:** Camp–Stein equation and paddle drag power — both must be memorised step-by-step.
- **"Walk and look" rule for design:** high-G first, then taper — same for mechanical (variable-speed) and hydraulic (variable-baffle-spacing) flocculators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sedimentation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sedimentation** is a physical water treatment process in which suspended solid particles that are heavier than water are allowed to settle out of the water column under the influence of **gravity**, in a quiescent or low-velocity basin, thereby reducing the turbidity and suspended solids load of the water. It is the **second** major unit operation in a conventional water treatment scheme, immediately following coagulation-flocculation and preceding filtration. The process is governed by the **gravitational settling velocity** ($v_s$) of discrete particles, which is a function of particle size, density difference, and the kinematic viscosity of the fluid, as formalized by **Stokes' Law** (for laminar settling) and the **Newtonian settling equation** (for turbulent settling). ### Types of Settling (Camp-Hazen Classification) Sedimentation is broadly classified into four discrete types, depending on the concentration and nature of the suspended solids: | Type | Name | Particle Behaviour | Typical Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Type I** | Discrete Particle Settling | Each particle settles independently; no flocculation | Grit chambers, sand removal, plain sedimentation | | **Type II** | Flocculent Settling | Particles flocculate as they settle, increasing in size and $v_s$ | Primary sedimentation tanks in water treatment | | **Type III** | Hindered / Zone Settling | High concentration; particles settle as a mass with a clear liquid above | Secondary clarifiers, thickeners in activated sludge | | **Type IV** | Compression Settling | Particles at very high concentration support overlying particles | Sludge thickening zone, bottom of clarifiers | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Rain-Clearing Puddle"** > Imagine a muddy puddle after a heavy rainstorm. As the water sits still, you can literally *see* the dirt slowly drifting downward and the water above becoming clearer. Sedimentation is the engineering-scale version of this natural process. We simply give the dirt enough *time*, *space*, and *calm* (i.e., low turbulence) to fall to the bottom of a specially designed tank. The two key factors we control are: > 1. **How slow the water moves horizontally** (so particles have time to fall), and > 2. **How big the tank's surface area is** (so particles have a "shorter" distance to fall before reaching the bottom). > In essence, we *trick* gravity into doing the heavy lifting for free. ### Key Design Constants & Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$ - **Standard surface overflow rate (SOR) for plain sedimentation:** 15 to 30 m³/m²·d - **Detention time for primary clarifiers:** 2 to 4 hours (typical 3 h) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Particle Settling — Discrete Settling (Type I) When a spherical particle is released in a quiescent fluid, three forces act on it: 1. **Gravity (downward)** $F_g = \rho_p \cdot g \cdot V_p = \rho_p \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$ 2. **Buoyancy (upward)** $F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_p = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$ 3. **Drag (opposing motion)** $F_D = C_D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$ At **terminal settling velocity**, the net gravitational force equals the drag force: $$\rho_p \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$ ### 2.1.1 Flow Regime Classification | Reynolds Number Range | Regime | Applicable Law | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | $Re < 0.3$ (or 1) | **Laminar** (viscous) | **Stokes' Law** | | $0.3 < Re < 10^3$ | **Transition** | Intermediate drag coefficient | | $Re > 10^3$ | **Turbulent** (inertia) | **Newton's Law** | Where the **particle Reynolds number** is defined as: $$Re = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{\nu}$$ ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all examinable equations** for sedimentation under the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize the variable definitions, units, and validity range for each formula. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Validity | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (s-1) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \nu}$ | **Stokes' Law** — Laminar settling | $Re < 1$, $d < 0.1$ mm | | **2** | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{g \cdot (s-1) \cdot d}{C_D}}$ | **Newton's Law** — Turbulent settling | $Re > 10^3$, $C_D \approx 0.4$ | | **3** | $Re = \dfrac{v_s \cdot d}{\nu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless | | **4** | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{Re}} + 0.34$ | Drag coefficient (valid $Re < 10^4$) | dimensionless | | **5** | $v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | **Hazen's Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** | m³/m²·d (or m/d) | | **6** | $\eta \% = \dfrac{v_o}{v_s} \times 100$ | **Removal efficiency** (Type I) | % (all particles with $v_s > v_o$ removed) | | **7** | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | **Detention (residence) time** | hours (typically 2–4 h) | | **8** | $L = v_h \cdot t_d$ | Tank length requirement | m, where $v_h$ = horizontal velocity | | **9** | $WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | **Weir loading rate** | m³/m·d, typical $< 300$ | | **10** | $h_o = v_o \cdot t_d$ | Critical depth (no resuspension) | m | | **11** | $v_s(t) = v_1 \cdot t^{-\beta}$ | **Type II flocculent settling** (power law) | $\beta$ is a constant (0 < β < 1) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by environmental engineers to design grit chambers, primary clarifiers, secondary clarifiers, and lamella plate settlers. SOR is the single most important design parameter — it dictates the surface area of the tank and not its depth. Depth is governed by detention time. ### 2.2.1 Concept of "Ideal Sedimentation Basin" (Hazen Model) Hazen's ideal basin theory is built on three core assumptions: 1. All particles reaching the sludge zone (bottom) are **permanently removed** (no resuspension). 2. The flow is **uniform and steady**, distributed evenly across the basin. 3. Concentration of suspended solids is **uniform** at the inlet. Under these assumptions, **all particles having a settling velocity $v_s \geq v_o$ are 100% removed**, and the **fractional removal** for particles of velocity $v_p$ is: $$\text{Removed fraction} = \frac{v_p}{v_o}$$ This leads to the **ideal removal efficiency** equation stated in row 6 of the formula sheet. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stokes' Law ### 3.1.1 Force Balance on a Spherical Particle Consider a spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ settling at terminal velocity $v_s$ in a fluid of density $\rho_w$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$. **Step 1 — Compute the volume of the spherical particle:** $$V_p = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute the gravitational force acting downward:** $$F_g = \rho_p \cdot g \cdot V_p = \rho_p \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the buoyant force acting upward (Archimedes' principle):** $$F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_p = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the viscous drag force opposing motion (laminar regime, Stokes drag):** For **laminar flow** around a sphere, the drag coefficient is $C_D = 24/Re$. Substituting into the general drag equation: $$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} = \frac{24}{Re} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the definition of Reynolds number** $Re = v_s d \rho_w / \mu$, and simplify the drag force: $$\begin{aligned} F_D &= \frac{24 \cdot \mu}{v_s d \rho_w} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} \\ &= 3 \pi \mu d v_s \end{aligned}$$ > The above is the famous **Stokes' drag law** for a sphere at low Reynolds numbers. **Step 6 — Apply Newton's second law at terminal velocity** (acceleration = 0, so net force = 0): $$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$ **Step 7 — Substitute the three force expressions:** $$\begin{aligned} \rho_p \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} - 3 \pi \mu d v_s &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8 — Factor the gravity terms and isolate the drag term:** $$\begin{aligned} g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_w) &= 3 \pi \mu d v_s \end{aligned}$$ **Step 9 — Express the result in terms of specific gravity $s = \rho_p / \rho_w$:** $$\begin{aligned} g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot \rho_w (s - 1) &= 3 \pi \mu d v_s \end{aligned}$$ **Step 10 — Solve for $v_s$ by dividing both sides by $3 \pi \mu d$:** $$v_s = \frac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (s - 1)}{18 \cdot \nu}$$ > (using the conversion $\mu = \rho_w \cdot \nu$) This is the **final Stokes' Law** for the terminal settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle in a viscous (laminar) regime. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial insight for KTU exam:** Stokes' Law is applicable only when $Re < 1$ (some texts use $Re < 0.3$). A common exam trick: the examiner will give you a particle of $d = 1$ mm and ask you to compute $v_s$ via Stokes' — this is a **trap**, because the answer is mathematically valid but physically *wrong*. You must verify the Reynolds number first. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Settling Velocity of a Sand Particle **Problem:** Compute the terminal settling velocity of a sand particle of diameter $d = 0.05$ mm and specific gravity $s = 2.65$ in water at 20 °C ($\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Law:** $$v_s = \frac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (s-1)}{18 \cdot \nu}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$\begin{aligned} v_s &= \frac{9.81 \times (0.05 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (2.65 - 1)}{18 \times 1.003 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= \frac{9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.65}{1.8054 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= \frac{4.0466 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8054 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= 2.241 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \\ &\approx 2.24 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Verify the Reynolds number for laminar regime validity:** $$Re = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{\nu} = \frac{2.241 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-3}}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.112$$ Since $Re = 0.112 < 1$, **Stokes' Law is valid**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Removal Efficiency for a Discrete Settling Basin **Step 1 — Define the surface overflow rate (Hazen velocity):** The water rises vertically in the tank at a velocity equal to the SOR, $v_o = Q/A_s$. **Step 2 — Consider a particle of settling velocity $v_p$.** The time it takes to fall to the bottom is $H/v_p$, while the water remains in the tank for $t_d = V/Q = H \cdot A_s / Q = H/v_o$. **Step 3 — If $v_p \geq v_o$**, the particle settles before the water leaves → 100% removal. **Step 4 — If $v_p < v_o$**, the particle only settles a fraction $(v_p/v_o)$ of the depth → removal fraction is: $$\text{Removed fraction} = \frac{v_p}{v_o} = \frac{v_p \cdot A_s}{Q}$$ **Step 5 — For a polydisperse suspension with fraction $dF$ having velocity $v_p$:** $$d(\text{removal}) = \frac{v_p}{v_o} \cdot dF$$ **Step 6 — Integrate over the entire particle size distribution:** $$\eta = \int_{0}^{v_o} \frac{v_p}{v_o} dF + \int_{v_o}^{v_\text{max}} dF = 1 - F(v_o) + \frac{1}{v_o} \int_{0}^{v_o} v_p \, dF$$ This is the **Camp's integral** for fractional removal efficiency. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Tank Sizing **Problem:** Design a rectangular sedimentation tank for a town with population 50,000; per capita water demand = 150 L/c·d. Assume detention time = 3 hours and depth = 3.5 m. Adopt L:B = 4:1. **Step 1 — Compute the flow rate $Q$:** $$Q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/d} = 7500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the volume of the tank:** $$V = Q \cdot t_d = 7500 \times \frac{3}{24} = 937.5 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute the surface area:** $$A_s = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{937.5}{3.5} = 267.86 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Using the L:B = 4:1 ratio:** $$\begin{aligned} 4 B^2 &= 267.86 \\ B &= \sqrt{267.86 / 4} = 8.18 \text{ m} \approx 8.2 \text{ m} \\ L &= 4 \times 8.2 = 32.8 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the surface overflow rate:** $$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{7500}{267.86} = 28.0 \text{ m/d}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the horizontal velocity:** $$v_h = \frac{L}{t_d} = \frac{32.8}{3 \times 3600} = 3.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 3.04 \text{ mm/s}$$ This falls within the typical 1–5 mm/s range, confirming the design is safe. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Sedimentation Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Raw Water Inlet] --> nodeB[Coagulation Tank] nodeB --> nodeC[Flocculation Tank] nodeC --> nodeD[Inlet Zone of Sedimentation Tank] nodeD --> nodeE[Settling Zone
Discrete Flocculent Particles Fall] nodeE --> nodeF[Sludge Zone
Accumulated Solids at Bottom] nodeE --> nodeG[Effluent Weir Zone] nodeG --> nodeH[Clarified Water Outlet to Filtration] nodeF --> nodeI[Sludge Hopper and Scraper Mechanism] nodeI --> nodeJ[Sludge Digestion or Dewatering Unit] nodeK[Surface Overflow Rate Q per A] -.controls.-> nodeE nodeL[Detention Time V per Q] -.controls.-> nodeE nodeM[Inlet Outlet Baffles] -.controls.-> nodeD nodeM -.controls.-> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Settling Equation ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Given Particle Diameter d and Specific Gravity s] --> check{Compute Re using Stokes guess} check -->|Re less than 1| stokes[Apply Stokes Law v equals gd squared s minus 1 over 18 nu] check -->|Re greater than 1000| newton[Apply Newton Law with CD equal to 0.4] check -->|Re between 1 and 1000| trial[Use trial and error on combined equation
CD equals 24 over Re plus 3 over square root Re plus 0.34] trial -->|Re converges less than 1| stokes trial -->|Re converges greater than 1000| newton stokes --> verify[Verify Re less than 1 holds] newton --> verify2[Verify Re greater than 1000 holds] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Forces Acting on a Settling Particle ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:forces columns 3 Fgrav["Gravity Fg downward Density particle g Volume particle"] Fbuoy["Buoyancy Fb upward Density water g Volume particle"] Fdrag["Drag FD opposing motion Drag coeff Area Dynamic pressure"] end particle["Spherical Particle
Diameter d
Density rho_p
Velocity v_s"] fluid["Quiescent Water
Density rho_w
Viscosity mu"] particle --> Fgrav particle --> Fbuoy particle --> Fdrag fluid -.contains.-> particle ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Define the term "Surface Overflow Rate" (SOR) as applied to the design of a sedimentation tank. State its typical range for plain sedimentation of municipal water.** **Model Answer:** The Surface Overflow Rate (SOR), also called Hazen's velocity, is defined as the volumetric flow rate of water per unit surface area of the sedimentation tank. It is mathematically expressed as $v_o = Q/A_s$, where $Q$ is the flow rate (m³/d) and $A_s$ is the surface area of the tank (m²). For plain sedimentation of municipal water, the typical SOR ranges from **15 to 30 m³/m²·d** (or 15–30 m/d). SOR is the critical parameter governing the removal of discrete particles in an ideal sedimentation tank, and it dictates the required surface area, *not* the depth, of the tank. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Differentiate between Type I (discrete) and Type II (flocculent) settling.** **Model Answer:** | Criterion | Type I Settling | Type II Settling | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Particle behaviour** | Particles settle individually without changing size | Particles flocculate and grow in size as they settle | | **Settling velocity** | Constant with depth | Increases with depth and time | | **Concentration** | Low (< 1000 mg/L) | Moderate (1000–5000 mg/L) | | **Applicable law** | Stokes' Law (constant $v_s$) | Power law: $v_s = v_1 \cdot t^{-\beta}$ | | **Removal** | Fractional removal based on Hazen SOR | Higher removal due to aggregation | | **Example** | Grit chambers, plain sedimentation | Primary clarifiers in water treatment | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — **(14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** State and derive **Stokes' Law** for the terminal settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle settling in a viscous fluid under laminar conditions. Clearly state the assumptions made and the limits of its validity in terms of the particle Reynolds number. **(b) [7 Marks]** A water treatment plant treats a flow of **25 MLD** (million litres per day). A rectangular sedimentation tank is to be designed with: - Detention time $t_d = 4$ hours - Depth of the settling zone $H = 3.5$ m - Length-to-breadth ratio $L:B = 4:1$ - Surface overflow rate not to exceed **30 m³/m²·d** Determine the **length, breadth, and surface area** of the tank, and check whether the computed SOR is within the prescribed limit. #### Model Solution to Q-A(a): **Stokes' Law Statement:** The terminal settling velocity of a small spherical particle settling under gravity in a viscous fluid under laminar flow conditions is given by $v_s = g(s-1)d^2 / 18\nu$. **Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive 10-step derivation. **Assumptions:** 1. The particle is a smooth, rigid sphere. 2. The fluid is homogeneous, isotropic, and unbounded. 3. Settling occurs in the **laminar flow regime** ($Re < 1$). 4. The particle reaches terminal velocity (no acceleration). 5. There is no interaction with other particles (low concentration, Type I). 6. The fluid is Newtonian. 7. The particle is not subject to Brownian motion or electrostatic effects. 8. The container walls do not affect the velocity (unbounded medium). **Validity:** Stokes' Law is valid strictly when the particle Reynolds number $Re = v_s d / \nu < 1$. For most practical engineering purposes, it is conservatively valid up to $Re \approx 0.3$. #### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Step 1 — Convert flow rate to m³/d:** $$Q = 25 \text{ MLD} = 25 \times 10^3 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the volume of the tank:** $$V = Q \cdot t_d = 25{,}000 \times \frac{4}{24} = 4166.67 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute the required surface area from volume and depth:** $$A_s = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{4166.67}{3.5} = 1190.48 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Use the L:B = 4:1 ratio:** $$L \cdot B = 4B^2 = 1190.48$$ $$B = \sqrt{\frac{1190.48}{4}} = 17.25 \text{ m} \approx 17.3 \text{ m}$$ $$L = 4B = 4 \times 17.25 = 69.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Check the SOR:** $$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{25{,}000}{1190.48} = 21.0 \text{ m/d}$$ Since $v_o = 21.0 \text{ m/d} < 30 \text{ m/d}$ (the prescribed upper limit), the design is **acceptable**. **Final Design Summary:** - Surface area $A_s = 1190.5 \text{ m}^2$ - Length $L = 69.0 \text{ m}$ - Breadth $B = 17.3 \text{ m}$ - SOR = 21.0 m/d ✓ --- ### Question B — **(14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **"ideal sedimentation tank"** as proposed by Hazen, listing its assumptions. Derive the expression for the **fractional removal efficiency** in such a tank. **(b) [7 Marks]** An ideal sedimentation tank has a surface overflow rate of **20 m/d**. The water contains sand particles of specific gravity 2.65 with the following settling velocity distribution: | Settling velocity $v_p$ (m/d) | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Mass fraction $F$ | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.10 | Calculate the **overall removal efficiency** of the tank. #### Model Solution to Q-B(a): **Concept of the Ideal Sedimentation Tank (Hazen, 1904):** Hazen idealized the sedimentation process by assuming the flow through the tank is uniform and steady, with uniform distribution across the basin cross-section. In an ideal tank, any particle that reaches the sludge zone is permanently removed. The performance depends only on the surface overflow rate, not on depth or detention time (for discrete particles). **Assumptions:** 1. Plug flow — uniform horizontal velocity across the entire cross-section. 2. Uniform distribution of suspended solids across the inlet. 3. Particles are distributed uniformly in the vertical direction at the inlet. 4. Once a particle reaches the sludge zone, it is permanently removed. 5. No resuspension of settled sludge. 6. Discrete settling (Type I); no flocculation. **Derivation of fractional removal efficiency:** Refer to Section 3.3 of these notes for the complete derivation. Final result: $$\eta = 1 - F(v_o) + \frac{1}{v_o} \int_{0}^{v_o} v_p \, dF$$ For a continuous distribution, this can be approximated as a summation over the particle size classes. #### Model Solution to Q-B(b): Given $v_o = 20 \text{ m/d}$, the cutoff velocity is 20 m/d. All particles with $v_p \geq 20$ m/d are 100% removed. Particles with $v_p < 20$ m/d are partially removed in proportion to $v_p / v_o$. **Step 1 — Identify particles with $v_p \geq 20$ m/d** (fully removed): Particles at $v_p = 20$ m/d (fraction 0.10) and $v_p = 24$ m/d (fraction 0.10) are completely removed. Fully removed fraction = $0.10 + 0.10 = 0.20$ **Step 2 — Compute partial removal for $v_p < 20$ m/d:** For each class, the removed fraction is $(v_p / v_o) \times \Delta F$: | $v_p$ (m/d) | $\Delta F$ | $v_p / v_o$ | Removed = $(v_p/v_o) \cdot \Delta F$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 4 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.020 | | 8 | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.080 | | 12 | 0.30 | 0.60 | 0.180 | | 16 | 0.20 | 0.80 | 0.160 | | **Sum** | | | **0.440** | **Step 3 — Compute total removal efficiency:** $$\eta = 0.20 + 0.440 = 0.64 = \mathbf{64\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Many students forget to **add the 100%-removed fraction** (particles with $v_p \geq v_o$) to the partial-removal contribution. Always sum both. > - Do not use $v_p / v_o$ for particles *above* the cutoff — use 1.0 instead. > - Always **re-check the SOR limit** at the end of the problem; KTU examiners often award a 1-mark grace for confirming the design constraint. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Sedimentation" — KTU Module 2** - ✅ **Sedimentation** is a **gravity-based** solid-liquid separation process; it is the **second** unit operation in conventional water treatment. - ✅ The four settling types are: **Discrete (I)**, **Flocculent (II)**, **Hindered / Zone (III)**, and **Compression (IV)** — learn one example for each. - ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = g(s-1)d^2 / 18\nu$ — valid for $Re < 1$, small particles ($d < 0.1$ mm), laminar regime. - ✅ **Newton's Law:** $v_s = \sqrt{4g(s-1)d / 3C_D}$ — valid for $Re > 1000$, $C_D \approx 0.4$, coarse particles. - ✅ **Always verify the Reynolds number** before applying any settling law — a common KTU exam trap. - ✅ **Hazen's Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $v_o = Q/A_s$ — this is the **single most important design parameter** for ideal basins. - ✅ **Removal efficiency (Type I):** All particles with $v_s \geq v_o$ are 100% removed; fractional removal = $v_p / v_o$ for $v_p < v_o$. - ✅ **Detention time** is typically **2 to 4 hours** for water treatment; **weir loading rate** < 300 m³/m·d. - ✅ **SOR controls area**; **detention time controls depth**; **length-to-breadth ratio** is typically **4:1**. - ✅ **Type II flocculent settling** follows a power law: $v_s \propto t^{-\beta}$; removal is *higher* than discrete settling because particles grow. - ✅ **Zone settling** uses **solids flux theory** (Dick & Ewing) for thickener design — $G_i = v_i C_i + v_s C_i$. - ✅ Design the tank for **maximum flow** (e.g., 1.8 × average daily flow for water treatment). - ✅ Provide **inlet and outlet baffles** to dampen inlet jet turbulence and prevent short-circuiting. - ✅ Typical dimensions: depth 3–5 m, horizontal velocity < 5 mm/s, length 15–90 m. - ✅ Sludge is removed via **scrapers, hoppers, or vacuum tubes**; typical sludge concentration = 1–2% solids. > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU theory questions almost always involve either (a) derivation of Stokes' Law, or (b) design of a sedimentation tank given $Q$, $t_d$, $H$, and $L:B$ ratio. Be prepared to compute SOR and to compare it against the prescribed limit.

topics.title: Analysis of Discrete and Flocculent Settling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Discrete and Flocculent Settling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Sedimentation — The Foundational Process

Sedimentation is the **gravity-driven separation of suspended solids from a liquid** by exploiting the density difference between the particle and the surrounding fluid. In any municipal or industrial water/wastewater treatment plant, sedimentation tanks (clarifiers) are the workhorses that follow coagulation–flocculation. KTU Module 2 focuses on the **theoretical analysis** of what happens inside these tanks for two specific settling regimes: **Discrete (Type I) Settling** and **Flocculent (Type II) Settling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET861 Module 2):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to *"analyze the settling behavior of particles in quiescent water using Stokes' Law, Newton's Law, and the settling column test for both discrete and flocculent suspensions."*

## 1.2 Formal KTU Definitions

| Settling Type | KTU Definition | Particle Behavior |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Discrete (Type I)** | Settling of **non-flocculating** particles in a dilute suspension where each particle settles **independently** without interaction with neighbors. | Size, shape, and density **remain constant**. Path is **straight vertical**. |
| **Flocculent (Type II)** | Settling of **flocculating** particles in a dilute-to-medium suspension where particles **coalesce into larger aggregates** as they descend. | Size **increases with depth/time**. Effective settling velocity **increases downward**. Path is **still vertical** but velocity is depth-dependent. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Examiner's Favourite!):**
> The fundamental difference is **particle–particle interaction**. In *discrete* settling, the suspension is so dilute (< ~1000 mg/L) that neighbouring particles never touch. In *flocculent* settling, the concentration is higher and Van der Waals / electrostatic forces cause agglomeration.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Discrete Settling — The "Marbles in Honey" Analogy:**
Imagine dropping thousands of small, identical, **non-sticky marbles** into a deep, viscous jar. Each marble falls straight down at its own terminal speed, completely unaware of its neighbours. The marbles at the centre and at the walls reach the bottom at the same time. *This is Type I settling — clean, predictable, governed purely by fluid dynamics.*

**Flocculent Settling — The "Snowflake in a Cloud" Analogy:**
Now picture snowflakes falling through a moist cloud. As a tiny crystal descends, it collides with other crystals and water droplets, **growing heavier and falling faster** the longer it stays in the air. A snowflake that started near the top of the cloud arrives at the ground as a large, fluffy aggregate. *This is Type II settling — the deeper the particle goes, the faster it travels.*

## 1.4 Governing Physical Constants (Highlighted for Quick Revision)

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at 20 °C: **ρ_w = 998.2 kg/m³**
- Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C: **μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m² (Pa·s)**
- Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C: **ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- Typical sand particle diameter: **0.1 – 1.0 mm**
- Typical floc diameter: **0.01 – 5.0 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Forces Acting on a Settling Particle

When a single spherical particle falls through a stagnant fluid under gravity, three forces come into equilibrium to produce a **constant terminal velocity (v_s)**:

1. **Gravity (Weight) Force, F_g**
 The downward pull of the Earth on the particle.
 $F_g = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot V_p$
 where $V_p$ is the particle volume = $\frac{\pi d^3}{6}$ for a sphere.

2. **Buoyancy Force, F_b**
 The upward push of the displaced fluid (Archimedes' Principle).
 $F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_p$

3. **Hydrodynamic Drag Force, F_D**
 The resistance offered by the fluid to the particle's motion. This depends on the **Reynolds Number** of the settling particle.

At terminal velocity, the net downward force equals the drag:

$$\rho_s g V_p - \rho_w g V_p = F_D$$

$$\underbrace{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g V_p}_{\text{Net Submerged Weight}} = \underbrace{F_D}_{\text{Drag}}$$

## 2.2 The Three Regimes of Drag — Why Stokes, Newton, and Transition Coexist

The drag force expression depends entirely on the **Particle Reynolds Number**:

$$Re_p = \frac{v_s \, d \, \rho_w}{\mu}$$

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Drag Law | Settling Velocity Formula |
|--------|-----------------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Laminar (Stokes' Law)** | $Re_p < 1$ | $F_D = 3\pi \mu d v_s$ | $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18\mu}$ |
| **Transition (Intermediate)** | $1 < Re_p < 10^4$ | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p} + \frac{3}{\sqrt{Re_p}} + 0.34$ | Solved iteratively |
| **Turbulent (Newton's Law)** | $Re_p > 10^4$ | $C_D \approx 0.4$ (constant) | $v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d}{3 C_D \rho_w}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the drag law so different in each regime?**
> - In the **laminar** regime, viscous forces dominate. Fluid streams around the particle smoothly.
> - In the **turbulent** regime, inertial forces dominate. A low-pressure wake forms behind the particle, and the drag coefficient becomes essentially constant.
> - In the **transition** regime, both effects matter, requiring an empirical composite equation (often attributed to Schiller–Naumann or to von Karman).

## 2.3 Stokes' Law — The Cornerstone of Discrete Settling Analysis

For the **laminar regime** (the regime most relevant to water-treatment particles of 10–100 μm), substituting $V_p = \pi d^3/6$ and $F_D = 3\pi \mu d v_s$ into the force balance:

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_w)g = 3\pi \mu d v_s$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \frac{g\,(\rho_s - \rho_w)\,d^2}{18\,\mu}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> Notice that **v_s ∝ d²**. Doubling the particle diameter **quadruples** its settling velocity. This is why coagulation–flocculation (which grows particle size) is so effective before sedimentation.

## 2.4 Newton's Law — For Coarse Sand and Grit

For larger particles (grit, sand) where inertial drag dominates and $C_D$ is approximately constant at **0.4**, force balance gives:

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_w)g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g\,(\rho_s - \rho_w)\,d}{3\,C_D\,\rho_w}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This equation is used to design **grit chambers** in wastewater treatment plants. The goal is to settle out particles of diameter ≥ **0.2 mm** (sand) while keeping organic matter suspended.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Terminal settling velocity (Stokes)** | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | Discre

topics.title: Sedimentation Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sedimentation** is a physical water treatment process in which suspended particles having a specific gravity greater than that of water (typically $\rho_s \geq 1.0$ to $1.2 \text{ g/cm}^3$ for inorganic silts, and $\rho_s \approx 1.02$ to $1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$ for organic flocs) are removed from the water column by gravitational settling under quiescent or near-quiescent hydraulic conditions.

A **Sedimentation Tank** (also called a **Settling Tank**, **Clarifier**, or **Sedimentation Basin**) is the engineered hydraulic structure that provides the controlled retention time, cross-sectional area, and flow regime required to achieve this particle–water separation as a unit operation in a water or wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET861 Module 2):**
> Sedimentation is studied under the category of **"Suspended Solids Removal — Primary Treatment Unit Operation"** and is a mandatory precursor to **Coagulation–Flocculation** and **Filtration** in the conventional water treatment train (Screening → Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sand–Water Bucket" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you fill a transparent glass bucket with muddy river water and leave it untouched on a windowsill for 2 hours. The heavier sand and silt particles slowly drift downward and form a dense layer at the bottom, while the clearer water rises to the top. Now scale this simple glass bucket up to the size of a swimming pool, give it a controlled inlet (to spread incoming water without stirring the bottom sludge), a controlled outlet (a weir that draws the *clearest* water from the top), and a sludge scraper at the bottom — **that is exactly what a sedimentation tank is**.

The three engineering challenges the tank must solve are:

1. **Distribute** the incoming water evenly across the entire cross-section (so no shortcut "jet" of dirty water shoots straight to the outlet).
2. **Hold** the water long enough for particles to fall to the bottom (this is called **detention time**, measured in hours).
3. **Collect** the clarified water from the top **and** the settled sludge from the bottom without re-suspending it.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Sedimentation (Foundational Typology)

| Type | Description | Typical Particle | Governing Law |
|------|-------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Type 1 — Discrete Settling** | Particles settle as individual units, no flocculation | Sand, silt (> ~100 µm) | **Stokes' Law** |
| **Type 2 — Flocculent Settling** | Particles coalesce and grow in mass as they settle | Chemical flocs (Al/Fe hydroxides) | Empirical settling curves |
| **Type 3 — Hindered / Zone Settling** | High concentration; particles settle as a blanket | Activated sludge, lime softening | Solids flux theory |
| **Type 4 — Compression Settling** | Very high concentration; compaction at the bottom | Thickened sludge | Empirical (no simple law) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **water treatment plants (drinking water)**, sedimentation tanks typically handle **Type 1** and **Type 2** settling. In **wastewater treatment plants**, they handle **Type 3** and **Type 4** (in secondary clarifiers).

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Categorization of Sedimentation Tanks (By Shape)

1. **Rectangular Sedimentation Tank** — Long, narrow, with horizontal flow.
2. **Circular / Radial-Flow Sedimentation Tank** — Cylindrical with central feed or peripheral feed.
3. **Square Sedimentation Tank** — Compromise design; used in smaller plants.
4. **Hopper-Bottom / Conical Tank** — Used when sludge is removed by gravity through the conical bottom (e.g., in **Pulsator Clarifiers** and **Tube Settlers**).

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Design Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference values (must be **memorized** for KTU board exam):
> - **Design horizontal velocity in rectangular tanks:** $v_h = 0.3 \text{ m/min}$ (typically $\leq 1 \text{ m/min}$).
> - **Typical detention time:** $t_d = 2$ to $4 \text{ hours}$ (water treatment) ; $1.5$ to $2.5 \text{ hours}$ (wastewater primary).
> - **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $v_o = 15$ to $30 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$ (water), $30$ to $50 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$ (wastewater).
> - **Weir Loading Rate (WLR):** $\leq 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}/\text{day}$ (IS: 8419, CPHEEO Manual norms).
> - **Freeboard:** $0.3 \text{ m}$ minimum above the water surface.
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
> - **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 4°C, maximum).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Design Philosophy — The Three Pillars

A sedimentation tank is governed by **three independent hydraulic constraints**, and the design must satisfy the **largest** of the three:

### Pillar 1 — Surface Overflow Rate (SOR), $v_o$

This represents the **upward-equivalent velocity** that a particle must exceed in order to be carried *out* of the tank. Any particle whose terminal settling velocity $v_s \geq v_o$ will be removed with 100% efficiency (under ideal plug-flow conditions).

$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

where $Q$ is the design discharge and $A_s$ is the **plan area (surface area)** of the tank.

### Pillar 2 — Detention Time (Hydraulic Retention Time), $t_d$

The average time a water parcel spends inside the tank.

$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A_s \cdot H}{Q}$$

where $V$ is the volume, $H$ is the effective sidewater depth.

### Pillar 3 — Weir Loading Rate (WLR)

The discharge per unit length of the outlet weir. Prevents short-circuiting near the outlet.

$$\text{WLR} = \frac{Q}{L_w} \leq 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}/\text{day}$$

---

## 2.2 Discrete Settling — Stokes' Law Derivation Foundation

For a spherical particle settling under gravity in a quiescent viscous fluid, force balance gives:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

$$\tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g - \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g - 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s = 0$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** — valid only for laminar flow ($Re \leq 1$) and $d \leq 0.1$ mm. Beyond this, transition law or Newton's law applies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When given a particle size, density, and water temperature, examiners expect the student to compute $v_s$ using Stokes' law and then compare it to the design SOR $v_o$ to determine whether the particle is **captured** or **escapes** the tank.

---

## 2.3 Type 2 Settling — Fractional Removal Concept

Because flocs grow in size as they descend, a single particle doesn't have a single $v_s$. Engineers use **fractional removal curves** (also called **isoremoval plots** or **Camp's curves**).

For a tank of depth $H$ and surface area $A_s$, a particle with settling velocity $v_s$ has **probability of removal**:

$$P = 1 - p_o \quad \text{where} \quad p_o = 1 - \frac{v_s}{v_o}$$

(ideal plug flow; Hazen's theory).

More generally, for a floc with settling velocity ranging from $v_s$ to $v_s + dv_s$ and a frequency $f(v_s) dv_s$:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \int_0^{v_o} \frac{v_s}{v_o} f(v_s) \, dv_s + \int_{v_o}^{\infty} f(v_s) \, dv_s$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Mandatory Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table — Every equation you must memorize for the KTU ESE under PECET861 Module 2.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) | $v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{d}$ or $\text{m/d}$ | "Overflow velocity" |
| 2 | Detention Time | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A_s \cdot H}{Q}$ | hours | $V$ = tank volume |
| 3 | Weir Loading Rate (WLR) | $\text{WLR} = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{m}/\text{d}$ | $L_w$ = total weir length |
| 4 | Stokes' Settling Velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Laminar regime only |
| 5 | Reynolds Number of particle | $Re = \dfrac{v_s d}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\leq 1$ for Stokes |
| 6 | Drag coefficient (Stokes) | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re}$ | dimensionless | Spherical particles |
| 7 | Sludge storage volume | $V_{\text{sludge}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C \cdot t_c}{\rho_s \cdot (1-p)}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | $C$ = SS conc., $t_c$ = cleaning interval |
| 8 | Tank length (rectangular) | $L = v_h \cdot t_d$ | m | $v_h$ = horiz. velocity |
| 9 | Tank width (rectangular) | $B = \dfrac{Q}{v_h \cdot H}$ | m | |
| 10 | Circular tank diameter | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_s}{\pi}}$ | m | Radial-flow |
| 11 | Hazen fractional removal | $P = 1 - p_o$ ; $p_o = 1 - v_s/v_o$ | dimensionless | Discrete settling |
| 12 | Critical settling velocity (Type 2) | $v_c = \dfrac{H}{t_d} = \dfrac{H \cdot v_o}{H} = v_o$ | $\text{m/d}$ | For ideal tank |
| 13 | Efficiency of ideal settling basin | $\eta = \dfrac{v_s}{v_o}$ | dimensionless | $v_s \leq v_o$ |
| 14 | Surface area from SOR | $A_s = \dfrac{Q}{v_o}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Core design equation |
| 15 | Volume of sludge | $V = Q \cdot C \cdot t_c \cdot \dfrac{100}{S \cdot (100 - M)}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | $S$ = sp.gr., $M$ = moisture% |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drinking water treatment:** Plain sedimentation (without coagulants) removes only large sand/silt; chemically-aided sedimentation removes turbidity (NTU) down to 1–5 NTU before filtration.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Primary clarifiers remove 50–70% of suspended solids and 25–40% of BOD before biological treatment.
- **Industrial water treatment:** Pre-treatment for cooling towers, boilers, and RO systems — protects downstream membranes from fouling.
- **Stormwater management:** Used in detention/retention basins for first-flush pollutant removal.

---

## 2.6 Design Criteria Summary (CPHEEO Manual, India)

> [!NOTE]
> **CPHEEO Design Norms for Sedimentation Tanks:**
> - **Detention time:** 2 to 4 hours (water treatment)
> - **Surface loading rate:** 20 to 40 m³/m²/day
> - **Weir loading rate:** $\leq 300$ m³/m/day
> - **Depth of tank:** 3.0 to 4.5 m (water), 2.5 to 3.5 m (wastewater)
> - **Length : Width ratio (rectangular):** 4 : 1 to 5 : 1
> - **Bottom slope (rectangular):** 1 in 100 to 1 in 200 toward sludge hopper
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law from First Principles

### Starting Premise
A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_s$ settles through a fluid of density $\rho_w$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ at terminal velocity $v_s$ (constant velocity = net force = 0).

### Step 1: Identify the Three Forces

1. **Gravitational force (downward):**
   $$F_g = m_p \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \cdot g$$

2. **Buoyant force (upward) by Archimedes' principle:**
   $$F_b = \rho_w \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \cdot g$$

3. **Viscous drag force (upward, opposing motion) for laminar flow around a sphere — Stokes' drag law:**
   $$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$$

### Step 2: Equilibrium Condition

At terminal velocity, the net force on the particle is zero:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\rho_s \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 g - \rho_w \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 g - 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s = 0$$

### Step 3: Isolate the Settling Velocity

Factor out common terms:

$$\tfrac{\pi d^3 g}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu d \, v_s$$

Cancel $d$ from both sides (since $d > 0$):

$$\tfrac{\pi d^2 g}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \mu \, v_s$$

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:

$$\tfrac{d^2 g}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \mu \, v_s$$

Divide both sides by $3\mu$:

$$v_s = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{18 \mu}$$

### Step 4: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\, v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu} \,}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, valid when:
- $Re = \dfrac{v_s d}{\nu} \leq 1$ (laminar)
- Particle is smooth and spherical
- No interaction with other particles (dilute suspension)
- No wall effects

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Overflow Rate & Critical Particle Size

### Starting Premise
A rectangular sedimentation tank of length $L$, width $B$, and water depth $H$ receives a discharge $Q$ with horizontal velocity $v_h$. We want the diameter $d_{\min}$ of the smallest particle that is **always** removed (100% capture).

### Step 1: Travel Time of Water Through the Tank

$$t_{\text{travel}} = \frac{L}{v_h}$$

### Step 2: Detention Time Available for Settling

$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{L \cdot B \cdot H}{Q}$$

### Step 3: Relationship Between $v_h$ and $v_o$

By continuity, the horizontal flow velocity is:

$$v_h = \frac{Q}{B \cdot H}$$

And the **surface overflow rate** is:

$$v_o = \frac{Q}{B \cdot L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{v_h}{v_o} = \frac{L}{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_h = v_o \cdot \frac{L}{H}$$

### Step 4: Settling Path for the Critical Particle

A particle entering at the water surface at the inlet must travel:
- Horizontally: $L$ meters
- Vertically: $H$ meters (to reach the bottom)

The time available to settle is the **detention time** $t_d$. For the critical particle, this time must exactly equal the time to fall through depth $H$:

$$t_{\text{settle}} = \frac{H}{v_s} \leq t_d$$

### Step 5: Critical Settling Velocity

Setting the time to settle equal to the detention time gives the **critical settling velocity**:

$$v_s^{\text{crit}} = \frac{H}{t_d} = \frac{H \cdot Q}{A_s \cdot H} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = v_o$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\, v_s^{\text{crit}} = v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} \,}$$

This is a **beautiful result**: the smallest particle that is *guaranteed* to be removed has a settling velocity exactly equal to the SOR. All particles with $v_s \geq v_o$ are 100% captured; all particles with $v_s < v_o$ are partially captured.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hazen's Ideal Fractional Removal Model

### Starting Premise
Consider a continuously-fed, ideal plug-flow tank. Particles enter uniformly across the entire water depth and are uniformly distributed. The fractional removal of a particle class with settling velocity $v_s$ is the ratio of the time it takes to settle the full depth to the detention time.

### Step 1: Removal Probability of a Single Particle Class

A particle entering at depth $y$ from the surface (with $0 \leq y \leq H$) is removed if it settles the full distance $y$ to the bottom before the water exits:

$$t_{\text{settle}} = \frac{y}{v_s} \leq t_d$$

$$\Rightarrow y \leq v_s \cdot t_d$$

The fraction of particles that are **removed** equals the fraction of the depth that is "shallow enough":

$$p_{\text{removed}} = \frac{v_s \cdot t_d}{H} = \frac{v_s}{v_o}$$

### Step 2: Fractional Removal

$$\boxed{\, \eta = \frac{v_s}{v_o} \quad \text{for} \quad v_s \leq v_o \,}$$

And $\eta = 1$ (100%) for $v_s \geq v_o$.

### Step 3: Overall Removal for a Distribution

If the influent contains a size distribution with frequency $f(v_s)$ (i.e., the fraction of particles with settling velocity between $v_s$ and $v_s + dv_s$ is $f(v_s) dv_s$), then the **overall removal efficiency** is:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \int_0^{v_o} \frac{v_s}{v_o} f(v_s) \, dv_s + \int_{v_o}^{\infty} f(v_s) \, dv_s$$

This integral is the foundation of **Camp's settling analysis** for flocculent suspensions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (14-mark variant):**
> A water treatment plant serves a population of 1,00,000 with per-capita water demand of 150 Lpcd. The sedimentation tank is rectangular with a depth of 3.5 m and detention time of 4 hours. The length-to-width ratio is 4:1. Determine:
> (a) The dimensions (L × B) of the tank.
> (b) The surface overflow rate.
> (c) Whether a particle of diameter 0.05 mm, density 2650 kg/m³ will be completely removed. Assume water temperature = 20°C.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute design discharge Q:**

$$Q = P \times q = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 150 \text{ L/day} = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

$$Q = 15{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ mL/day} = 15{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = \frac{15000}{24 \times 3600} = 0.1736 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of tank from detention time:**

$$V = Q \cdot t_d = 0.1736 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times (4 \times 3600) \text{ s} = 2500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Surface area from volume and depth:**

$$A_s = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{2500}{3.5} = 714.29 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Find L and B using L:B = 4:1:**

Let $B = x$ and $L = 4x$. Then:

$$L \cdot B = 4x^2 = 714.29 \Rightarrow x^2 = 178.57 \Rightarrow x = 13.36 \text{ m}$$

$$\therefore B \approx 13.36 \text{ m}, \quad L = 53.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Surface Overflow Rate:**

$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{15000}{714.29} = 21.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day} = 21.0 \text{ m/day}$$

Converting: $v_o = 21.0 / 86400 = 2.43 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 6 — Settling velocity of the particle (Stokes' Law):**

Given: $d = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$, $\rho_s = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\mu_{\text{water @ 20°C}} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$.

$$v_s = \frac{(9.81)(2650 - 1000)(5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_s = \frac{(9.81)(1650)(2.5 \times 10^{-9})}{0.018036}$$

$$v_s = \frac{4.0466 \times 10^{-5}}{0.018036} = 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert $v_s$ to m/day:**

$$v_s = 2.244 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \times 86400 \text{ s/day} = 193.9 \text{ m/day}$$

**Step 8 — Compare with SOR:**

$$v_s = 193.9 \text{ m/day} \quad \text{vs} \quad v_o = 21.0 \text{ m/day}$$

Since $v_s \gg v_o$, the particle is **completely removed** with very high margin. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 50-micron sand particle (very fine sand) is captured with 9× the safety margin. The design is conservative.

---

## 3.5 Validation — Reynolds Number Check

To verify Stokes' Law applies:

$$Re = \frac{v_s d}{\nu} = \frac{(2.244 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-5})}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.112$$

Since $Re = 0.112 \ll 1$, **Stokes' regime is confirmed valid**. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rectangular Sedimentation Tank — Longitudinal Section (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INF["INFLOW CHANNEL\nRaw water inlet"]

    SET["SETTLING ZONE\nLength L, Depth H\nHorizontal flow vh\nDetention time td"]
    OUTLET["OUTLET LAUNDER\nV-notch weirs\nWLR < 300 m3/m/d"]

    SLUDGE["SLUDGE HOPPER\nBottom collection\n1:100 to 1:200 slope"]
    SCRAPER["CHAIN SCRAPER\nSlow-moving blades\n0.3 to 0.6 m/min"]
    SLUDGEOUT["SLUDGE OUTLET\nPumping to sludge\ntreatment plant"]

    INF --> INLET
    INLET --> SET
    SET --> OUTLET
    OUTLET --> EFF
    SET -.-> SLUDGE
    SLUDGE --> SCRAPER
    SCRAPER --> SLUDGEOUT

Sludge settles to the bottom, is collected in a hopper, and is **scraped** toward the sludge outlet by a slow-moving chain-and-flight scraper.
