# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) of a sedimentation tank. State its design value as per IS/CPHEEO norms for a municipal water treatment plant.

**Model Answer:**
The **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)**, also called **overflow velocity** or **critical settling velocity**, is defined as the volumetric flow rate per unit of plan (surface) area of the sedimentation tank.

$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

where $Q$ is the design discharge (m³/day) and $A_s$ is the surface area of the tank (m²). Numerically, it represents the upward-equivalent velocity above which a particle will be carried out of the tank without settling. As per **CPHEEO Manual norms**, the design value of SOR for a municipal water treatment plant is **20 to 40 m³/m²/day** (typical design value: **30 m³/m²/day**).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

### Q2. Differentiate between Type 1 (discrete) and Type 2 (flocculent) settling.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Type 1 — Discrete | Type 2 — Flocculent |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Particle behavior | Settles as individual unit, no change in size/mass | Particle grows in size by flocculation while settling |
| Settling velocity | Constant (terminal $v_s$ from Stokes' Law) | Variable — increases with depth |
| Governing analysis | Hazen's simple $v_s/v_o$ ratio | Camp's fractional removal curves from settling column tests |
| Typical application | Plain sedimentation of sand/silt | Coagulated water with chemical flocs |
| Example particles | > 100 µm sand grains | Al(OH)₃ or Fe(OH)₃ flocs |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 - Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat functional sketch, describe the working of a **rectangular sedimentation tank** with horizontal flow. Explain the role of the inlet and outlet baffles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedimentation tank handles a flow of $25 \text{ ML/d}$ with a detention time of 4 hours. The depth of water is 3.5 m. Compute:
   1. The surface overflow rate (SOR)
   2. The plan area of the tank
   3. The length and width of the tank if L:B = 4:1
   4. The weir length if two outlet launders (one on each side) are provided, each of length equal to the tank width. Verify the weir loading rate is within CPHEEO limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*(Description, sketch, and roles are to be written in the answer sheet. Key points:)*

A rectangular sedimentation tank is a long narrow basin (L:B = 4:1 to 5:1) with horizontal flow. Raw water enters at one end through an **inlet baffle** (a perforated wall or diffuser) that distributes the flow uniformly across the entire cross-section, preventing the formation of high-velocity inlet "jets" that would disturb the settling zone. The water then flows slowly (typically $v_h = 0.3$ m/min) through the **settling zone**, where suspended particles settle to the bottom under gravity. The clarified water exits at the other end over an **outlet weir launder** equipped with V-notch weirs, which ensures that the *clearest* water (drawn from the top) is collected. The outlet launder may be preceded by an **outlet baffle** (a submerged wall) that prevents floating scum from escaping.

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 25 \text{ ML/d} = 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$
- $t_d = 4 \text{ hours}$
- $H = 3.5 \text{ m}$
- L:B = 4:1
- Two outlet launders, each of length $B$

**Step 1: Compute Volume of the Tank**
$$V = Q \times t_d = 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times \frac{4}{24} \text{ d} = 4166.67 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Surface Area of the Tank**
$$A_s = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{4166.67}{3.5} = 1190.48 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Surface Overflow Rate**
$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{25000}{1190.48} = 21.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{d}$$

*(Acceptable: 21.0 m/d — within CPHEEO range of 20–40 m³/m²/d ✅)*

**Step 4: Length and Width (L:B = 4:1)**
Let $B = x$ and $L = 4x$:
$$L \times B = 4x^2 = 1190.48$$
$$x^2 = 297.62 \implies x = 17.25 \text{ m}$$

$$\therefore B \approx 17.25 \text{ m}, \quad L = 69.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Total Weir Length**
$$L_w = 2 \times B = 2 \times 17.25 = 34.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Weir Loading Rate (WLR)**
$$\text{WLR} = \frac{Q}{L_w} = \frac{25000}{34.5} = 724.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}/\text{d}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL — COMMON MISTAKE ALERT:**
> The calculated WLR = 724.6 m³/m/d **EXCEEDS** the CPHEEO limit of 300 m³/m/d. The design is **NOT acceptable** with only two launders.
> **Correction required:** Provide **5 launders** of length $B$ each (or increase weir length to at least $L_w \geq 25000/300 = 83.3 \text{ m}$). Many students lose **2 marks** here by not validating the WLR against the standard limit.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** What is **Stokes' Law**? State its assumptions. A spherical sand particle of diameter 0.08 mm and specific gravity 2.65 settles in water at 20°C. Compute its terminal settling velocity. Take dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C = $1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular sedimentation tank has a diameter of 20 m and a water depth of 3.0 m. The flow rate is 8 MLD. Calculate:
   1. The detention time
   2. The surface overflow rate
   3. Whether a particle with $v_s = 1.5 \text{ m/hr}$ is completely removed.
   4. The fraction of the same particle class that is removed (using Hazen's ideal model). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Stokes' Law Definition:**
The terminal settling velocity $v_s$ of a spherical particle falling under gravity in a viscous fluid under laminar flow conditions is given by:

$$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is **smooth, rigid, and spherical**.
2. Flow around the particle is **laminar** ($Re \leq 1$).
3. Particle settles in an **infinite, quiescent fluid** (no wall effects).
4. Suspension is **dilute** — no particle–particle interaction.
5. Constant gravitational and buoyancy forces (constant $g$, $\rho_w$).

**Calculation:**

Given: $d = 0.08 \text{ mm} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$, $G_s = 2.65 \Rightarrow \rho_s = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (8 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times 1650 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-9}}{0.018036}$$

$$v_s = \frac{1.0359 \times 10^{-4}}{0.018036} = 5.744 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Convert to m/hr:**
$$v_s = 5.744 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \times 3600 = 20.68 \text{ m/hr}$$

**Validity Check — Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{v_s d}{\nu} = \frac{(5.744 \times 10^{-3})(8 \times 10^{-5})}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.458$$

Since $Re = 0.458 < 1$, **Stokes' Law is valid**. ✅

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $D = 20 \text{ m}$, $H = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $Q = 8 \text{ MLD} = 8000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$.

**Step 1: Surface Area and Volume**
$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$
$$V = A_s \times H = 314.16 \times 3.0 = 942.48 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Detention Time**
$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{942.48 \text{ m}^3}{8000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}} = 0.11781 \text{ d} = 2.83 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Surface Overflow Rate**
$$v_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{8000}{314.16} = 25.46 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{d} = 25.46 \text{ m/d}$$

Converting: $v_o = 25.46 / 24 = 1.061 \text{ m/hr}$

**Step 4: Is the particle completely removed?**

Given: $v_s = 1.5 \text{ m/hr}$, and $v_o = 1.061 \text{ m/hr}$.

Since $v_s = 1.5 \text{ m/hr} > v_o = 1.061 \text{ m/hr}$, the particle **IS completely removed** (100% capture). ✅

**Step 5: Fractional Removal (Hazen's model)**

If we hypothetically had $v_s < v_o$, fractional removal would be:
$$\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_o} = \frac{1.5}{1.061} = 1.41$$

Since $\eta > 1$ (clipped to 1), the removal is **100%** — consistent with Step 4.

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:**
> Students often confuse $v_o$ in m/day with $v_s$ in m/hr. **Always convert to consistent units** (m/day vs m/day OR m/s vs m/s) before comparing. Mixing units leads to a **wrong conclusion of incomplete removal** and costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 🧾 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Sedimentation = gravity-driven removal of suspended particles; **the 2nd unit operation** in conventional water treatment (after screening, before filtration).
- ✅ **Three hydraulic design pillars:** Surface Overflow Rate (SOR), Detention Time ($t_d$), Weir Loading Rate (WLR).
- ✅ **Core equation triplet:** $v_o = Q/A_s$ ; $t_d = V/Q$ ; $\text{WLR} = Q/L_w$.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2 / 18\mu$ — valid only for **laminar flow** ($Re \leq 1$).
- ✅ **Critical particle size:** Particle is 100% removed iff $v_s \geq v_o$ (Hazen's criterion).
- ✅ **CPHEEO design values:** $t_d = 2$–$4$ h, $\text{SOR} = 20$–$40$ m³/m²/d, $\text{WLR} \leq 300$ m³/m/d, $H = 3$–$4.5$ m.
- ✅ **Tank shapes:** Rectangular (large plants, $L:B = 4:1$), Circular (radial flow, center or peripheral feed), Square (small plants).
- ✅ **Inlet baffle** distributes flow uniformly; **outlet weir launder** collects clearest water; **sludge hopper** stores settled solids.
- ✅ **Sludge removal:** Chain-and-flight scraper (rectangular) or rotating bridge scraper (circular).
- ✅ **Type 1 settling** uses Stokes' + Hazen; **Type 2 settling** uses Camp's isoremoval curves (from column tests).
- ✅ **Units trap:** Always convert $v_o$ and $v_s$ to **same units** (m/s or m/day) before comparison.
- ✅ **Freeboard:** Provide minimum **0.3 m** above design water surface.
- ✅ **Bottom slope (rectangular):** 1 in 100 to 1 in 200 toward sludge hopper.
- ✅ **Number of tanks:** Provide minimum **2 units** (one standby) for any plant > 5 MLD.
- ✅ **Velocity check:** Horizontal velocity in rectangular tank must be $< 1 \text{ m/min}$ to prevent re-suspension.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filtration: Types and Design of Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Filtration: Types and Design of Filters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Filtration** is a unit operation in water and wastewater engineering wherein a fluid (water) is passed through a **porous granular medium** to separate suspended and colloidal particles, producing a clarified effluent. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, filtration is positioned as the **tertiary polishing step** following coagulation–flocculation–sedimentation, targeting residual turbidity (typically reducing from **1–10 NTU** down to **< 0.5 NTU**). Mathematically, it is a coupled **fluid flow + particle transport** problem governed by **Darcy's Law** and **stochastic pore-channel mechanics**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Module 2):** "Filtration is the physical–chemical process of removing suspended particles from water by passing it through a granular bed, where removal occurs by **straining, sedimentation, diffusion, and adhesion** mechanisms within the pore channels." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine pouring muddy water through a kitchen colander lined with sand and gravel. The gravel holds the sand in place, the sand traps the dirt, and clean water trickles out. The deeper the sand, the cleaner the water — but the slower the flow. Now upscale this: - The **kitchen sink** → a small **slow sand filter** (used in rural water supply). - A **thick sand pile under pressure** → a **rapid sand filter** (used in urban municipal plants). - A **coffee filter with paper** → **membrane filtration / microfiltration** (modern advanced treatment). The **dirt gets trapped** in two ways: (1) by physical straining (large particles), and (2) by **sticking** to the sand grains (fine particles — same principle as dust sticking to a sticky surface). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A filter doesn't just *sieve* water — it's a **biological, physical, and chemical reactor** where each sand grain acts as a mini-collector. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Filtration Engineering | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Filtration rate | $v$ | 5 – 15 (rapid); 0.1 – 0.2 (slow) | m/h | | Effective size of sand | $d_{10}$ | 0.45 – 0.70 | mm | | Uniformity coefficient | $UC$ | 1.3 – 1.7 | – | | Filter bed depth | $L$ | 0.6 – 1.8 | m | | Head loss (clean bed) | $h_0$ | 0.3 – 0.6 | m | | Terminal head loss | $h_f$ | 2.5 – 3.0 | m | | Backwash rate | $v_{bw}$ | 36 – 60 | m/h | | Bed expansion | $E$ | 20 – 50 | % | | Filtration run time | $T$ | 24 – 72 | h | | Filtrate turbidity | – | < 0.5 | NTU | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Four Removal Mechanisms When water flows through a filter bed, particle removal occurs via **four simultaneous mechanisms**: 1. **Straining (Mechanical):** Particles larger than pore openings are physically intercepted. Effective for particles > pore throat size. 2. **Sedimentation (Inertial):** Particles settle into quiescent zones within the pore structure (Stokes' regime). Dominant for particles of size **1–10 µm**. 3. **Diffusion (Brownian):** Sub-micron particles undergo random Brownian motion and collide with grain surfaces. Dominant for particles **< 1 µm**. 4. **Adhesion / Attachment (Electrochemical):** Attractive **van der Waals forces** overcome repulsive **electrostatic double-layer forces** (per **DLVO theory**), allowing particles to stick to the filter media. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The **overall removal efficiency** is the *sum* of these mechanism-specific contributions. There is an **"optimum particle size"** (~1 µm) where removal is *minimum*, as both straining and diffusion mechanisms are individually weak here. This is the famous **Yao–Habibian–O'Melia (1971) filtration theory**. ## 2.2 Classification of Filters (KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus) ### A. Slow Sand Filters (SSF) - **Rate:** 0.1 – 0.2 m/h - **Mechanism:** Predominantly **biological** (Schmutzdecke layer) - **Cleaning:** Scraping top 1–2 cm of sand - **Application:** Rural communities, low-turbidity waters ### B. Rapid Sand Filters (Gravity Type) - **Rate:** 5 – 15 m/h - **Mechanism:** Physical–chemical straining + adhesion - **Cleaning:** Backwashing with water + air scour - **Application:** Municipal water treatment plants ### C. Pressure Sand Filters - Enclosed in a pressure vessel - Operates under 0.3 – 0.7 MPa - Used for industrial water, swimming pools, boiler feed ### D. Multimedia Filters - **Anthracite (top) + Sand (middle) + Garnet (bottom)** - Coarse-to-fine gradient → higher solids capacity - Operates at 15 – 25 m/h ### E. Membrane Filters - **MF (0.1–10 µm), UF (0.01–0.1 µm), NF, RO** - Absolute barrier; *not* a granular filter - Used for advanced reuse applications ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 ESE — MEMORIZE THE TABLE BELOW.** | # | Formula | Description | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = v \cdot A$ | Filtration discharge | Design Q | | 2 | $h = \dfrac{f \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v}{\phi^2 \cdot g \cdot d^2} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$ | **Kozeny–Carman equation** (clean bed head loss) | Filter hydraulics | | 3 | $h_f = \dfrac{36 \cdot k \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot L \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi^2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}$ | Rose's equation (filter run head loss with deposits) | Filter run time | | 4 | $h_L = h_0 + \dfrac{k \cdot v \cdot C \cdot t}{1 - k \cdot v \cdot C \cdot t \cdot (h_f - h_0)}$ | Iwasaki's equation (head loss vs time) | Filter clogging | | 5 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = -\lambda \cdot C$ | Filter coefficient equation | Removal efficiency | | 6 | $v_{mf} = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d^2}}{1800 \cdot \mu} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon^3 \cdot \phi^2}{(1-\varepsilon)^2}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity | Backwash design | | 7 | $L_e = L \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1-E) \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_0)}{1 - \varepsilon_0}$ | Expanded bed depth | Backwash design | | 8 | $UC = \dfrac{d_{60}}{d_{10}}$ | Uniformity coefficient | Media specification | | 9 | $v = \dfrac{1.79 \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s/\rho_w - 1)}{\mu}$ | Stoke's settling velocity check | Particle analysis | | 10 | $C/C_0 = \exp(-\lambda \cdot L)$ | Filter performance equation | Effluent quality | Where: - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) - $\rho_s$ = density of filter media (kg/m³) - $\rho_w$ = density of water (kg/m³) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $\varepsilon$ = porosity of bed (typically **0.40**) - $\phi$ = sphericity of grains (~0.8 for sand) - $k$ = deposit constant (depends on water quality) - $C$ = suspended solids concentration - $\lambda$ = filter coefficient (m⁻¹) - $E$ = fractional bed expansion (e.g., 0.30 for 30%) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Filtration is the **backbone of safe potable water supply** worldwide. Without effective filtration, pathogens (e.g., *Cryptosporidium*, *Giardia*) can slip through sedimentation. In modern **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** plants, filtration (especially UF + RO) is critical for water reuse. The **design of filters** determines: - **Plant footprint** (faster rate = smaller area) - **Energy cost** (backwash water + pumps) - **Treated water quality** (NTU, pathogen log-removal) - **Chemical consumption** (coagulant dose coupling) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kozeny–Carman Equation for Clean Bed Head Loss This derivation gives the **theoretical foundation** for filter hydraulics and is a KTU-favorite 7-mark derivation. ### Step 1: Start with Darcy's Law Darcy's law for flow through a porous bed: $$ Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{h}{L} $$ Where $K$ is the hydraulic conductivity, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $h$ is the head loss, and $L$ is the bed depth. ### Step 2: Express Velocity Filtration velocity is $v = Q/A$, so: $$ v = K \cdot \frac{h}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{v \cdot L}{K} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute Hydraulic Conductivity For laminar flow through granular media, the **Kozeny–Carman** form of $K$ is: $$ K = \frac{\rho_w \cdot g}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\phi^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}{180 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2} $$ The factor **180** combines the shape factor and empirical constants for flow around spheres. ### Step 4: Combine and Simplify Substituting $K$ into the Darcy equation: $$ h = \frac{v \cdot L \cdot \mu \cdot 180 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot \phi^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3} $$ ### Step 5: Define Friction Factor Define the friction factor $f = 150 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)/\text{Re}$ (for laminar regime) and rearrange: $$ \boxed{h = \frac{f \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi^2 \cdot g \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot \varepsilon^3 / \rho_w}} $$ Or equivalently, in standard form: $$ \boxed{\,h = \frac{f \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi^2 \cdot g \cdot d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Summary:** We transformed Darcy's macroscopic flow law into a **microstructurally-explicit** equation by accounting for grain shape ($\phi$), grain size ($d$), and pore fraction ($\varepsilon$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backwash Bed Expansion ### Step 1: Force Balance on an Expanded Particle At **minimum fluidization**, the upward drag force = effective weight of the particle: $$ \underbrace{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}}_{\text{Buoyancy-corrected weight}} = \underbrace{C_D \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_{mf}^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}}_{\text{Drag force}} $$ Where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (in laminar regime, $C_D = 24/\text{Re}$). ### Step 2: Cancel Common Terms Dividing both sides by $\pi d^2/4$: $$ (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{2d}{3} = C_D \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_{mf}^2}{2} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute Drag Coefficient For the laminar regime, $C_D = 24\mu/(\rho_w \cdot v_{mf} \cdot d)$: $$ (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{24\mu}{\rho_w \cdot v_{mf} \cdot d} \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_{mf}^2}{2} $$ ### Step 4: Simplify Algebraically Cancel $\rho_w$ and $v_{mf}$: $$ (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{12 \mu \cdot v_{mf}}{d} $$ ### Step 5: Solve for $v_{mf}$ $$ v_{mf} = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu} $$ But this must be **corrected for bed porosity** at fluidization. The standard result, known as the **Ergun equation** for fluidization, is: $$ \boxed{\,v_{mf} = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d^2 \cdot \phi^2 \cdot \varepsilon_{mf}^3}{180 \cdot \mu \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})^2}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** We equated **gravity + buoyancy** (downward) to **hydrodynamic drag** (upward) — the fundamental condition at incipient fluidization. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Filter Types | Parameter | Slow Sand | Rapid Sand (Gravity) | Multimedia | Pressure | Membrane (UF) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Filtration rate (m/h)** | 0.1 – 0.2 | 5 – 15 | 15 – 25 | 15 – 30 | 0.05 – 0.2 m³/m²·h | | **Mechanism** | Biological + physical | Physical-chemical | Physical-chemical | Physical-chemical | Sieving | | **Media size (mm)** | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.45 – 0.70 | 0.5 – 1.0 (anthr.) | 0.45 – 0.70 | 0.01 – 0.1 µm pores | | **Bed depth (m)** | 0.9 – 1.2 | 0.6 – 0.9 | 0.9 – 1.2 | 1.0 – 1.5 | – | | **Head loss (m)** | 0.1 – 0.2 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 5 – 15 | 1 – 5 bar | | **Run time (h)** | weeks–months | 24 – 72 | 24 – 72 | 24 – 48 | 1 – 4 (CIP) | | **Backwash** | Manual scraping | Water + air | Water + air | Water | Backpulse + CIP | | **Turbidity effluent (NTU)** | < 1 | < 0.5 | < 0.3 | < 0.5 | < 0.1 | | **Pathogen removal** | High (*E. coli* 99.9%) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Absolute (> 4 log) | | **Capital cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Very High | | **Footprint** | Very Large | Moderate | Moderate | Small | Small | | **Skill needed** | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Decision Rule:** Slow sand for **< 50 m³/d** in rural areas; rapid sand for **municipal plants**; pressure filter for **industrial process water**; multimedia where **high solids load** exists; membrane for **recycled water reuse**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Filter Sizing for a Municipal Plant > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern (5–7 marks):** "Design a rapid gravity filter for a town..." **Problem:** A town requires **45,000 m³/d** of filtered water. Design a rapid gravity filter with the following data: - Filtration rate $v = 6 \text{ m/h}$ - Number of filter units = 6 (with one standby) - Filter run = 24 h - Backwash rate = 40 m/h for 10 min **Solution:** ### Step 1: Total filtration area $$ A_{total} = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{45000/24}{6} = \frac{1875}{6} = 312.5 \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 2: Area per working filter (excluding standby) $$ A_{unit} = \frac{312.5}{5} = 62.5 \text{ m}^2 $$ ### Step 3: Dimensions of each filter (square) $$ L = B = \sqrt{62.5} \approx 7.91 \text{ m} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Adopt } 8 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m} $$ Actual area = 64 m² (slightly oversized). ### Step 4: Backwash water required $$ V_{bw} = 64 \times 40 \times \frac{10}{60} = 426.7 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per filter per backwash} $$ ### Step 5: Bed depth (assume UC = 1.5, $d_{10} = 0.5$ mm) $$ L_{bed} = 0.7 \text{ m} \; (\text{sand}) + 0.15 \text{ m} \; (\text{gravel}) = 0.85 \text{ m} $$ ### Step 6: Wash water trough height Set above expanded bed. With $E = 30\%$, $L_{expanded} = 0.7 \times 1.30 = 0.91$ m. Trough placed at **1.0 m** above the sand surface. > [!NOTE] > **Final Design Output:** 6 filters of **8 m × 8 m**, sand depth **0.7 m**, gravel **0.15 m**, wash water trough at **1.0 m**, backwash rate **40 m/h**, run time **24 h**, filtrate turbidity **< 0.5 NTU**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rapid Gravity Sand Filter ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent from sedimentation tank
Turbidity 1-10 NTU] --> B[Inlet channel with weir
Distributes water to filter] B --> C[Filter bed
Sand d10 = 0.5 mm
UC = 1.5
Depth = 0.7 m] C --> D[Supporting gravel layer
0.15 m
Graded 2-20 mm] D --> E[Underdrain system
Laterals with strainers
Air and water distribution] E --> F[Treated water reservoir
Clear well] F --> G[Disinfection and pumping] H[Backwash water pump
40 m/h rate] --> E I[Air scour blower
40-60 m/h] --> E E --> J[Wash water troughs
During backwash only] J --> K[Wash water drain
To recycling sump] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The forward path (top → bottom) shows the **filtration cycle**. The bottom three blocks (H, I, E, J, K) show the **backwash cycle** — the system is reversible. In actual plant, backwash is initiated when head loss reaches the terminal value **2.5–3.0 m**. ## 4.2 Filter Media Stratification — Mermaid Block View ```mermaid graph TD L1[Layer 1: Anthracite
d = 0.8-1.2 mm
Depth = 0.45 m
Density = 1400 kg/m3] L2[Layer 2: Silica Sand
d10 = 0.5 mm
UC = 1.5
Depth = 0.20 m
Density = 2650 kg/m3] L3[Layer 3: Garnet Sand
d = 0.2-0.3 mm
Depth = 0.10 m
Density = 4100 kg/m3] L4[Layer 4: Graded Gravel
20 mm to 2 mm
Depth = 0.45 m
Support function] L5[Layer 5: Underdrain
Strainer laterals
False floor bottom] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multimedia Filter Logic:** Heavy **garnet at bottom** (smallest grains), light **anthracite on top** (largest grains) → creates a **"coarse-to-fine"** flow path. This **inverts** the natural hydraulic stratification of a single-media filter, leading to **higher solids storage capacity** and longer filter runs. ## 4.3 Operational State Diagram — Filter Run Cycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> FilterInService FilterInService --> FilterInService: Normal operation
Head loss rising FilterInService --> TerminalHeadLoss: Head loss = 2.5-3.0 m FilterInService --> TurbidityBreakthrough: Effluent NTU > 0.5 TerminalHeadLoss --> BackwashMode TurbidityBreakthrough --> BackwashMode BackwashMode --> AirScour: 1-2 min
Air at 40-60 m/h AirScour --> WaterBackwash: 8-10 min
Water at 36-50 m/h WaterBackwash --> FilterSettling: 1-2 min quiescent FilterSettling --> FilterInService BackwashMode --> [*]: Filter taken offline for maintenance ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Trivia:** The total backwash sequence is typically **12–15 minutes** and uses **2–4%** of the total filtered water. This water is **recycled** to the plant head (after settling in a wash-water recovery basin). ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Membrane Filtration Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Water] --> B[MF
Microfiltration
0.1-10 um] B --> C[UF
Ultrafiltration
0.01-0.1 um] C --> D[NF
Nanofiltration
1-10 nm] D --> E[RO
Reverse Osmosis
0.1-1 nm] E --> F[Reuse quality water
Conductivity less than 50 uS/cm] G[Reject Stream] --> H[Brine concentrator
RO reject handling] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Process Selection Logic:** Each step removes progressively smaller contaminants. In ZLD plants, this hierarchy is the **core process train**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** > *List the four mechanisms of particle removal in a rapid sand filter. State the dominant mechanism for particles in the size range 1–10 µm.* **Model Answer:** The four mechanisms are: 1. **Straining** – physical interception of particles larger than pore throats. Dominant for particles **> 10 µm**. 2. **Sedimentation** – particles settle into the low-velocity zones of the pore structure. Dominant for particles in the range **1–10 µm**. 3. **Diffusion (Brownian)** – sub-micron particles diffuse across streamlines to grain surfaces. Dominant for particles **< 1 µm**. 4. **Adhesion** – electrostatic/van der Waals attachment onto grain surfaces; effective when DLVO attractive forces dominate. **For 1–10 µm particles, SEDIMENTATION is the dominant mechanism** (Yao–Habibian–O'Melia theory). > **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** > *Define "uniformity coefficient" of a filter sand. Why is a low UC preferred?* **Model Answer:** **Uniformity Coefficient (UC)** is defined as the ratio of the sieve size that passes **60%** of the sand to the sieve size that passes **10%** of the sand: $$ UC = \frac{d_{60}}{d_{10}} $$ A **low UC (1.3–1.7)** indicates that the sand grains are of **nearly uniform size**, which is preferred because: - **Prevents stratification** during backwash (small grains don't segregate into a fine top layer). - Ensures **uniform flow distribution** across the bed. - **Reduces short-circuiting** and increases filter run time. - Provides **predictable head loss** behavior. ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B numerical problems, students frequently lose **3 marks** by: > 1. Not converting units correctly (e.g., mm → m, hours → seconds). > 2. Confusing the friction factor $f$ with the Darcy–Weisbach factor. > 3. Forgetting to subtract **standby** filter area when computing units in service. > 4. Omitting units in the final answer. --- ### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **A.** *(a)* Explain with neat sketches the working of a **rapid gravity sand filter**. Discuss the role of the **schmutzdecke layer** and the **underdrain system** in filter performance. *(7 marks)* **A.** *(b)* A rapid sand filter has the following data: sand depth 0.7 m, $d_{10} = 0.5$ mm, UC = 1.5, porosity $\varepsilon = 0.40$, sphericity $\phi = 0.85$, filtration rate 6 m/h. Compute the **clean-bed head loss** at 20 °C. Take $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_w = 998$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Working of a Rapid Gravity Sand Filter:** A rapid sand filter is a **rectangular concrete tank** filled with graded sand supported on a gravel layer and an underdrain system. The sequence of operation is: 1. **Filtration Phase:** Pre-treated water (after coagulation–flocculation–sedimentation) enters through an **inlet flume** and is distributed uniformly over the sand surface. As water percolates downward, suspended particles are trapped within the pore spaces by the four mechanisms. The filtrate exits through the **underdrain** to a clear well. 2. **Schmutzdecke Layer:** This is a **biologically-active, gelatinous film** that forms on the top of the sand bed after several weeks of operation. It contains **algae, bacteria, protozoa, and organic matter** that produce **exopolysaccharides (EPS)**. The schmutzdecke acts as a **fine biological filter**, removing particles smaller than the sand pores and contributing to organic matter biodegradation. It is **critical in slow sand filters** and **secondary** in rapid sand filters. 3. **Underdrain System:** This serves **four essential functions**: - **Collects filtered water** uniformly. - **Distributes backwash water** evenly to fluidize the sand. - **Distributes air** during air-scour sub-phase. - **Supports the weight** of the filter media (sand + gravel). Common designs include: - **Pipe and strainer** system (perforated laterals with nozzles). - **False bottom with porous plates**. - **Leopold-type** underdrain blocks. - **Air-water distribution by a single lateral grid** (modern design). **Part (b) — Clean-Bed Head Loss Calculation:** **Given Data:** - $L = 0.7$ m - $d_{10} = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m - $UC = 1.5$ (used to verify media uniformity, not in the formula directly) - $\varepsilon = 0.40$ - $\phi = 0.85$ - $v = 6$ m/h $= 6/3600 = 1.667 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s - $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s - $\rho_w = 998$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Calculate the friction factor $f$.** For flow in a granular bed, the Kozeny-Carman friction factor is: $$ f = \frac{150 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)}{\text{Re}} $$ Reynolds number (using $d_{10}$): $$ \text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ \text{Re} = \frac{8.317 \times 10^{-4}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.83 $$ So: $$ f = \frac{150 \times 0.60}{0.83} = 108.4 $$ **Step 2: Apply Kozeny-Carman equation.** $$ h = \frac{f \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi^2 \cdot g \cdot d^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3} $$ Numerator: $$ 108.4 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.60)^2 $$ $$ = 108.4 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.36 $$ $$ = 108.4 \times 4.207 \times 10^{-7} = 4.56 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m (numerator dimensioned)} $$ Denominator: $$ (0.85)^2 \times 9.81 \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (0.40)^3 $$ $$ = 0.7225 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.064 $$ $$ = 0.7225 \times 9.81 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-8} = 1.134 \times 10^{-7} $$ **Step 3: Compute $h$.** $$ h = \frac{4.56 \times 10^{-5}}{1.134 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 402 \text{ m} $$ **Sanity check & rescaling:** The above result is in **mm-equivalent units**. The **dimensionless** Kozeny–Carman equation gives head loss in **m of water column**: **Re-checking with direct formula:** $$ h = \frac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \varepsilon^3} $$ $$ h = \frac{150 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.36}{0.7225 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 998 \times 9.81 \times 0.064} $$ Numerator = $150 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.36 = 6.31 \times 10^{-5}$ Denominator = $0.7225 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 998 \times 9.81 \times 0.064 = 1.131 \times 10^{-4}$ $$ h = \frac{6.31 \times 10^{-5}}{1.131 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.558 \text{ m} $$ **Result: $h \approx 0.56$ m of water column.** This is within the typical clean-bed head loss range (**0.3–0.6 m**) ✓. --- ### **QUESTION B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **B.** *(a)* Compare **slow sand filter** and **rapid sand filter** on the basis of: filtration rate, mechanism, cleaning method, area required, and treated water quality. Mention **two advantages and two limitations** of each. *(7 marks)* **B.** *(b)* Design a rapid gravity filter for a municipal water treatment plant with capacity **30 MLD** (million litres per day). Assume: filtration rate = 5 m/h, 5 working units + 1 standby, backwash rate = 45 m/h for 8 min, sand depth = 0.6 m, gravel depth = 0.15 m, freeboard = 0.5 m. Calculate: (i) area of each filter, (ii) backwash water volume per cycle, (iii) total filter bed depth, (iv) total height of filter box. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Slow Sand Filter (SSF) | Rapid Sand Filter (RSF) | |---|---|---| | **Filtration rate** | 0.1 – 0.2 m/h | 5 – 15 m/h | | **Dominant mechanism** | Biological (schmutzdecke) | Physical-chemical (straining + adhesion) | | **Cleaning method** | Scraping top 1–2 cm of sand | Backwash with water + air | | **Run time between cleaning** | Weeks to months | 24 – 72 h | | **Area required** | ~ 50–100× larger | Compact | | **Treated water turbidity** | < 1 NTU | < 0.5 NTU | | **Pathogen log-removal** | 99.9% (high) | 90–99% (moderate) | | **Skill level** | Low | Medium–High | | **Cost** | Low capital, high land | Medium capital, low land | | **Pretreatment requirement** | None needed | Coagulation–flocculation required | **Advantages of SSF:** 1. Low O&M cost; uses unskilled labor. 2. Excellent pathogen removal including viruses and bacteria. 3. No chemical pre-treatment needed. 4. Sustainable for rural applications. **Limitations of SSF:** 1. Requires very large land area — not suitable for urban areas. 2. Cannot handle high-turbidity raw water (> 50 NTU). 3. Slow process — not suitable for large populations. 4. Cleaning is labor-intensive (manual scraping). **Advantages of RSF:** 1. Compact footprint — ideal for urban plants. 2. Handles higher turbidity influents (up to 100 NTU). 3. Automation-friendly with backwash control. 4. High throughput. **Limitations of RSF:** 1. Requires pre-treatment (coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation). 2. Skilled operators needed. 3. Backwash water recycling required. 4. Pathogen removal is lower; disinfection is essential. **Part (b) — Filter Design Calculations:** **Given:** - $Q = 30 \text{ MLD} = 30000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ - $v = 5$ m/h - Working units = 5; Standby = 1 - Backwash: $v_{bw} = 45$ m/h, duration = 8 min - Sand depth = 0.6 m, Gravel = 0.15 m, Freeboard = 0.5 m **(i) Area of each filter:** Total area: $$ A_{total} = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{30000/24}{5} = \frac{1250}{5} = 250 \text{ m}^2 $$ Area per working unit: $$ A_{unit} = \frac{250}{5} = 50 \text{ m}^2 $$ Assuming square plan: $L = B = \sqrt{50} = 7.07$ m. Adopt **7.5 m × 7.0 m** = **52.5 m²** (with safety margin). **(ii) Backwash water volume per cycle:** $$ V_{bw} = A_{unit} \times v_{bw} \times t = 52.5 \times 45 \times \frac{8}{60} $$ $$ = 52.5 \times 45 \times 0.1333 = 315 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per filter per backwash} $$ **(iii) Total filter bed depth:** $$ L_{bed} = 0.60 + 0.15 = 0.75 \text{ m} $$ **(iv) Total height of filter box:** Box must accommodate: - Bottom underdrain space = **0.30 m** - Gravel = 0.15 m - Sand = 0.60 m - Freeboard = 0.50 m - Allowance for expanded bed during backwash (e.g., 0.30 m) $$ H_{total} = 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 1.85 \text{ m} $$ **Design Summary:** - Number of filters: **6 (5 working + 1 standby)** - Filter plan: **7.5 m × 7.0 m** each - Sand depth: **0.6 m**, $d_{10} = 0.50$ mm - Gravel depth: **0.15 m** - Backwash: **45 m/h for 8 min** using **315 m³** per cycle - Total filter box height: **1.85 m** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Filtration** ### A. Concept Block - ☐ **Filtration** = separation of suspended particles from water using a **porous granular medium**. - ☐ **Four mechanisms**: Straining, Sedimentation, Diffusion, Adhesion. - ☐ **Optimum particle size** (~1 µm) is the hardest to remove (minimum filtration efficiency). - ☐ **Slow sand** = biological; **Rapid sand** = physical-chemical. - ☐ **Multimedia filters** = coarse-to-fine flow path (anthracite → sand → garnet). ### B. Formula Block (Must Memorize) - ☐ **Kozeny–Carman** for clean-bed head loss - ☐ **Darcy's Law** $h = vL/K$ — basis of filter hydraulics - ☐ **Ergun** equation for $v_{mf}$ (backwash design) - ☐ **$UC = d_{60}/d_{10}$** (specification of filter sand) - ☐ **Yao filter performance**: $C/C_0 = e^{-\lambda L}$ - ☐ **Filter run time** is governed by **head loss** OR **effluent turbidity breakthrough**, whichever comes first. ### C. Numerical Block (Common Pitfalls) - ☐ **Convert** mm → m, h → s before substituting. - ☐ **Subtract** standby unit when computing area per working filter. - ☐ **Head loss limit** = 2.5–3.0 m (typical). Run time = 24–72 h. - ☐ **Backwash rate** = 36–60 m/h, duration 8–10 min. - ☐ **Bed expansion** during backwash = 20–50%. - ☐ **Wash water** ≈ 2–4% of total filtered water. ### D. Design Block (Standard Sizing) - ☐ **Filtration rate** (rapid): 5–15 m/h; (slow): 0.1–0.2 m/h. - ☐ **Sand**: $d_{10} = 0.45$–0.70 mm, UC = 1.3–1.7, depth = 0.6–0.9 m. - ☐ **Trough height** = 0.5 m above expanded sand surface. - ☐ **Freeboard** = 0.5 m minimum. - ☐ **Filter bed support**: graded gravel 2–20 mm, depth 0.15–0.45 m. ### E. Exam Strategy (KTU Pattern) - ☐ **Part A (3 marks):** Define, list, or state with a specific example. Be precise. - ☐ **Part B (14 marks):** Always have **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Numerical part requires **all steps shown** for full credit. - ☐ **Common 7-mark topics**: Filter hydraulics, Filter sizing numerical, Comparison of filter types, Mechanism explanation. - ☐ **Examiner's tip:** When solving a numerical, draw a labelled diagram first; this fetches **2 extra marks** for communication.

topics.title: Disinfection: Chemical and Non-Chemical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Disinfection: Chemical and Non-Chemical Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Disinfection** is the selective destruction of pathogenic (disease-causing) microorganisms in water — such as bacteria, viruses, protozoan cysts, and helminths — through the application of chemical agents or physical/physico-chemical means, with the explicit objective of rendering the water microbiologically safe for its intended use (potable, recreational, or industrial). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Disinfection is **NOT** synonymous with Sterilization. Sterilization implies the destruction of *all* forms of microbial life, including highly resistant bacterial spores. Disinfection is a *selective* destruction of pathogens and is a **logarithmic reduction** (typically 99.9% i.e., 3-log) of indicator organisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pathogenic Indicator Organisms (KTU Standard):** The standard microbial indicators of disinfection efficacy are:
> - **Total Coliforms (TC)** — ≤ 0 MPN/100 mL (BIS 10500:2012)
> - **Faecal Coliforms (FC)** / *E. coli* — ≤ 0 MPN/100 mL
> - **Faecal Streptococci (FS)**
> - **Heterotrophic Plate Count (HPC)** — ≤ 500 CFU/mL

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Strike" Analogy

Imagine your water supply is a **crowded city** (millions of microbes living in a litre of untreated water). Among them are a few **dangerous criminals** (pathogens like *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*, *Rotavirus*). 

- **Disinfection** is a **surgical strike** — it is a targeted operation that eliminates the dangerous criminals (pathogens) while leaving most of the innocent civilians (harmless environmental microbiota) alive and undisturbed.
- **Sterilization** would be a **nuclear strike** — wiping out the entire city, criminals and civilians alike (impractical and energy-prohibitive for municipal water supplies).
- A **disinfectant** is the **intelligence-led weapon** (chlorine, ozone, UV light) that identifies and neutralizes the threat selectively.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **CT Value** | mg/L × min | Disinfectant Concentration × Contact Time |
| **UV Wavelength** | **253.7 nm** | Germicidal peak (UV-C) |
| **Free Chlorine Residual (FCR)** | **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** | BIS Drinking Water Standard |
| **Total Chlorine Residual (TCR)** | ≤ **1.0 mg/L** | BIS Drinking Water Standard |
| **Ozone Dose** | 0.5 – 2.0 mg/L | Typical post-filtration dose |
| **Log Removal (LRV)** | ≥ **3-log (99.9%)** | Standard target for pathogens |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Disinfection is Necessary?** Coagulation-Flocculation-Sedimentation-Filtration (the conventional water treatment train) can reduce bacterial counts by 90–95%, but the **last 0.001% to 0.1% of pathogens** that survive are precisely the dose capable of causing epidemic outbreaks. Disinfection provides this **polishing microbiological barrier**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Disinfection Methods (Taxonomy)

#### **A. Chemical Disinfection Methods**

1. **Chlorination (most widely used globally)** — uses free chlorine in the form of Cl₂(g), NaOCl (sodium hypochlorite), or Ca(OCl)₂ (bleaching powder).
2. **Chloramination** — combined chlorine using NH₃ + HOCl → NH₂Cl (monochloramine).
3. **Ozonation (O₃)** — strongest commercially available oxidant (E° = +2.07 V).
4. **Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂)** — selective oxidant, no THM formation.
5. **Bromine / Iodine** — emergency/short-term disinfection (military field use).
6. **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** — H₂O₂/UV, O₃/H₂O₂, Fenton's reagent.

#### **B. Non-Chemical (Physical / Physico-Chemical) Disinfection Methods**

1. **Ultraviolet (UV-C) Radiation** — 200–280 nm, peak at **253.7 nm**.
2. **Boiling** — 100 °C for **≥ 1 min** (3 min at high altitude).
3. **Slow Sand Filtration (SSF)** — biological schmutzdecke layer.
4. **Solar Disinfection (SODIS)** — PET bottles, 6 hours of strong sunlight.
5. **Membrane Filtration** — microfiltration (0.1 µm), ultrafiltration.
6. **Ultrasonication** — cavitation-induced lysis.
7. **Pasteurization** — 65 °C for 30 min (low-temperature thermal).

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)**

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Statement | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chick's Law** | $\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$ | k = min⁻¹ | First-order microbial inactivation kinetics |
| 2 | **Integrated Chick's Law** | $N = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | — | Surviving fraction at time *t* |
| 3 | **Chick-Watson Law (Modified)** | $\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k \cdot C^n \cdot t}$ | k = (mg/L)⁻ⁿ·min⁻¹ | Concentration-time effect (n = dilution coefficient) |
| 4 | **Log Inactivation (LRV)** | $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}$ | dimensionless | Standard disinfection target (3-log = 99.9%) |
| 5 | **CT Concept** | $\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times t$ | mg·min/L | Regulatory disinfection credit |
| 6 | **Chlorine Speciation (HOCl/OCl⁻)** | $\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{7.5 - pH}$ | dimensionless | pH determines active species |
| 7 | **Ozone Henry's Constant** | $H_{O_3} = 0.105 \text{ mol/(L·atm) at 20°C}$ | mol/(L·atm) | Low solubility — must be contactor-dissolved |
| 8 | **UV Fluence (Dose)** | $D = I \cdot t$ | mJ/cm² (or µW·s/cm²) | UV-C inactivation energy |
| 9 | **Breakpoint Chlorination** | $C_{\text{applied}} \geq C_{\text{breakpoint}}$ | mg/L | Stoichiometric Cl₂ demand satisfied |
| 10 | **THM Formation Potential** | $\text{TTHM} \propto [\text{TOC}] \times [\text{Cl}_2] \times t \times T$ | µg/L | DBPs carcinogenicity index |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety Rule:** The vertical bar `|` symbol is FORBIDDEN inside table cells (it breaks the table renderer). For absolute-value or modular expressions, use the LaTeX commands `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` inside the cell.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Chlorination (Why It Works)

When **Cl₂ gas** is dissolved in water, the following hydrolytic disproportionation occurs:

**Step 1: Hydrolysis (fast, t₁/₂ ≈ 10⁻³ s)**

$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$

**Step 2: Dissociation of Hypochlorous Acid (pH-dependent)**

$$\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad ; \quad pK_a = 7.5 \text{ at 25°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pH is Critical (KTU High-Yield):** HOCl (hypochlorous acid) is **80–100 times more effective** as a disinfectant than OCl⁻ (hypochlorite ion). Since HOCl is a small, neutral molecule, it diffuses **through the microbial cell wall** easily. OCl⁻ is negatively charged and is **repelled** by the similarly charged cell membrane. Therefore, **lower pH → better disinfection** (within the practical range of 6.5–7.5).

**Step 3: Microbial Inactivation Mechanisms (Chick-Watson Kinetics)**
- **Oxidation of sulfhydryl (–SH) groups** on critical enzymes (lethal).
- **Inhibition of essential enzyme systems** (e.g., glucose oxidation).
- **Damage to cell membrane** proteins and phospholipids.
- **Reaction with nucleic acids** (DNA/RNA damage — less effective than UV).

### 2.4 The Five-Stage Breakpoint Chlorination Curve

When chlorine is added to natural water containing **ammonia (NH₃)** and **organic nitrogen**, the residual chlorine behaves in **five distinct zones**:

1. **Zone 1 (Free Residual)**: All incoming Cl₂ oxidizes Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NO₂⁻, etc.
2. **Zone 2 (Combined Residual Rise)**: Cl₂ reacts with NH₃ to form **chloramines** (NH₂Cl, NHCl₂, NCl₃) — *monochloramine* dominates.
3. **Zone 3 (Peak)**: Maximum combined chlorine residual.
4. **Zone 4 (Breakpoint / Dip)**: Combined chloramines are **destroyed** by excess Cl₂ → N₂↑ gas.
5. **Zone 5 (Free Residual Rise)**: Beyond breakpoint, **free chlorine (HOCl/OCl⁻)** dominates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** For drinking water, we always dose chlorine to operate in **Zone 5** (past the breakpoint) to ensure the disinfectant reaching consumers is **free chlorine** (HOCl), which is rapidly lethal. Combined chlorine (chloramines) is **40–60 times less effective** as a microbicide than free chlorine.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Disinfection is the **non-negotiable final barrier** in municipal water treatment plants serving 4+ billion people globally. Without it, waterborne diseases like cholera, typhoid, dysentery, and hepatitis A would resurge. In Indian smart-city projects (KTU-relevant context), UV + chlorination (dual disinfection) is the gold standard: UV handles chlorine-resistant *Cryptosporidium* and *Giardia* cysts, while chlorine provides a **residual disinfectant** in the distribution network (a critical feature UV cannot provide).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Chick's Law and Chick-Watson Law

**Step 1: Setting Up the Rate Equation**

The rate of microbial inactivation is empirically observed to be **first-order** with respect to the surviving microbial population *N*. The constant of proportionality *k* depends on disinfectant type, microbial species, temperature, and pH.

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N \quad \text{...(Chick's Law)}$$

**Step 2: Separation of Variables**

We rearrange to separate the dependent variable *N* from the independent variable *t*:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3: Integration (from N₀ at t = 0 to N at t = t)**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

This evaluates to:

$$[\ln N]_{N_0}^{N} = -k \cdot [t]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 4: Solving for N**

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k \cdot t$$

Applying the exponential function on both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kt}$$

**Step 5: Final Integrated Form of Chick's Law**

$$\boxed{\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

**Step 6: Generalization to the Chick-Watson Law**

Watson extended Chick's Law by recognizing that the inactivation rate constant *k* is itself a function of disinfectant concentration *C*. The most widely used form introduces the **dilution coefficient n** (typically 0.5 to 1.0):

$$\boxed{\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k \cdot C^n \cdot t}}$$

**Step 7: Derivation of the Log Reduction Value (LRV)**

The Log Reduction Value (LRV) is defined as the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of initial to final microbial count. Starting from Chick-Watson (assuming n = 1 for simplicity):

$$\text{LRV} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}\left(e^{k \cdot C \cdot t}\right)$$

Using the identity $\log_{10}(e^x) = \frac{x}{\ln(10)} = \frac{x}{2.303}$:

$$\boxed{\text{LRV} = \frac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}}$$

This is the **master equation** for CT-based disinfection design.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: CT Value for Chlorination

**Problem:** A municipal water treatment plant uses **free chlorine** at a residual concentration of **C = 0.5 mg/L** and a contact time of **t = 30 min** in a baffled clear-well. Calculate:
- (a) The CT value.
- (b) The log reduction, given that the Chick-Watson rate constant for *E. coli* inactivation by HOCl at 20 °C is **k = 50 L/(mg·min)**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (a): CT Calculation**

The CT concept is a **regulatory simplification**: it is the product of the residual disinfectant concentration (measured at the outlet of the contact tank) and the contact time.

$$\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times t$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{CT} = 0.5 \text{ mg/L} \times 30 \text{ min} = 15 \text{ mg·min/L}$$

**Part (b): Log Reduction Calculation**

Using the master LRV equation derived above:

$$\text{LRV} = \frac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}$$

Substituting *k* = 50 L/(mg·min), C = 0.5 mg/L, t = 30 min:

$$\text{LRV} = \frac{50 \times 0.5 \times 30}{2.303} = \frac{750}{2.303}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LRV} = 325.7 \approx 326}$$

**Interpretation (KTU Exam Style):** An LRV of 326 corresponds to a survival fraction of:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-326}$$

This is **astronomically over-disinfected**. In real practice, the rate constant for *E. coli* at neutral pH is much lower (k ≈ 0.5 to 5 L/(mg·min)), giving realistic LRVs of 3–5 logs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the HOCl/OCl⁻ pH Equilibrium

**Step 1: The Equilibrium Reaction**

$$\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^-$$

**Step 2: Equilibrium Constant Expression**

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]}$$

**Step 3: Taking the Negative Logarithm (10-base)**

$$-\log K_a = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]}$$

Using the definitions $pK_a = -\log K_a$ and $pH = -\log[\text{H}^+]$:

$$pK_a = pH - \log\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]}$$

**Step 4: Solving for the Ratio**

$$\log\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = pH - pK_a = pH - 7.5$$

**Step 5: Final Working Equation**

$$\boxed{\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{pH - 7.5}}$$

**Step 6: Worked Numerical Check**

At **pH 7.0** (slightly acidic), the ratio is $10^{-0.5} = 0.316$, meaning HOCl is **76%** of total free chlorine.

At **pH 8.0** (slightly alkaline), the ratio is $10^{0.5} = 3.16$, meaning HOCl is only **24%** of total free chlorine.

**Engineering Conclusion:** Operate chlorination at pH **6.5–7.5** to maximize the HOCl fraction (most lethal species).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Disinfection Methods

| Parameter | Chlorination | Ozonation | UV-C Radiation | Chloramination |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxidation Potential (V)** | +1.49 | **+2.07** | — (radiative) | +1.16 |
| **Active Species** | HOCl / OCl⁻ | O₃ / ·OH radicals | 253.7 nm photons | NH₂Cl (monochloramine) |
| **Residual** | **Yes** (long-lasting) | No (decomposes in <30 min) | **No** | Yes (longer than Cl₂) |
| **pH Sensitivity** | **High** (HOCl↔OCl⁻) | Low (pH-independent) | None | High |
| **Bactericidal / Virucidal** | Excellent / Excellent | Excellent / **Excellent** | Excellent / Good | Good / Poor |
| **Cysticidal (*Cryptosporidium*)** | Poor (high CT needed) | **Good** | **Excellent** | Very Poor |
| **DBP Formation** | THMs, HAAs (carcinogenic) | Bromate (if Br⁻ present) | **None** | Lower THMs, N-DBPs |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | **High** | Moderate | Low |
