## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define disinfection. How does it differ from sterilization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Disinfection is the process of selective destruction of pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa) in water through chemical or physical means to render it microbiologically safe for its intended use, typically achieving ≥ 3-log (99.9%) reduction of indicator organisms.

**Differentiation from Sterilization (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Disinfection | Sterilization |
|---|---|---|
| Microbial Kill | Selective (pathogens) | Total (all microbes + spores) |
| Log Reduction | ≥ 3-log (99.9%) | 6-log (100% theoretical) |
| Practical Use | Municipal water, hospitals | Surgical instruments, pharma |
| Energy/Chemical | Low dose | High dose |

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **State and explain the significance of the CT concept in water disinfection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** The CT value is the product of the residual disinfectant concentration (C, in mg/L) measured at the outlet of the contact tank and the effective contact time (T, in minutes):
$$\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times t \quad \text{(units: mg·min/L)}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):** 
1. It is a **regulatory metric** used by USEPA, BIS, and WHO to grant disinfection credit.
2. It encapsulates the two controllable variables of disinfection: **dose (C)** and **time (T)** — their product determines microbial inactivation.
3. Minimum CT values are prescribed for specific pathogens (e.g., 6 mg·min/L for *Giardia* inactivation by free chlorine at pH 7 and 10 °C).

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)**

---

> ### **QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2 Choice-1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the chemistry of chlorination with relevant equations. Discuss the effect of pH on chlorine speciation and disinfection efficiency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **breakpoint chlorination** phenomenon. Why is it essential to operate past the breakpoint for drinking water disinfection? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Chlorine Forms in Water Treatment (2 marks):**
- **Cl₂ (gas)** — liquefied chlorine cylinders, most economical at large scale.
- **NaOCl (sodium hypochlorite)** — 10–12% liquid, used in medium plants.
- **Ca(OCl)₂ (calcium hypochlorite)** — solid bleaching powder/granules, used in small plants and emergencies.

**(ii) Hydrolysis and Dissociation (2.5 marks):**

When Cl₂ dissolves:
$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \quad \text{(pH} > 4\text{)}$$

Hypochlorous acid partially dissociates:
$$\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad ; \quad pK_a = 7.5$$

The equilibrium constant expression:
$$\frac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{pH - 7.5}$$

**(iii) Effect of pH on Disinfection Efficiency (2.5 marks):**
- At **pH < 7.5**: HOCl dominates (small, neutral, readily diffuses through cell wall → highly effective).
- At **pH > 7.5**: OCl⁻ dominates (negatively charged, repelled by microbial cell membrane → less effective).
- **HOCl is 80–100 times more potent** than OCl⁻.
- **Practical implication:** Maintain pH 6.5–7.5 to maximize HOCl fraction and ensure efficient disinfection while preventing pipe corrosion.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Concept of Breakpoint (2 marks):** Breakpoint chlorination is the process of adding chlorine to water in doses sufficient to **completely oxidize ammonia** and other reducing agents, thereby ensuring that the residual chlorine exists in the form of **free chlorine** (HOCl/OCl⁻) rather than combined chlorine (chloramines).

**(ii) Five-Zone Curve Description (3 marks):**
- **Zone 1:** Cl₂ reacts with Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S, NO₂⁻ (reducing substances); no residual.
- **Zone 2:** HOCl reacts with NH₃ → NH₂Cl (monochloramine) — combined residual rises.
  $$\text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Zone 3:** Maximum combined chlorine residual achieved.
- **Zone 4 (Breakpoint Dip):** Excess Cl₂ destroys chloramines:
  $$2\text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Zone 5:** Past breakpoint, **free chlorine** dominates and rises linearly with added dose.

**(iii) Why Operate Past the Breakpoint? (2 marks):**
1. **Free chlorine (HOCl) is 40–60× more effective** than combined chlorine (NH₂Cl) against bacteria and viruses.
2. **No taste/odor issues** from chloramines.
3. **Consistent residual** maintained across the distribution network.
4. **Complies with BIS 10500 standards** for free residual chlorine (0.2–0.5 mg/L).
5. **Ensures inactivation of chlorine-resistant organisms** like *Giardia*.

---

### **QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 Choice-2]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the principle, mechanism, and applications of **ozonation** as a disinfection method. Compare it with chlorination. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **UV-C disinfection** with a process flow diagram. What are its limitations when used as a standalone method in a municipal water supply? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Principle and Generation (2.5 marks):** Ozone (O₃) is an allotrope of oxygen generated on-site by **corona discharge** in dry air or high-purity oxygen:
$$3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{20-30 \text{ kV electric discharge}} 2\text{O}_3$$

Ozone is the **third strongest oxidant** known (after fluorine and hydroxyl radical), with an oxidation potential of **+2.07 V**. It must be generated on-site (t½ ≈ 16 min in water) and is supplied via bubble-diffuser contactors.

**(ii) Mechanism of Disinfection (2 marks):**
- **Direct molecular oxidation** by O₃ — disrupts microbial cell wall proteins and unsaturated lipids.
- **Generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** through O₃ decomposition in water:
  $$\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\cdot\text{OH} + \text{O}_2$$
  ·OH radicals are **even stronger** oxidants and non-selectively destroy biomolecules.

**(iii) Comparison with Chlorination (2.5 marks):**

| Feature | Ozonation | Chlorination |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidation potential | **+2.07 V (stronger)** | +1.49 V |
| Residual | **None** (decomposes) | Long-lasting |
| THM formation | **None** (bromate risk) | Yes (carcinogenic) |
| Capital cost | High | Low |
| *Cryptosporidium* inactivation | **Excellent** | Poor |
| pH sensitivity | Low | **High** |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Principle and Mechanism (3 marks):** UV-C disinfection uses **low-pressure mercury vapor lamps** emitting monochromatic light at **253.7 nm (UV-C region)**. This wavelength is selectively absorbed by microbial DNA, causing **formation of pyrimidine dimers** (covalently bonded adjacent thymine/cytosine bases). These dimers **block DNA replication and transcription**, rendering microbes reproductively inactive (but not dead — they cannot multiply).

**UV Dose:** $D = I \times t$ (mJ/cm²)
- Typical dose: **40 mJ/cm²** (US EPA standard) for 3-log inactivation of *E. coli*, *Cryptosporidium*, *Giardia*.

**(ii) Process Flow (2 marks):** Filtered water → Inlet chamber → UV reactor (with quartz-sleeved lamps arranged parallel to flow) → UV intensity sensor (254 nm) → Disinfected water → Distribution.

**(iii) Limitations as Standalone Municipal Method (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students often list only *cost* as a UV limitation. Examiners expect at least the following four points:

1. **No residual disinfectant** — provides no protection against microbial regrowth in the distribution network. Microbial recontamination can occur at pipe breaks or cross-connections.
2. **Turbidity sensitivity** — particles > 10 NTU shield microbes from UV, reducing efficacy. Requires pre-filtration to < 1 NTU.
3. **Lamp aging and fouling** — quartz sleeves accumulate iron, manganese, and biofilm, reducing UV transmittance. Requires regular (3–6 month) cleaning.
4. **No action on chemical contaminants** — unlike chlorine/ozone, UV cannot oxidize taste/odor compounds, iron, or manganese.
5. **Energy dependence** — lamp failure goes unnoticed without alarms, leading to silent loss of disinfection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting the pH effect on HOCl/OCl⁻** — worth 2 marks; easily missed.
> 2. **Confusing *combined* vs *free* chlorine** in the breakpoint curve.
> 3. **Not stating units** for CT value (mg·min/L) — examiner deducts 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the by-product issue** (THMs, HAAs) when comparing chlorination with ozonation.
> 5. **Sketching the breakpoint curve without labels** for the five zones.
> 6. **Not specifying UV wavelength (253.7 nm)** — exact value earns full credit.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY LAST-MINUTE REVISION — Master Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ✅ Disinfection ≠ Sterilization. Disinfection = **selective, ≥ 3-log kill** of pathogens.
- ✅ **Chick's Law:** $N/N_0 = e^{-kt}$ ; **Chick-Watson:** $N/N_0 = e^{-kC^n t}$
- ✅ **LRV (Log Reduction Value):** $\text{LRV} = \dfrac{k \cdot C \cdot t}{2.303}$
- ✅ **CT Value:** $C_{\text{residual}} \times t$ in **mg·min/L** — regulatory metric.
- ✅ **HOCl ↔ OCl⁻ equilibrium:** $\dfrac{[\text{OCl}^-]}{[\text{HOCl}]} = 10^{pH - 7.5}$ ; HOCl is **80–100× more effective**.
- ✅ **Operating pH for chlorination:** **6.5 to 7.5** (sweet spot for HOCl dominance).
- ✅ **Five zones of breakpoint curve:** demand → chloramine rise → peak → dip → free Cl₂ rise.
- ✅ **Reactions:** $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$ ; $\text{NH}_3 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ ; $2\text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ✅ **BIS 10500 Drinking Water Standards:** Free Cl₂ = **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L**; Total Cl₂ ≤ **1.0 mg/L**; *E. coli* = **0 MPN/100 mL**.
- ✅ **Ozone:** strongest oxidant (**+2.07 V**); **no residual**; bromate risk; excellent cysticidal.
- ✅ **UV-C:** wavelength = **253.7 nm**; dose ≥ **40 mJ/cm²**; **no residual, no DBPs**; *Cryptosporidium*-effective.
- ✅ **Chloramines:** less effective than free Cl₂; **no THMs**; useful in long distribution networks.
- ✅ **Key DBPs of chlorine:** Trihalomethanes (THMs — CHCl₃, CHCl₂Br) and Haloacetic Acids (HAAs) — both **probable human carcinogens**.
- ✅ **Solar Disinfection (SODIS):** PET bottle + **6 hours of strong sunlight** — WHO-endorsed household method.
- ✅ **Boiling:** **100 °C for ≥ 1 min** (3 min at altitude) — most reliable emergency method.
- ✅ **Slow Sand Filtration:** biological **schmutzdecke** layer removes pathogens biologically — low-cost, sustainable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Water Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Availability** is defined as the quantity of water that can be reliably extracted from natural sources (surface water bodies and aquifers) at a given location and time, to meet the competing demands of domestic, agricultural, industrial, and ecological sectors, while maintaining the long-term sustainability of the hydrological system.

In engineering hydrology, water availability is quantified as the **net usable water resource** after accounting for natural losses (evaporation, transpiration) and inaccessible fractions (deep groundwater, locked polar ice). The standard unit of measurement in KTU board examinations is **Million Cubic Metres (MCM)** or **Million Litres per Day (MLD)**, with per capita availability expressed in **cubic metres per person per year (m³/person/year)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant overhead water tank supplying a housing colony. The tank receives water from rainfall (input), but some water evaporates from the open top (loss), some leaks through the walls (seepage), and only the water above a certain pipe level is accessible to residents. The "available water" is the portion that residents can actually draw — not the total water the tank holds.

Similarly, Earth's total water is huge, but most of it is locked up in oceans (saline) or polar ice (frozen). Only a tiny fraction — the freshwater in rivers, lakes, and shallow aquifers — is *available* for human use in a useful timeframe.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Global Total Water**: ~**1.386 billion km³**
> - **Freshwater fraction**: **2.5 %** of total
> - **Readily accessible freshwater**: **~0.3 %** of total (rivers, lakes, shallow aquifers)
> - **Standard per capita water requirement**: **150 m³/person/year** (minimum for livelihood)
> - **Water-stressed threshold**: **< 1700 m³/person/year**
> - **Water-scarce threshold**: **< 1000 m³/person/year**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Global Water Distribution — The "Hydrosphere Pyramid"

Earth's water is distributed across four major reservoirs:

- **Oceans (Saline)** — The dominant reservoir, holding **~97.5 %** of all water. Unusable for drinking/irrigation without expensive desalination.
- **Ice Caps, Glaciers & Permanent Snow** — **~1.74 %**. Temporarily locked; long-term (geological) freshwater reserve.
- **Groundwater (Fresh)** — **~1.69 %**. The largest *practical* freshwater store; the deeper fraction (> 800 m) is largely unrecoverable.
- **Surface Water (Rivers, Lakes)** — **~0.013 %**. Smallest in volume, but the most economically and ecologically critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Out of the **2.5 %** freshwater on Earth, only about **0.3 %** of total global water is readily available in rivers, lakes, and shallow aquifers for human use in a human-relevant timeframe.

## 2.2 The Hydrological Cycle — Driver of Water Availability

Water availability is dynamically sustained by the **hydrological cycle**, governed by solar energy and gravity. The major components are:

- **Precipitation (P)** — Primary input.
- **Evaporation (E)** — Loss from open water bodies and soil.
- **Transpiration (T)** — Loss via plants; combined with E as **Evapotranspiration (ET)**.
- **Runoff (R)** — Lateral movement of water over land into streams/rivers.
- **Infiltration (I)** — Vertical movement into soil and rock.
- **Groundwater Storage (G)** — Subsurface accumulation in aquifers.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Water Availability

1. **Climatic factors** — Precipitation pattern, temperature, humidity, wind.
2. **Geological factors** — Porosity, permeability, aquifer recharge capacity.
3. **Geographical factors** — Topography, drainage density, basin shape.
4. **Anthropogenic factors** — Over-extraction, pollution, land-use change, urbanization, deforestation.
5. **Temporal factors** — Seasonality, inter-annual variability, climate change.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Water Budget (Continental) | $P = R + ET + \Delta S$ | $P$ = Precip., $R$ = Runoff, $ET$ = Evapotrans., $\Delta S$ = Storage change (mm or m³) |
| 2 | Runoff Coefficient | $C = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Dimensionless; $0 < C < 1$ |
| 3 | Per Capita Availability | $q = \dfrac{\text{Total usable water}}{\text{Population}}$ | m³/person/year |
| 4 | Annual Usable Water | $W_u = P \cdot A \cdot C$ | $A$ = Basin area, $P$ = Annual precip. |
| 5 | Water Demand | $D = D_d + D_a + D_i + D_e$ | Domestic + Agri + Industrial + Ecological |
| 6 | Water Scarcity Index | $WSI = \dfrac{D}{W_u}$ | If $WSI > 1$, region is water-scarce |
| 7 | Static Reserve Life | $t = \dfrac{V_{res}}{R_{annual}}$ | Years; $V_{res}$ = reservoir volume |
| 8 | Recharge (Groundwater) | $R_g = R_f \cdot P \cdot A$ | $R_f$ = recharge factor (0.05–0.20) |
| 9 | Effective Rainfall | $P_{eff} = P - ET$ | Drives actual runoff/soil moisture |
| 10 | Over-exploitation ratio | $OE = \dfrac{\text{extraction}}{\text{recharge}}$ | $> 1$ ⇒ critical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by KTU-affiliated civil engineers in designing water supply schemes, evaluating dam feasibility, drafting watershed management plans, and computing safe yield of borewells for municipal corporations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Budget Equation of a Catchment

The water budget is the **continuity equation** applied to a hydrological control volume (a catchment/basin).

**Step 1 — Identify the control volume.**
A catchment of area $A$ is chosen. The boundary is the topographic watershed divide; the top is the atmosphere; the bottom is the impervious bedrock.

**Step 2 — List all inflows and outflows over a time $\Delta t$.**

- Inflows: Precipitation $P$, Surface inflow $I_s$, Groundwater inflow $I_g$.
- Outflows: Runoff $R$, Evapotranspiration $ET$, Surface outflow $O_s$, Groundwater outflow $O_g$.

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of conservation of mass.**

$$
\text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} = \Delta \text{Storage}
$$

$$
(P + I_s + I_g) - (R + ET + O_s + O_g) = \Delta S
$$

**Step 4 — For a closed catchment (no cross-boundary flow), $I_s = I_g = O_s = O_g = 0$.**

$$
P - R - ET = \Delta S
$$

$$
P = R + ET + \Delta S
$$

This is the **fundamental hydrological water-budget equation** universally tested in KTU board papers.

**Step 5 — Special case: Steady state (long-term average).**
Over decades, $\Delta S \rightarrow 0$, hence:

$$
\overline{P} = \overline{R} + \overline{ET}
$$

This is the **long-term mean water balance** used in regional water resource planning.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per Capita Water Availability

**Step 1 — Express total annual usable water.**

$$
W_u = P \cdot A \cdot C \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{year})
$$

**Step 2 — Express population served.**

$$
N = \text{Population (persons)}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the per capita ratio.**

$$
q = \frac{W_u}{N} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot C}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting typical values for India-class basins.**
Let $A = 100$ km² $= 10^8$ m², $P = 1.0$ m/year, $C = 0.30$, $N = 100{,}000$.

$$
q = \frac{1.0 \times 10^8 \times 0.30}{100000} = 300 \ \text{m}^3/\text{person/year}
$$

This places the hypothetical basin in the **water-stressed** category.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Runoff Coefficient and Rational Method Link

For **Rational Method** storm runoff estimation, the peak runoff is:

$$
Q = C \cdot I \cdot A
$$

Where $Q$ = peak discharge (m³/s), $I$ = rainfall intensity (m/s or mm/hr), $A$ = catchment area (m² or ha). $C$ is the same runoff coefficient that appears in the water budget. For long-term average:

$$
C = \frac{\overline{R}}{\overline{P}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration.**
Given $\overline{P} = 1200$ mm/yr and observed $\overline{R} = 360$ mm/yr:

$$
C = \frac{360}{1200} = 0.30
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference

| Scenario | Dominant Loss | $C$ Range | Water Availability Class |
|----------|---------------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Dense forest, high humidity | ET (high) | $0.20 - 0.40$ | Moderate |
| Urban paved catchment | Surface storage | $0.70 - 0.95$ | Flashy, low storage |
| Agricultural flat land | Infiltration + ET | $0.10 - 0.30$ | Low surface, high groundwater |
| Arid / desert | ET (extreme) | $< 0.10$ | Scarce |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hydrological Cycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Moisture] --> B[Condensation]
    B --> C[Precipitation - P]
    C --> D[Overland Flow - R]
    C --> E[Infiltration - I]
    C --> F[Interception Loss]
    D --> G[Stream Channel Network]
    G --> H[Stream Flow to Ocean]
    E --> I[Soil Moisture Zone]
    I --> J[Percolation to Aquifer]
    J --> K[Groundwater Storage - G]
    K --> L[Base Flow to River]
    I --> M[Evapotranspiration - ET]
    H --> N[Open Water Surface]
    K --> N
    N --> O[Evaporation - E]
    M --> P[Atmospheric Moisture]
    O --> P
    F --> P
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Global Water Distribution — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Total Earth Water = 100 percent] --> node2[Saline Water = 97.5 percent]
    node1 --> node3[Fresh Water = 2.5 percent]
    node3 --> node4[Glaciers and Ice Caps = 1.74 percent]
    node3 --> node5[Groundwater = 1.69 percent]
    node3 --> node6[Surface Water = 0.013 percent]
    node5 --> node7[Accessible GW = 0.30 percent]
    node5 --> node8[Deep GW = 1.39 percent]
    node6 --> node9[Rivers and Streams]
    node6 --> node10[Lakes and Reservoirs]
    node6 --> node11[Atmospheric Water]
```

## 4.3 Water Availability Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Compute Per Capita q in m cube per person per year] --> s2{q greater than 1700}
    s2 -- Yes --> s3[Water Secure Region]
    s2 -- No --> s4{q between 1000 and 1700}
    s4 -- Yes --> s5[Water Stressed]
    s4 -- No --> s6{q less than 1000}
    s6 -- Yes --> s7[Water Scarce]
    s6 -- No --> s8{q less than 500}
    s8 -- Yes --> s9[Absolute Scarcity]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define water availability. State the global distribution of water on Earth. (3 Marks)
**Model Answer:**
Water availability is the fraction of total water in a region that can be extracted and used reliably to meet anthropogenic and ecological demands. Globally, Earth holds about **1.386 billion km³** of water, of which **97.5 % is saline (oceans)**, and only **2.5 % is fresh**. Of this freshwater, **~1.74 %** is locked in glaciers/ice caps, **~1.69 %** is groundwater, and merely **~0.013 %** is surface water (rivers, lakes). Practically, only about **0.3 %** of total water is readily usable.

### Q2. List four major factors affecting water availability. (3 Marks)
**Model Answer:**
1. Climatic factors — precipitation amount, intensity, distribution.
2. Geological factors — aquifer porosity, permeability, recharge capacity.
3. Anthropogenic factors — over-extraction, deforestation, urbanization.
4. Temporal factors — seasonality and climate change-driven variability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Long Answer

**(a) Explain the hydrological cycle in detail with a neat sketch. Discuss the water budget equation of a catchment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The hydrological cycle is the continuous circulation of water within the Earth–atmosphere system, driven by solar radiation and gravity. The major stages are:

1. **Evaporation** — Solar energy converts surface water into water vapour from oceans, lakes, rivers, and soil.
2. **Transpiration** — Water released by plants through stomata; combined with evaporation, termed **evapotranspiration (ET)**.
3. **Condensation** — Water vapour cools at altitude and condenses into cloud droplets.
4. **Precipitation (P)** — Water returns to the Earth as rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
5. **Runoff (R)** — Excess water flows over land into streams, rivers, and ultimately the ocean.
6. **Infiltration (I)** — A portion seeps into the ground, recharging soil moisture and aquifers.
7. **Storage** — Water resides in oceans, glaciers, groundwater, lakes, and the atmosphere.

**Water Budget Equation:**
For a catchment, applying conservation of mass:

$$
P = R + ET + \Delta S
$$

where $\Delta S$ is change in storage (soil moisture + groundwater + surface). For long-term averages, $\Delta S \to 0$, giving:

$$
\overline{P} = \overline{R} + \overline{ET}
$$

This equation is the cornerstone of water resource planning and is used to estimate runoff, design reservoir capacity, and assess drought vulnerability.

**(b) A river basin of area 5000 km² receives an average annual precipitation of 1100 mm. The annual evapotranspiration loss is 700 mm. Estimate the average annual runoff volume in MCM and the runoff coefficient. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert basin area to m².**

$$
A = 5000 \ \text{km}^2 = 5000 \times 10^6 \ \text{m}^2 = 5 \times 10^9 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute runoff depth using the water budget (assuming steady state).**

$$
\overline{R} = \overline{P} - \overline{ET} = 1100 - 700 = 400 \ \text{mm/yr} = 0.40 \ \text{m/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual runoff volume.**

$$
V_R = R \times A = 0.40 \times 5 \times 10^9 = 2.0 \times 10^9 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MCM.**

$$
V_R = 2.0 \times 10^9 \ \text{m}^3 = 2000 \ \text{MCM/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute runoff coefficient.**

$$
C = \frac{R}{P} = \frac{400}{1100} = 0.364
$$

**Result:** Annual runoff = **2000 MCM**; Runoff coefficient = **0.36**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Discuss the classification of global water resources. Explain the concept of per capita water availability and its significance in water resource planning. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Global water is classified into:

| Class | % of Total | % of Freshwater | Practical Use |
|-------|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Saline (oceans) | 97.5 % | — | Desalination required |
| Ice caps/glaciers | 1.74 % | 68.7 % | Long-term reserve |
| Groundwater | 1.69 % | 30.1 % | Major usable source |
| Surface water | 0.013 % | 0.3 % | Direct withdrawal |
| Atmospheric | 0.001 % | ~0.04 % | Rainfall source |

**Per Capita Availability:**

$$
q = \frac{\text{Total usable freshwater}}{\text{Population}}
$$

Measured in m³/person/year. Significance:

- **Indicator of water stress** — Falling per capita values signal impending scarcity.
- **Planning tool** — Used to design dams, supply networks, and demand-side management.
- **Policy trigger** — Crossing 1700 m³ flags a region as "water-stressed"; below 1000 m³, it becomes "water-scarce," prompting intervention.

**(b) The total annual usable water in a region is 3500 MCM and the population is 5 million. Classify the region based on per capita water availability. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute per capita availability.**

$$
q = \frac{W_u}{N} = \frac{3500 \times 10^6 \ \text{m}^3}{5 \times 10^6 \ \text{persons}} = 700 \ \text{m}^3/\text{person/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with standard thresholds.**

| Threshold | Value (m³/p/yr) | Status |
|-----------|------------------|--------|
| Water secure | > 1700 | — |
| Water stressed | 1000 – 1700 | — |
| **Water scarce** | **< 1000** | **This region (700 m³)** |
| Absolute scarcity | < 500 | — |

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**
Since $q = 700$ m³/person/year is below 1000, the region is classified as **"Water Scarce."** This warrants urgent water-conservation measures, rainwater harvesting, wastewater recycling, and demand-side regulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Water Availability Questions:**
> - **Unit mismatch** — Conflating mm with m³. Always convert mm of depth over an area (in m²) to m³.
> - **Forgetting the $\Delta S$ term** — In water-budget questions, mention storage change; do not assume $\Delta S = 0$ unless explicitly stated as "long-term average."
> - **Misclassifying water-scarcity thresholds** — KTU examiners deduct marks if you confuse 1700 m³ (stressed) with 1000 m³ (scarce).
> - **MCM vs m³ conversion error** — 1 MCM $= 10^6$ m³, not $10^3$.
> - **Runoff coefficient is dimensionless** — Never tag it with units.
> - **Not stating assumptions** — Always declare "assuming steady state" or "assuming no groundwater outflow" before solving.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Only ~2.5 %** of Earth's water is fresh, and **only ~0.3 %** is readily usable.
- The **water budget equation** is $P = R + ET + \Delta S$; for long-term mean, $\Delta S = 0$.
- **Per capita availability** is the key indicator: **< 1700 m³ = stressed**, **< 1000 m³ = scarce**.
- **Runoff coefficient $C = R/P$** — dimensionless, depends on land use.
- **Anthropogenic factors** (over-extraction, deforestation, climate change) reduce availability faster than natural variability.
- **India's per capita availability** is declining — from ~5000 m³ (1950) to ~1500 m³ (2024) — making management a national priority.
- **Engineering tools** like the Rational Method, Water Balance, and Water Scarcity Index are KTU-board favourites.
- **Sustainable yield** of an aquifer must never exceed its **recharge rate**; otherwise, mining of water occurs.
- **Demand = Domestic + Agricultural + Industrial + Ecological**; supply is a function of $P$, $A$, and $C$.
- For KTU numericals, always show **units at every step** and end with a clear **boxed answer** in MCM, m³, or m³/p/yr.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Stress Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Water Stress Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — WATER STRESS INDEX (WSI)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Water Stress Index (WSI)** is a dimensionless quantitative indicator used in environmental and water-resource engineering to measure the pressure exerted by **total freshwater demand** (withdrawal for agricultural, industrial, and domestic uses) on the **total renewable freshwater supply** (surface water + renewable groundwater) of a region, basin, or country within a defined hydrological year.

$$WSI = \dfrac{W_{demand}}{W_{supply}}$$

where:
- $W_{demand}$ = Total freshwater withdrawal (km³/yr or Mm³/yr)
- $W_{supply}$ = Total renewable freshwater resources (km³/yr or Mm³/yr)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected in Exams):**
> *Water Stress Index (WSI) is the ratio of total water withdrawal for human activities to the total renewable freshwater resources available in a region. It quantifies the degree to which water demand approaches or exceeds the locally available supply.*

The index is endorsed by international bodies such as the **World Resources Institute (WRI)**, **FAO AQUASTAT**, and the **European Environment Agency (EEA)** as a primary sustainability metric under **SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bank account** that receives a fixed monthly "salary" (rainfall recharging rivers and aquifers). Your **monthly expenses** (drinking, bathing, farming, factory cooling) are the withdrawals.

- If your expenses are far less than your salary → **No stress** (savings grow).
- If expenses consume half the salary → **Moderate stress** (tight budget).
- If expenses exceed the salary → **Critical stress** (you start eating into past savings — mining groundwater).

The **Water Stress Index** is essentially a **"budget balance ratio"** for any country or watershed — telling engineers and planners whether the region is living within its water means or depleting its natural capital.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Boundary Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reference Thresholds (Memorise for KTU Exams):**
>
> - **Falkenmark Water Stress Threshold:** $\mathbf{1{,}700 \; m^3/\text{person/year}}$
> - **Falkenmark Water Scarcity Threshold:** $\mathbf{1{,}000 \; m^3/\text{person/year}}$
> - **Absolute Water Scarcity:** $\mathbf{<500 \; m^3/\text{person/year}}$
> - **WSI — Low Stress:** $< 10\%$
> - **WSI — Low-to-Medium:** $10\% - 20\%$
> - **WSI — Medium-to-High:** $20\% - 40\%$
> - **WSI — High Stress:** $40\% - 80\%$
> - **WSI — Extremely High Stress:** $> 80\%$
>
> Source: WRI Aqueduct & FAO AQUASTAT global classifications (peer-reviewed and standardised).

The **Renewable Freshwater Resources (RFR)** itself is defined as the long-term average annual flow of rivers plus the effective recharge of aquifers, calculated over a hydrological cycle of typically **30 years**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the WSI Calculation

The WSI calculation proceeds through a structured engineering pipeline:

1. **Quantify Renewable Supply ($W_{supply}$)** — Measure long-term mean annual surface runoff + renewable groundwater recharge.
2. **Quantify Total Demand ($W_{demand}$)** — Sum agricultural (irrigation), industrial, and municipal withdrawals (excluding in-stream uses like hydropower and navigation).
3. **Normalise Both Quantities** to the same hydrological year and spatial boundary (basin / state / country).
4. **Compute the Ratio** WSI = Demand / Supply (dimensionless, often expressed as %).
5. **Classify** the result into low, medium, high, or extremely-high stress using standard threshold tables.
6. **Interpret & Recommend** — If WSI > 40%, interventions such as rainwater harvesting, recycling, demand management, or inter-basin transfer become mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is WSI preferred over raw withdrawal data?**
> Because two regions may withdraw the same volume of water, yet one may be under severe stress while the other is not — depending on local renewable supply. WSI inherently accounts for this geographic asymmetry.

---

### 2.2 Related Indices in the Water Stress Family

The WSI is part of a broader family of indices, all of which are part of the KTU Module-3 syllabus:

| Index | Formula | Purpose | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WSI (Water Stress Index)** | $W_{demand}/W_{supply}$ | Overall demand-supply balance | $0 - >1$ |
| **WEI+ (Water Exploitation Index)** | $W_{demand}/W_{supply}$ | EEA variant of WSI | $0 - >1$ |
| **Falkenmark Indicator** | $W_{supply}/Population$ | Per-capita availability | $\text{m}^3/\text{capita/yr}$ |
| **WPI (Water Poverty Index)** | Composite of 5 sub-indices | Socio-economic water deprivation | $0 - 100$ |
| **Water Footprint (WF)** | Volume of water used to produce goods | Consumption-based water use | $\text{m}^3/\text{yr}$ |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Reference Table)

| Sl. No. | Parameter / Equation | Expression | Units | KTU Board Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water Stress Index | $WSI = \dfrac{W_D}{W_S}$ | dimensionless | $\star\star\star$ |
| 2 | Falkenmark Indicator | $FI = \dfrac{W_S}{P}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$ | $\star\star\star$ |
| 3 | Per-Capita Availability | $PCA = \dfrac{Q_{mean}}{P}$ | $\text{litres/capita/day (lpcd)}$ | $\star\star\star$ |
| 4 | WPI (simplified) | $WPI = \dfrac{R + A + C + U + E}{5}$ | score $0-100$ | $\star\star$ |
| 5 | Annual Renewable Water | $W_S = Q_{surface} + G_{recharge}$ | $\text{km}^3/\text{yr}$ | $\star\star$ |
| 6 | Demand Sector Split | $W_D = W_{agri} + W_{ind} + W_{dom}$ | $\text{km}^3/\text{yr}$ | $\star\star\star$ |
| 7 | lpcd from Annual Q | $lpcd = \dfrac{Q \times 10^9}{P \times 365}$ | lpcd | $\star\star$ |

> Where $W_D =$ total demand, $W_S =$ total renewable supply, $Q_{mean} =$ mean annual flow, $P =$ population, $G_{recharge} =$ renewable groundwater, $R,A,C,U,E =$ Resource/Access/Capacity/Use/Environment components of WPI.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Basin-level water budgeting** for irrigation command areas.
- **Industrial site selection** — power plants, textile units, and semiconductor fabs prefer WSI < 20% zones.
- **Policy formulation** — Kerala State Water Policy uses WSI as a benchmark for declaring drought-affected taluks.
- **SDG 6.4.2 reporting** — every UN member state must publish its WSI biennially.
- **Climate adaptation planning** — identifying "hotspot" districts vulnerable to climate-induced supply contraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the WSI Mathematical Foundation

The WSI emerges from a simple **demand-supply mass balance** principle applied to a hydrological control volume.

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume**

Consider a hydrological basin of area $A$ ($\text{km}^2$) as a control volume. Its long-term hydrological budget is:

$$P - ET = Q_{surface} + G_{recharge}$$

where $P =$ precipitation, $ET =$ evapotranspiration, $Q_{surface} =$ surface runoff, $G_{recharge} =$ groundwater recharge.

**Step 2 — Define Renewable Supply**

The total renewable freshwater resource is the sum of usable surface and renewable groundwater:

$$W_S = Q_{surface} + G_{recharge} \quad [\text{km}^3/\text{yr}]$$

**Step 3 — Define the Demand Vector**

Water withdrawal is partitioned into three sectors:

$$W_D = W_{agri} + W_{ind} + W_{dom}$$

where $W_{agri}$ = agricultural irrigation withdrawal, $W_{ind}$ = industrial withdrawal, $W_{dom}$ = domestic/municipal withdrawal.

**Step 4 — Form the Stress Ratio**

Taking the ratio of demand to supply gives a dimensionless index (the WSI):

$$
\begin{aligned}
WSI &= \dfrac{W_D}{W_S} \\
&= \dfrac{W_{agri} + W_{ind} + W_{dom}}{Q_{surface} + G_{recharge}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as Percentage (Common Reporting Convention)**

$$WSI \, (\%) = \dfrac{W_D}{W_S} \times 100$$

This completes the derivation. The index is purely a ratio — it is **scale-invariant**, meaning the same equation applies whether the basin is 10 km² or 10,000 km².

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Numerical WSI Calculation (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement (Model Question):**
A district has the following hydrological data:

- Mean annual surface runoff $Q_s = 2.50 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Renewable groundwater recharge $G = 0.75 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Agricultural withdrawal $W_{agri} = 1.80 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Industrial withdrawal $W_{ind} = 0.45 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Domestic withdrawal $W_{dom} = 0.20 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$

**Calculate the WSI and classify the stress level.**

**Solution:**

*Step A — Calculate Total Renewable Supply:*

$$W_S = Q_s + G = 2.50 + 0.75 = 3.25 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$$

*Step B — Calculate Total Demand:*

$$W_D = W_{agri} + W_{ind} + W_{dom} = 1.80 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 2.45 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$$

*Step C — Compute WSI (as a ratio):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
WSI &= \dfrac{W_D}{W_S} \\
&= \dfrac{2.45}{3.25} \\
&= 0.7538
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step D — Express as Percentage:*

$$WSI \, (\%) = 0.7538 \times 100 = 75.38\%$$

*Step E — Classify Using Standard Thresholds:*

Since $75.38\% \in (40\%, 80\%]$, the district falls in the **HIGH STRESS** category, approaching the extremely-high zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Recommendation:** Demand-management measures (drip irrigation, grey-water reuse, and rainwater harvesting) are critical; otherwise the district risks crossing into Extremely High Stress (>80%) within 5–10 years.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Falkenmark Per-Capita Indicator

**Problem Statement:**
A city has a total renewable freshwater resource $W_S = 1.20 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$ and a population of 1.5 million. Compute the Falkenmark Indicator and classify water scarcity.

**Solution:**

*Step A — Convert km³ to m³:*

$$W_S = 1.20 \times 10^9 \; \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$$

*Step B — Per-Capita Annual Availability:*

$$FI = \dfrac{W_S}{P} = \dfrac{1.20 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$$

*Step C — Classification (Falkenmark):*

| Range ($\text{m}^3/\text{capita/yr}$) | Classification |
|---|---|
| $> 1700$ | No stress |
| $1000 - 1700$ | Stress |
| $500 - 1000$ | Scarcity |
| $< 500$ | Absolute scarcity |

Since $FI = 800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$, the city falls in the **SCARCITY** category (between 500 and 1000).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Conversion of Per-Capita Annual Q to lpcd (Board Favourite)

**Given:** $W_S = 0.55 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$, population = 2.0 million.

**Step A — Convert to m³/yr:**

$$W_S = 0.55 \times 10^9 = 5.5 \times 10^8 \; \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step B — Per-Capita Per-Year:**

$$= \dfrac{5.5 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^6} = 275 \; \text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$$

**Step C — Convert to Litres Per Capita Per Day (lpcd):**

$$lpcd = \dfrac{275 \times 1000}{365} = 753.42 \; \text{lpcd}$$

> [!NOTE]
> WHO recommends a minimum of **50 lpcd** for survival, **100 lpcd** for basic needs, and **150–200 lpcd** for a dignified life. Indian urban average is ~135 lpcd.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Stress vs. Population Pressure)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Critical Condition} &: \quad WSI \geq 0.80 \quad \lor \quad FI < 500 \\
\text{Action Threshold} &: \quad WSI \in [0.40, 0.80) \\
\text{Monitor} &: \quad WSI \in [0.20, 0.40) \\
\text{Sustainable} &: \quad WSI < 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **withdrawal** (gross abstraction including return flows) with **consumption** (net depletion). WSI uses **withdrawal**, not consumption. Return flows are NOT subtracted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — WSI Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the end-to-end logic of computing and classifying the Water Stress Index at a basin or regional scale.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Hydrological Control Volume] --> B[Collect Long-Term Hydrological Data]
    B --> C[Quantify Surface Runoff Qs]
    B --> D[Quantify Groundwater Recharge G]
    C --> E[Compute Total Renewable Supply Ws]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Quantify Agricultural Withdrawal Wagri]
    E --> G[Quantify Industrial Withdrawal Wind]
    E --> H[Quantify Domestic Withdrawal Wdom]
    F --> I[Compute Total Demand Wd]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute WSI = Wd / Ws]
    J --> K{Apply Threshold Classification}
    K -->|WSI < 0.10| L[Low Stress - Green Zone]
    K -->|WSI 0.10 to 0.20| M[Low-to-Medium Stress]
    K -->|WSI 0.20 to 0.40| N[Medium-to-High Stress]
    K -->|WSI 0.40 to 0.80| O[High Stress - Action Threshold]
    K -->|WSI greater than 0.80| P[Extremely High Stress - Critical]
    L --> Q[Recommend Monitoring Only]
    M --> Q
    N --> R[Recommend Demand Management]
    O --> S[Recommend Reuse, Recycling, RWH]
    P --> T[Recommend Emergency Allocation and Inter-Basin Transfer]
    Q --> U[End Report]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Water Stress Indicators (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WS[Water Stress Index Family] --> WSI[WSI - Demand Supply Ratio]
    WS --> WEI[WEI Plus - EEA Variant]
    WS --> FI[Falkenmark Indicator]
    WS --> WPI[Water Poverty Index]
    WS --> WF[Water Footprint]
    WSI --> A1[Used by WRI Aqueduct]
    WSI --> A2[Used by FAO AQUASTAT]
    WEI --> B1[Used by European Environment Agency]
    FI --> C1[Used by UN and WHO globally]
    WPI --> D1[Used by UNDP for socio-economic mapping]
    WF --> E1[Used by Hoekstra for consumption accounting]
```

### 4.3 WSI Classification Logic with Sectoral Demand Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input: Annual Withdrawal and Supply Data] --> SEC[Sectoral Demand Vector]
    SEC --> S1[Agriculture - Usually 70 to 90 percent]
    SEC --> S2[Industry - Usually 5 to 20 percent]
    SEC --> S3[Domestic - Usually 5 to 15 percent]
    S1 --> TOT[Total Demand Wd]
    S2 --> TOT
    S3 --> TOT
    IN --> SUP[Total Renewable Supply Ws]
    TOT --> RATIO[Ratio Computation]
    SUP --> RATIO
    RATIO --> CAT[Threshold Categorisation]
    CAT --> OUT1[Sustainable]
    CAT --> OUT2[Moderate]
    CAT --> OUT3[High]
    CAT --> OUT4[Critical]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** Define the Water Stress Index (WSI). State its classification thresholds.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Water Stress Index (WSI) is a dimensionless indicator defined as the ratio of total freshwater withdrawal (demand) to the total renewable freshwater resources (supply) within a defined geographical boundary over a year.

$$WSI = \dfrac{W_D}{W_S}$$

**Classification thresholds (WRI Aqueduct / FAO AQUASTAT):**

| WSI Range | Category |
|---|---|
| $< 10\%$ | Low Stress |
| $10\% - 20\%$ | Low-to-Medium |
| $20\% - 40\%$ | Medium-to-High |
| $40\% - 80\%$ | High Stress |
| $> 80\%$ | Extremely High Stress |

*(Self-explanatory, 3-mark standard answer.)*

---

**Q2.** What is the Falkenmark Indicator? Mention its threshold values.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Falkenmark Indicator (FI), proposed by hydrologist Malin Falkenmark in 1989, measures per-capita water availability as:

$$FI = \dfrac{W_S}{P} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{person/year}]$$

**Threshold values:**

- **> 1700 m³/capita/yr** → No stress
- **1000 – 1700 m³/capita/yr** → Water stress
- **500 – 1000 m³/capita/yr** → Water scarcity
- **< 500 m³/capita/yr** → Absolute water scarcity

*(Complete 3-mark answer.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3 Choice Q1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various indicators used to assess water stress, including the Water Stress Index, Falkenmark Indicator, and Water Poverty Index. State their formulas and classification thresholds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A river basin has the following hydrological data:

- Mean annual surface runoff $= 5.20 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Groundwater recharge $= 1.30 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Agricultural withdrawal $= 3.10 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Industrial withdrawal $= 0.80 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Domestic withdrawal $= 0.40 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$
- Population $= 4.5 \times 10^6$

Compute the **WSI** and the **Falkenmark Indicator**, classify the stress level, and recommend two engineering interventions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Water Stress Index (WSI):**

$$
WSI = \dfrac{W_D}{W_S}
$$

where $W_D$ is total annual withdrawal and $W_S$ is the total renewable freshwater resource. WSI is dimensionless and is widely used by WRI Aqueduct and FAO AQUASTAT to benchmark nations.

**Classification:**

| WSI (%) | Stress Category |
|---|---|
| $< 10$ | Low |
| $10-20$ | Low-to-Medium |
| $20-40$ | Medium-to-High |
| $40-80$ | High |
| $> 80$ | Extremely High |

**2. Falkenmark Indicator (FI):**

$$
FI = \dfrac{W_S}{P} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{person/year}]
$$

**Thresholds:** >1700 (no stress), 1000–1700 (stress), 500–1000 (scarcity), <500 (absolute scarcity).

**3. Water Poverty Index (WPI):**

$$
WPI = \dfrac{R + A + C + U + E}{5}
$$

where $R$ = Resources, $A$ = Access, $C$ = Capacity, $U$ = Use, $E$ = Environment. Each sub-index is normalised to a 0–100 scale; the WPI provides a socio-economic view of water deprivation, complementing the purely hydrological WSI.

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Supply:**

$$W_S = 5.20 + 1.30 = 6.50 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2 — Total Demand:**

$$W_D = 3.10 + 0.80 + 0.40 = 4.30 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 3 — WSI Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WSI &= \dfrac{4.30}{6.50} = 0.6615 \\
WSI \, (\%) &= 66.15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:** Since $66.15\% \in (40\%, 80\%]$, the basin is in **HIGH STRESS**.

**Step 5 — Falkenmark Indicator:**

$$FI = \dfrac{6.50 \times 10^9}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 1444.44 \; \text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$$

This lies in the **WATER STRESS** band (1000–1700 m³).

**Step 6 — Recommended Interventions:**

1. **Drip & Sprinkler Irrigation** to reduce agricultural withdrawal (currently ~72% of total demand).
2. **Industrial Water Recycling** (closed-loop cooling) to cut industrial withdrawal by 30–50%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3 Choice Q2]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of the Water Stress Index in sustainable water-resource planning. Highlight at least two Indian case studies or scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The per-capita annual water availability of a state is $1450 \; \text{m}^3$. The population is 35 million, and total demand is $48 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$. Calculate the WSI, Falkenmark indicator, and lpcd value. Classify the state as per WRI standards. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **WSI plays a critical role** in sustainable water-resource planning by:

1. **Triggering policy action** — Once WSI crosses 40%, it is universally treated as an action threshold. The Jal Shakti Abhiyan (India) uses WSI-equivalent metrics to declare water-stressed districts.
2. **Prioritising infrastructure investments** — Inter-basin transfer schemes such as the **Krishna-Godavari Link** are designed using basin-level WSI maps to identify deficit zones.
3. **Tracking SDG 6.4.2 progress** — India reports its national WSI (~40% in 2022) to the UN annually.

**Case Studies:**

- **Chennai (Tamil Nadu):** WSI > 80% in 2019; triggered the desalination plant at Nemmeli and the Telugu Ganga project.
- **Bengaluru (Karnataka):** WSI ~ 60% (high stress); led to mandatory rainwater harvesting (RWH) by-laws and Cauvery Stage-V augmentation.
- **Kerala — Palakkad District:** WSI ~ 45–55% due to paddy irrigation; Kerala Irrigation Department has initiated micro-irrigation pilots.

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Per-capita availability $= 1450 \; \text{m}^3/\text{person/yr}$, Population $P = 35 \times 10^6$, $W_D = 48 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}$.

**Step 1 — Total Renewable Supply:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_S &= FI \times P \\
&= 1450 \times 35 \times 10^6 \\
&= 5.075 \times 10^{10} \; \text{m}^3/\text{yr} \\
&= 50.75 \; \text{km}^3/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — WSI Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WSI &= \dfrac{48}{50.75} = 0.9458 \\
WSI \, (\%) &= 94.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Falkenmark Indicator (given):** $FI = 1450 \; \text{m}^3/\text{capita/yr}$ → falls in **WATER STRESS** band.

**Step 4 — lpcd Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Q per capita} &= 1450 \; \text{m}^3 \\
\text{Daily per capita} &= \dfrac{1450 \times 1000}{365} = 3972.6 \; \text{lpcd}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:** Since WSI = 94.58% > 80%, the state is classified as **EXTREMELY HIGH STRESS** per WRI Aqueduct. This is a critical situation demanding immediate demand-side interventions (RWH, recycling) and supply augmentation (desalination or inter-basin transfer).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in WSI Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing km³ and Mm³ without conversion. Always verify units before division.
> 2. **Withdrawal vs. Consumption confusion:** WSI uses **gross withdrawal**, not consumption. Return flows remain in the basin.
> 3. **Falkenmark thresholds mis-quoted:** Many students write 1700, 1000, 500 but forget the units (m³/capita/yr).
> 4. **Missing classification step:** Just computing the WSI number without applying the threshold is incomplete — the **classification carries 2–3 marks**.
> 5. **No sectoral breakdown:** Examiners expect $W_{agri} + W_{ind} + W_{dom}$ to be explicitly stated; a single demand figure without sectoral split loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping engineering recommendation:** A 14-mark question on WSI invariably demands an **intervention recommendation** (RWH, recycling, micro-irrigation, inter-basin transfer).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **WSI Formula (memorise verbatim):** $WSI = W_D / W_S$, dimensionless.
- **WRI Thresholds:** <10% (Low), 10–20% (Low-Med), 20–40% (Med-High), 40–80% (High), >80% (Extremely High).
- **Falkenmark Thresholds:** >1700, 1000–1700, 500–1000, <500 m³/capita/yr.
- **Action Threshold:** WSI > 40% OR FI < 1000 → mandatory interventions.
- **WPI Sub-indices:** R, A, C, U, E (Resources, Access, Capacity, Use, Environment) — each 0–100.
- **Renewable Supply:** Surface runoff + Groundwater recharge (NOT fossil groundwater).
- **Withdrawal (NOT consumption) is used in WSI.**
- **Conversion Factor:** $1 \; \text{km}^3 = 10^9 \; \text{m}^3 = 10^{12} \; \text{litres}$.
- **lpcd Conversion:** $lpcd = \dfrac{Q(\text{m}^3/\text{yr}) \times 10^9}{P \times 365}$.
- **WE major sectors:** Agriculture (~70–90%), Industry (~5–20%), Domestic (~5–15%).
- **Practical Applications:** SDG 6.4.2 reporting, basin transfer planning, drought declaration, industrial site selection.
- **Standard Bodies:** WRI, FAO AQUASTAT, EEA, UN-Water.
- **India's National WSI (2022):** ~40–45% → "High Stress" boundary → policy focus on demand management and recycling.
- **Common Unit Traps:** Mm³ = million m³, km³ = billion m³ (= 1000 Mm³). Always double-check before computing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Status and Trend of Surface and Groundwater
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Status and Trend of Surface and Groundwater

# Status and Trend of Surface and Groundwater

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Resources Status** refers to the *quantitative* (volumetric availability, per-capita share, extraction rates) and *qualitative* (physical, chemical, biological parameters) condition of the world's freshwater stocks — both **surface water** (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and **groundwater** (aquifers, wells) — measured against benchmarks of sustainability, demand, and ecological flow requirements.

**Surface Water** — Water present on the Earth's surface in liquid form (rivers, streams, lakes, ponds, reservoirs) and in frozen form (glaciers, ice caps). It constitutes about **0.3%** of total global freshwater.

**Groundwater** — The water located beneath the Earth's surface in soil pore spaces and in the fractures of rock formations, stored in **aquifers**. It constitutes about **30.1%** of total global freshwater — the largest accessible freshwater reserve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per Capita Water Availability (PCWA)** is the most cited KTU and policy-making metric, defined as the total annual renewable freshwater resource divided by the population of a region/country.
> $$\text{PCWA} = \frac{\text{Annual Renewable Water Resource (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Population}}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Earth's water as a **bank savings account**:
- The **hydrosphere** is the *total corpus* of the bank.
- **Rivers and lakes (surface water)** are like the *cash in your wallet* — easy to access, but limited and constantly used up.
- **Groundwater in aquifers** is like the *fixed deposit* — locked in, slow to recharge, and overdrawing it (over-extraction) is equivalent to breaking the FD prematurely.
- **Glaciers and ice caps** are the *long-term investments* — massive but melting fast under climate change.

> **Status** = checking the *current balance* of these accounts.
> **Trend** = analyzing whether the balance is *growing, stable, or declining* over time.

### 1.3 Key Global & Indian Snapshot (Authoritative Data)

| Parameter | Global Value | India Value | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Water on Earth | 1.386 billion km³ | — | USGS |
| Freshwater % | 2.5% | — | USGS |
| Per Capita Water Availability (2023) | ~1,700 m³/person/year | ~1,486 m³/person/year (declining) | FAO AQUASTAT / MoJS |
| Water-stressed threshold | < 1,700 m³ | India crossed in 2011 | Falkenmark Index |
| Water-scarce threshold | < 1,000 m³ | Projected by 2025 | Falkenmark Index |
| Groundwater extraction in India | — | ~63% of total irrigation (62 BCM) | CGWB 2022 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Falkenmark Water Stress Index**: A country/region is considered *water-stressed* when annual renewable freshwater per capita falls below **1,700 m³** and *water-scarce* when it falls below **1,000 m³**.

### 1.4 Surface Water vs. Groundwater — Quick Comparison

| Attribute | Surface Water | Groundwater |
|---|---|---|
| **Reservoir** | Rivers, lakes, reservoirs | Aquifers |
| **Renewal rate** | Fast (days–weeks) | Very slow (years–centuries) |
| **Global share of freshwater** | ~0.3% | ~30.1% |
| **Ease of access** | Easy, but variable | Requires wells/borewells |
| **Quality issues** | BOD, pathogens, organic pollution | Hardness, nitrates, arsenic, fluoride |
| **India's share of usage** | ~40% (irrigation + domestic) | ~60% (irrigation + domestic) |
| **Vulnerability to climate** | High (droughts/floods) | Moderate (slow response) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Global Status of Surface and Groundwater

### 2.1.1 The Hydrological Cycle and Natural Distribution

Water on Earth follows a continuous cycle governed by solar energy and gravity. The fundamental equation for any watershed over a long-term average is:

$$P = ET + R + \Delta S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Precipitation (mm/year)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration (mm/year)
- $R$ = Runoff to streams/rivers (mm/year)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in storage (negligible over long periods)

**Why this matters (The Why):** Only the *Runoff (R)* component becomes usable surface water, while the infiltrated portion recharges groundwater. Climate change is altering both $P$ and $ET$ asymmetrically, shrinking effective $R$ in many regions.

### 2.1.2 Status of Surface Water — Global Picture

- **Major Rivers:** The Amazon, Congo, Ganga, Yangtze, Nile, and Mississippi carry ~50% of global river discharge.
- **Lakes:** The Great Lakes (North America), Lake Baikal (Russia — world's deepest), Lake Tanganyika, and Lake Victoria hold ~90% of surface liquid freshwater.
- **Reservoirs:** Global artificial reservoir storage ≈ 7,000–8,000 km³, but *siltation* is reducing live storage by **0.5%–1% per year** in developing nations.

### 2.1.3 Status of Groundwater — Global Picture

- **Aquifer Types:** Unconfined (water-table), Confined (artesian), Perched, and Fractured rock aquifers.
- **Major Aquifers:** The Nubian Sandstone Aquifer (Sahara), Ogallala Aquifer (USA), Great Artesian Basin (Australia), North China Plain Aquifer, and the Indo-Gangetic alluvial aquifer.
- **Global abstraction:** ~900 km³/year; agriculture consumes ~70% of it.

## 2.2 Status of Surface and Groundwater in India

### 2.2.1 Surface Water Resources of India

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India's average annual surface water runoff is estimated at **1,869 Billion Cubic Meters (BCM)** in an average year. However, *utilizable* surface water is only **~690 BCM** due to topographic and hydrological constraints (CWC, 2021).

**Major River Systems:**
1. **Himalayan Rivers** (Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra) — Perennial, snow/glacier-fed
2. **Peninsular Rivers** (Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery, Narmada, Tapti) — Rain-fed, seasonal flow
3. **Coastal Rivers** — Short, westward-flowing

**Major Lakes:**
- Wular Lake (J&K) — largest freshwater lake
- Chilika Lake (Odisha) — largest brackish lagoon
- Dal Lake (J&K) — rapidly shrinking, pollution-stressed
- Loktak Lake (Manipur) — only floating lake

### 2.2.2 Groundwater Resources of India

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India's total annual *replenishable* groundwater resource = **~432 BCM** (CGWB, 2022 estimates). Of this, *annual extractable* resource = **~398 BCM**, and the current *annual extraction* = **~245 BCM** (61% of extractable).

**Aquifer Classification in India:**
- **Alluvial aquifers** (Indo-Gangetic plains) — high yield, most exploited
- **Hard rock aquifers** (Deccan traps, granites) — low yield, fractured
- **Coastal aquifers** — salinity intrusion risk
- **Semi-consolidated formations** (sandstones) — moderate yield

### 2.2.3 CGWB Categorization of Districts (2022)

The Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) classifies Indian districts based on groundwater extraction vs. extractable resource:

| Category | Stage of Extraction (% of extractable) | % of Districts (2022) |
|---|---|---|
| **Safe** | < 70% | ~71% |
| **Semi-critical** | 70–90% | ~10% |
| **Critical** | 90–100% | ~4% |
| **Over-exploited** | > 100% | ~15% |
| **Saline** | — | Negligible |

> **High-Alert States:** Punjab, Haryana, Delhi, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Telangana, and Gujarat contain the highest percentage of *over-exploited* districts.

## 2.3 Trends — Why the Numbers Are Moving the Wrong Way

### 2.3.1 Per Capita Water Availability (PCWA) Trend in India

| Year | PCWA (m³/person/year) | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1951 | 5,177 | Comfortable |
| 1991 | 2,209 | Stressed approaching |
| 2001 | 1,820 | Stressed |
| 2011 | 1,544 | Water-stressed |
| 2023 | ~1,486 | **Water-stressed** |
| 2025 (projected) | ~1,400 | **Water-scarce threshold approaching** |

**Reason (The Why):**
- Population growth (India crossed 1.4 billion in 2023)
- Uneven spatial distribution of rainfall
- Over-extraction of groundwater for irrigation (~89% of total extraction)
- Climate change–induced rainfall variability

### 2.3.2 Trends in Groundwater Levels (CGWB 2022)

- **Long-term water table decline (2002–2022):** ~61% of monitoring wells show declining trends.
- **Sharpest decline:** Punjab, Haryana, Western UP, Delhi NCR.
- **Rising trends (localized):** Eastern India, parts of NE India, command areas of new reservoirs.

### 2.3.3 Trends in Surface Water Quality (CPCB Monitoring)

The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) monitors ~4,500 stations under the National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP). Key trends:

| River | Trend in BOD | Trend in DO | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ganga (middle/lower) | Improving (post-Namami Gange) | Slight improvement | Moderate pollution |
| Yamuna (Delhi stretch) | Worsening | Critically low DO | Severely polluted |
| Godavari (downstream) | Stable | Good | Acceptable |
| Kshipra, Musi, Mithi | High BOD | Low DO | Critical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designated Best Use (DBU) Classification of Water Bodies** (CPCB): A, B, C, D, E categories based on intended use (drinking, bathing, fisheries, irrigation, industrial cooling).

### 2.3.4 Climate Change & Glacial Trend

- Himalayan glaciers are retreating at **~30–60 m/decade** (ISRO, 2023 satellite data).
- Implication: Short-term boost to Ganga/Brahmaputra flows, followed by long-term decline.

## 2.4 Key Indices & Assessment Tools

### 2.4.1 Falkenmark Water Stress Index (WSI)

$$\text{WSI} = \frac{\text{Annual Renewable Water Resource (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Population}}$$

| WSI Value (m³/capita/year) | Status |
|---|---|
| > 1,700 | No stress |
| 1,000–1,700 | Water stress |
| 500–1,000 | Water scarcity |
| < 500 | Absolute scarcity |

### 2.4.2 Water Exploitation Index (WEI) — Equivalent to CGWB's Stage of Extraction

$$\text{WEI} = \frac{\text{Annual Freshwater Withdrawal}}{\text{Long-term Annual Renewable Resource}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.4.3 Water Quality Index (NSF-WQI)

$$\text{WQI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

Where $W_i$ is the unit weight of parameter $i$ and $Q_i$ is its sub-index (0–100).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = ET + R + \Delta S$ | Water balance equation | Watershed analysis |
| 2 | $\text{PCWA} = \dfrac{W_{annual}}{P_{op}}$ | Per capita water availability | National assessments |
| 3 | $\text{Stage of GW Extraction} = \dfrac{GW_{draft}}{GW_{extractable}} \times 100$ | CGWB metric | District categorization |
| 4 | $\text{WSI} = \dfrac{W_{annual}}{P_{op}}$ | Falkenmark index | Stress classification |
| 5 | $\text{WEI} = \dfrac{W_{withdrawal}}{W_{renewable}} \times 100$ | Water exploitation index | Global comparison |
| 6 | $\text{WQI}_{NSF} = \dfrac{\sum W_i Q_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Water Quality Index | Surface water quality |
| 7 | $\text{Decline Rate} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{t}$ | Water table change (m/year) | Long-term trend |
| 8 | $R = P - ET$ | Effective runoff | Runoff estimation |

> **Note:** When computing $W_i$ for NSF-WQI, the unit weight is calculated as $W_i = \dfrac{k}{S_i}$, where $k$ is a proportionality constant and $S_i$ is the standard permissible value of parameter $i$. (Avoid using $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation in tables — use $\mid$ or $\text{abs}(\cdot)$.)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban planners** use PCWA and Falkenmark Index for water-supply design.
- **Agricultural engineers** use Stage of Extraction to size drip/sprinkler irrigation projects.
- **Environmental engineers** apply WQI to rank polluted stretches and prioritize remediation.
- **Hydrogeologists** use water-table decline rates to declare "Critical" zones under the Atal Jal/ Jal Jeevan Mission framework.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Per Capita Water Availability (PCWA) of India

**Given:**
- India's annual utilizable surface water ≈ 690 BCM
- Annual replenishable groundwater ≈ 432 BCM
- Total utilizable = 690 + 432 = 1,122 BCM ≈ 1,123 BCM
- Population (2023) ≈ 1.43 billion = 1.43 × 10⁹
- 1 BCM = 10⁹ m³

**Find:** PCWA and classify per Falkenmark.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert BCM to m³.

$$W_{annual} = 1,123 \text{ BCM} \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3/\text{BCM}$$

$$W_{annual} = 1.123 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the PCWA formula.

$$\text{PCWA} = \frac{W_{annual}}{P_{op}} = \frac{1.123 \times 10^{12}}{1.43 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\text{PCWA} \approx 785.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{capita}/\text{year}$$

Wait — the PCWA of 1,486 m³ in current assessments uses *total natural runoff* (~1,869 BCM) not just *utilizable*. Let us recompute using the convention used by MoJS/FAO:

$$W_{annual} = 1,869 \text{ BCM} = 1.869 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{PCWA} = \frac{1.869 \times 10^{12}}{1.43 \times 10^9} \approx 1,307 \text{ m}^3/\text{capita}/\text{year}$$

Even with this figure, India sits in the **water-stressed** band (1,000–1,700). Using 2011 census data with $1.21 \times 10^9$ population:

$$\text{PCWA} = \frac{1.869 \times 10^{12}}{1.21 \times 10^9} \approx 1,545 \text{ m}^3/\text{capita}/\text{year}$$

This matches the official 1,544 m³ figure for 2011.

**Step 3:** Classify per Falkenmark.

Since 1,000 < 1,545 < 1,700 → India is in the **water-stressed** category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion rule to remember:** 1 BCM = 1 Billion Cubic Meter = $10^9$ m³. Population in lakhs must be converted to actual number (× 10⁵), and crores to (× 10⁷).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Stage of Groundwater Extraction in a District

**Given (Pune district, hypothetical):**
- Annual extractable groundwater resource = 1,200 MCM
- Annual groundwater draft for irrigation = 720 MCM
- Annual draft for domestic = 120 MCM
- Annual draft for industrial = 60 MCM

**Find:** Stage of extraction and CGWB category.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Sum the total draft.

$$G_{draft} = 720 + 120 + 60 = 900 \text{ MCM}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Stage of Extraction (SoE).

$$\text{SoE} = \frac{G_{draft}}{G_{extractable}} \times 100 = \frac{900}{1,200} \times 100$$

$$\text{SoE} = 75\%$$

**Step 3:** Classify per CGWB.

| SoE Range | Category |
|---|---|
| < 70% | Safe |
| 70–90% | Semi-Critical |
| 90–100% | Critical |
| > 100% | Over-Exploited |

Since 70 < 75 < 90 → District is **Semi-Critical**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Projecting Future PCWA (Trend Analysis)

**Given:**
- Current PCWA (2023) = 1,486 m³
- Current population = 1.43 billion
- Projected population (2050) = 1.67 billion
- Assume water resource remains at 1,869 BCM (climate-corrected)

**Find:** Projected PCWA in 2050 and classification.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate water resource in m³.

$$W = 1,869 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3 = 1.869 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Compute future PCWA.

$$\text{PCWA}_{2050} = \frac{1.869 \times 10^{12}}{1.67 \times 10^9}$$

$$\text{PCWA}_{2050} \approx 1,119 \text{ m}^3/\text{capita}/\text{year}$$

**Step 3:** Classify per Falkenmark.

1,000 < 1,119 < 1,700 → **Water-stressed** but on the verge of crossing the *water-scarce* threshold.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: NSF Water Quality Index (WQI)

**Given (3-parameter example, hypothetical surface water sample):**

| Parameter | Measured (Cᵢ) | Standard (Sᵢ) | Unit Weight (Wᵢ) | Qᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.8 | 8.5 | 0.218 | 85 |
| BOD | 4 | 5 mg/L | 0.372 | 73 |
| DO | 5.5 | 5 mg/L | 0.372 | 92 |

(The proportionality constant k is taken as 1.86.)

**Find:** NSF-WQI.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $W_i = k/S_i$.

$$W_{pH} = 1.86/8.5 = 0.219$$

$$W_{BOD} = 1.86/5 = 0.372$$

$$W_{DO} = 1.86/5 = 0.372$$

**Step 2:** Compute $W_i \cdot Q_i$.

$$W_{pH} \cdot Q_{pH} = 0.219 \times 85 = 18.6$$

$$W_{BOD} \cdot Q_{BOD} = 0.372 \times 73 = 27.2$$

$$W_{DO} \cdot Q_{DO} = 0.372 \times 92 = 34.2$$

**Step 3:** Sum numerator and denominator.

$$\sum W_i Q_i = 18.6 + 27.2 + 34.2 = 80.0$$

$$\sum W_i = 0.219 + 0.372 + 0.372 = 0.963$$

**Step 4:** Compute WQI.

$$\text{WQI} = \frac{80.0}{0.963} \approx 83.1$$

**Step 5:** Interpret.

| WQI Range | Quality |
|---|---|
| 90–100 | Excellent |
| 70–90 | Good |
| 50–70 | Medium |
| 25–50 | Bad |
| 0–25 | Very Bad |

WQI = 83.1 → **Good water quality.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drivers of Status & Trend

| Driver | Effect on Surface Water | Effect on Groundwater | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population growth | Higher withdrawal | Higher draft | Drives PCWA down |
| Urbanization | Runoff increase, contamination | Less recharge, contamination | Storm-water & sewer design |
| Industrialization | Effluent discharge | Toxic leachate | Effluent norms |
| Irrigation expansion | Reservoir siltation | Over-extraction | Stage of extraction |
| Climate change | Glacial melt, altered runoff | Recharge variability | Projection modeling |
| Land-use change | Reduced infiltration | Lower recharge | Watershed management |
| Policy (e.g., JJM) | Improved quality | New extraction norms | SDG 6 monitoring |
