# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the *Falkenmark Water Stress Index*. Mention the threshold values for water-stressed and water-scarce conditions. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Model Answer:**

The Falkenmark Water Stress Index (WSI) is defined as the *annual renewable freshwater resource available per capita per year*, expressed in cubic meters.

$$\text{WSI} = \frac{\text{Annual Renewable Water Resource (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Population}}$$

- **No stress:** WSI > 1,700 m³/capita/year
- **Water-stressed:** 1,000 < WSI < 1,700 m³/capita/year
- **Water-scarce:** 500 < WSI < 1,000 m³/capita/year
- **Absolute scarcity:** WSI < 500 m³/capita/year

India crossed the *water-stressed* threshold in 2011 and is projected to enter *water-scarce* by 2025 if trends persist. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between *Safe*, *Semi-Critical*, *Critical*, and *Over-Exploited* categories of groundwater assessment as per CGWB. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer:**

The Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) classifies districts based on the *Stage of Groundwater Extraction* (SoE):

$$\text{SoE} = \frac{\text{Annual Groundwater Draft}}{\text{Annual Extractable Resource}} \times 100$$

| Category | SoE Range | Indicative Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Safe** | < 70% | Comfortable recharge-draft balance |
| **Semi-Critical** | 70–90% | Approaching over-extraction |
| **Critical** | 90–100% | Draft almost equals recharge |
| **Over-Exploited** | > 100% | Draft exceeds recharge; water table falling |

Around **15%** of India's districts are over-exploited, concentrated in Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, and Tamil Nadu. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE — Attempt any ONE of the two sub-questions per main question**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Assessment of India's Water Resources Status*

**(a)** Discuss the spatial and temporal status of surface water resources in India. Highlight the contribution of major river systems and the constraints on utilizable runoff. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the *status* and *trend* of groundwater in India, with specific reference to CGWB 2022 data. Discuss the role of irrigation in driving over-extraction. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Total Surface Water Resource:**
- Average annual surface water runoff in India = **1,869 BCM** (CWC).
- However, only ~**690 BCM** is *utilizable* due to:
  - Topographic constraints (Himalayan rivers drain into Bay of Bengal after crossing neighboring countries).
  - Hydrological losses (evaporation, seepage).
  - Flood flows that cannot be stored.
  - Inter-state disputes (e.g., Cauvery, Krishna).

**2. Major River Systems:**
- **Himalayan rivers (Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra):** Perennial, glacier-fed, contribute ~60% of runoff; high flow variability.
- **Peninsular rivers (Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery, Narmada, Tapti):** Rain-fed, seasonal; ~40% of runoff.
- **Coastal short rivers (west-flowing):** Limited storage potential.

**3. Lakes and Reservoirs:**
- India has ~10,000+ lakes larger than 1 ha.
- Wular, Dal, Chilika, Loktak, Vembanad — many shrinking under pollution and siltation.
- Reservoir storage ~253 BCM; siltation rate = 0.5–1% per year → live storage loss.

**4. Trends:**
- Glacial retreat in Himalayas (30–60 m/decade).
- Erratic monsoons due to climate change.
- Pollution: 70% of surface water (CPCB) is unfit for direct potable use.

**Conclusion:** Surface water is geographically skewed, seasonally variable, and increasingly polluted, with declining utilizable proportion.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Quantitative Status (CGWB 2022):**
- Total replenishable groundwater = **432 BCM/year**.
- Annual extractable = **398 BCM**.
- Annual draft = **245 BCM** (61% of extractable).
- Over-exploited districts: **~15% of total** (~80+ districts).
- States with high over-exploitation: **Punjab, Haryana, Delhi, Rajasthan, TN, Karnataka, Telangana, Gujarat**.

**2. Trends (2002–2022):**
- ~61% of CGWB monitoring wells show a *declining* trend.
- Average decline in Punjab/Haryana: 0.5–1.5 m/year.
- Rising trends in eastern India (higher rainfall, lower abstraction).

**3. Drivers of Over-Extraction:**
- **Irrigation dominance:** ~89% of groundwater is used for irrigation.
- Subsidized/free electricity for pumps in Punjab, Haryana, AP, Telangana, TN.
- Shift from *kharif* to *rabi* (wheat, paddy in non-traditional areas) — paddy alone in Punjab uses ~5,000 L/kg of water.
- Cropping pattern mismatch with agro-climatic zones.

**4. Quality Concerns:**
- Arsenic: Ganga–Brahmaputra delta (West Bengal, Assam).
- Fluoride: Rajasthan, AP, Telangana.
- Nitrate: Intensive agricultural belts.
- Salinity: Coastal aquifers (Chennai, Mumbai).

**5. Policy Response:**
- Atal Jal (Aquifer Mapping).
- Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — piped water to every rural household.
- Draft Model Groundwater Bill (2017, revised 2021).
- Solar-powered pumping to reduce free-electricity-driven draft.

**Conclusion:** Groundwater is being mined faster than it is recharged in many regions, and a paradigm shift in irrigation and water-pricing policy is essential.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Indices, Trends, and Water Quality*

**(a)** Explain the *Falkenmark Water Stress Index* and the *Water Exploitation Index*. Calculate the PCWA of India for 2024 given annual renewable resource = 1,869 BCM and projected population = 1.44 billion. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP) of CPCB. Explain the *Designated Best Use (DBU)* classification system. Compute the NSF Water Quality Index for a sample with three parameters given below. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**

| Parameter | Measured | Standard (Sᵢ) | k |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.5 | 8.5 | 1.86 |
| BOD | 3.0 mg/L | 5 mg/L | 1.86 |
| DO | 6.0 mg/L | 5 mg/L | 1.86 |

Assume Qᵢ values: pH = 88, BOD = 80, DO = 90.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Falkenmark Water Stress Index (WSI):**

$$\text{WSI} = \frac{\text{Annual Renewable Water Resource (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Population}}$$

- No stress: > 1,700 m³
- Stressed: 1,000–1,700 m³
- Scarce: 500–1,000 m³
- Absolute scarcity: < 500 m³

**2. Water Exploitation Index (WEI):**

$$\text{WEI} = \frac{\text{Annual Freshwater Withdrawal}}{\text{Long-term Renewable Resource}} \times 100\%$$

- Global safe: < 25%
- Stress: 25–60%
- Critical: > 60%

**3. PCWA Calculation:**

Given: $W_{annual} = 1,869$ BCM = $1.869 \times 10^{12}$ m³; Population = $1.44 \times 10^9$.

$$\text{PCWA} = \frac{1.869 \times 10^{12}}{1.44 \times 10^{9}} = 1,297.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{capita}/\text{year}$$

**4. Interpretation:**

Since 1,000 < 1,298 < 1,700 → **India is in the WATER-STRESSED category.** Without policy intervention, India will cross the *water-scarce* threshold (1,000 m³) within the next two decades.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP):**
- Operated by **CPCB** under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.
- **~4,500 monitoring stations** across India on rivers, lakes, ponds, tanks, and ground water.
- Sampling frequency: **Monthly** (surface), **Half-yearly** (ground water).
- Parameters measured: 9 core (pH, EC, TDS, DO, BOD, COD, nitrate, total coliform, fecal coliform) + 18 trace metals/parameters.

**2. Designated Best Use (DBU) Classification:**

| Class | Best Use |
|---|---|
| **A** | Drinking water source without conventional treatment but with disinfection |
| **B** | Outdoor bathing (organized) |
| **C** | Drinking with conventional treatment + disinfection |
| **D** | Fish culture, wild life propagation |
| **E** | Irrigation, industrial cooling, controlled waste disposal |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> Many students write *pH* in the WQI table without converting to a $Q_i$ sub-index (0–100). Marks are deducted if you skip the $W_i = k/S_i$ unit-weight calculation step. Always show the table **and** the numerator/denominator summation separately.

**3. NSF-WQI Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute unit weights $W_i = k/S_i$:

$$W_{pH} = 1.86/8.5 = 0.2188$$

$$W_{BOD} = 1.86/5 = 0.372$$

$$W_{DO} = 1.86/5 = 0.372$$

**Step 2:** Compute $W_i \cdot Q_i$:

$$W_{pH} Q_{pH} = 0.2188 \times 88 = 19.25$$

$$W_{BOD} Q_{BOD} = 0.372 \times 80 = 29.76$$

$$W_{DO} Q_{DO} = 0.372 \times 90 = 33.48$$

**Step 3:** Sum:

$$\sum W_i Q_i = 19.25 + 29.76 + 33.48 = 82.49$$

$$\sum W_i = 0.2188 + 0.372 + 0.372 = 0.9628$$

**Step 4:** WQI:

$$\text{WQI} = \frac{82.49}{0.9628} = 85.7$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: WQI 85.7 → **Good water quality** (range 70–90).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this topic:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often forget to convert BCM to m³ (1 BCM = 10⁹ m³) — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing WSI and PCWA:** Both use the *same formula* but represent different things. WSI classifies; PCWA is a number. KTU expects a *classification statement* after computing PCWA.
> 3. **Mixing up total and utilizable water:** India's *total* runoff = 1,869 BCM, but *utilizable* ≈ 690 BCM (surface) + 432 BCM (ground) = 1,122 BCM. Always specify which.
> 4. **CGWB categories:** Forgetting to include the **'Saline'** category. Use *all five* categories for full marks.
> 5. **WQI computation:** Skipping the unit-weight step. Always write $W_i = k/S_i$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Total Earth water:** 1.386 billion km³; only 2.5% is fresh.
- [ ] **Freshwater distribution:** 68.7% glaciers, 30.1% groundwater, 1.2% surface.
- [ ] **Falkenmark thresholds:** 1,700 (stress) and 1,000 (scarce) m³/capita/year.
- [ ] **India's PCWA:** 5,177 (1951) → 1,486 (2023) → projected ~1,100 by 2050.
- [ ] **India's total annual runoff:** 1,869 BCM; utilizable ≈ 1,122 BCM.
- [ ] **CGWB categories:** Safe, Semi-Critical (70–90%), Critical (90–100%), Over-Exploited (>100%), Saline.
- [ ] **Over-exploited states:** Punjab, Haryana, Delhi, Rajasthan, TN, Karnataka, Telangana, Gujarat.
- [ ] **Irrigation share of groundwater draft:** ~89%.
- [ ] **CPCB DBU classes:** A, B, C, D, E.
- [ ] **NWMP:** ~4,500 stations, 9 core parameters, monthly sampling.
- [ ] **Key pollution trends:** Yamuna-Delhi critical, Ganga improving, Godavari stable.
- [ ] **Glacial retreat:** 30–60 m/decade in Himalayas.
- [ ] **Hydrological equation:** $P = ET + R + \Delta S$.
- [ ] **WEI:** Withdrawal ÷ Renewable × 100.
- [ ] **NSF-WQI formula:** $\sum(W_i Q_i) / \sum W_i$ with $W_i = k/S_i$.
- [ ] **National Missions:** Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM), Atal Jal, Namami Gange.
- [ ] **SDG 6:** Clean Water and Sanitation — central to all current policy.

> **Final Tip for KTU Board Exam:** Always convert units, always show the formula *before* substituting values, and always end with a *classification* or *interpretation* statement. This is where most of the easy 1–2 marks are won or lost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues and Policy Interventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Issues and Policy Interventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Environmental Issues and Policy Interventions** in the context of water and air quality management refer to the systematic identification of anthropogenic and natural stressors that degrade the quality of water bodies and the atmosphere, combined with the regulatory, legislative, technological, and economic mechanisms deployed by governmental and non-governmental bodies to prevent, control, and remediate pollution.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this domain encompasses:
- **Sources and consequences of pollution** (point, non-point, and line sources).
- **Statutory framework** (Acts, Rules, Notifications, Standards).
- **Institutional architecture** (CPCB, SPCBs, MoEFCC, Pollution Control Committees).
- **Programmatic interventions** (NCAP, Namami Gange, Swachh Bharat, Smart Cities Mission).
- **International obligations** (Stockholm, Rio, Montreal, Kyoto, Paris agreements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The learner is expected to understand the linkage between real-world environmental issues and the corresponding national/international policy response, with emphasis on the *Indian regulatory ecosystem*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a city hospital. The doctors (scientists) diagnose diseases (pollution issues) — like asthma, cholera, kidney failure. The hospital administration (government) drafts treatment protocols (policies) — like prescribing clean water, masks, vaccines, and quarantines. Now imagine one patient (the planet Earth) being treated by hundreds of doctors (multiple agencies) using different protocols. **Policy intervention is the standardized treatment protocol that ensures every patient gets consistent, evidence-based care.**

In the same way:
- **Issues** = Symptoms (smog, contaminated rivers, acid rain).
- **Policy** = The prescription (Acts, Standards, Programs).
- **Implementation** = The hospital (CPCB, SPCB, Municipal Bodies).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A policy without enforcement is a *paper shield*; an issue without a policy is a *blind spot*. Both must coexist for effective environmental governance.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Policy Formulation

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PM2.5 (24-hr)** | **60 µg/m³** (NAAQS) | Respirable particulate threshold |
| **PM10 (24-hr)** | **100 µg/m³** | Coarse particulate threshold |
| **SO₂ (24-hr)** | **80 µg/m³** | Sulphur dioxide limit |
| **NO₂ (24-hr)** | **80 µg/m³** | Nitrogen dioxide limit |
| **BOD (inland water)** | **3 mg/L** | Organic load indicator |
| **pH (drinking water)** | **6.5 – 8.5** | Acceptable range |
| **AQI Categories** | **0–500** | Air quality health index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold constants above** are the *legally enforceable* numbers prescribed under the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009** and **BIS IS 10500:2012** for drinking water.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Environmental Issues

#### 2.1.1 Water Quality Issues
- **Point source pollution** — discharge from identifiable outlets (industrial effluents, sewage pipes).
- **Non-point (diffuse) source pollution** — agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, atmospheric deposition.
- **Eutrophication** — nutrient over-enrichment (N, P) leading to algal blooms and hypoxia.
- **Groundwater contamination** — arsenic, fluoride, nitrates, heavy metals.
- **Thermal pollution** — discharge of hot water from power plants reducing dissolved oxygen.
- **Emerging contaminants** — pharmaceuticals, microplastics, PFAS, endocrine disruptors.
- **Water scarcity and stress** — demand exceeding renewable supply (per capita availability < 1,700 m³ = *water stress*; < 1,000 m³ = *water scarcity*).

#### 2.1.2 Air Quality Issues
- **Particulate Matter (PM2.5, PM10)** — fine particles penetrate alveoli, cause cardiovascular and respiratory morbidity.
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — forms sulphuric acid → **acid rain** (pH < 5.6).
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)** — photochemical smog, tropospheric ozone formation.
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — benzene, toluene; carcinogenic.
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — binds haemoglobin → carboxyhaemoglobin.
- **Lead (Pb)** — neurotoxic, banned in petrol but persists in legacy environments.
- **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** — CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs → climate change.
- **Indoor Air Pollution (IAP)** — solid biomass burning, kerosene, tobacco smoke.

### 2.2 The Indian Policy Framework (Statutory & Programmatic)

#### 2.2.1 Core Legislations
1. **The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** — established CPCB and SPCBs; empowers state bodies to set effluent standards, prosecute polluters.
2. **The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** — extends the framework to air, empowers boards to declare "air pollution control areas."
3. **The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** — the **umbrella Act** post-Bhopal disaster; empowers central government to prescribe standards, procedures, and penalties for *any* environmental pollutant.
4. **The Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016** — cradle-to-grave management of industrial hazardous waste.
5. **The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977** — economic instrument (cess) on water consumption by industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The 1974 Act was the *first* environmental statute in India and remains the foundation. The 1986 EPA is the *catch-all* statute that allows the Centre to act even in unlisted areas.

#### 2.2.2 National Programs
- **Namami Gange Programme (2014)** — ₹20,000+ crore mission to rejuvenate the Ganga.
- **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP, 2019)** — targets 40% PM reduction by 2025–26 (revised to 2025–30 in some sectors); 131 non-attainment cities.
- **Swachh Bharat Mission (2014)** — open-defecation-free (ODF) status, solid and liquid waste management.
- **Smart Cities Mission** — urban infrastructure including air-quality monitoring.
- **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT)** — water supply, sewerage.
- **Jal Jeevan Mission (2019)** — functional household tap connections (FHTC).

#### 2.2.3 International Agreements
| Agreement | Year | Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stockholm Conference** | 1972 | First UN conference on human environment |
| **Montreal Protocol** | 1987 | CFCs / Ozone Depleting Substances |
| **UNFCCC / Rio Earth Summit** | 1992 | Climate change framework |
| **Kyoto Protocol** | 1997 | Binding GHG reduction targets |
| **Paris Agreement** | 2015 | Limit warming to 1.5–2 °C above pre-industrial |
| **Stockholm+50** | 2022 | Renewed environmental governance |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AQI Sub-Index** | $I_{p} = \dfrac{(I_{Hi} - I_{Lo})}{(BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo})} \cdot (C_{p} - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Convert pollutant concentration to a normalized index |
| **Per Capita Water Availability** | $A = \dfrac{Q_{annual}}{P}$ | Where Q = annual run-off, P = population |
| **Water Stress Index (Falkenmark)** | $S = \dfrac{Q_{per\,capita}}{1700}$ | < 1700 m³ = stress; < 1000 m³ = scarcity |
| **BOD Loading** | $L = Q \times BOD_{5}$ | g/day organic load into a stream |
| **Acid Rain pH** | $pH = -\log[H^{+}]$ | Natural rain ≈ 5.6; acid rain < 5.6 |
| **Dissolved Oxygen (Streeter-Phelps)** | $D = \dfrac{k_{d} \cdot L_{0}}{k_{r} - k_{d}} \left(e^{-k_{d}t} - e^{-k_{r}t}\right) + D_{0} e^{-k_{r}t}$ | Stream self-purification |
| **Carbon Footprint** | $CF = \sum (EF_{i} \times A_{i})$ | Emission factor × activity data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial exam point:** AQI is reported as the **maximum sub-index** among the eight criteria pollutants. This is the single most-tested quantitative concept in this module.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility
- **Industrial compliance** — Industries use these standards to design ETP (Effluent Treatment Plants) and APCD (Air Pollution Control Devices).
- **Urban planning** — Smart Cities and NCAP use AQI and water-stress indices for site selection and budgeting.
- **Litigation** — The National Green Tribunal (NGT, est. 2010) adjudicates environmental disputes using these standards.
- **Carbon markets** — India's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme uses energy efficiency metrics akin to carbon footprint logic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the AQI Sub-Index (CPCB Methodology)

The **Air Quality Index** is computed using **linear interpolation** between two adjacent breakpoints of a pollutant.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify the Pollutant Concentration
A monitoring station reports concentration $C_{p}$ for pollutant $p$ (e.g., PM2.5 = 78 µg/m³).

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Locate the Breakpoint Range
Consult the **CPCB breakpoint table**. For PM2.5:
- Breakpoint 1: $0$ to $30$ µg/m³ → AQI range $0$ to $50$.
- Breakpoint 2: $31$ to $60$ µg/m³ → AQI range $51$ to $100$.
- Breakpoint 3: $61$ to $90$ µg/m³ → AQI range $101$ to $200$.

Since $C_{p} = 78$ lies in **Breakpoint 3**, we set:
- $BP_{Lo} = 60$ (concentration lower bound)
- $BP_{Hi} = 90$ (concentration upper bound)
- $I_{Lo} = 100$ (AQI lower bound)
- $I_{Hi} = 200$ (AQI upper bound)

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply the Linear Interpolation Formula
The fundamental relationship is:

$$
I_{p} \;=\; \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \cdot (C_{p} - BP_{Lo}) \;+\; I_{Lo}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Substitute the Values
Plugging in the identified numbers:

$$
I_{p} \;=\; \frac{200 - 100}{90 - 60} \cdot (78 - 60) \;+\; 100
$$

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Simplify the Slope
Compute the slope (AQI change per µg/m³ of pollutant):

$$
\frac{200 - 100}{90 - 60} \;=\; \frac{100}{30} \;=\; 3.333\ \text{AQI}/\mu g/m^{3}
$$

#### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Multiply by the Concentration Excess
Concentration excess above the lower breakpoint:

$$
C_{p} - BP_{Lo} \;=\; 78 - 60 \;=\; 18\ \mu g/m^{3}
$$

So:

$$
3.333 \times 18 \;=\; 60.0
$$

#### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Add the Base AQI

$$
I_{p} \;=\; 60.0 + 100 \;=\; 160
$$

#### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Round to the Nearest Integer and Classify
Final sub-index for PM2.5 = **160**, which falls in the AQI band **101–200 = "Moderate"** (some texts use "Unhealthy for sensitive groups" at 151–200; confirm with latest CPCB chart).

#### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Repeat for All Eight Pollutants
Compute $I_{p}$ for PM10, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, CO, NH₃, and Pb. The **reported AQI = max($I_{p}$)** across all pollutants.

#### 3.1.10 Step 10 — Apply Health Advisory
The AQI is mapped to a colour code and health message:

| AQI Range | Category | Colour | Health Advisory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 50 | Good | Green | Minimal impact |
| 51 – 100 | Satisfactory | Yellow | Minor breathing discomfort for sensitive people |
| 101 – 200 | Moderate | Orange | May affect heart/lung patients |
| 201 – 300 | Poor | Red | Prolonged exposure harms general public |
| 301 – 400 | Very Poor | Purple | Avoid outdoor activity |
| 401 – 500 | Severe | Maroon | Health emergency |

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — AQI Calculation

**Problem:** A station reports the following 24-hour concentrations: PM2.5 = 78 µg/m³, PM10 = 130 µg/m³, NO₂ = 55 µg/m³, SO₂ = 28 µg/m³. Compute the AQI.

#### Step 1 — PM2.5
Already computed above: $I_{PM2.5} = 160$.

#### Step 2 — PM10 (130 µg/m³)
PM10 breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 100$, $BP_{Hi} = 250$, $I_{Lo} = 100$, $I_{Hi} = 200$.

$$
I_{PM10} \;=\; \frac{200 - 100}{250 - 100} \times (130 - 100) + 100
$$

$$
I_{PM10} \;=\; \frac{100}{150} \times 30 + 100 \;=\; 20 + 100 \;=\; 120
$$

#### Step 3 — NO₂ (55 µg/m³)
NO₂ breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 40$, $BP_{Hi} = 80$, $I_{Lo} = 50$, $I_{Hi} = 100$.

$$
I_{NO_{2}} \;=\; \frac{100 - 50}{80 - 40} \times (55 - 40) + 50 \;=\; 1.25 \times 15 + 50 \;=\; 18.75 + 50 \;=\; 68.75 \approx 69
$$

#### Step 4 — SO₂ (28 µg/m³)
SO₂ breakpoint: $BP_{Lo} = 0$, $BP_{Hi} = 40$, $I_{Lo} = 0$, $I_{Hi} = 50$.

$$
I_{SO_{2}} \;=\; \frac{50 - 0}{40 - 0} \times (28 - 0) + 0 \;=\; 1.25 \times 28 \;=\; 35
$$

#### Step 5 — Reported AQI
$$
AQI \;=\; \max(160,\ 120,\ 69,\ 35) \;=\; \mathbf{160}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** The day's AQI is **160 (Moderate)** and is driven by **PM2.5**. The dominant pollutant is always reported alongside the AQI as: *"AQI = 160 (PM2.5)."*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Policy to Issue Mapping

| Issue | Primary Cause | Key Policy / Act | Implementing Agency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **River pollution (Ganga)** | Untreated sewage, industrial effluents | Water Act 1974 + Namami Gange | NMCG, CPCB, State PCBs |
| **Urban smog (Delhi NCR)** | Stubble burning, vehicles, dust | Air Act 1981, NCAP, CAQM | CAQM, DPCC, CPCB |
| **Groundwater fluoride** | Geogenic + industrial | EPA 1986, BIS IS 10500 | CGWB, PHED |
| **E-waste** | Informal recycling | E-Waste Management Rules 2016 | CPCB, SPCBs |
| **Plastic pollution** | Single-use plastics | Plastic Waste Mgmt Rules 2016 | ULBs, SPCB |
| **Climate change** | GHG emissions | UNFCCC, Paris Agreement, NDC | MoEFCC, BEE |
| **Ozone depletion** | CFCs / ODS | Montreal Protocol, Ozone Depl. Substances Rules | CPCB, Ozone Cell |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Indian Environmental Governance — Institutional Hierarchy


### 4.2 Policy Intervention Flow — From Issue to Action

```mermaid

```

### 4.3 AQI Computation Block Architecture

```mermaid

    IN5[Pollutant 5 O3] --> N1
    IN6[Pollutant 6 CO] --> N1
    IN7[Pollutant 7 NH3] --> N1
    IN8[Pollutant 8 Pb] --> N1
    N1[Sub Index Calculator using CPCB Breakpoint Table] --> COMP[Maximum Selector]
    COMP --> RPT[Report AQI and Dominant Pollutant]
    RPT --> COLOUR[Apply Colour Code and Health Advisory]
```

### 4.4 National vs International Policy Linkage

```mermaid

    subgraph G1[International Layer]
        G1A[Stockholm 1972]
        G1B[Rio 1992 UNFCCC]

    end
    subgraph G2[National Statutes]
        G2A[Water Act 1974]
        G2B[Air Act 1981]
```
