```mermaid
    end
    subgraph G3[Programs and Missions]
        G3A[NCAP]
        G3B[Namami Gange]
        G3C[Swachh Bharat]
        G3D[Jal Jeevan]
        G3E[NDC Implementation]
    end
    G1A --> G2A
    G1A --> G2B
    G1B --> G2C
    G1C --> G2C
    G1D --> G2C
    G1E --> G3E
    G2A --> G3B
    G2A --> G3D
    G2B --> G3A
    G2C --> G3A
    G2C --> G3C
    G2C --> G3E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP). Mention its two primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
The **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** launched by the MoEFCC in January 2019 is India's flagship air quality management initiative. Its primary objectives are:
1. To **augment and evolve an air quality monitoring network** across the country for comprehensive assessment.
2. To achieve a **reduction in PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations by 20%–30%** by 2024, revised progressively to **40% reduction by 2025–26** in 131 non-attainment cities (cities not meeting NAAQS).

NCAP is a **time-bound, city-level** intervention that emphasizes **city action plans**, sectoral interventions (vehicles, industry, biomass burning, dust), and performance-based funding.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between the Water Act 1974 and the Air Act 1981 in terms of their scope and key provisions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution | Prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution |
| **Institutions** | Established CPCB and SPCBs | Extended the same CPCB and SPCBs to air |
| **Key Powers** | Effluent standards, consent to establish/operate, sample collection | Declaration of "air pollution control areas," emission standards, consent regime |
| **Penalties** | Imprisonment up to 6 years + fine | Imprisonment up to 6 years + fine (similar scale) |
| **Amendments** | Amended 1988 to align with EPA 1986 | Amended 1987 to align with EPA 1986 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Trick:** Water Act 1974 is the *parent* — the Air Act 1981 is essentially a *clone* with a different medium.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choose ONE alternative

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Model Paper]**
**(a) Discuss in detail the major water quality issues in India with suitable examples. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974 and the role of CPCB in water quality management. (7 marks)**

**OR**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choose ONE alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Model Paper]**
**(a) Discuss the major air quality issues in urban India, with reference to Delhi NCR. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) and discuss any two other major air quality policies/programmes. (7 marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solutions

#### Solution to Q.A.

**(a) Major Water Quality Issues in India (7 marks)**

1. **Municipal Sewage Discharge** — Class-II and III towns generate ~70% of sewage that remains untreated. The Ganga receives ~12,000 MLD of sewage of which only ~40% is treated.
2. **Industrial Effluents** — Tanneries (Kanpur), textile (Tirupur), pharmaceutical (Hyderabad) discharges heavy metals (Cr, Pb, Hg), salts, and dyes. Common parameters: BOD, COD, TDS, sulphides.
3. **Agricultural Runoff** — Excessive NPK fertilizers cause nitrate leaching → methaemoglobinaemia (blue baby syndrome); pesticides enter food chains → bioaccumulation.
4. **Groundwater Contamination** — **Arsenic** in West Bengal, Assam, Bihar; **Fluoride** in Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu; **Salinity** in coastal aquifers.
5. **Eutrophication** — Phosphates from detergents and fertilizers trigger algal blooms; lakes like Dal Lake (Srinagar), Powai (Mumbai) are examples.
6. **Thermal Pollution** — Discharge of condenser cooling water from coal/nuclear power plants reduces DO downstream.
7. **Emerging Contaminants** — Microplastics, PFAS, pharmaceutical residues detected in rivers like the Yamuna.

**(b) Water Act 1974 and Role of CPCB (7 marks)**

The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** is the foundational statute for water quality management in India. Salient features:

- **Establishment of CPCB and SPCBs**: Section 3 constitutes CPCB; Sections 4-5 constitute SPCBs.
- **Functions of CPCB**:
  1. Advise the Central Government on water pollution matters.
  2. Coordinate the activities of State Boards and resolve disputes.
  3. Lay down standards for water quality (effluent and ambient).
  4. Collect and disseminate data on water quality.
  5. Plan and execute national programmes for the prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution.
- **Powers**: CPCB can lay down effluent standards for industries; SPCB can grant/refuse "consent to establish" and "consent to operate" under Sections 25 and 26.
- **Penalties**: Section 24 imposes imprisonment (1.5–6 years) and fine for contravention.
- **Amendments**: 1988 amendment aligned it with the EPA 1986; 2003 amendment brought in noise pollution and cess mechanisms.

**Role of CPCB — Real Examples**: CPCB's *MINARS* (Monitoring of Indian National Aquatic Resources) network, *Ganga Action Plan I & II* technical support, *ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)* directives for industries.

---

#### Solution to Q.B.

**(a) Major Air Quality Issues in Urban India with Delhi NCR Case (7 marks)**

1. **PM2.5 and PM10 Pollution** — Delhi NCR consistently records PM2.5 > 250 µg/m³ in winter — 5× the NAAQS. Sources: vehicles (40%), stubble burning in Punjab/Haryana (25% in winter), road dust, industry.
2. **NO₂ and Photochemical Smog** — Vehicular emissions + sunlight → ozone formation → smog events.
3. **SO₂ and Acid Rain** — Coal-based power plants, refineries; affects monuments (e.g., Taj Mahal → "Marble Cancer").
4. **CO and VOCs** — Incomplete combustion, especially from two-wheelers and old vehicles.
5. **Indoor Air Pollution** — Biomass burning in rural households (chulha smoke); ~5 lakh premature deaths annually.
6. **Stubble Burning** — A trans-boundary issue; contributes ~30–40% of Delhi's PM during Oct–Nov.
7. **Health Burden** — WHO estimates ~16.7 lakh deaths in India annually due to air pollution (2022 report).

**Institutional Response in Delhi**: Commission for Air Quality Management (CAQM, 2021) — an overarching body to coordinate stubble burning, GRAP (Graded Response Action Plan), and city-level action plans.

**(b) NCAP and Two Other Air Quality Policies (7 marks)**

**NCAP (2019)**:
- **Objective**: PM2.5 and PM10 reduction by 40% by 2025–26 in **131 non-attainment cities**.
- **Approach**: City-level clean air action plans; sectoral interventions; performance-based funding.
- **Key features**: AQI monitoring expansion, technology support, public outreach.

**Two Other Major Policies:**

1. **Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)** — Implemented in Delhi NCR from 2017; classified actions under four stages (AQI 101–200, 201–300, 301–400, 401–500) — bans construction, diesel generators, brick kilns, etc. Activated dynamically by CAQM.

2. **Bharat Stage (BS-VI) Emission Standards** — Equivalent to Euro 6; mandates strict emission norms for vehicles. Coupled with **FAME-II (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles)** to promote EVs.

3. **(Bonus)** **Commission for Air Quality Management in NCR and Adjoining Areas Act, 2021** — Statutory body with overriding powers over multiple agencies to handle the NCR air-shed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: Students write "CPCB was established by the EPA 1986" — **WRONG.** CPCB was established by the **Water Act 1974**. EPA 1986 only *enhanced* CPCB's powers.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing **NCAP** with **NAMP** (National Air Quality Monitoring Programme). NCAP is *action-oriented*; NAMP is *monitoring-only*.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: AQI formula is often misquoted. Always remember: **AQI = max(sub-indices)**, not average.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to mention the *amendments* of the 1974 and 1981 Acts (both amended in 1987/88 to align with EPA 1986). Examiners expect this detail for full marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5**: In 14-mark answers, students often miss the **examples**. A bare list of points scores partial marks. Always add **specific examples** (e.g., "Arsenic in West Bengal" not just "groundwater contamination").

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Foundational Acts**: Water Act 1974 → Air Act 1981 → EPA 1986 (umbrella). Remember the chronology.
- **CPCB** is established by Water Act 1974; **EPA 1986** is the most powerful *catch-all* statute.
- **AQI** is the **maximum** of 8 pollutant sub-indices; computed by **linear interpolation** between breakpoints.
- **NAAQS 2009** prescribes 12 criteria pollutants with 24-hour and annual standards.
- **NCAP** is the current air-quality flagship; 131 non-attainment cities; 40% PM reduction target.
- **Namami Gange** focuses on river Ganga; **Jal Jeevan** focuses on rural tap water.
- **International Agreements**: Stockholm (1972) → Montreal (1987) → Rio/UNFCCC (1992) → Kyoto (1997) → Paris (2015).
- **Institutional Hierarchy**: MoEFCC → CPCB → SPCB → Industries/Citizens.
- **NGT (2010)** is the adjudicating body for environmental disputes.
- **Acid rain** is defined as pH < 5.6; primary cause: SO₂ and NOx emissions.
- **Eutrophication** is caused by excessive N and P; primary symptom: algal blooms + hypoxia.
- **Indoor air pollution** from biomass burning causes more deaths in rural India than outdoor PM2.5 — do not overlook.
- **For numerical problems**, always show **all substitution steps** in the AQI formula — partial credit is awarded for correct formula identification even if the final number is wrong.
- **Linkage Question** (high probability): Be ready to draw a flowchart showing how a real issue (e.g., Delhi smog) maps to a policy (NCAP), to a body (CAQM), to an instrument (GRAP), to a standard (BS-VI).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution of Rivers, Lakes and Groundwater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pollution of Rivers, Lakes and Groundwater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is Water Body Pollution?

**Pollution of water bodies** is defined as the introduction of contaminants into rivers, lakes, and groundwater in such quantities and of such duration that they adversely affect the natural chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the water resource, making it unfit for intended uses (drinking, irrigation, recreation, aquatic life support).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Framework**
> Pollution = $P_{input} > P_{assimilative\;capacity}$ of the water body
> When the pollutant loading exceeds the **assimilative capacity** (natural self-purification ability), the system fails and pollution manifests as degraded water quality.

### 1.2 Classification of Water Bodies (As per KTU Syllabus)

| Water Body | Definition | Pollution Sensitivity |
|------------|------------|----------------------|
| **River** | Flowing, lotic system with continuous unidirectional flow | High — pollution carried downstream |
| **Lake** | Standing, lentic system with limited flushing | Very high — accumulation of pollutants |
| **Groundwater** | Sub-surface water in aquifers | Very high — slow recovery, persistent contamination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lotic vs Lentic**: *Lotic* = flowing water (rivers). *Lentic* = still water (lakes, ponds). The flow regime fundamentally controls how pollution is distributed and diluted.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy 1 — The Drain Pipe (Rivers):**
Imagine a river as a long, flowing conveyor belt. If you pour ink at one end, the moving water carries it away and dilutes it. This is why a small amount of pollution in a fast-flowing river may be acceptable — but constant dumping overwhelms the dilution capacity.

**Analogy 2 — The Bathtub (Lakes):**
A lake is like a bathtub with no drain. Whatever you pour in stays there. Nutrients accumulate over years, leading to algal blooms and **eutrophication** — analogous to a bathtub gradually turning green if you keep adding nutrients (fertilizer).

**Analogy 3 — The Underground Sponge (Groundwater):**
Groundwater is like a massive underground sponge. Once contaminants soak in (leakage from a septic tank or chemical spill), they remain trapped for **decades to centuries** because the sponge only flushes very slowly.

### 1.4 Sources of Pollution (Point vs Non-Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source Categorization:**
> - **Point Source**: Discharge at a specific, identifiable location (e.g., industrial outfall pipe, municipal STP)
> - **Non-Point Source (NPS)**: Diffuse runoff from agriculture, urban areas, atmospheric deposition

### 1.5 Core Pollutants Inventory

- **Organic Matter** → measured as **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** and **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)**
- **Pathogens** → *E. coli*, coliforms, viruses
- **Nutrients** → Nitrogen (N) and Phosphorus (P) — cause **eutrophication**
- **Heavy Metals** → Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As)
- **Toxic Organics** → Pesticides, PAHs, PCBs
- **Thermal Pollution** → Heated water discharge from power plants
- **Suspended Solids** → TSS, turbidity
- **Salinity / TDS** → from irrigation return flows
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 River Pollution — Mechanisms

Rivers are **flowing systems**, so pollution is governed by:

#### 2.1.1 Advection–Dispersion Equation (ADE)

The fundamental mass-balance equation for a pollutant in a river:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -U \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} + D_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} - k C + S(x,t)$$

Where:
- $C$ = pollutant concentration (mg/L)
- $U$ = mean stream velocity (m/s)
- $D_x$ = longitudinal dispersion coefficient (m²/s)
- $k$ = first-order decay rate (day⁻¹)
- $S(x,t)$ = source/sink term

#### 2.1.2 Streeter–Phelps Equation (Oxygen Sag)

The classic model for dissolved oxygen (DO) deficit downstream of a BOD discharge:

$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$$

Where:
- $D_t$ = oxygen deficit at time $t$ (mg/L) downstream
- $L_0$ = initial BOD (ultimate) at point of discharge (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = BOD deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1–0.5**
- $k_2$ = reaeration rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.2–0.5**
- $D_0$ = initial DO deficit at point of discharge (mg/L)
- $t$ = time of travel downstream (days)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Oxygen Sag Curve** plots DO vs. distance/time downstream. Critical point = **maximum deficit (D_c)** at **critical time (t_c)**.

**Critical Time Formula:**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

### 2.2 Lake Pollution — Eutrophication

**Eutrophication** is the nutrient-driven enrichment of lakes leading to excessive algal growth, oxygen depletion, and ecosystem collapse.

**Carlson's Trophic State Index (TSI):**

$$TSI = 10\left(6 - \frac{\ln 2.3}{\ln 2}\right)\left[\ln \sigma \right]$$

For Secchi Disk depth (in meters):

$$TSI_{SD} = 60 - 14.41 \ln(SD)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trophic States (KTU Definition):**
> - **Oligotrophic** (TSI < 40): Nutrient-poor, clear water
> - **Mesotrophic** (TSI 40–50): Moderate nutrients
> - **Eutrophic** (TSI 50–70): Nutrient-rich, algal blooms likely
> - **Hypereutrophic** (TSI > 70): Severe pollution, fish kills

### 2.3 Groundwater Pollution — Mechanisms

Groundwater pollution follows different transport physics:

#### 2.3.1 Advection

$$v = \frac{K \cdot i}{n_e}$$

Where:
- $v$ = seepage/groundwater velocity (m/day)
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity (m/day)
- $i$ = hydraulic gradient (dimensionless)
- $n_e$ = effective porosity (dimensionless)

#### 2.3.2 Retardation Factor (for contaminants)

$$R = 1 + \frac{\rho_b \cdot K_d}{n_e}$$

Where:
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of soil (kg/m³)
- $K_d$ = distribution coefficient (mL/g)
- $R$ > 1 means contaminant moves **slower** than water

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f)$ | 5-day BOD test | mg/L |
| 2 | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}$ | Remaining BOD after time t | mg/L |
| 3 | $D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | Streeter–Phelps DO Deficit | mg/L |
| 4 | $t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln(k_2/k_1)$ | Critical time | days |
| 5 | $D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | Critical deficit (approx.) | mg/L |
| 6 | $U = Q / A$ | Stream velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $TSI_{SD} = 60 - 14.41 \ln(SD)$ | Carlson Trophic Index | unitless |
| 8 | $v = K \cdot i / n_e$ | Groundwater velocity (Darcy) | m/day |
| 9 | $R = 1 + \rho_b K_d / n_e$ | Retardation factor | unitless |
| 10 | $C_{mix} = \frac{Q_s C_s + Q_w C_w}{Q_s + Q_w}$ | Complete mix at confluence | mg/L |

### 2.5 Self-Purification in Rivers (Natural Attenuation)

Rivers have an inherent ability to recover through:
1. **Dilution** — dispersion into the flow
2. **Sedimentation** — settleable solids drop out
3. **Oxidation** — aerobic microbial decomposition
4. **Sunlight (UV)** — photolysis of certain organics
5. **Predation** — natural die-off of pathogens

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Self-purification is **NOT infinite**. The 4 zones of river pollution (Degradation → Active Decomposition → Recovery → Clean Zone) end only if no new pollution is added.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Streeter–Phelps Oxygen Sag Equation

This is the **most-tested derivation in KTU** for this module.

**Step 1: Mass Balance for Dissolved Oxygen**

The rate of change of DO deficit $D$ along the river is:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D$$

Where:
- $k_1 L$ = deoxygenation rate (BOD exertion)
- $k_2 D$ = reaeration rate (oxygen recovery)

**Step 2: Express BOD L as a Function of Time**

The remaining BOD decays exponentially:

$$L(t) = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the Mass Balance**

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** of the form:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 D = k_1 L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 4: Find the Integrating Factor**

$$\mu(t) = e^{\int k_2 \, dt} = e^{k_2 t}$$

**Step 5: Multiply Both Sides by the Integrating Factor**

$$e^{k_2 t} \frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 e^{k_2 t} D = k_1 L_0 \cdot e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

The left-hand side is a perfect derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(D \cdot e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 L_0 \cdot e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

**Step 6: Integrate Both Sides**

$$D \cdot e^{k_2 t} = \int k_1 L_0 \cdot e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} \, dt + C$$

$$D \cdot e^{k_2 t} = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \cdot e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} + C$$

**Step 7: Solve for D(t)**

$$D(t) = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \cdot e^{-k_1 t} + C \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$$

**Step 8: Apply Initial Condition**

At $t = 0$, $D(0) = D_0$ (initial deficit). Therefore $C = D_0 - \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}$.

**Step 9: Final Streeter–Phelps Equation**

$$\boxed{D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Time (Maximum Deficit)

**Step 1:** Take the derivative of $D_t$ with respect to $t$ and set to zero (for maximum):

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$$

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) - k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t} = 0$$

**Step 2:** For the simplified case $D_0 = 0$ (river already saturated with DO before discharge):

$$\frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t_c} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t_c}\right) = 0$$

$$k_2 e^{-k_2 t_c} = k_1 e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$e^{(k_1 - k_2) t_c} = \frac{k_1}{k_2}$$

$$(k_1 - k_2) t_c = \ln\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A river has DO = **8.5 mg/L**, velocity **0.4 m/s**, and receives an effluent with BOD₅ = **50 mg/L**, DO = **1.0 mg/L** at a flow ratio such that mixed BOD (L₀) = **20 mg/L**. Given $k_1 = 0.23$ day⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.4$ day⁻¹. Find the critical time and maximum deficit.

**Step 1: Initial Deficit**

$$D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$$

Assume $DO_{sat} = 9.2$ mg/L:

$$D_0 = 9.2 - 8.5 = 0.7 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: Critical Time**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.4 - 0.23} \ln\left(\frac{0.4}{0.23}\right)$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.17} \ln(1.739)$$

$$t_c = \frac{0.553}{0.17} = 3.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Critical Deficit**

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t_c} - e^{-k_2 t_c}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t_c}$$

$$D_c = \frac{0.23 \times 20}{0.17}\left(e^{-0.23 \times 3.25} - e^{-0.4 \times 3.25}\right) + 0.7 \times e^{-1.3}$$

$$D_c = 27.06 \left(e^{-0.7475} - e^{-1.3}\right) + 0.7 \times 0.2725$$

$$D_c = 27.06 (0.4735 - 0.2725) + 0.1908$$

$$D_c = 27.06 \times 0.201 + 0.1908$$

$$D_c = 5.44 + 0.19 = 5.63 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Critical DO Concentration**

$$DO_{min} = DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.2 - 5.63 = 3.57 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** DO below **4 mg/L** is lethal to most fish. Students often forget to convert $t_c$ (days) into **distance** using $x_c = U \cdot t_c$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollution Parameters Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Threshold/Standard |
|-----------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | BOD₅ | $DO_i - DO_f$ | < 3 mg/L (drinking source) |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | Dichromate reflux | < 10 mg/L (Class A) |
| Total Dissolved Solids | TDS | Evaporation residue | < 500 mg/L (drinking) |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | Filtration + weighing | < 50 mg/L (river) |
| Dissolved Oxygen | DO | Winkler / probe | > 5 mg/L (aquatic life) |
| pH | pH | $-\log[H^+]$ | 6.5–8.5 |
| Heavy Metals | Pb, Hg, Cd | AAS / ICP | 0.01–0.05 mg/L |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Oxygen Sag Curve (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point of Discharge - t equals 0] -->|High BOD| B[Deoxygenation Zone]
    B -->|DO decreases| C[Critical Point - Minimum DO]
    C -->|Reaeration dominates| D[Recovery Zone]
    D -->|Approaches saturation| E[Clean Zone - DO Recovery]
    
    F[BOD Exertion - k1] -.-> B
    G[Atmospheric Reaeration - k2] -.-> C
    H[Sedimentation] -.-> B
    I[Photo-oxidation] -.-> D
```

### 4.2 Lake Eutrophication Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nutrient Input - N and P] --> B[Light Penetration - Clear Water]
    B --> C[Algal Bloom Growth]
    C --> D[Surface Algal Mat]
    D --> E[Light Blockage Below]
    E --> F[Bottom Plants Die]
    F --> G[Aerobic Decomposition]
    G --> H[Oxygen Depletion - Hypolimnion]
    H --> I[Anoxic Conditions]
    I --> J[Release of P from Sediments]
    J --> A
```

### 4.3 Groundwater Contamination Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Surface Contaminant Source] --> A2[Landfill - Septic Tank - Spill]
    A2 --> B1[Unsaturated Zone - Vadose]
    B1 --> B2[Filtration - Adsorption - Biodegradation]
    B2 --> C1[Water Table - Saturated Zone]
    C1 --> C2[Aquifer - Lateral Flow]
    C2 --> D1[Contaminant Plume Migration]
    D1 --> D2[Extraction Well or Discharge Zone]
    
    P1[Retardation - R factor] -.-> B2
    P2[Dispersion] -.-> C2
    P3[Advection - Darcy Velocity] -.-> D1
```

### 4.4 River Pollution Zones (4-Zone Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Zone 1 - Degradation] --> Z2[Zone 2 - Active Decomposition]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 - Recovery]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4 - Clean Water]
    
    Z1Desc[Slightly cloudy - fish begin dying] -.- Z1
    Z2Desc[DO at minimum - septic conditions - H2S] -.- Z2
    Z3Desc[DO rises - algae - fungi - clean appearance] -.- Z3
    Z4Desc[DO near saturation - normal biota restored] -.- Z4
```

### 4.5 Water Body Pollution Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Pollutant Discharge] --> Q1{Receiving Water Body?}
    Q1 -->|River| R1[Advection-Dispersion Dominant]
    Q1 -->|Lake| L1[Accumulation - Stratification]
    Q1 -->|Groundwater| G1[Slow Transport - Retardation]
    
    R1 --> R2[Apply Streeter-Phelps]
    L1 --> L2[Apply Trophic State Index]
    G1 --> G2[Apply Darcy's Law and Retardation]
    
    R2 --> R3[Check DO Sag]
    L2 --> L3[Check Eutrophication Risk]
    G2 --> G3[Check Plume Migration Time]
    
    R3 --> End{Mitigation Required?}
    L3 --> End
    G3 --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Define "Eutrophication" of lakes and list any two causes.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Eutrophication** is the nutrient-driven enrichment of a lake or water body, leading to excessive growth of algae and aquatic plants, followed by oxygen depletion when the biomass decomposes.
>
> **Causes (any two):**
> 1. Excessive inflow of **nitrates and phosphates** from agricultural runoff and detergents.
> 2. Discharge of **untreated or partially treated domestic sewage** containing organic nitrogen.
> 3. Soil erosion carrying nutrient-rich sediments into the lake.
>
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark for each cause = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Differentiate between "Point Source" and "Non-Point Source" pollution with examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Point Source | Non-Point Source |
> |---------|--------------|------------------|
> | **Discharge location** | Specific, identifiable | Diffuse, widespread |
> | **Monitoring** | Easy | Difficult |
> | **Example** | Industrial outfall pipe | Agricultural runoff |
> | **Control** | End-of-pipe treatment | Best Management Practices |
>
> Point sources are regulated under consent limits (e.g., CPCB norms), while non-point sources require watershed-scale interventions. *(1.5 marks each)*

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (CHOOSE ANY ONE)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Streeter-Phelps equation** for dissolved oxygen deficit in a river receiving a BOD discharge. Explain the oxygen sag curve with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A river flowing with a velocity of **0.5 m/s** receives an effluent discharge. The mixed BOD ($L_0$) = **30 mg/L** and initial DO deficit $D_0$ = **2 mg/L**. Given $k_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.45$ day⁻¹, and DO saturation = **9.0 mg/L**. Calculate:
- (i) Critical time $t_c$
- (ii) Critical deficit $D_c$
- (iii) Minimum DO at critical point
- (iv) Distance downstream to critical point **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Derivation:** The mass balance on the dissolved oxygen deficit in a river is:
>
> $$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D$$
>
> This is a first-order linear ODE. Using integrating factor $e^{k_2 t}$:
>
> $$\frac{d}{dt}(D \cdot e^{k_2 t}) = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1)t}$$
>
> Integrating and applying initial condition $D(0) = D_0$:
>
> $$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$
>
> **Sketch explanation (refer to Section 4.1):** The DO curve dips to a minimum at critical time $t_c$, then recovers asymptotically to saturation. The BOD curve decays exponentially. The zone between is the **active decomposition zone** with low DO.
>
> **Mark distribution:** 4 marks derivation + 2 marks sketch explanation + 1 mark units/conditions.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Critical Time:**
>
> $$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{1}{0.45 - 0.25}\ln\left(\frac{0.45}{0.25}\right)$$
>
> $$t_c = \frac{1}{0.20}\ln(1.80) = \frac{0.5878}{0.20} = 2.94 \text{ days}$$
>
> **(ii) Critical Deficit:**
>
> $$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t_c} - e^{-k_2 t_c}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t_c}$$
>
> $$D_c = \frac{0.25 \times 30}{0.20}\left(e^{-0.25 \times 2.94} - e^{-0.45 \times 2.94}\right) + 2 \times e^{-0.45 \times 2.94}$$
>
> $$D_c = 37.5\left(e^{-0.735} - e^{-1.323}\right) + 2 \times e^{-1.323}$$
>
> $$D_c = 37.5(0.4794 - 0.2663) + 2(0.2663)$$
>
> $$D_c = 37.5 \times 0.2131 + 0.5326$$
>
> $$D_c = 7.99 + 0.53 = 8.52 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> **(iii) Minimum DO:**
>
> $$DO_{min} = DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.0 - 8.52 = 0.48 \text{ mg/L}$$
>
> **(iv) Distance to Critical Point:**
>
> Convert $t_c$ from days to seconds: $t_c = 2.94 \times 86400 = 254,016$ s
>
> $$x_c = U \times t_c = 0.5 \times 254016 = 127,008 \text{ m} \approx 127 \text{ km}$$
>
> **Mark distribution:** 2 marks (i) + 3 marks (ii) + 1 mark (iii) + 1 mark (iv).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget to convert $t_c$ (days) into seconds before multiplying by velocity to get **distance**. Lose 1 mark.
> - Students often write $D_c$ with positive sign even when DO saturation is exceeded — check that $D_c \le DO_{sat}$ physically. Here $D_c = 8.52$ mg/L is at the saturation limit, indicating **nearly anoxic conditions**.
> - Always state units for $k_1$, $k_2$ as **day⁻¹**, $t_c$ in **days**, and $x_c$ in **km or m**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **sources, mechanisms, and effects of groundwater pollution**. Discuss the role of the **retardation factor** in contaminant transport. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal aquifer has the following properties: hydraulic conductivity $K$ = **15 m/day**, hydraulic gradient $i$ = **0.005**, effective porosity $n_e$ = **0.30**, bulk density $\rho_b$ = **1.7 g/cm³**, and distribution coefficient $K_d$ = **0.8 mL/g**. Calculate:
- (i) Groundwater (seepage) velocity
- (ii) Retardation factor
- (iii) Time for a contaminant to travel 1 km
- (iv) Comment on the recovery time of contaminated aquifers **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Sources of Groundwater Pollution (2 marks):**
> - Landfills and waste dumps
> - Septic tanks and leaking sewers
> - Industrial chemical spills
> - Agricultural chemicals (pesticides, fertilizers)
> - Saltwater intrusion in coastal aquifers
>
> **Mechanisms (3 marks):**
> 1. **Advection** — bulk movement with groundwater flow
> 2. **Dispersion** — mechanical spreading
> 3. **Sorption/Retardation** — adsorption onto soil grains
> 4. **Biodegradation** — microbial breakdown (limited in aquifers)
> 5. **Volatilization** — loss of volatile organics
>
> **Retardation Factor (2 marks):**
>
> $$R = 1 + \frac{\rho_b K_d}{n_e}$$
>
> $R > 1$ means the contaminant moves slower than groundwater. Higher $K_d$ (more hydrophobic) means higher retardation. This protects wells from rapid contamination but creates long-term persistent plumes.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Groundwater (Seepage) Velocity:**
>
> $$v = \frac{K \cdot i}{n_e} = \frac{15 \times 0.005}{0.30} = \frac{0.075}{0.30} = 0.25 \text{ m/day}$$
>
> **(ii) Retardation Factor:**
>
> $$R = 1 + \frac{\rho_b K_d}{n_e} = 1 + \frac{1.7 \times 0.8}{0.30}$$
>
> $$R = 1 + \frac{1.36}{0.30} = 1 + 4.533 = 5.53$$
>
> **(iii) Time for Contaminant to Travel 1 km:**
>
> The contaminant velocity is $v_c = v / R$:
>
> $$v_c = \frac{0.25}{5.53} = 0.0452 \text{ m/day}$$
>
> Time to travel 1000 m:
>
> $$t = \frac{1000}{0.0452} = 22,124 \text{ days} = 60.6 \text{ years}$$
>
> **(iv) Comment on Recovery (1 mark):**
> The contaminant will take approximately **60 years** to migrate just 1 km in this aquifer. This demonstrates that **groundwater recovery is extremely slow**, often requiring decades to centuries. Once contaminated, aquifers are very difficult and expensive to remediate — prevention is critical.
>
> **Mark distribution:** 2 marks (i) + 2 marks (ii) + 2 marks (iii) + 1 mark (iv).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students commonly compute Darcy velocity $v_d = K \cdot i$ and forget to divide by porosity. Lose 1 mark.
> - When calculating travel time, divide by **contaminant velocity $v_c = v/R$**, not $v$ itself. Lose 2 marks.
> - Always convert units consistently: $K_d$ in mL/g, $\rho_b$ in g/cm³ (= kg/L for water), or use SI consistently.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] **Streeter-Phelps equation** is the cornerstone of river pollution analysis — memorize the final form, not the derivation steps alone.
- [ ] Critical time $t_c$ depends only on $k_1$ and $k_2$ — **not on $L_0$**.
- [ ] Critical deficit $D_c$ increases with higher $L_0$ and lower $k_2$ (slow reaeration).
- [ ] DO < **4 mg/L** is the typical threshold for fish kills — use this in viva/comparisons.
- [ ] Reaeration rate $k_2$ is **depth dependent**: $k_2 \propto 1/H^2$ (shallow streams reaerate faster).
- [ ] **Eutrophication** is primarily controlled by **phosphorus** in freshwater lakes (N in marine systems).
- [ ] Carlson's TSI uses Secchi disk transparency as a key indicator of trophic state.
- [ ] Groundwater contamination is governed by **Darcy's Law** (advection) + **Retardation factor** (sorption).
- [ ] Higher $K_d$ (hydrophobic compounds) → higher retardation → **slower but persistent** contamination.
- [ ] **Self-purification zones** in rivers: Degradation → Active Decomposition → Recovery → Clean (memorize the order).
- [ ] Lake pollution accumulates over time (no flushing like rivers) — prevention > treatment.
- [ ] Groundwater plume **retardation** protects wells short-term but creates **long-term liability**.
- [ ] Always state **units** in numerical answers: $k_1, k_2$ in day⁻¹; $t_c$ in days; $x_c$ in km; $v$ in m/day.
- [ ] Saturation DO at **25°C = 8.24 mg/L**; at **20°C ≈ 9.2 mg/L** — remember temperature correction.
- [ ] IS 10500 (drinking) and CPCB effluent standards must be referenced in design questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GAP and National River Action Programme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GAP and National River Action Programme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ganga Action Plan (GAP) — Formal Definition

The **Ganga Action Plan (GAP)** is the first major, comprehensive river conservation programme launched by the Government of India in **June 1984**. It was formulated by the **Central Ganga Authority (CGA)** under the chairmanship of the then Prime Minister. The plan was primarily directed at reducing the **pollution load** of the river Ganga — India's most sacred and economically important river — to a level acceptable for designated beneficial uses such as drinking, bathing, and irrigation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** GAP is often treated as a *case study* in the National River Action Plan module, focusing on its institutional framework, technical interventions, and lessons learned for replication in other Indian rivers.

### 1.2 National River Action Plan (NRAP) — Formal Definition

The **National River Action Plan (NRAP)** is the umbrella framework evolved from the experience of GAP. It extends the river-cleanup philosophy beyond the Ganga to cover **all major rivers of India** through a series of state-specific and river-specific action plans (e.g., Yamuna Action Plan, Gomti Action Plan, Mahanadi Action Plan, etc.). NRAP is operationalized through the **National Mission for Clean Ganga (Namami Gange)** and the **National River Conservation Plan (NRCP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Institutional Bodies Involved:**
> - **Central Ganga Authority (CGA)** — Apex body (1985)
> - **National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA)** — Replaced CGA in **2009**
> - **National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG)** — Operational arm since **2014**
> - **Ministry of Jal Shakti** — Administrative ministry

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Hospital for Sick Rivers":** Imagine a doctor who has a critically ill patient (River Ganga) suffering from multiple diseases (sewage, industrial effluent, solid waste). The doctor orders a full diagnostic check (river quality monitoring), prescribes medicine (interception & diversion of sewage), and puts the patient on a long-term rehabilitation program (afforestation, public awareness). **GAP is that initial prescription for Ganga**, and the **National River Action Plan is the public-health policy** that applies the same prescription to *all* the rivers of the country.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Board-Exam Favorites)

The pollution control performance is measured using the following standard parameters:

- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** The flagship target was to reduce BOD from **>10 mg/L to <3 mg/L** at the point of discharge into the river.
- **DO (Dissolved Oxygen):** Must be maintained at **>5 mg/L** (per IS:2296 Class C water quality).
- **Total Coliforms:** Must be below **500 MPN/100 mL** for bathing (primary contact, IS:2296).

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard (Bathing) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | BOD | $\le 3$ | mg/L |
| Dissolved Oxygen | DO | $\ge 5$ | mg/L |
| Total Coliforms | TC | $\le 500$ | MPN/100 mL |
| pH | — | $6.5 - 8.5$ | — |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | $\le 100$ | mg/L |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phased Operational Structure of GAP

GAP was executed in **two distinct phases**, each with clear deliverables:

- **Phase-I (1985–1990):** Focused on the **1,725 km stretch** from Haridwar (Uttarakhand) to Hooghly (West Bengal). Targeted **Class-I towns (27 towns)** along the river.
- **Phase-II (1991–2000):** Extended coverage to **48 towns** in 5 states (UP, Bihar, West Bengal, Jharkhand, Uttarakhand) and addressed tributaries such as the Yamuna, Gomti, and Damodar.

### 2.2 Core Technical Interventions (The "4 Pillars of GAP")

The engineering strategy of GAP can be classified into four functional pillars:

1. **Sewage Interception & Diversion (I&D)**
   - Construction of *interceptor sewers* along the riverbank to capture raw sewage from outfalls before it reaches the river.
   - Diverted sewage is conveyed to **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** for processing.

2. **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) Construction**
   - Deployment of conventional treatment: **Screening → Grit Removal → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank (ASP/SBR) → Secondary Clarifier → Chlorination**.

3. **River-Front Development (RFD)**
   - Construction of *ghats*, low-cost sanitation, and *dhobi ghats* to prevent direct human-waste contact with the river.

4. **Afforestation & Public Awareness**
   - Planting native species along the *riparian zone* to control soil erosion, and educational campaigns to instill community ownership.

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Pillars

- **I&D prevents the pollutant from entering the water column**, drastically reducing the load on natural river self-purification (which is finite).
- **STPs convert dissolved/suspended organic pollutants** into biomass + CO₂ + H₂O via aerobic microbial metabolism.
- **RFD addresses *non-point* sanitation failures** (open defecation, bathing detergents) that are invisible to I&D networks.
- **Afforestation acts as a *bio-shield***, providing shade (reducing thermal stress), filtering run-off, and stabilizing banks.

### 2.4 National River Conservation Plan (NRCP) — The Bigger Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **NRCP ≠ NRAP in strict terminology**, but in KTU syllabi they are used interchangeably as a *national-level framework for river conservation*. The NRCP covers **all rivers except the Ganga** (handled separately by Namami Gange) and is jointly funded by the **Centre and the States** (70:30 ratio, with NE states at 90:10).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the formulas a student **must memorize** for the ESE (End Semester Examination) on this topic.

| Concept | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| BOD Decay (Streeter-Phelps) | $D = \dfrac{k \cdot L_0}{k - r} \left[ e^{-r \cdot t} - e^{-k \cdot t} \right]$ | Oxygen deficit $D$ in a polluted stream | mg/L |
| Streeter-Phelps Critical Point | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k - r} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{k}{r}\right)$ | Time to maximum oxygen deficit | days |
| BOD Exertion (1st order) | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | Remaining BOD after $t$ days | mg/L |
| Deoxygenation Constant | $k_d = K_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | Temperature correction; $\theta \approx 1.047$ | day$^{-1}$ |
| Sewage Discharge (per capita) | $Q = P \times q \times 0.001$ | Where $q$ is in Lpcd | m³/day |
| Population Equivalent | $PE = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD Load (kg/day)}}{0.06}$ | Reference: 1 PE = 60 g BOD/day | persons |
| % Treatment Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{(\text{In} - \text{Out})}{\text{In}} \times 100$ | STP removal efficiency | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** The Streeter-Phelps equation is the *bedrock* of river water-quality modelling. It is used by **NEERI, CPCB, and state pollution control boards** to predict the minimum DO (or "oxygen sag") downstream of a sewage outfall — exactly the kind of scenario GAP was designed to address.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BOD Remaining After *t* Days (Used in GAP Pollution Load Calculations)

**Statement:** The biochemical oxidation of organic matter in a river follows first-order kinetics with respect to the remaining oxidizable substrate.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of BOD exertion.**
The rate at which oxygen is consumed is proportional to the remaining BOD at time $t$.

$$\dfrac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.**

$$\ln(L_t) - \ln(L_0) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to obtain the standard BOD equation.**

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 5 — Define *y* (the BOD exerted at time *t*).**
Since the *exerted* BOD equals the *initial* minus the *remaining*:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for GAP:** $L_0$ is the *ultimate BOD* of the sewage entering the river. $L_t$ is what's *left* to exert oxygen. The difference $y_t$ is the *actual oxygen demand* that the river must absorb. Reducing $L_0$ via STPs directly **shrinks the oxygen sag curve** and pushes the *critical deficit point* further downstream — a primary objective of GAP.

### 3.2 Derivation: Streeter-Phelps Critical Deficit (Used to Justify STP Locations)

**Step 1 — State the Streeter-Phelps dissolved-oxygen model.**
Two opposing processes act simultaneously: deoxygenation (BOD exertion at rate $k$) and reaeration (oxygen absorption at rate $r$).

$$\dfrac{dD}{dt} = k \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} - r \cdot D_t$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear ODE with integrating factor $e^{r \cdot t}$.**
After solving (assuming $D(0) = D_0$, the initial deficit):

$$D_t = \dfrac{k \cdot L_0}{k - r} \left[ e^{-r \cdot t} - e^{-k \cdot t} \right] + D_0 \cdot e^{-r \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Find the critical point by setting $dD/dt = 0$.**

$$0 = k \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t_c} - r \cdot D_{t_c}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $D_{t_c}$ and simplify.**
After algebraic manipulation, the critical time is:

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{k - r} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{k}{r} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Practical substitution for a typical GAP scenario.**
Assume $k = 0.23$ /day, $r = 0.40$ /day (typical for an Indian river in summer):

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.23 - 0.40} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{0.23}{0.40} \right)$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{-0.17} \cdot \ln(0.575) = \dfrac{-0.553}{-0.17} = 3.25 \text{ days}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A **minimum flow travel time of $\sim 3.3$ days** is required for the river to recover to the *maximum deficit* point. This metric was used by GAP planners to **decide where to locate STPs** so that the discharged effluent's critical deficit point falls *outside* the next town's water-intake zone.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Table of Major Indian River Action Plans

| Plan | Year | River | Towns Covered | Status (KTU Update) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ganga Action Plan (GAP) I** | 1985 | Ganga | 27 | Completed |
| **Ganga Action Plan (GAP) II** | 1991 | Ganga + tributaries | 48 | Largely completed |
| **Yamuna Action Plan (YAP) I** | 1993 | Yamuna | 15 | Completed |
| **Yamuna Action Plan (YAP) II** | 2003 | Yamuna | 19 | Completed |
| **Gomti Action Plan** | 1999 | Gomti | 9 | Completed |
| **Mahanadi Action Plan** | 1996 | Mahanadi | 4 | Completed |
| **Tungabhadra Action Plan** | 1997 | Tungabhadra | 5 | Completed |
| **National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG)** | 2014 | Ganga (revival) | 4 Ganga states | **Ongoing (Namami Gange II)** |
| **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation (AMRUT)** | 2015 | Urban water bodies | 500 cities | Ongoing |

### 3.4 Analytical Calculation: Pollution Load Reduction by GAP

**Given:**
- A Class-I town with population $P = 500{,}000$
- Per-capita sewage flow $q = 135$ Lpcd
- Influent BOD $L_0 = 250$ mg/L
- STP removal efficiency $\eta = 80\%$

**Step 1 — Calculate sewage discharge.**

$$Q = P \times q \times 0.001 = 500{,}000 \times 135 \times 0.001 = 67{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate raw BOD load (kg/day).**

$$\text{Load}_{\text{raw}} = Q \times L_0 \times 0.001 \cdot 1000 = 67{,}500 \times 250 \times 10^{-3} = 16{,}875 \text{ kg/day}$$

Wait — re-check the unit conversion:
$$67{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 250 \text{ g/m}^3 = 16{,}875{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 16{,}875 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate load *after* STP (effluent BOD = 20% of influent).**

$$L_{\text{out}} = 250 \times (1 - 0.80) = 50 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate load saved per day.**

$$\text{Load}_{\text{saved}} = 16{,}875 \times 0.80 = 13{,}500 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Population Equivalent (PE) for impact communication.**

$$PE = \dfrac{13{,}500}{0.06} = 225{,}000 \text{ persons}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** By installing a single 67.5 MLD STP with 80% BOD removal, the *equivalent organic load* of **2.25 lakh persons** is prevented from entering the Ganga every day. This is the precise rationale that justified the construction of **34 STPs in GAP Phase-I alone** at a cumulative investment of ~₹462 crores (1984 prices).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Institutional Evolution of GAP → NRAP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pollution of River Ganga Identified 1980s] --> B[Central Ganga Authority CGA 1985]
    B --> C[Ganga Action Plan GAP Phase I 1985 to 1990]
    C --> D[Ganga Action Plan GAP Phase II 1991 to 2000]
    D --> E[Lessons Learned Slow Progress Inadequate Sewerage]
    E --> F[National Ganga River Basin Authority NGRBA 2009]
    F --> G[National Mission for Clean Ganga NMCG 2014]
    G --> H[Namami Gange Programme 2014 to 2020]
    H --> I[Namami Gange II 2021 onwards]
    D --> J[National River Conservation Plan NRCP for non Ganga rivers]
    J --> K[State River Action Plans Yamuna Gomti Mahanadi]
    A --> L[Rivers of India facing pollution]
    L --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Functional Architecture of GAP Interventions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Urban Domestic Sewage Source] --> B[Interceptor Sewer along River Bank]
    B --> C[Sewage Pumping Station]
    C --> D[Sewage Treatment Plant STP]
    D --> E[Screen Chamber]
    E --> F[Grit Chamber]
    F --> G[Primary Clarifier]
    G --> H[Aeration Tank ASP or SBR]
    H --> I[Secondary Clarifier]
    I --> J[Chlorination Tank]
    J --> K[Treated Effluent Discharged to River]
    L[Industrial Effluents] --> M[Common Effluent Treatment Plant CETP]
    M --> K
    N[Open Defecation at Ghats] --> O[River Front Development RFD]
    O --> P[Low Cost Sanitation Units]
    P --> Q[Community Toilets]
    Q --> D
    R[Erosion and Siltation] --> S[Afforestation Programme]
    S --> T[Native Tree Species on Banks]
    T --> K
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Gap Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sewage Generation in Town] --> B[Was Sewage Entering River Before GAP?]
    B --> C[Gap Before Intervention: 100 percent untreated load]
    C --> D[GAP Intervention: Interception and STP]
    D --> E[Gap After Intervention: Treated effluent meets BOD less than 30 mg per litre]
    E --> F{Is Treated Effluent Quality Acceptable?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Success: Reaches Class B criteria]
    F -->|No| H[Persistent Issues: Industrial pollution, Incomplete sewerage]
    H --> I[Lesson for NRAP: Strengthen industrial regulation]
    I --> J[NRAP framework: NMCG and NRCP adopt multipronged strategy]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** Use them as the basis for a *flowchart* answer to a 7-mark question on "Explain the institutional structure and key interventions of GAP." Always label each block with a clear engineering function, as shown above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the Ganga Action Plan (GAP)? List any four of its key objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ganga Action Plan (GAP) is the first comprehensive river pollution control programme of India, launched in **June 1984** and implemented in phases from **1985**, primarily aimed at reducing the pollution load of the river Ganga to maintain its water quality for beneficial uses.

**Four key objectives of GAP:**
1. **Pollution Load Reduction:** Lower the BOD load of sewage discharged into the Ganga to <3 mg/L.
2. **Sewage Interception & Treatment:** Capture municipal sewage through interceptor sewers and treat it in STPs.
3. **River-Front Sanitation:** Construct low-cost sanitation, ghats, and dhobi-ghats to prevent direct human-waste contact.
4. **Afforestation & Awareness:** Plant trees along the riparian zone and run mass-awareness campaigns to foster community ownership.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the Ganga Action Plan (GAP) and the National River Action Plan (NRAP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ganga Action Plan (GAP) | National River Action Plan (NRAP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Confined to River Ganga and a few tributaries | Covers **all major rivers** of India |
| **Launched** | 1985 (Phase-I) | Conceptualized post-2000; operational via NRCP and Namami Gange |
| **Implementing Body** | Central Ganga Authority (CGA) | National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) + NRCP under Ministry of Jal Shakti |
| **Funding Pattern** | 100% Central (initially) | 70:30 (Centre:State); 90:10 for NE states |
| **Key Output** | 34 STPs in Phase-I; BOD reduction at 27 Class-I towns | Cumulative state river action plans; basin-level management |
| **Approach** | End-of-pipe treatment (Sewage → STP → River) | Basin-level, demand-side, and source-control approach |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For any 14-mark question on GAP/NRAP, students *must* cover: **(i) historical context, (ii) institutional structure with year-wise bodies, (iii) technical interventions with examples, and (iv) outcomes/limitations.** A common mistake is to write only the objectives and skip the *limitations* — this typically costs **3–4 marks** per question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **institutional evolution** of India's national river conservation programme from the Central Ganga Authority (1985) to the Namami Gange II Mission. Highlight the key shifts in **policy thinking** that occurred between these milestones. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat flow diagram**, explain the **engineered interventions** of GAP in detail. Also state **any four limitations** of GAP-I. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Institutional Evolution (7 Marks)

1. **Pre-GAP Era (Pre-1984):** Ganga treated as a *sacrificial* river. River pollution was first formally recognized after the **Bharat Singh Committee Report (1982)** and studies by **NEERI (1980–84)** which showed that ~70% of the Ganga's pollution in Class-I towns was due to untreated domestic sewage.

2. **Central Ganga Authority (CGA, 1985):** Established by then PM **Rajiv Gandhi** as an *apex coordinating body*. Set up the **Ganga Project Directorate (GPD)** in Delhi for day-to-day implementation. Funded **34 STPs** and 14 electric crematoria.

3. **National River Conservation Plan (NRCP, 1995):** Realized that *Ganga alone is not the problem*; extrapolated the GAP model to **Yamuna, Gomti, Mahanadi, Tungabhadra**, etc., under the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**.

4. **National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA, 2009):** A *statutory authority* registered as a society under the Societies Registration Act, with the PM as chair. The mandate shifted from *river-cleaning* to *river-basin management* (holistic, including water-resource allocation).

5. **National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG, 2014) and Namami Gange:** Operational arm of NGRBA. Adopted an **integrated approach** — *sewage infrastructure, industrial regulation, river-surface cleaning, biodiversity, public outreach*.

6. **Namami Gange II (2021):** A 10-year *mission-mode* programme with a budget of ₹2,65,000 crore, focusing on **sustainability, reuse of treated wastewater, and 100% sewerage coverage** in 5 Ganga states.

> **Key Policy Shifts:**
> - From **end-of-pipe treatment** → **source control and basin planning**.
> - From **single-river focus** → **multi-river national framework**.
> - From **government-only execution** → **PPP and hybrid annuity models** for STP operation.
> - From **capital expenditure** → **O&M sustainability** (₹1,000 cr/year dedicated).

#### (b) Engineered Interventions & Limitations (7 Marks)

**Flow Diagram of GAP Interventions:**

The student's diagram should replicate the Mermaid block in **Section 4.2** (Sewage → Interceptor → STP → Treated effluent → River), supplemented by *Afforestation* and *River-Front Development* branches.

**Four Key Limitations of GAP-I:**

1. **Neglect of Industrial Pollution:** GAP-I focused almost exclusively on *domestic sewage*. Industrial effluents — which contribute ~30% of the toxic load (heavy metals, dye) — were barely addressed. CPCB's 2010 audit revealed **414 grossly polluting industries** still discharging into the Ganga.

2. **Non-Point Source Pollution:** GAP-I addressed *point* sources (outfalls) but failed to control *non-point* pollution from agriculture (fertilizer run-off), open defecation, and idol immersion, which are diffuse and harder to capture.

3. **Operational & Maintenance Deficit:** Many of the 34 STPs were built but **not operated to design capacity**. Studies (2004–2008) by NEERI showed that up to **40% of STPs were non-functional** due to power failures, staff shortages, and broken aerators.

4. **Demand-Side Failure:** Without **interceptor sewer lines** connecting *all* nallahs (drains) in a town, much of the sewage bypasses the STP. GAP-I was criticized for building STPs *before* the sewerage network was complete, leading to "STPs with nothing to treat."

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **objectives, scope, and engineering interventions** of the **Yamuna Action Plan (YAP)** as a model of GAP replication. What lessons did the Yamuna experience offer for the National River Conservation Plan? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class-II town on the Ganga basin has a population of **2,00,000**. Per-capita sewage flow is **135 Lpcd** and raw BOD is **220 mg/L**. The proposed STP is designed for **80% BOD removal**. Calculate: **(i)** Total sewage discharge in MLD, **(ii)** Raw BOD load in kg/day, **(iii)** Effluent BOD load in kg/day, and **(iv)** Equivalent population saved (PE) per day. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Yamuna Action Plan (YAP) (7 Marks)

**Background:** The Yamuna — a major tributary of the Ganga, traversing **1,376 km** through Delhi, Agra, Mathura, Etawah, and Allahabad — was *almost as polluted* as the Ganga. **YAP-I (1993–2003)** was launched in 15 towns of UP, Delhi, and Haryana with a budget of ~₹1,300 crores.

**Objectives:**
- Reduce BOD load at 6 river-monitoring stations
- Interception of 190+ open nallahs
- Construction of STPs and CETPs
- Public sanitation improvements (especially in Delhi)

**Engineering Interventions:**
- **27 STPs** + 12 CETPs were constructed.
- **Interception and Diversion (I&D)** networks in Delhi, Agra, and Mathura.
- **Bhalswa landfill** diversion to prevent leachate into Yamuna.
- **Afforestation** of the Yamuna floodplains (Delhi stretch).

**Lessons for NRCP / NRAP:**
1. **Delhi experience showed that urban encroachment on the floodplain is the single biggest threat** to a river — leading to *river-front zoning* norms in subsequent plans.
2. **I&D network must precede or be synchronous with STP commissioning** — a lesson GAP-I had violated.
3. **Industrial monitoring requires real-time effluent monitoring** — YAP introduced **online effluent monitoring stations (OCEMS)** at CETPs.
4. **Source segregation and decentralized STPs** for small towns are more sustainable than one large STP — adopted in Namami Gange Phase-II.

#### (b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Population $P = 2{,}00{,}000$
- Per-capita sewage flow $q = 135$ Lpcd
- Raw BOD $L_0 = 220$ mg/L
- Removal efficiency $\eta = 80\%$

**(i) Total Sewage Discharge (MLD):**

$$Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 135 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q = 27{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 27 \text{ MLD}$$

**(ii) Raw BOD Load (kg/day):**

$$\text{Load}_{\text{raw}} = Q \times L_0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (kg)} = 27{,}000 \times 220 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{Load}_{\text{raw}} = 5{,}940 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**(iii) Effluent BOD Load (kg/day):**

$$L_{\text{out}} = L_0 \times (1 - \eta) = 220 \times 0.20 = 44 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Load}_{\text{out}} = 27{,}000 \times 44 \times 10^{-3} = 1{,}188 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**(iv) Equivalent Population Saved (PE/day):**

$$\text{Load}_{\text{saved}} = 5{,}940 - 1{,}188 = 4{,}752 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$PE = \dfrac{\text{Load}_{\text{saved}}}{0.06 \text{ kg/person/day}} = \dfrac{4{,}752}{0.06}$$

$$PE = 79{,}200 \text{ persons}$$

> **Final Answer:** **(i) 27 MLD, (ii) 5,940 kg/day, (iii) 1,188 kg/day, (iv) 79,200 persons.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ESE:**
> - **GAP launched in 1984 (formulation) and 1985 (implementation)** by **Central Ganga Authority** under PM's chairmanship.
> - **Two phases:** Phase-I (1985–90, 27 towns, ₹462 cr) and Phase-II (1991–2000, 48 towns, ₹559 cr).
> - **4 Pillars:** Interception & Diversion, STPs, River-Front Development, Afforestation.
> - **34 STPs** built in Phase-I; designed for BOD reduction to <30 mg/L effluent.
> - **CPCB norms:** BOD <3 mg/L, DO >5 mg/L, Coliforms <500 MPN/100 mL (for bathing).
> - **NGRBA (2009)** replaced CGA; **NMCG (2014)** is the current operational body.
> - **NRCP** covers *all rivers except Ganga*; 70:30 funding (90:10 for NE).
> - **Namami Gange II (2021)** has a budget of ~₹2.65 lakh crore for 10 years.
> - **Major limitations of GAP-I:** ignored industrial pollution, weak O&M, non-point sources, demand-side sewerage gap.
> - **Streeter-Phelps equation** is essential for understanding river DO sag — memorize $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k-r} \ln(k/r)$.
> - **PE (Population Equivalent):** 1 PE = 60 g BOD/day = 0.06 kg BOD/day.
> - **First-order BOD:** $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ — used in all river-quality calculations.
> - Always quote **IS:2296 (Class C)** water-quality standards in your answers for the bathing use case.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of National and International Agencies in Water Health and Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of National and International Agencies in Water Health and Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Health and Sanitation Agencies** are governmental, intergovernmental, and non-governmental organizations (NGOs) that establish policies, formulate standards, fund projects, conduct research, and implement programs to ensure safe drinking water supply, effective wastewater management, and hygienic sanitation practices for public health protection.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, these agencies operate at three distinct tiers:

| Tier | Scope | Examples |
|------|-------|----------|
| **National** | Country-specific legislation, standards, missions | CPCB, CPHEEO, Jal Jeevan Mission |
| **International** | Global guidelines, funding, cross-border cooperation | WHO, UNICEF, World Bank |
| **Research / Academic** | Scientific R&D, technology development | NEERI, IWA, UN-Water |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Traffic Police of the Water World"**
>
> Imagine a city's water supply as a complex highway system. Just as traffic police regulate vehicles, set speed limits (standards), build new roads (infrastructure projects), and rescue accident victims (emergency response), these agencies regulate water quality, set permissible limits, build treatment plants, and respond to disease outbreaks. The **WHO** acts like the **United Nations Traffic Council** (sets global rules), while **CPCB** is the **National Highway Authority** (enforces rules within India), and **NEERI** is the **Research Lab** that designs better traffic systems.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

The agencies universally operate on the following **key performance indicators**:

- **SDG 6.1.1**: Proportion of population using safely managed drinking water services
- **SDG 6.2.1**: Proportion of population using safely managed sanitation services
- **JMP Service Ladders**: Classification of water/sanitation services into "Surface water," "Limited," "Basic," and "Safely Managed"
- **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)**: Burden of disease metric used by WHO
- **MDG Target 7.C (historical)**: Halving the proportion of people without sustainable access to safe drinking water by 2015

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU examinations, focus primarily on the **mandates, key functions, and Indian missions** (Jal Jeevan Mission, Swachh Bharat Mission, Namami Gange) alongside the **global standards-setting role of WHO and UNICEF**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Matrix

## 2.1 National Agencies in India — Operational Framework

### 2.1.1 Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO)

- **Parent Ministry**: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)
- **Established**: Under the Ministry of Health in 1953
- **Primary Mandate**: Advise the Central Government on public health engineering, sanitation, and urban water supply
- **Key Publications**:
  - *Manual on Water Supply and Treatment* (Third Edition, **1999**)
  - *Manual on Sewerage and Sewage Treatment* (Second Edition, **2012**)
  - *Manual on Municipal Solid Waste Management* (**2000**)
- **Operational Logic**:
  1. Receives technical proposals from state agencies
  2. Reviews and approves techno-economic feasibility
  3. Releases central grants for **AMRUT** and **Smart Cities Mission**
  4. Publishes design standards for engineers

### 2.1.2 Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)

- **Statutory Body**: Established under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**
- **Core Functions**:
  1. Advise the Central Government on water and air pollution matters
  2. Coordinate state pollution control boards (SPCBs)
  3. Establish **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** and **Effluent Discharge Standards**
  4. Operate the **National Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** — **804 stations** across India
  5. Maintain the **Ganga Monitoring Network**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CPCB Matters for KTU:** Questions on **consent to establish/operate**, **zonal classification of industries**, and **MINAS (Minimum National Standards)** are frequently tested.

### 2.1.3 Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)

- **Standard**: **IS 10500:2012** — Drinking Water Specification
- **Key Parameters** (must-know for KTU):

| Parameter | Acceptable Limit | Permissible Limit (No Alternative) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------------------------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |
| Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L |
| Turbidity (NTU) | 1 NTU | 5 NTU |
| Fluoride (F⁻) | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | 45 mg/L | No relaxation |
| Iron (Fe) | 0.3 mg/L | No relaxation |
| Total Coliforms | 0 MPN/100 mL | 0 MPN/100 mL |

### 2.1.4 Ministry of Jal Shakti

- **Formed**: **31 May 2019** by merging Ministry of Water Resources with Ministry of Drinking Water and Sanitation
- **Two Key Departments**:
  1. **Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation (DDWS)** — oversees **Jal Jeevan Mission**
  2. **Department of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation** — oversees **Namami Gange Programme**

### 2.1.5 Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)

- **Launched**: **15 August 2019**
- **Vision**: Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) to every rural household by **2024**
- **Implementation Budget**: ₹3.60 lakh crore (estimated)
- **Key Strategy**:
  - Source sustainability through **catchment area treatment**
  - Treatment plants (preferably **decentralized** for villages with population < 5000)
  - Greywater management
  - Capacity building of village water committees (**Pani Samiti / VWSC**)

### 2.1.6 Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM)

- **Phase 1 (2014–2019)**: Eliminated **open defecation** — India declared ODF on **2 October 2019**
- **Phase 2 (2020–2025)**: Focus on **ODF Plus** — sustainable solid and liquid waste management
- **Key Components**:
  1. Individual Household Latrines (IHHL)
  2. Community Toilet Complexes
  3. Biogas plants, compost pits
  4. Plastic waste management

### 2.1.7 National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) — Namami Gange Programme

- **Registered as Society**: **12 August 2011**
- **Restructured**: June 2014 as NMCG
- **Approach**:
  - **Sewage Treatment Infrastructure**: New STPs and rehabilitation of existing ones
  - **River Surface Cleaning**: Mechanical skimmers
  - **Industrial Effluent Monitoring**: Real-time **CETP** and **ETP** compliance
  - **Riverfront Development**: Ghats and crematoria
- **Key Statistic**: **5862 million litres per day (MLD)** of sewage generated in Ganga basin; treatment capacity being augmented to match.

### 2.1.8 Other Key National Bodies

- **NEERI (National Environmental Engineering Research Institute)**: Located in **Nagpur**; CSIR lab; develops low-cost water testing kits and pollution remediation technologies.
- **ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research)**: Sets **health-based guidelines** for water quality.
- **Central Water Commission (CWC)**: Apex body for water resources assessment and flood forecasting.
- **National Water Mission**: One of the **8 missions** under **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)**; target — **20% increase in water use efficiency**.

## 2.2 International Agencies — Operational Framework

### 2.2.1 World Health Organization (WHO)

- **Parent Body**: **United Nations (UN)** specialized agency
- **Headquarters**: **Geneva, Switzerland**
- **Established**: **7 April 1948** (World Health Day)
- **Key Publication**: *Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality (GDWQ)* — 4th Edition, **2017**
- **Operational Logic**:
  1. Sets **health-based targets** (e.g., microbial, chemical)
  2. Develops **Water Safety Plans (WSP)** — risk-based approach
  3. Collaborates with UNICEF on **JMP (Joint Monitoring Programme)**
  4. Issues the **International Travel and Health (ITH)** vaccination guidelines

### 2.2.2 UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund)

- **Mandate Focus**: Child survival, including **WASH** (Water, Sanitation, Hygiene) programs
- **Key Initiatives**:
  - **WASH in Schools**: Sanitation facilities for 1.1 million schools globally
  - **WASH in Health Care Facilities**: Promotes hand hygiene, safe water in hospitals
  - **JMP Partnership**: Joint monitoring with WHO on SDG 6 progress
- **Three Star Approach** for WASH in schools: 1 star (basic), 2 stars (medium), 3 stars (full)

### 2.2.3 World Bank

- **Financial Role**: Largest multilateral financier of water projects in developing countries
- **India Portfolio** (recent): Hydrology Project, National Hydrology Project, water supply schemes in **Uttarakhand, Punjab, Tamil Nadu**
- **Key Concept**: **IDA (International Development Association)** — concessional loans/credits to poorest countries

### 2.2.4 UN-Water

- **Formation**: Established in **2003**; coordinated by UN-DESA
- **Members**: 30+ UN entities
- **Core Function**:
  - Coordinates UN system's work on water and sanitation
  - Administers **World Water Development Report (WWDR)** — published annually on **World Water Day (22 March)**

### 2.2.5 Other International Bodies

| Agency | Headquarters | Specific Role |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| **UNEP** | Nairobi, Kenya | Environmental policy, ecosystem protection |
| **UNDP** | New York, USA | Sustainable development projects |
| **FAO** | Rome, Italy | Water for agriculture, AQUASTAT database |
| **World Water Council (WWC)** | Marseille, France | World Water Forum organizer (held every 3 years) |
| **Global Water Partnership (GWP)** | Stockholm, Sweden | Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) |
| **International Water Association (IWA)** | London, UK | Professional network, conferences, publishing |

## 2.3 The Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6) — High-Yield Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDG 6 — "Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all"** was adopted by the UN General Assembly in **September 2015** as part of the **2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development**. It has **8 targets** and **11 indicators**.

### SDG 6 Targets (Sub-set for KTU Focus):

- **Target 6.1**: By 2030, achieve universal and equitable access to safe and affordable drinking water for all
- **Target 6.2**: By 2030, achieve access to adequate and equitable sanitation and hygiene for all and end open defecation
- **Target 6.3**: Improve water quality by reducing pollution, halving the proportion of untreated wastewater
- **Target 6.4**: Increase water-use efficiency and address water scarcity
- **Target 6.5**: Implement IWRM at all levels
- **Target 6.6**: Protect and restore water-related ecosystems (wetlands, rivers, aquifers)
- **Target 6.A**: International cooperation and capacity-building
- **Target 6.B**: Support local participation in water and sanitation management

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Key Numerical / Factual Element | Significance |
|---------|----------------------------------|---------------|
| **JMP Service Ladder (Water)** | Safely Managed $\rightarrow$ Basic $\rightarrow$ Limited $\rightarrow$ Surface Water | SDG 6.1.1 indicator |
| **JMP Service Ladder (Sanitation)** | Safely Managed $\rightarrow$ Basic $\rightarrow$ Limited $\rightarrow$ Open Defecation | SDG 6.2.1 indicator |
| **WHO GDWQ Limit — Lead (Pb)** | 0.01 mg/L (Provisional) | Chemical guideline value |
| **WHO GDWQ Limit — Arsenic (As)** | 0.01 mg/L (Provisional) | Chemical guideline value |
| **BIS IS 10500:2012 — pH** | 6.5 to 8.5 | National standard |
| **CPCB Effluent BOD (Industrial)** | $\leq$ 30 mg/L (inland surface water) | Discharge standard |
| **CPCB Effluent COD (Industrial)** | $\leq$ 250 mg/L (inland surface water) | Discharge standard |
| **India Per Capita Water Availability** | $\sim$ 1486 m³/person/year (2021) | Below water-stressed threshold of 1700 m³ |
| **UN Water Stress Threshold** | $< 1700$ m³/person/year = water-stressed; $< 1000$ m³ = water-scarce | Falkenmark Indicator |
| **SDG 6 Target Year** | 2030 | UN 2030 Agenda |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding these agencies' roles is critical for environmental engineers because (a) **designs must comply with their standards** (e.g., BIS 10500 for water treatment plant design), (b) **funding often requires environmental clearance** through CPCB/SPCB, and (c) **cross-border projects** need international agency coordination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Frameworks

## 3.1 Comparative Matrix: National vs. International Agency Roles

The KTU examiner often asks students to differentiate or compare the functions. The following analytical matrix establishes a clear comparative framework.

### Step 1: Identifying the Function Type

Every agency performs one or more of the following five universal functions:

$$
\text{Agency Function} = f(\text{Standards}, \text{Policy}, \text{Funding}, \text{Research}, \text{Implementation})
$$

### Step 2: Mapping Indian National Agencies to Functions

| Agency | Standards | Policy | Funding | Research | Implementation |
|--------|:---------:|:------:|:-------:|:--------:|:--------------:|
| **CPHEEO** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (advisory) |
| **CPCB** | ✓ (NAAQS, MINAS) | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (monitoring) |
| **BIS** | ✓ (IS 10500) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **NMCG** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **JJM** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **SBM** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **NEERI** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **ICMR** | ✓ (health-based) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |

### Step 3: Mapping International Agencies to Functions

| Agency | Standards | Policy | Funding | Research | Implementation |
|--------|:---------:|:------:|:-------:|:--------:|:--------------:|
| **WHO** | ✓ (GDWQ) | ✓ (global) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **UNICEF** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **World Bank** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **UNEP** | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **UN-Water** | ✗ | ✓ (coordination) | ✗ | ✓ (reports) | ✗ |
| **FAO** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **IWA** | ✓ (technical) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |

### Step 4: Logically Deriving the Roles

From the above matrix, we can **logically derive** the following conclusions:

$$
\text{Role Classification} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Regulatory/Standards-Setting} & \text{if Standards = } \checkmark \\
\text{Financial/Implementation} & \text{if Funding = } \checkmark \text{ and Implementation = } \checkmark \\
\text{Research-Driven} & \text{if Research = } \checkmark \\
\text{Coordination} & \text{if Policy = } \checkmark \text{ and not Standards}
\end{cases}
$$

Applying this logic:
- **CPCB and BIS** are *Regulatory/Standards-Setting* agencies
- **World Bank, UNICEF, JJM, SBM, NMCG** are *Financial/Implementation* agencies
- **NEERI, WHO (partially), IWA, FAO** are *Research-Driven* agencies
- **UN-Water, UNEP** are *Coordination* agencies
- **CPHEEO** is an *Advisory* body (standards + policy + advisory implementation)

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the SDG 6 Service Ladder

The **JMP Service Ladder** is a hierarchical classification. For the KTU exam, you must understand the **derivation logic** — how a service moves from one level to the next.

### Step 1: Define the Three Criteria

For **Drinking Water** classification, the three criteria are:

$$
C_1 = \text{Availability on premises}, \quad C_2 = \text{Free of contamination}, \quad C_3 = \text{Available when needed}
$$

### Step 2: Hierarchical Logic

$$
\text{Service Level} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Safely Managed} & \text{if } C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = \text{YES} \text{ and Improved Source} \\
\text{Basic} & \text{if Improved Source, Round trip} \leq 30 \text{ min} \\
\text{Limited} & \text{if Improved Source, Round trip} > 30 \text{ min} \\
\text{Unimproved} & \text{if Source = unprotected well/spring or surface water}
\end{cases}
$$

For **Sanitation**, the analogous ladder is:

$$
\text{Service Level} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Safely Managed} & \text{Improved facility + Excreta safely disposed} \\
\text{Basic} & \text{Improved facility not shared with other households} \\
\text{Limited} & \text{Improved facility shared between } \geq 2 \text{ households} \\
\text{Open Defecation} & \text{No facility or disposal in open spaces}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for Engineering:** The Service Ladder drives **funding prioritization** by World Bank, UNICEF, and India's SBM. Rural areas at "Limited" or "OD" levels receive priority intervention.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Linking BIS Limits to WHO Guidelines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-frequency KTU question type:** "Compare BIS IS 10500:2012 and WHO GDWQ limits for [parameter]."

### Step-by-Step Analysis for **Fluoride (F⁻)**:

| Step | Action | Result |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Read BIS IS 10500:2012 acceptable limit | **1.0 mg/L** |
| 2 | Read BIS IS 10500:2012 permissible limit | **1.5 mg/L** |
| 3 | Read WHO GDWQ (4th Ed, 2017) guideline value | **1.5 mg/L** |
| 4 | Compare | WHO upper limit = BIS permissible limit |
| 5 | Note reason for difference | BIS is more stringent to account for Indian groundwater fluoride hotspots (Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana) |

### Analytical Equation for Compliance Decision:

For a water sample with measured fluoride concentration $C_{\text{sample}}$:

$$
\text{Compliance} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Acceptable (BIS)} & \text{if } C_{\text{sample}} \leq 1.0 \text{ mg/L} \\
\text{Permissible (BIS)} & \text{if } 1.0 < C_{\text{sample}} \leq 1.5 \text{ mg/L} \\
\text{Non-Compliant} & \text{if } C_{\text{sample}} > 1.5 \text{ mg/L}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application in Real Engineering:** When designing a community defluoridation plant in a fluoride-endemic area, the engineer uses the BIS acceptable limit (1.0 mg/L) as the **design target**, not the permissible limit. The permissible limit is only allowed when no alternative source is available.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Water Health & Sanitation Governance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UN General Assembly] --> B[SDG 6 - 2030 Agenda]
    A --> C[UN-Water Coordination Body]
    C --> D[WHO]
    C --> E[UNICEF]
    C --> F[World Bank]
    C --> G[UNEP]
    C --> H[FAO]
    D --> D1[GDWQ Standards]
```
