<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 3]**

> State any **three** key objectives of the **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM)**.

**Model Answer:**

The three key objectives of the Jal Jeevan Mission are:

1. **Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC)** to every rural household by **2024**, ensuring potable drinking water supply at the rate of **55 litres per capita per day (lpcd)**.

2. **Source Sustainability** through aquifer recharge, rainwater harvesting, and watershed development, ensuring long-term water security.

3. **Capacity Building and Community Participation** through the formation of **Village Water and Sanitation Committees (VWSCs / Pani Samitis)**, with at least 50% women members, to manage the in-village water supply system.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per objective = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3]**

> Differentiate between the **roles of CPCB and CPHEEO** in water quality management.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CPCB | CPHEEO |
|--------|------|--------|
| **Statutory Status** | Statutory body under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** | Advisory body under the **Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)** |
| **Primary Function** | Pollution control, consent to establish/operate industries, sets **NAAQS** and **MINAS** | Advises on public health engineering, urban water supply, and sewerage design manuals |
| **Powers** | Can prosecute polluters, issue closure notices | Advisory only, no penal powers |

*(Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks each comparison pair = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **CPCB** (pollution control) with **CPHEEO** (urban design guidance) — a common error costing 2-3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** state that **WHO sets legally binding standards** — WHO issues *guideline values*, not legally binding limits. India follows **BIS IS 10500:2012** as the national standard.
> - **Always mention the year of formation** for agencies when possible; it is a **favourite sub-part (a) trigger** for KTU.
> - **Avoid** saying "UNICEF funds India" — UNICEF co-funds specific projects (WASH in schools, arsenic remediation) but is not a primary funder. The **World Bank** is the largest multilateral funder.

---

### Part B — Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 3 — Q3(a) or Q3(b) Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **mandate, structure, and key functions of the World Health Organization (WHO)** with specific reference to its role in **drinking water quality guidelines**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**1. Mandate and Formation (2 marks):**
The WHO is a specialized agency of the United Nations, established on **7 April 1948** (now commemorated as World Health Day). Its constitution defines its objective as "the attainment by all peoples of the highest possible level of health." Headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**, WHO has 194 member states and operates through six regional offices (including the South-East Asia Regional Office — SEARO, based in **New Delhi**).

**2. Structure (1 mark):**
The supreme decision-making body is the **World Health Assembly (WHA)**, comprising delegations from all member states. The Executive Board, comprising 34 members, implements WHA decisions. The Director-General heads the WHO Secretariat.

**3. Key Functions Related to Water (4 marks):**
- **Setting Standards:** Publishes the *Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality (GDWQ)* — the global benchmark, currently in its **4th edition (2017)**. The GDWQ provides guideline values for over **200 chemical and microbiological parameters**.
- **Water Safety Plans (WSP):** Promotes a **risk-based management approach** covering catchment to consumer.
- **Disease Surveillance:** Monitors waterborne disease outbreaks such as cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis A/E.
- **Collaborative Monitoring:** Co-administers the **Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP)** with **UNICEF** to track SDG 6.1 and 6.2 indicators globally.
- **Capacity Building:** Provides training to national agencies in developing countries on water quality testing and laboratory protocols.

---

> **(b)** Describe the **key features of Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6)** and discuss how **India's national missions (JJM, SBM, Namami Gange)** contribute to achieving it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**1. Key Features of SDG 6 (3 marks):**

Adopted by the UN General Assembly in **September 2015** as part of the **2030 Agenda**, SDG 6 aims to *"Ensure availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all"* by 2030. It comprises:

- **8 Targets** (6.1 to 6.6, plus 6.A and 6.B) addressing drinking water, sanitation, hygiene, water quality, water-use efficiency, IWRM, ecosystem protection, and international cooperation.
- **11 Indicators** to measure progress (e.g., 6.1.1 — population using safely managed drinking water; 6.2.1 — population using safely managed sanitation; 6.3.2 — proportion of water bodies with good ambient water quality).
- **Cross-cutting linkages** with **SDG 3** (health), **SDG 5** (gender), and **SDG 11** (sustainable cities).

**2. India's Contributions (4 marks):**

- **Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) — launched 15 August 2019:** Directly addresses **SDG Target 6.1** by providing FHTC to every rural household. As of 2024, **over 14.5 crore** households have been connected, raising rural tap connection coverage from **16.6% (2019) to over 74%**.

- **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) — launched 2 October 2014:** Directly addresses **SDG Target 6.2**. India was declared **Open Defecation Free (ODF)** in October 2019. **SBM Phase 2 (2020-2025)** focuses on ODF-Plus (sustainable solid and liquid waste management).

- **Namami Gange Programme:** Addresses **SDG Target 6.3** (improving water quality) and **6.6** (ecosystem protection). The programme has sanctioned **158 sewerage infrastructure projects** for creating and upgrading **~5800 MLD** of STP capacity in the Ganga basin.

- **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT 2.0):** Addresses urban water supply and **SDG Target 6.1** for cities, aiming for universal water supply coverage in 500 cities.

---

### Part B — Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 3 — Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **role of CPCB** in **water pollution control in India**. Explain its structure, statutory powers, and **key functions** with reference to the **Water Act, 1974**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**1. Statutory Foundation (1.5 marks):**
CPCB was constituted under **Section 3 of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**. It is a **statutory body** under the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**, headquartered in **Delhi**.

**2. Structure (1.5 marks):**
- Chaired by a full-time **Chairman** appointed by the Central Government.
- Comprises **up to 15 members** representing Central and State Governments, technical institutions, and the public.
- Coordinates with **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** in all 28 states and 8 UTs.
- Operates regional directorates, laboratories, and **804 air quality monitoring stations** under the NAMP.

**3. Statutory Powers (2 marks):**
- **Consent Management:** Under **Section 25/26 of the Water Act**, industries must obtain "Consent to Establish" and "Consent to Operate" from SPCBs/CPCB.
- **Penal Powers:** Can prosecute violators under **Section 43-44 of the Water Act** (imprisonment up to 6 years and/or fine).
- **Closure Orders:** Issue directions to SPCBs to take action against defaulting industries.

**4. Key Functions (2 marks):**
- **Standards Setting:** Establishes **Minimum National Standards (MINAS)** for industrial effluents and **National Water Quality Standards**.
- **Monitoring:** Operates the **National Water Quality Monitoring Network** with **~4400 stations** across India.
- **Advisory Role:** Advises the Central Government on water pollution matters.
- **Research:** Conducts studies on pollution control technologies.

---

> **(b)** Write notes on: **(i) UNICEF's role in WASH programs**, and **(ii) The role of UN-Water in coordinating global water initiatives. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) UNICEF's Role in WASH Programs (3.5 marks):**

The **United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF)**, established in **1946**, is mandated to advocate for the protection of children's rights. Its **WASH (Water, Sanitation and Hygiene)** programme is one of the largest in the world.

- **Global Programmes:**
  - **WASH in Schools:** Provides safe drinking water, separate sanitation facilities for boys and girls, and handwashing stations in over **1,100,000 schools globally**.
  - **WASH in Health Care Facilities:** Promotes safe water, sanitation, and hygiene in hospitals and primary health centres to prevent healthcare-associated infections.
  - **Emergency Response:** Provides portable water kits (e.g., in flood/drought-affected regions).

- **Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP):** UNICEF co-leads JMP with WHO, producing the **global estimates** for SDG 6.1 and 6.2 indicators published annually.

- **Behavioural Change Communication:** Develops community-led total sanitation (CLTS) programmes.

- **In India:** UNICEF has supported arsenic remediation in West Bengal, fluoride mitigation in Rajasthan, and the Swachh Bharat Mission through technical assistance.

**(ii) Role of UN-Water (3.5 marks):**

**UN-Water**, established in **2003**, is the **United Nations inter-agency coordination mechanism** for all freshwater-related issues, including sanitation.

- **Membership:** Comprises **30+ UN entities** (WHO, UNICEF, UNEP, FAO, World Bank, etc.) that work on water and sanitation.

- **Key Functions:**
  - **Coordination:** Acts as the single UN voice on water issues, avoiding duplication and silos.
  - **World Water Development Report (WWDR):** Published annually on **World Water Day (22 March)**, this is the flagship UN report on global water status.
  - **International Decade for Action — 'Water for Sustainable Development' (2018-2028):** UN-Water coordinates this decade proclaimed by the UN General Assembly.
  - **SDG 6 Reporting:** UN-Water consolidates inputs from all UN agencies for the annual SDG 6 Progress Report submitted to the UN High-Level Political Forum.
  - **Capacity Development:** Facilitates knowledge exchange among member agencies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Memorize before the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **WHO** — Geneva, established **1948**; publishes *Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality (GDWQ), 4th Edition, 2017*.
- ✅ **UNICEF** — Established **1946**; co-leads **JMP** with WHO.
- ✅ **World Bank** — Largest multilateral funder of water projects.
- ✅ **UN-Water** — Established **2003**; publishes **WWDR** on **22 March (World Water Day)**.
- ✅ **CPCB** — Statutory body under **Water Act, 1974**; sets **NAAQS** and **MINAS**.
- ✅ **CPHEEO** — Advisory body under **MoHUA**; publishes *Manual on Water Supply and Treatment*.
- ✅ **BIS** — Sets **IS 10500:2012** for drinking water.
- ✅ **Jal Jeevan Mission** — Launched **15 August 2019**; target FHTC to all rural households; 55 lpcd.
- ✅ **Swachh Bharat Mission Phase 1** — Launched **2 October 2014**; India declared ODF on **2 October 2019**.
- ✅ **Namami Gange / NMCG** — Registered as society on **12 August 2011**; restructured June 2014.
- ✅ **SDG 6** — Adopted **September 2015**; 8 targets; target year **2030**.
- ✅ **NEERI** — Located in **Nagpur**; CSIR laboratory.
- ✅ **BIS pH range** — **6.5 to 8.5** (acceptable); no relaxation in permissible limit.
- ✅ **BIS TDS limit** — 500 mg/L (acceptable); 2000 mg/L (permissible).
- ✅ **Water-stressed country threshold** — Below **1700 m³/person/year**; water-scarce below **1000 m³/person/year**.
- ✅ **JMP Service Ladder** — Safely Managed > Basic > Limited > Unimproved/OD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **(i) Mandate/Establishment**, **(ii) Structure**, **(iii) Key Functions**, **(iv) Indian Context / Applications**. KTU examiners award full marks only when the answer demonstrates **contextual understanding**, not just rote listing.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — TYPES (OF AIR POLLUTANTS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** In the *Water and Air Quality Management* course (PECET861), **Module 4** transitions from water management to **Atmospheric / Air Quality Engineering**. The opening topic **"Types"** refers to the **classification of Air Pollutants** — a fundamental building block before moving to dispersion models, control devices, and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.

---

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

An **Air Pollutant** is defined (per the *Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981* and the *Central Pollution Control Board — CPCB, India*) as:

> *"Any solid, liquid, or gaseous substance present in the atmosphere in such concentration as may be or tend to be injurious to human beings, other living creatures, plants, property, or the environment as a whole."*

**Classification (Types) of Air Pollutants** is the systematic grouping of these contaminants based on their **origin (primary/secondary)**, **physical state (gaseous/particulate)**, **source (natural/anthropogenic)**, and **chemical nature**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine the **Earth's atmosphere as a giant restaurant kitchen**:
- The **stove, ovens, and chimneys** represent pollution sources (factories, vehicles, volcanoes).
- The **smoke, steam, and grease droplets** released are **primary pollutants** (released *directly*).
- Once in the kitchen air, the smoke reacts with steam to form **new sticky, irritating compounds** (like smog forming on a mirror) — these are **secondary pollutants** (formed *in-situ* by chemical reactions).
- Some pollutants you can **see** (dust, soot — **particulates**); others are **invisible but smelly** (VOCs, SO₂ — **gaseous pollutants**).

Thus, just as a chef must identify each ingredient (salt, spice, oil) before cooking, an environmental engineer must **classify pollutants by type** to design the right control technology (filter, scrubber, electrostatic precipitator, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Ambient air quality in India is measured in **µg/m³ (micrograms per cubic metre)** for particulates and **µg/m³ or ppb (parts per billion)** for gases, as prescribed by the **National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** under CPCB.

---

## 1.2 Why "Types" Matter in KTU Board Exams

- A **2-mark / 3-mark** question in Part A frequently asks: *"Classify air pollutants with examples."*
- A **7 or 14-mark** Part B question often begins with: *"Discuss the types of air pollutants and their sources/effects."*
- Marks are lost when students **memorize lists without explaining the classification basis** (e.g., saying "CO is a primary pollutant" but not stating *why* — because it is emitted directly from combustion).

---

## 1.3 Master Classification Matrix (at a glance)

| **Basis of Classification** | **Category 1** | **Category 2** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin in atmosphere | **Primary Pollutants** | **Secondary Pollutants** |
| Source | **Natural** | **Anthropogenic (Man-made)** |
| Physical state | **Gaseous** | **Particulate Matter (PM)** |
| Regulation (US EPA / CPCB) | **Criteria Pollutants** | **Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs)** |
| Spatial domain | **Indoor** | **Outdoor (Ambient)** |
| Chemical nature | **Organic** | **Inorganic** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Seven Pillars of Air Pollutant Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Every time you write "Pollutant X is *this type*," immediately justify it with the **criterion** you used. Justification carries 60% of the marks.

---

## 2.1 TYPE 1 — Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants

### 🔹 Primary Pollutants
- **Definition:** Substances **emitted directly** from a source into the atmosphere in a harmful form.
- **Why primary?** Because their *original chemical identity* is the polluting agent — no atmospheric reaction is required to make them harmful.
- **Examples:**
  - **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — from coal combustion
  - **Nitrogen Oxides (NO, NO₂ collectively written as NOₓ)** — from vehicle exhausts
  - **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — incomplete combustion
  - **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, soot)** — from diesel engines, construction
  - **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — benzene, toluene from paints/solvents
  - **Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg)** — metallurgical processes

### 🔹 Secondary Pollutants
- **Definition:** Substances **formed in the atmosphere** by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents (O₂, H₂O, N₂, sunlight).
- **Why secondary?** The *source* itself does not emit them; the *atmosphere* is the "chemical reactor."
- **Examples:**
  - **Ozone (O₃)** — formed by photochemical reaction of NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight → the chief component of **photochemical smog**
  - **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄) mist** — from SO₂ + OH· radical → H₂SO₄ (acid rain component)
  - **Nitric Acid (HNO₃) vapour** — from NO₂ + OH· → HNO₃
  - **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate, CH₃COOONO₂)** — a powerful eye-irritant in smog
  - **Secondary Organic Aerosols (SOA)**

### 📐 Governing Photochemical Reactions (must know)

$$ \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \;(\lambda < 430\;\text{nm}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{NO} + \text{O} $$

$$ \text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{O}_3 + \text{M} $$

where **M** is a third-body molecule (typically N₂ or O₂) that absorbs excess energy.

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle is **completed** when NO reacts with O₃ back to NO₂, but in the presence of VOCs (RH), the NO is "titrated away," allowing **net O₃ accumulation** — this is the foundation of photochemical smog.

---

## 2.2 TYPE 2 — Natural vs. Anthropogenic Sources

| **Natural Sources** | **Anthropogenic (Man-made) Sources** |
|---|---|
| Volcanic eruptions (SO₂, ash) | Power plants (SO₂, NOₓ, PM) |
| Forest fires (CO, PM) | Motor vehicles (CO, NOₓ, VOCs, Pb) |
| Sea-salt spray (Na⁺, Cl⁻ aerosols) | Industrial processes (HF, HCl, metals) |
| Pollen, dust storms (bioaerosols) | Domestic cooking (PM, CO) |
| Biological decay (CH₄, H₂S) | Agricultural burning (PM, CO, CH₄) |
| Lightning (NOₓ via N₂ + O₂) | Waste incineration (dioxins, furans) |

> [!WARNING]
> Although natural sources are larger *in bulk*, anthropogenic sources are **concentrated, toxic, and urban** — hence they drive air quality management policies.

---

## 2.3 TYPE 3 — Gaseous vs. Particulate Pollutants

This is the **single most important classification** for designing control equipment.

### 🔸 A. Gaseous Pollutants
Exist as discrete molecules in the atmosphere; controlled using **absorption, adsorption, or combustion**.

**Sub-classification:**

1. **Sulphur Compounds:** SO₂, SO₃, H₂S
2. **Nitrogen Compounds:** NO, NO₂, N₂O, NH₃, HNO₃
3. **Carbon Compounds:** CO, CO₂
4. **Halogens:** Cl₂, HF, HCl
5. **Ozone & Oxidants:** O₃, PAN
6. **Organic Vapours:** Benzene, Toluene, Formaldehyde, PAHs

### 🔸 B. Particulate Matter (PM)
A **dispersion of solid or liquid droplets in air**, controlled using **settling chambers, cyclones, filters, ESPs, scrubbers**.

**Sub-classification by Aerodynamic Diameter (Dₐ):**

| **Class** | **Dₐ (Aerodynamic Diameter)** | **Penetration** | **Health Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)** | ≤ 100 µm | Nose, throat | Nuisance, soiling |
| **PM₁₀ (Thoracic / Coarse)** | ≤ 10 µm | Bronchi, lungs | Asthma, bronchitis |
| **PM₂.₅ (Respirable / Fine)** | ≤ 2.5 µm | Alveoli (deep lung) | COPD, cardiovascular |
| **PM₁ (Ultrafine)** | ≤ 1 µm | Bloodstream | Systemic toxicity |
| **Nucleation mode** | < 0.1 µm | Cellular | Most toxic per mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerodynamic Diameter (Dₐ)** is defined as the diameter of a **unit-density (1 g/cm³) sphere** that has the same settling velocity as the particle in question. This unifies particles of any shape/density onto a single comparable scale.

---

## 2.4 TYPE 4 — Criteria Pollutants vs. Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs)

### 🔹 Criteria Pollutants
The **US EPA / CPCB "Big Six"** pollutants for which **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** are set because they are **ubiquitous, measurable, and have well-documented health effects**.

| **#** | **Pollutant** | **Symbol** | **Primary Source** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Coal/oil combustion |
| 2 | Nitrogen Dioxide | NO₂ | Vehicles, power plants |
| 3 | Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion |
| 4 | Ozone | O₃ | Photochemistry (secondary) |
| 5 | Particulate Matter | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | Combustion, dust |
| 6 | Lead | Pb | Leaded petrol (phased out), smelters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India added two more:** **NH₃ (Ammonia)** and **CO (already counted)** — actually, the Indian NAAQS lists **12 parameters** including PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, CO, Pb, NH₃, C₆H₆ (Benzene), BaP (Benzo(a)Pyrene), As (Arsenic), Ni (Nickel).

### 🔹 Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs / Air Toxics)
**188 substances** listed by the US EPA Clean Air Act Amendments, 1990 — known or suspected to cause **cancer (carcinogens)** or **serious non-cancer health effects** (e.g., reproductive, neurological).

**Examples:** Benzene, Formaldehyde, Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Vinyl Chloride, PCBs, Dioxins, Asbestos, PAHs.

---

## 2.5 TYPE 5 — Indoor vs. Outdoor (Ambient) Pollutants

- **Indoor** pollutants often reach **2–5× higher concentrations** than outdoors (since modern buildings are airtight).
- Major indoor pollutants: **RSPM from cooking, ETS (Environmental Tobacco Smoke), formaldehyde from furniture, Radon (²²²Rn) from soil, biological allergens (mold, mites).**
- Relevant for KTU because the WHO estimates **3.8 million premature deaths/year** from indoor air pollution (mostly from solid-fuel cooking in developing nations).

---

## 2.6 TYPE 6 — Organic vs. Inorganic

| **Organic** | **Inorganic** |
|---|---|
| Contain C–H or C–C bonds (Benzene, CH₄, PAHs) | Do not (SO₂, NO₂, CO, HF) |
| Often VOCs or SVOCs | Often acids, bases, salts |
| Control: thermal/catalytic oxidation | Control: wet scrubbers |

---

## 2.7 TYPE 7 — Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)

A special sub-class of **organic pollutants** that are:
- **Resistant to degradation** (chemical, biological, photolytic)
- **Bioaccumulative** in fatty tissue
- **Toxic** to humans and ecosystems
- **Long-range transportable** across continents

**The Dirty Dozen (Stockholm Convention, 2001):** Aldrin, Chlordane, DDT, Dieldrin, Endrin, Heptachlor, Hexachlorobenzene, Mirex, Toxaphene, PCBs, Dioxins, Furans.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{µg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ | ppm → µg/m³ at **25 °C, 1 atm** (Ideal Gas) | µg/m³ |
| $C_{\text{ppm}} = C_{\text{µg/m}^3} \times \dfrac{24.45}{M}$ | µg/m³ → ppm at **25 °C, 1 atm** | ppm |
| $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ (Gay-Lussac) | Concentration correction for temperature (constant P, V) | ratio |
| $C_2 = C_1 \times \dfrac{298}{T_2}$ | Correcting to 298 K | varied |
| $\text{AQI}_i = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{C_{\text{Hi}} - C_{\text{Lo}}} \times (C_i - C_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$ | **Piecewise-linear AQI** for pollutant *i* | dimensionless (0–500) |
| $\text{PM}_{2.5} = \text{PM}_{10} \times (0.4\text{–}0.6)$ | Typical coarse-to-fine ratio (site-specific) | µg/m³ |
| $V_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18\,\mu}$ | **Stokes' settling velocity** (Re < 0.1) | m/s |

Where:
- $M$ = molecular weight of the gas (g/mol)
- $24.45$ = molar volume of ideal gas at 25 °C, 1 atm (L/mol)
- $C_{\text{Hi}}, C_{\text{Lo}}$ = concentration breakpoints
- $I_{\text{Hi}}, I_{\text{Lo}}$ = corresponding AQI breakpoints (e.g., 100, 200, 300, 400, 500)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_p, \rho_a$ = densities (kg/m³), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of air (≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s at 25 °C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The **24.45 factor** is used in every CPCB stack monitoring report and every air-shed modelling exercise (AERMOD, CALPUFF). The AQI formula is what powers the **CPCB's SAFAR app** that students check daily.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The classifications above are **conceptual**, but KTU often tests **the numerical conversion formulas** (ppm ↔ µg/m³) and the **AQI calculation**. Both are derived and worked out below in exhaustive detail.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Conversion between ppm and µg/m³

**Given:** Ideal Gas Law $PV = nRT$, with $P = 1$ atm, $T = 25\,°\text{C} = 298$ K, $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K).

**Step 1 — Molar Volume of an Ideal Gas at 25 °C, 1 atm**

From $PV = nRT$:

$$ V_m = \dfrac{V}{n} = \dfrac{RT}{P} $$

Substituting numerical values:

$$ V_m = \dfrac{0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 24.4658 \;\text{L/mol} \approx 24.45 \;\text{L/mol} $$

**Step 2 — Definition of ppm (volumetric)**
1 ppm = 1 part of pollutant gas per **10⁶ parts of air by volume** = 1 µL pollutant per L of air.

**Step 3 — Express 1 ppm in moles per litre of air**

$$ C_{\text{ppm}} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{volume fraction} = 10^{-6} $$

Moles of pollutant per litre of air:

$$ n_{\text{pollutant}} = \dfrac{10^{-6}\;\text{L}}{V_m\;\text{L/mol}} = \dfrac{10^{-6}}{24.45}\;\text{mol per L of air} $$

**Step 4 — Convert moles to mass (using molecular weight $M$)**

$$ m = n \times M = \dfrac{10^{-6} \times M}{24.45}\;\text{g per L of air} $$

**Step 5 — Convert g per L to µg per m³**

$$ 1\;\text{g/L} = 10^{6}\;\text{µg}/10^{3}\;\text{L} = 10^{3}\;\text{µg/L} = 10^{6}\;\text{µg/m}^{3} $$

$$ m = \dfrac{10^{-6} \times M}{24.45} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{M}{24.45}\;\text{µg/m}^{3} $$

**Final Master Formula:**

$$ \boxed{\;C_{\text{µg/m}^{3}} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}\;} \quad\text{at }25\,°\text{C},\;1\;\text{atm} $$

**Inverse Formula:**

$$ \boxed{\;C_{\text{ppm}} = C_{\text{µg/m}^{3}} \times \dfrac{24.45}{M}\;} $$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Piecewise-Linear Air Quality Index (AQI)

**Indian National AQI** (CPCB, 2014) follows the US EPA piecewise-linear formulation, with **8 pollutants** as inputs and 8 sub-indices computed; the **reported AQI = MAX of all sub-indices**.

**Generic Sub-Index Formula for Pollutant $i$:**

$$ \text{AQI}_i = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}}^{(i)} - I_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)}}{C_{\text{Hi}}^{(i)} - C_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)}} \times \big(C_i - C_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)}\big) + I_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)} $$

Where for each pollutant $i$:
- $C_i$ = measured concentration
- $C_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)}, C_{\text{Hi}}^{(i)}$ = concentration breakpoints that bracket $C_i$
- $I_{\text{Lo}}^{(i)}, I_{\text{Hi}}^{(i)}$ = corresponding AQI breakpoints (e.g., 0, 51, 101, 201, 301, 401 → 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500)

**Final AQI:**

$$ \boxed{\;\text{AQI} = \max\big(\text{AQI}_{\text{PM}_{2.5}},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{PM}_{10}},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{SO}_2},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{NO}_2},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{O}_3},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{CO}},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{NH}_3},\;\text{AQI}_{\text{Pb}}\big)\;} $$

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — ppm to µg/m³ conversion

**Problem (KTU-style):** The ambient SO₂ concentration at a Kerala traffic intersection is measured as **0.08 ppm** at 25 °C, 1 atm. Convert to µg/m³. Given $M_{\text{SO}_2} = 64$ g/mol.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the formula:
$$ C_{\text{µg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45} $$

Step 2 — Substitute values:
$$ C_{\text{µg/m}^3} = 0.08 \times \dfrac{64}{24.45} $$

Step 3 — Compute the fraction:
$$ \dfrac{64}{24.45} = 2.6178 $$

Step 4 — Final multiplication:
$$ C_{\text{µg/m}^3} = 0.08 \times 2.6178 = 0.2094\;\text{µg/m}^3 $$

Step 5 — Compare with NAAQS (24-hour SO₂ standard = 80 µg/m³):
$$ 0.21\;\text{µg/m}^3 \;\ll\; 80\;\text{µg/m}^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{well within limit} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student error:** Using $M = 32$ (atomic weight of S) instead of $M = 64$ (molecular weight of SO₂). The *molecular* weight is used in the ideal gas conversion.

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — AQI Sub-Index Calculation

**Problem:** The 24-hour average PM₂.₅ concentration at a KTU campus monitoring station is **86 µg/m³**. Compute the AQI sub-index for PM₂.₅.

**PM₂.₅ (24-hr) CPCB Breakpoints:**

| $C_{\text{Lo}}$ | $C_{\text{Hi}}$ | $I_{\text{Lo}}$ | $I_{\text{Hi}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 30 | 0 | 50 |
| 31 | 60 | 51 | 100 |
| 61 | 90 | 101 | 200 |
| 91 | 120 | 201 | 300 |
| 121 | 250 | 301 | 400 |
| 251+ | 380+ | 401 | 500 |

**Step 1 — Locate the bracket for $C_i = 86$:**
$61 \le 86 \le 90$, so use breakpoints $C_{\text{Lo}} = 61$, $C_{\text{Hi}} = 90$, $I_{\text{Lo}} = 101$, $I_{\text{Hi}} = 200$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the AQI formula:**

$$ \text{AQI}_{i} = \dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \times (86 - 61) + 101 $$

**Step 3 — Numerators & Denominators:**

$$ \text{AQI}_{i} = \dfrac{99}{29} \times 25 + 101 $$

**Step 4 — Compute fraction:**

$$ \dfrac{99}{29} = 3.4138 $$

**Step 5 — Multiply by concentration offset:**

$$ 3.4138 \times 25 = 85.34 $$

**Step 6 — Add $I_{\text{Lo}}$:**

$$ \text{AQI}_{\text{PM}_{2.5}} = 85.34 + 101 = 186.34 \approx 186 $$

**Step 7 — Interpret (CPCB bands):**

| AQI | Category |
|---|---|
| 0–50 | Good |
| 51–100 | Satisfactory |
| 101–200 | **Moderate** ← 186 falls here |
| 201–300 | Poor |
| 301–400 | Very Poor |
| 401–500 | Severe |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often pick the *wrong concentration bracket* (e.g., using 0–30 instead of 61–90). Always check: $C_{\text{Lo}} \le C_i \le C_{\text{Hi}}$.

---

## 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 3 — Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

**Problem:** A fly-ash particle of diameter $d_p = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ and density $\rho_p = 2200\;\text{kg/m}^3$ settles in air ($\rho_a = 1.2\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·s}$). Calculate the terminal settling velocity.

**Step 1 — Check Reynolds number to confirm Stokes regime:**
$Re < 0.1$ for Stokes; assumed for fine particles.

**Step 2 — Apply Stokes' law:**

$$ V_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,g\,(\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18\,\mu} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $d_p = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:**

$$ V_s = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times 9.81 \times (2200 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $(50 \times 10^{-6})^2$:**

$$ (50 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 2500 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$ 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.81 \times 2198.8 = 5.393 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator:**

$$ 18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 32.58 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Step 7 — Divide:**

$$ V_s = \dfrac{5.393 \times 10^{-5}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1655\;\text{m/s} $$

$$ \boxed{\;V_s \approx 0.166\;\text{m/s} = 5.95\;\text{km/h}\;} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **cyclone separators** and **settling chambers** are effective for *coarse* particulates (>10 µm) but not for fine PM₂.₅ — for which **bag filters, ESPs, and HEPA filters** are required.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Classify air pollutants based on their origin in the atmosphere. Give two examples for each class."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for examples, 1 for clarity):**

Air pollutants are classified based on their origin in the atmosphere into two types:

1. **Primary Pollutants** — Substances emitted **directly** into the atmosphere in a harmful form by natural or anthropogenic sources.
   *Examples:* **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** from coal combustion; **Carbon monoxide (CO)** from vehicle exhausts; Particulate Matter (PM) from diesel engines.

2. **Secondary Pollutants** — Substances **formed in the atmosphere** by chemical reactions (often photochemically driven) between primary pollutants and natural constituents like O₂, H₂O, and N₂.
   *Examples:* **Ozone (O₃)** formed by the photolysis of NO₂ in the presence of sunlight; **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) mist** formed from the oxidation of SO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip:** Award 1.5 marks for each class, with 0.5 marks reserved for correct examples.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*"Differentiate between PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅ with respect to size, sources, and health impacts."*

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **PM₁₀ (Thoracic / Coarse)** | **PM₂.₅ (Respirable / Fine)** |
|---|---|---|
| Aerodynamic diameter | ≤ 10 µm | ≤ 2.5 µm |
| Sources | Crushing, grinding, sea spray, pollen, road dust | Combustion, secondary aerosols, wildfires |
| Penetration in lungs | Trachea & primary bronchi | Bronchioles & alveoli |
| Health impact | Asthma, bronchitis, irritation | COPD, lung cancer, cardiovascular mortality, systemic inflammation |
| NAAQS (India, 24-hr) | 100 µg/m³ | 60 µg/m³ |

PM₂.₅ is **more dangerous per unit mass** because it reaches the alveolar region and enters systemic circulation.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has TWO sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, OR is a single 14-mark question with internal choice between two options. The model below provides the **standard a/b format**.*

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
*"Explain with examples the classification of air pollutants based on physical state, source, and chemical nature."*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by Physical State (3 marks)**

Air pollutants exist in two physical states:

- **Gaseous Pollutants** — discrete molecules dispersed in air.
   *Examples:* SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, H₂S, HCl, VOCs (Benzene, Toluene). These are controlled by **absorption, adsorption, condensation, or combustion** methods.

- **Particulate Pollutants (PM)** — discrete solid or liquid droplets suspended in air. These are classified by aerodynamic diameter as **TSP (<100 µm), PM₁₀ (<10 µm), PM₂.₅ (<2.5 µm), and Ultrafine (<0.1 µm)**. They are controlled by **settling chambers, cyclones, scrubbers, bag filters, and ESPs**.

**(ii) Classification by Source (2 marks)**

- **Natural** — volcanic eruptions, sea-salt spray, pollen, forest fires, biological decay (CH₄, H₂S), lightning.
- **Anthropogenic** — power plants, motor vehicles, industries, biomass burning, waste incineration, agricultural activities.

**(iii) Classification by Chemical Nature (2 marks)**

- **Organic Pollutants** — contain carbon-hydrogen bonds; e.g., Benzene, Toluene, Formaldehyde, PAHs, Dioxins, PCBs.
- **Inorganic Pollutants** — no C–H framework; e.g., SO₂, NO₂, CO, CO₂, H₂S, HF, NH₃.

> [!NOTE]
> A **special category** is **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** — 12 substances listed under the Stockholm Convention that resist degradation and bioaccumulate.

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
*"The 24-hour average concentrations measured at a KTU campus station are: PM₂.₅ = 86 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 142 µg/m³, NO₂ = 68 µg/m³, SO₂ = 28 µg/m³, O₃ = 75 µg/m³, CO = 2.1 mg/m³, NH₃ = 110 µg/m³. Compute the overall AQI using CPCB breakpoints."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute sub-indices for each pollutant using the AQI formula:**

| Pollutant | $C_i$ | Bracket ($C_{\text{Lo}}, C_{\text{Hi}}$) | $I_{\text{Lo}}$ | $I_{\text{Hi}}$ | Sub-AQI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 86 | (61, 90) | 101 | 200 | (200−101)/(90−61) × (86−61) + 101 = 3.414 × 25 + 101 = **186.3** |
| PM₁₀ | 142 | (101, 250) | 101 | 200 | (200−101)/(250−101) × (142−101) + 101 = 0.6655 × 41 + 101 = **128.3** |
| NO₂ | 68 | (40, 80) | 51 | 100 | (100−51)/(80−40) × (68−40) + 51 = 1.225 × 28 + 51 = **85.3** |
| SO₂ | 28 | (0, 40) | 0 | 50 | (50−0)/(40−0) × (28−0) + 0 = 1.25 × 28 = **35.0** |
| O₃ | 75 | (58, 100) | 51 | 100 | (100−51)/(100−58) × (75−58) + 51 = 1.1905 × 17 + 51 = **71.2** |
| CO | 2.1 | (1.0, 2.0) | 51 | 100 | Out of bracket — clamp to AQI = **100+** (use next band 2.0–10: AQI = 101) |
| NH₃ | 110 | (0, 200) | 0 | 50 | (50−0)/(200−0) × 110 = 0.25 × 110 = **27.5** |

**Step 2 — Overall AQI = MAX of all sub-indices:**

$$ \text{AQI} = \max(186.3,\;128.3,\;85.3,\;35.0,\;71.2,\;101,\;27.5) = 186.3 \approx 186 $$

**Step 3 — Interpret (CPCB Band):** AQI = 186 → **"Moderate"** (band 101–200).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often compute sub-AQIs correctly but then forget that the *overall AQI* is the **MAXIMUM**, not the average. This single error loses 2 marks.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021, Adapted]**
*"With neat sketches, describe the formation of photochemical smog. List the secondary pollutants involved and their adverse effects."*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Photochemical smog is a **brownish haze** formed over urban areas due to the photochemical reaction of **NOₓ** and **VOCs** in the presence of **sunlight**, producing a mixture of **oxidants** like O₃, PAN, formaldehyde, and acrolein.

**Mechanism (4 marks):**

1. **NO₂ Photolysis** — UV radiation of wavelength < 430 nm dissociates NO₂ into NO and atomic oxygen.
2. **O₃ Formation** — atomic O reacts with O₂ (with a third-body M = N₂ or O₂) to form O₃.
3. **VOC Oxidation** — VOCs (RH) react with OH· radicals to form peroxy radicals (RO₂·), which convert NO → NO₂ *without* consuming O₃.
4. **Net O₃ Build-up** — this "tricks" the Leighton ratio, leading to **O₃ accumulation**.
5. **PAN Formation** — CH₃COO₂· (acetylperoxy) + NO₂ → **CH₃COOONO₂ (PAN)** — a powerful lachrymator and phytotoxin.

**Sketch (1 mark):** *(Students should draw a flowchart: NOₓ + VOCs + Sunlight → Smog)*

**Effects (1 mark):**
- Human health: eye irritation (PAN), reduced lung function (O₃), chest pain
- Vegetation: leaf necrosis, crop yield reduction
- Materials: rubber cracking, corrosion
- Visibility: reduced (golden-brown haze)

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Adapted]**
*"A flue gas stream contains 800 ppm of NO₂ at 200 °C and 1 atm. Convert this concentration to (i) µg/m³ at 25 °C, 1 atm and (ii) µg/m³ at the actual stack conditions (200 °C, 1 atm). Comment on which value should be reported to CPCB. M(NO₂) = 46 g/mol."*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):**

Using the standard formula:

$$ C_{\text{µg/m}^{3}} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45} $$

$$ C = 800 \times \dfrac{46}{24.45} = 800 \times 1.8814 = 1505.1\;\text{µg/m}^{3} $$

**Part (ii) — At actual stack conditions (200 °C = 473 K, 1 atm):**

The molar volume changes with temperature (at constant P):

$$ V_{m,\text{stack}} = \dfrac{RT}{P} = 0.0821 \times 473 = 38.83\;\text{L/mol} $$

Corrected formula:

$$ C_{\text{µg/m}^{3}} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{V_{m,\text{stack}}} = 800 \times \dfrac{46}{38.83} $$

$$ C = 800 \times 1.1846 = 947.7\;\text{µg/m}^{3} $$

**Step 3 — Equivalent conversion via ratio:**

$$ \dfrac{C_{T_1}}{C_{T_2}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{25\,°\text{C}} = C_{200\,°\text{C}} \times \dfrac{473}{298} = 947.7 \times 1.587 = 1504.9\;\text{µg/m}^{3} \;\checkmark $$

**Comment (1 mark):** **CPCB and the Indian emission regulations require concentrations to be reported at *standard reference conditions* — 25 °C and 1 atm (and often dry basis, 3% O₂ for combustion sources)**. The stack-condition value (947.7 µg/m³) is operationally relevant for dispersion calculations but **not** for compliance reporting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students use $M = 14$ (atomic N) instead of $M = 46$ (NO₂ molecular weight), or forget to convert temperature → Kelvin. Both errors cost 2–3 marks.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots — Module 4 "Types of Air Pollutants"**
>
> 1. **Missing the "basis of classification":** Writing "O₃ is a secondary pollutant" without saying *why* (it is *formed* in the atmosphere by photochemical reactions, not emitted directly) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating "PM₁₀" with "Total Suspended Particulates"** — TSP is **<100 µm**, not <10 µm. PM₁₀ is the thoracic sub-fraction.
> 3. **Using atomic weight instead of molecular weight** in the 24.45 conversion formula (e.g., using 14 for NO₂ instead of 46).
> 4. **Forgetting to report AQI as the MAX** of sub-indices — this is the single most common error, costing 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing primary and secondary PM** — Secondary PM is a *secondary pollutant*; primary PM is emitted directly (e.g., soot from diesel).
> 6. **Confusing "criteria pollutants" (12 in India) with "HAPs" (188 US-EPA listed)** — they are *two different regulatory categories*.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — "Types of Air Pollutants"**

- ✅ **Primary Pollutants** = emitted directly (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, PM, VOCs, Pb, Hg)
- ✅ **Secondary Pollutants** = formed in atmosphere (O₃, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, PAN, SOA)
- ✅ **Photochemical smog** requires **NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight**; O₃ and PAN are signature pollutants
- ✅ **Gaseous** pollutants controlled by **scrubbers, adsorbers, oxidisers**
- ✅ **Particulate** pollutants controlled by **cyclones, ESPs, bag filters, scrubbers**
- ✅ **PM₂.₅ < 2.5 µm** is the most dangerous; reaches alveoli and bloodstream
- ✅ **Aerodynamic Diameter (Dₐ)** normalises particle size to a 1 g/cm³ sphere
- ✅ **6 (US) / 12 (India) Criteria Pollutants** = PM, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, CO, Pb (+ NH₃, C₆H₆, BaP, As, Ni in India)
- ✅ **188 HAPs** = carcinogens / systemic toxics (Benzene, Hg, Cd, Dioxins, PCBs)
- ✅ **POPs (Stockholm Convention)** = 12 "Dirty Dozen" — bioaccumulative, persistent, toxic
- ✅ **Conversion formula:** $C_{\text{µg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ at 25 °C, 1 atm
- ✅ **Stokes' settling velocity:** $V_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2} g (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18\,\mu}$ (for $Re < 0.1$)
- ✅ **AQI** is the **MAX** of all pollutant sub-indices; piecewise linear formula
- ✅ **Always justify** a "type" claim with the **criterion** (origin / source / state / regulation)
- ✅ **Indoor** air is often 2–5× more polluted than outdoor; **Radon (²²²Rn)** is the second leading cause of lung cancer after tobacco
- ✅ **Acid rain** = H₂SO₄ + HNO₃ (both *secondary* pollutants)
- ✅ **Indian standards:** NAAQS (CPCB, 2009) for ambient air; emission standards under **EPAct 1986** and the **Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986**

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — "Types"**
> *Compiled as per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, with explicit alignment to Course Outcomes (CO4) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyse).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Water and Air Quality Management (PECET861)**, the term **"Sources"** refers to the **origins, points, or activities from which pollutants are released into the natural environment** (atmosphere or hydrosphere), thereby initiating a degradation cycle in air or water quality.

As per the KTU 2024 scheme, sources of pollution are systematically classified under the following taxonomic framework:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – Source Classification Framework**
> - **Natural (Biogenic) Sources** — Emissions occurring without human intervention.
> - **Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources** — Emissions resulting from human industrial, agricultural, transportation, and domestic activities.
> - **Stationary Sources** — Fixed geographical locations (e.g., power plants, factories).
> - **Mobile Sources** — Moving emitters (e.g., motor vehicles, aircraft).
> - **Area Sources** — Distributed over a geographical region (e.g., agricultural fields, residential heating).
> - **Point, Line, and Volume Sources** — Geometric/emission-distribution classification used in dispersion modeling.

A **pollutant** is defined as any substance (solid, liquid, or gaseous) or energy form (heat, noise, radiation) introduced into the environment in quantities that produce measurable adverse effects on living beings and ecosystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **hospital drip system** connected to a patient. The "source" is the IV bottle. If the bottle contains contaminated fluid, the entire circulatory system (the environment) gets polluted. Similarly, in environmental engineering, **every factory chimney, vehicle tailpipe, sewage outfall, or volcano** acts as a "drip source" feeding contaminants into the **atmosphere (air)** or **hydrosphere (water)**.

Think of the environment as a giant **bathtub**:
- **Sources** = Faucets pouring pollutants in.
- **Dispersal medium** (air/water) = The bathtub water.
- **Sinks** = The drain (where pollutants settle, react, or get removed).
- **Receptors** = Living organisms, materials, ecosystems.

The **rate of pouring (emission rate)** and **type of pollutant (chemistry)** determine how quickly the bathtub becomes toxic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> **Emission** = The act of releasing a pollutant from a source.
> **Effluent** = The total waste stream emerging from a source (e.g., flue gas, wastewater).
> **Emission Rate (E)** = Mass of pollutant released per unit time (typically **g/s, kg/hr, or tonnes/year**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Source Characterization

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Emission Rate | $E$ | g/s, kg/hr, t/y | Mass flux of pollutant leaving source |
| Concentration | $C$ | $\mu g/m^3$, ppm, mg/L | Quantity per unit volume of carrier medium |
| Emission Factor | $EF$ | g/kg fuel, g/km | Pollutant emitted per unit activity |
| Stack Height | $h$ | m | Effective release elevation for dispersion |
| Plume Rise | $\Delta h$ | m | Buoyant lift above physical stack |
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | $m^3/s$, Nm³/hr | Carrier gas/liquid volume flow |
| Buoyancy Flux | $F$ | $m^4/s^3$ | Thermal/momentum driving force for plume rise |
| Wind Speed | $u$ | m/s | Horizontal advection velocity |

---

**Intuitive Takeaway:** A "source" in environmental engineering is not merely a place — it is a **dynamic entity characterized by pollutant identity, emission rate, release geometry, and temporal pattern**. Understanding sources is the **first step** in any air/water quality management strategy, as famously stated in the pollution control hierarchy: *"You cannot control what you have not characterized."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Pollution Sources

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a structured, multi-tier classification. The following is the standard taxonomy used by the **U.S. EPA**, **CPCB (India)**, and adopted in KTU evaluation patterns.

### Tier 1: Origin-Based Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Universal Source Categories**
> 1. **Natural Sources** (Biogenic / Geophysical)
> 2. **Anthropogenic Sources** (Man-Made)

### Tier 2: Activity-Based Classification of Anthropogenic Sources

| Sub-Category | Description | Typical Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| **Stationary / Point Sources** | Fixed installations with identifiable discharge points | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO, Heavy Metals |
| **Mobile Sources** | Vehicles, aircraft, marine vessels | CO, HC, NOₓ, PM₂.₅, Lead |
| **Area Sources** | Distributed small emitters aggregated | VOCs, NH₃, Pesticides, NOₓ |
| **Line Sources** | Linear emitters (highways, pipelines) | CO, NOₓ, PM from tyre wear |
| **Volume Sources** | Three-dimensional distributed (e.g., building vents) | VOCs, Aerosols |

## 2.2 Detailed Source Inventory

### 2.2.1 Natural Sources of Air Pollution

Natural sources contribute **~85% of total global emissions by mass**, but are spatially dispersed.

1. **Volcanic Eruptions** — Release SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, dust, ash (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅).
2. **Forest Fires / Biomass Burning** — CO, CO₂, CH₄, NOₓ, VOCs, particulate matter.
3. **Dust Storms / Aeolian Erosion** — PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, silica, mineral dust.
4. **Sea Spray / Marine Aerosols** — Salt particles, sulfates, organic matter.
5. **Biogenic Emissions from Vegetation** — Isoprene (C₅H₈), terpenes, pollen.
6. **Lightning** — NOₓ (high-temperature atmospheric N₂ + O₂ fixation).
7. **Geothermal / Fumaroles** — H₂S, radon, CO₂.
8. **Microbial Activity in Soil/Water** — CH₄ (methanogenesis), N₂O, NH₃.

### 2.2.2 Natural Sources of Water Pollution

1. **Soil Erosion & Runoff** — Sediments, organic matter.
2. **Atmospheric Deposition** — Acid rain (H₂SO₄, HNO₃).
3. **Geological Leaching** — Heavy metals (As, F, Se) from mineral deposits.
4. **Decaying Organic Matter** — BOD/COD load from leaf litter.
5. **Volcanic Runoff** — Acidic water, suspended solids.

### 2.2.3 Anthropogenic Sources — Stationary Combustion

| Source | Primary Pollutants |
|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plants (Coal)** | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, Hg, CO₂ |
| **Diesel Generators** | PM, NOₓ, CO, HC |
| **Industrial Boilers** | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO |
| **Waste Incineration** | Dioxins, furans, HCl, Hg, PM |
| **Cement Plants** | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, CO₂, NOₓ |
| **Iron & Steel Plants** | CO, PM, SO₂, fluorides |
| **Petroleum Refineries** | VOCs, H₂S, HC, BTEX |
| **Pulp & Paper Mills** | H₂S, mercaptans, VOCs |

### 2.2.4 Anthropogenic Sources — Mobile Sources

- **Road Transport** — Largest urban source: CO, HC, NOₓ, PM₂.₅, Pb (historical).
- **Aircraft** — NOₓ, CO, unburned HC at altitude.
- **Marine Vessels** — SO₂ (high-sulfur bunker fuel), NOₓ, PM.
- **Railways** — Diesel PM, NOₓ.

### 2.2.5 Anthropogenic Sources — Area & Fugitive Sources

- **Agricultural Activities** — NH₃ (fertilizers, livestock), pesticides, CH₄ (paddy), N₂O.
- **Construction & Demolition** — PM₁₀, PM₂.₅.
- **Landfills** — CH₄, CO₂, VOCs, H₂S (landfill gas).
- **Solvent Use & Storage** — VOCs.
- **Domestic Cooking (Biomass/LPG)** — PM₂.₅, CO, BC.
- **Wastewater Discharge** — BOD, COD, nutrients, pathogens.

## 2.3 Primary vs Secondary Pollutants (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Pollutants** = Released **directly** from a source into the environment.
> **Secondary Pollutants** = Formed in the environment by **chemical reaction** of primary pollutants with each other or with natural atmospheric constituents.

| Primary Pollutant | Reacts With | Secondary Pollutant |
|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | O₂, H₂O | H₂SO₄ (acid rain) |
| NOₓ | VOCs, sunlight | **Ozone (O₃) / Photochemical Smog** |
| NOₓ | H₂O | HNO₃ (acid rain) |
| VOCs | NOₓ, sunlight | PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) |
| CO | — (relatively stable) | — |
| PM | Atmospheric moisture | Smog |

**Photolysis-driven smog formation (key reaction chain):**
$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}^\bullet$$
$$\text{O}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_3$$
$$\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$

## 2.4 Source Characterization Parameters

For **regulatory and modeling purposes**, every source is characterized by four cardinal attributes:

1. **Location** (x, y, z coordinates).
2. **Emission Rate** (mass/time).
3. **Release Geometry** (point, line, area, volume).
4. **Temporal Profile** (continuous, intermittent, cyclical, accidental).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Source Characterization Matters**
> - Forms the input for **Gaussian Plume Models** (ISC, AERMOD).
> - Required for **Emission Inventory Preparation** (CPCB NAQI).
> - Basis of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** under EPA Act 1986.
> - Determines **Effective Stack Height** design under Indian regulations.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 equations — they constitute ~80% of numerical questions on Sources.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = C \times Q$ | Emission Rate = Concentration × Volumetric Flow | g/s = (g/m³) × (m³/s) |
| 2 | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$ | Total emission = sum of individual stack emissions | g/s |
| 3 | $E = EF \times A$ | Emission Factor × Activity Level | tonnes/yr = (g/km) × (km/yr) |
| 4 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}$ | **Brigg's Plume Rise Equation** (for buoyant rise) | m |
| 5 | $F = g \cdot V_s \cdot D_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$ | **Buoyancy Flux Parameter** | m⁴/s³ |
| 6 | $H_{eff} = h + \Delta h$ | **Effective Stack Height** | m |

Where:
- $E$ = Emission rate
- $C$ = Pollutant concentration in flue gas
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate of flue gas
- $EF$ = Emission factor (pollutant per unit activity)
- $A$ = Activity rate (fuel burned, distance travelled, etc.)
- $\Delta h$ = Plume rise (buoyant)
- $F$ = Buoyancy flux
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $V_s$ = Stack gas exit velocity
- $D_s$ = Stack diameter
- $T_s$ = Stack gas temperature (K)
- $T_a$ = Ambient air temperature (K)
- $u$ = Wind speed at stack height
- $x$ = Downwind distance (typically taken as the distance to **maximum ground-level concentration**)
- $h$ = Physical stack height
- $H_{eff}$ = Effective stack height

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / Validity Conditions for Brigg's Equation**
> - Applicable for **buoyant plumes in neutral to unstable atmospheric conditions**.
> - $x$ is conventionally taken as the **downwind distance to maximum GLC** (often ~10$H_{eff}$ to 20$H_{eff}$ in standard applications).
> - The 1.6 coefficient is an empirical constant; some texts use **1.4** (Concawe) or **1.5** (Holland).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Emission Inventory Compilation** — Foundation of any airshed / watershed management plan.
2. **Regulatory Permitting** — Industries must declare source strength for consent (CTE/CTO from SPCB).
3. **Dispersion Modeling** — Source terms feed into AERMOD, CALPUFF, SCREEN3.
4. **Source Apportionment** — Receptor models (CMB, PMF) trace back pollution to specific sources.
5. **Pollution Control Device (PCD) Sizing** — Cyclones, ESPs, scrubbers are sized based on source emission strength.
6. **Carbon Accounting & ESG** — GHG source quantification for reporting under ISO 14064.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Emission Rate from a Stack ($E = C \times Q$)

This is the **most fundamental** relationship in source characterization and is the basis of every emission inventory.

### Given
- Pollutant concentration in flue gas: $C$ (in g/m³ or mg/Nm³)
- Volumetric flow rate of flue gas: $Q$ (in m³/s or Nm³/hr)

### Derivation (Dimensional Approach)

**Step 1 — Definition of Emission Rate**

Emission rate is defined as the **mass of pollutant leaving the source per unit time**.

$$E = \dfrac{dm}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Express Mass in Terms of Concentration and Volume**

The mass of pollutant contained in a differential volume $dV$ of carrier gas is:

$$dm = C \cdot dV$$

**Step 3 — Express Volume in Terms of Flow Rate and Time**

The differential volume passing the source in time $dt$ is:

$$dV = Q \cdot dt$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$dm = C \cdot Q \cdot dt$$

$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{dm}{dt} = C \cdot Q$$

$$\boxed{E = C \cdot Q}$$

### Numerical Worked Example 1

A coal-fired boiler exhaust has SO₂ concentration of **1200 mg/Nm³** and flue gas flow rate of **25 Nm³/s**.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$C = 1200 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/Nm}^3$$

$$Q = 25 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$E = C \times Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/Nm}^3 \times 25 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$E = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s} = 0.030 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to more practical units:**

$$E = 0.030 \text{ g/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 108 \text{ g/hr}$$

$$E = 108 \text{ g/hr} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} \times 365 \text{ day/yr}$$

$$E = 946{,}080 \text{ g/yr} \approx 0.946 \text{ tonnes/yr}$$

**Final Answer:** The SO₂ emission rate from the boiler is **0.030 g/s (≈ 0.946 t/yr)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Emission Factor Methodology ($E = EF \times A$)

Used primarily for **mobile sources** and **diffuse / area sources** where stack monitoring is not feasible.

### Concept
The emission factor is the **mass of pollutant emitted per unit of activity** (e.g., g of CO per km driven, kg of PM per tonne of coal burned).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Activity Definition**

Let $A$ be the **activity rate** — the rate at which the polluting activity occurs (km travelled/yr, tonnes of fuel burned/yr, etc.).

**Step 2 — Emission Factor Definition**

The emission factor $EF$ is defined as:

$$EF = \dfrac{E_{\text{emitted}}}{A_{\text{activity}}}$$

Units: g/km, kg/tonne, etc.

**Step 3 — Rearrange for Emission Rate**

$$E = EF \times A$$

**Step 4 — For Multiple Source Categories (Aggregation)**

$$\boxed{E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EF_i \times A_i}$$

### Numerical Worked Example 2

A city has a fleet of **50,000 diesel cars**, each travelling an average of **15,000 km/yr**. The emission factor for NOₓ from diesel cars is **0.6 g/km**. Estimate total annual NOₓ emissions from the fleet.

**Step 1 — Total Activity:**

$$A = 50{,}000 \text{ cars} \times 15{,}000 \text{ km/car·yr} = 7.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ km/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**

$$E = EF \times A = 0.6 \text{ g/km} \times 7.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ km/yr}$$

$$E = 4.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ g/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to tonnes:**

$$E = 4.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ g/yr} \div 10^6 \text{ g/tonne} = 450 \text{ tonnes/yr}$$

**Final Answer:** Total NOₓ emission = **450 tonnes/year**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brigg's Plume Rise Equation

This derives the **buoyant rise** of hot flue gas above a stack, critical for **effective stack height design**.

### Step 1 — Buoyancy Flux Definition

The buoyancy flux $F$ represents the upward driving force due to density difference between hot stack gas and ambient air:

$$F = g \cdot V_s \cdot D_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$$

**Where:**
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $V_s$ = stack exit velocity (m/s)
- $D_s$ = stack diameter (m)
- $T_s$ = stack gas absolute temperature (K)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature (K)

**Derivation Logic:** The density difference is $\rho_a - \rho_s = \rho_a (T_s - T_a)/T_s$ (from ideal gas law at constant pressure). The volumetric flow through a circular stack is $V_s \cdot \pi D_s^2 / 4$. Multiplying the buoyancy per unit volume by the volumetric flow gives the buoyancy flux in m⁴/s³.

### Step 2 — Brigg's Equation (Final Rise)

For buoyant plumes, the equilibrium plume rise at downwind distance $x$ is:

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}$$

The "1.6" coefficient embeds empirical constants from the Gaussian plume integration.

### Step 3 — Final Effective Stack Height

$$\boxed{H_{eff} = h + \Delta h = h + \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example 3

**Given:**
- Stack height: $h = 50$ m
- Stack diameter: $D_s = 2$ m
- Stack gas exit velocity: $V_s = 10$ m/s
- Stack gas temperature: $T_s = 423$ K
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 298$ K
- Wind speed at stack height: $u = 5$ m/s
- Downwind distance to max GLC: $x = 500$ m

**Step 1 — Compute Buoyancy Flux:**

$$F = 9.81 \times 10 \times (2)^2 \times \dfrac{423 - 298}{423}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 10 \times 4 \times \dfrac{125}{423}$$

$$F = 392.4 \times 0.2955 = 115.97 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Plume Rise:**

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \times (115.97)^{1/3} \times (500)^{2/3}}{5}$$

Compute each term:
$$F^{1/3} = (115.97)^{1/3} \approx 4.88$$
$$x^{2/3} = (500)^{2/3} \approx 62.99$$

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \times 4.88 \times 62.99}{5} = \dfrac{491.9}{5} \approx 98.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Stack Height:**

$$H_{eff} = h + \Delta h = 50 + 98.4 = 148.4 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Effective stack height = **148.4 m**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source vs Pollutant

| Source | CO | SO₂ | NOₓ | PM₁₀ | VOCs | CO₂ | Pb | Hg |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Motor Vehicles | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | — |
| Coal Power Plants | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | — | ✓✓✓ | — | ✓✓ |
| Refineries | — | ✓✓ | ✓ | — | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| Biomass Burning | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | — | — |
| Waste Incineration | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓✓ |
| Cement Industry | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | — | ✓✓ | — | — |
| Volcanoes | ✓ | ✓✓ | — | ✓✓ | — | ✓✓✓ | — | — |

(✓✓✓ = dominant source, ✓✓ = significant, ✓ = minor, — = negligible)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Use of This Matrix:** In KTU examinations, you may be asked: *"Identify the dominant source of SO₂ in an urban area."* The matrix helps you structure a logical answer: dominant sources are coal combustion and refineries; in a city without major industry, the dominant source shifts to coal-fired power plants in the regional vicinity or even distant volcanic activity (rare case).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Pollution Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POLLUTION SOURCES"]:::root --> B["NATURAL SOURCES"]
    A --> C["ANTHROPOGENIC SOURCES"]
    B --> D["GEOPHYSICAL"]
    B --> E["BIOGENIC"]
    D --> F["Volcanic Eruptions"]
    D --> G["Dust Storms"]
    D --> H["Sea Spray"]
    D --> I["Lightning"]

    E --> L["Microbial Activity"]

    C --> N["MOBILE SOURCES"]
    C --> O["AREA SOURCES"]
    C --> P["FUGITIVE SOURCES"]
    M --> Q["Power Plants"]
    M --> R["Industries"]
    M --> S["Incinerators"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every node in this taxonomy is a *potential KTU short-answer point*. Memorize the 4 main categories of anthropogenic sources — they are high-yield.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["SOURCES"]
        SRC["Stationary Combustion / Vehicles / Industries"]
    end

    end
    SRC --> P1
    SRC --> P2
    SRC --> P3
    SRC --> P4
    SRC --> P5

    P2 --> R1

    P3 --> R1 --> R3 --> SEC4
    P1 --> R2 --> SEC5
    P2 --> R2 --> SEC5
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Source Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOURCE IDENTIFICATION"]:::stage1 --> B["QUANTIFICATION"]
    B --> C["GEOMETRIC CLASSIFICATION"]
    C --> D["DISPERSION MODELING"]
    D --> E["RECEPTOR IMPACT ASSESSMENT"]
    E --> F["CONTROL TECHNOLOGY SELECTION"]
```
