
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The above architecture illustrates the **source → receptor → control** paradigm that underpins the entire discipline of environmental engineering. Every KTU problem on air quality management must touch at least one of these seven stages.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Mobile Source Emission Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FUEL TANK"] --> B["ENGINE COMBUSTION"]
    B --> C["EXHAUST MANIFOLD"]
    C --> D["CATALYTIC CONVERTER"]
    D --> E["TAILPIPE EMISSION"]
    B --> F["CRANKCASE BREATHS"]
    B --> G["EVAPORATIVE LOSSES"]
    F --> H["EMISSION TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    G --> H
    E --> H
    style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style H fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
```

**Emission Pathways Explained:**
- **Tailpipe (Exhaust)** — CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, CO₂ from combustion.
- **Crankcase (Blow-by)** — Unburned HC (controlled by PCV valve).
- **Evaporative** — VOCs from fuel tank and carburetor (controlled by charcoal canister).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **primary** and **secondary** air pollutants. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Complete Coverage):**

| Aspect | Primary Pollutant | Secondary Pollutant |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Released directly from a source | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reaction of primary pollutants |
| **Formation** | Direct emission | Photochemical / oxidation / hydrolysis reactions |
| **Examples** | (i) **Sulfur dioxide (SO₂)**; (ii) **Carbon monoxide (CO)** | (i) **Ozone (O₃)** from NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight; (ii) **Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** from SO₂ oxidation (acid rain) |
| **Control** | Source control / end-of-pipe treatment | Atmospheric chemistry control (precursor reduction) |

**Marking Allocation (KTU Style):**
- Definition of each — 1 mark
- Two examples of each — 1 mark
- Tabular differentiation — 1 mark

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Classify pollution sources with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Pollution sources are classified into the following categories:

**1. Natural Sources (Biogenic / Geophysical)**
- Volcanic eruptions (SO₂, ash)
- Forest fires (CO, PM)
- Sea spray (salt aerosols)
- Vegetation (VOCs, isoprene)

**2. Anthropogenic Sources (Man-Made)**
- **Stationary Sources:** Power plants, factories, incinerators
- **Mobile Sources:** Motor vehicles, aircraft, ships
- **Area Sources:** Agriculture, construction, domestic cooking
- **Fugitive Sources:** Landfills, solvent storage, wastewater discharge

**Marking Allocation:** 1 mark for natural sources, 1 mark for stationary/mobile, 1 mark for area/fugitive.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 4, Q1 | 14 Marks]**

### ❓ Question A — With Sub-Parts

**(a)** Explain in detail the **natural and anthropogenic sources** of air pollution. Discuss the contribution of each category with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal-fired power plant has the following parameters:
- Flue gas SO₂ concentration = **1500 mg/Nm³**
- Flue gas flow rate = **80 Nm³/s**
- Stack height = **60 m**
- Stack diameter = **3 m**
- Stack gas exit velocity = **12 m/s**
- Stack temperature = **450 K**
- Ambient temperature = **300 K**
- Wind speed = **6 m/s**
- Downwind distance = **600 m**

**Compute:**
**(i)** SO₂ emission rate in **g/s, kg/day, and tonnes/year**.
**(ii)** Effective stack height using **Brigg's equation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Natural Sources (3.5 Marks):**
Natural sources contribute the majority of global emissions by mass but are spatially dispersed. They include:

- **Volcanic Eruptions** — Emit SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, dust, ash, fluorine compounds.
- **Forest Fires / Biomass Burning** — CO, CO₂, CH₄, NOₓ, particulates.
- **Dust Storms** — Suspended PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, mineral dust.
- **Sea Spray** — Marine aerosols, sulfates, chlorides.
- **Biogenic VOCs** — Isoprene and terpenes from vegetation; precursors to ozone.
- **Lightning** — NOₓ from high-temperature N₂ + O₂ fixation.
- **Microbial Decay** — CH₄, H₂S, NH₃ from wetlands and paddy fields.

**Anthropogenic Sources (3.5 Marks):**

- **Stationary Combustion Sources** — Power plants, industrial boilers (SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂).
- **Mobile Sources** — Road vehicles dominate urban pollution (CO, HC, NOₓ, PM).
- **Industrial Processes** — Refineries (VOCs), cement (PM), iron & steel (CO, PM), fertilizer (NH₃).
- **Waste Disposal** — Incineration (dioxins, Hg), landfills (CH₄).
- **Agricultural Activities** — Fertilizer-induced NH₃, paddy CH₄, livestock CH₄, pesticides.
- **Domestic Sector** — Biomass cooking (PM₂.₅, CO, BC).
- **Fugitive Emissions** — Solvents, gas leaks, dust from construction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) SO₂ Emission Rate (3.5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$C = 1500 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 = 1.5 \text{ g/Nm}^3$$

$$Q = 80 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply E = C × Q:**

$$E = 1.5 \text{ g/Nm}^3 \times 80 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s} = 120 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/day:**

$$E = 120 \text{ g/s} \times \dfrac{3600 \times 24}{1000} \text{ kg/day}$$

$$E = 120 \times 86.4 = 10{,}368 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes/year:**

$$E = 10{,}368 \text{ kg/day} \times 365 \text{ day/yr} \div 1000$$

$$E = 3784.32 \text{ tonnes/yr}$$

**Answer:** SO₂ emission rate = **120 g/s = 10,368 kg/day ≈ 3784 t/yr**

---

**(ii) Effective Stack Height (3.5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Buoyancy Flux:**

$$F = g \cdot V_s \cdot D_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 12 \times (3)^2 \times \dfrac{450 - 300}{450}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 12 \times 9 \times \dfrac{150}{450}$$

$$F = 9.81 \times 12 \times 9 \times 0.3333$$

$$F = 1059.48 \times 0.3333 \approx 353.16 \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Brigg's Equation:**

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}$$

$$F^{1/3} = (353.16)^{1/3} \approx 7.06$$

$$x^{2/3} = (600)^{2/3} \approx 71.13$$

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \times 7.06 \times 71.13}{6} = \dfrac{803.5}{6} \approx 133.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Stack Height:**

$$H_{eff} = h + \Delta h = 60 + 133.9 = 193.9 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Effective stack height = **193.9 m**

---

### ❓ Question B — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Discuss the major **stationary, mobile, area, and fugitive sources** of air pollution. Compare their relative contributions to urban air quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial zone has **three stacks** with the following characteristics:

| Stack | SO₂ Concentration | Flue Gas Flow |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 800 mg/Nm³ | 40 Nm³/s |
| 2 | 1200 mg/Nm³ | 30 Nm³/s |
| 3 | 600 mg/Nm³ | 50 Nm³/s |

**(i)** Compute the total SO₂ emission rate in **kg/hr**.
**(ii)** If the CPCB standard is **1.5 kg/hr** for this type of industry, comment on compliance.
**(iii)** Recommend a control device to reduce emissions. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Stationary Sources (2 Marks):** Power plants, industrial boilers, refineries, cement, iron & steel. Dominated by SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂ from coal/oil combustion.

**Mobile Sources (2 Marks):** Motor vehicles (CO, HC, NOₓ, PM, Pb); aircraft and marine vessels. In urban areas, mobile sources typically contribute **60–80% of total air pollution load**.

**Area Sources (1.5 Marks):** Distributed small emitters — domestic biomass cooking, agricultural NH₃, construction dust, road dust re-suspension.

**Fugitive Sources (1.5 Marks):** Unintentional leaks — landfill CH₄, solvent evaporation, gas pipeline leaks, wastewater VOC emissions.

**Comparison:** In urban areas, mobile > area > stationary (residential) > fugitive. In industrial belts, stationary > mobile > area > fugitive.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Total SO₂ Emission Rate (3 Marks):**

**Stack 1:**
$$E_1 = 800 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \times 40 = 32{,}000 \text{ mg/s} = 32 \text{ g/s}$$

**Stack 2:**
$$E_2 = 1200 \times 30 = 36{,}000 \text{ mg/s} = 36 \text{ g/s}$$

**Stack 3:**
$$E_3 = 600 \times 50 = 30{,}000 \text{ mg/s} = 30 \text{ g/s}$$

**Total:**
$$E_{total} = 32 + 36 + 30 = 98 \text{ g/s}$$

**Convert to kg/hr:**
$$E_{total} = 98 \text{ g/s} \times \dfrac{3600}{1000} = 352.8 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Compliance (2 Marks):**

The CPCB standard is 1.5 kg/hr.
The total emission is **352.8 kg/hr**, which is **235 times** the standard.

**Verdict: The industry is in severe non-compliance. Immediate corrective action is mandatory.**

**(iii) Recommended Control Device (2 Marks):**

For SO₂ reduction, a **Wet Limestone Gypsum Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)** scrubber is recommended. It can achieve **90–98% SO₂ removal** by reacting SO₂ with limestone slurry to form gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O):

$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

Alternative: **Dry Sorbent Injection** (lower capital, suitable for smaller plants).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on "Sources" Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units in $E = C \times Q$ and Brigg's equation problems.
> 2. **Confusing T_s and T_a in Brigg's equation** — The temperature difference $(T_s - T_a)/T_s$ requires both in **Kelvin (K)**, NOT °C. A common student error is using °C and getting wrong plume rise.
> 3. **Skipping "natural sources" in classification questions** — Many students write only anthropogenic sources. KTU 2024 explicitly lists both. Missing natural = **1.5 marks lost** out of 3.
> 4. **Not distinguishing primary vs secondary pollutants** with reaction equations — KTU now awards 1 mark for a balanced equation (e.g., SO₂ → H₂SO₄).
> 5. **Forgetting the "1.6" constant in Brigg's equation** — Some students omit the empirical constant entirely, leading to incorrect dimensional or numerical results.
> 6. **In definition questions, not quoting CPCB/EPA definitions** — Examiners expect a precise definition in the first sentence; vague definitions lose **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 Quick-Fire Revision — Memorize Before Exam**

### ✅ Definitional Anchors
- ✅ **Source** = Origin of pollutant release into environment.
- ✅ **Primary Pollutant** = Emitted *directly* (e.g., SO₂, CO, NOₓ, PM).
- ✅ **Secondary Pollutant** = Formed *in situ* by atmospheric reaction (e.g., O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃).
- ✅ **Natural Sources** = Volcanic, biogenic, dust, sea spray, lightning, microbial.
- ✅ **Anthropogenic** = Stationary, Mobile, Area, Fugitive.

### ✅ Formula Anchors (Must Memorize)
- ✅ $E = C \times Q$ — Emission rate from concentration & flow.
- ✅ $E = EF \times A$ — Emission factor method.
- ✅ $F = g \cdot V_s \cdot D_s^2 \cdot (T_s - T_a)/T_s$ — Buoyancy flux.
- ✅ $\Delta h = (1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3})/u$ — Brigg's plume rise.
- ✅ $H_{eff} = h + \Delta h$ — Effective stack height.

### ✅ Units to Remember
- ✅ $C$ → g/m³, mg/Nm³, ppm (1 ppm ≈ 1000 ppb).
- ✅ $E$ → g/s, kg/hr, tonnes/year.
- ✅ $T$ → **Always Kelvin** for Brigg's equation.
- ✅ $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### ✅ Key Reactions (Bonus Marks)
- ✅ $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}^\bullet$ (Photo-dissociation)
- ✅ $\text{O}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_3$ (Ozone formation)
- ✅ $\text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (Acid rain)
- ✅ $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$ (FGD)

### ✅ High-Yield Facts
- ✅ Mobile sources contribute **60–80%** of urban air pollution.
- ✅ **Brigg's equation** is empirical; valid for buoyant plumes in neutral/unstable conditions.
- ✅ **Effective stack height** is twice the physical height for tall stacks (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **N₂O** from agriculture and **CH₄** from livestock are potent greenhouse gases.
- ✅ **PM₂.₅** is the most health-critical pollutant from combustion sources.

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using °C instead of K in Brigg's equation.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mg to g (or vice versa) in $E = C \times Q$.
- ❌ Confusing "anthropogenic" with "stationary" — anthropogenic is a **broader category**.
- ❌ Omitting the "1.6" coefficient in Brigg's equation.
- ❌ Not distinguishing **emission** (mass/time) from **concentration** (mass/volume).

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "discuss sources," always structure the answer as: (1) Definition → (2) Classification chart/table → (3) Examples with pollutants → (4) One numerical or reaction-based sub-part → (5) Closing statement on control. This template has historically secured **full marks** in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects on Human Health
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effects on Human Health

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Effects on Human Health**, in the context of environmental engineering and water/air quality management, refers to the **adverse physiological, biochemical, genetic, carcinogenic, teratogenic, and immunological responses** manifested in living organisms (especially humans) due to prolonged or acute exposure to **physical, chemical, and biological contaminants** present in water bodies and the ambient atmosphere.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECET861, this topic integrates principles of **environmental toxicology**, **epidemiology**, and **public health engineering** to evaluate how pollutants—ranging from pathogenic microorganisms in water to particulate matter and gaseous toxics in air—disrupt human homeostasis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to identify the major waterborne and airborne diseases, classify contaminants by their toxicological class, and relate pollutant concentrations to human health outcomes using standard dose-response frameworks and reference doses (RfD) defined by **WHO**, **EPA**, and **BIS (IS 10500)**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Ready Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Toxicity** | The degree to which a substance can cause harm to a living organism. |
| **Hazard** | The inherent property of a substance to cause adverse effects. |
| **Risk** | The probability that a hazard will cause harm under specific exposure conditions. |
| **Dose** | The amount of a substance that enters or contacts an organism (mg/kg body weight). |
| **Dose-Response Relationship** | The relationship between the dose of a chemical and the magnitude of the biological response. |
| **LD₅₀** | **Lethal Dose 50%** — the dose that kills 50% of the test population, expressed in **mg/kg body weight**. |
| **Reference Dose (RfD)** | An estimate of daily exposure unlikely to cause appreciable risk over a lifetime (mg/kg/day). |
| **Bioaccumulation** | The progressive accumulation of a substance in an organism's body over its lifetime. |
| **Biomagnification** | The increasing concentration of a substance up the food chain. |
| **Synergistic Effect** | Combined effect of two or more pollutants that is greater than the sum of their individual effects. |
| **Antagonistic Effect** | Combined effect that is less than the sum of individual effects. |
| **Carcinogen** | A substance capable of causing cancer. |
| **Teratogen** | A substance causing developmental malformations in a fetus. |
| **Mutagen** | A substance inducing genetic mutations. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Bucket of Tolerance"**
> Imagine each human body as a **bucket**. The bucket has a finite capacity (the body's ability to detoxify and excrete harmful substances). Every contaminant entering the body—through drinking water, inhalation of polluted air, or ingestion of contaminated food—pours a little more "toxic liquid" into the bucket. 
> - **Acute Exposure** is like a sudden downpour—the bucket overflows rapidly, leading to immediate, dramatic illness (e.g., cyanide poisoning).
> - **Chronic Exposure** is like a slow drip—individually harmless, but over years, the bucket fills until it overflows, causing silent, long-term damage (e.g., lead-induced neurological decline).
> - The **Overflow Point** differs for each pollutant and person. Children, pregnant women, the elderly, and immunocompromised individuals have smaller buckets, making them **vulnerable populations**.

## 1.4 Classification of Health Effects (KTU Framework)

### A. By Exposure Duration
1. **Acute Effects**: Manifest within hours/days of short-term high-dose exposure (e.g., CO poisoning, cholera).
2. **Chronic Effects**: Develop after months/years of low-dose continuous exposure (e.g., asbestosis, arsenicosis, cancer).
3. **Sub-chronic Effects**: Intermediate duration (typically 90 days in animal studies).

### B. By Target Organ System
1. **Respiratory System** (Air pollution: PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, O₃)
2. **Gastrointestinal System** (Waterborne pathogens: *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*)
3. **Nervous System** (Heavy metals: Pb, Hg, As, Mn)
4. **Cardiovascular System** (PM, CO, persistent organic pollutants)
5. **Renal System** (Cd, hard water contaminants)
6. **Hepatic System** (CCL₄, aflatoxins in water)
7. **Reproductive/Developmental** (Endocrine disruptors, BPA, pesticides)

### C. By Nature of Response
1. **Reversible** (most infections, mild respiratory irritation)
2. **Irreversible** (cancer, genetic mutations, teratogenic effects)
3. **Cumulative** (heavy metal bioaccumulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Every water treatment plant, every air quality control device, and every environmental standard exists because of the **Effects on Human Health**. The KTU 2024 Scheme places this topic at the convergence of chemistry, biology, and public policy—students must internalize that pollutant concentration limits are fundamentally *health-driven*, not arbitrarily chosen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How Pollutants Cause Harm

The pathway from a pollutant in the environment to a human health effect follows a **five-stage model**, often tested in KTU board examinations:

### Stage 1: **Emission / Release**
The pollutant enters the environment from natural or anthropogenic sources.

- *Water Example*: Industrial effluent discharge of lead (Pb²⁺) into a river.
- *Air Example*: Vehicular emissions releasing NOₓ into the atmosphere.

### Stage 2: **Transport & Transformation**
The pollutant migrates through environmental media (air, water, soil) and may undergo physical, chemical, or biological transformation.

- *Physical*: Dispersion, dilution, adsorption onto particulates.
- *Chemical*: Photochemical reactions (e.g., NO₂ + hν → NO + O; O + O₂ → O₃).
- *Biological*: Microbial degradation of organic contaminants.

### Stage 3: **Exposure**
A human comes into contact with the pollutant via:
- **Ingestion** (drinking water, food chain)
- **Inhalation** (ambient air, indoor air)
- **Dermal Contact** (bathing, swimming)
- **Trans-placental** (mother to fetus)

### Stage 4: **Dose (Uptake)**
The amount of pollutant that actually enters the body across biological membranes (lungs, GI tract, skin). This is the **biologically effective dose**.

### Stage 5: **Response**
The biological effect ranging from subclinical biochemical changes to overt disease and death.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** Examiners frequently frame questions as *"Trace the pathway of pollutant X from source to human health effect."* Memorizing these five stages ensures full marks on such questions.

## 2.2 Major Waterborne Diseases and Their Health Effects

| Pathogen Class | Example Organism | Disease | Health Effect |
|----------------|------------------|---------|---------------|
| **Bacteria** | *Vibrio cholerae* | Cholera | Severe watery diarrhea, dehydration, death |
| Bacteria | *Salmonella typhi* | Typhoid | High fever, GI ulceration, intestinal perforation |
| Bacteria | *Escherichia coli* O157:H7 | Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS) | Kidney failure, hemolytic anemia |
| Bacteria | *Legionella pneumophila* | Legionnaires' disease | Severe pneumonia |
| **Viruses** | Rotavirus | Gastroenteritis | Dehydration in infants |
| Viruses | Hepatitis A/E virus | Infectious hepatitis | Jaundice, liver failure |
| Viruses | Poliovirus | Poliomyelitis | Paralysis |
| **Protozoa** | *Giardia lamblia* | Giardiasis | Diarrhea, malabsorption |
| Protozoa | *Cryptosporidium parvum* | Cryptosporidiosis | Chronic diarrhea in immunocompromised |
| Protozoa | *Entamoeba histolytica* | Amoebiasis | Dysentery, liver abscess |
| **Helminths** | *Ascaris lumbricoides* | Ascariasis | Intestinal obstruction, malnutrition |
| Helminths | *Schistosoma* spp. | Schistosomiasis | Liver fibrosis, bladder cancer |

## 2.3 Chemical Contaminants in Water and Health Effects

| Contaminant | Source | Health Effect | WHO/BIS Limit |
|-------------|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Lead (Pb)** | Pipes, industrial waste | Neurological damage (children), anemia, hypertension | **0.01 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Arsenic (As)** | Natural geology, pesticides | Hyperkeratosis, skin cancer, arsenicosis | **0.01 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Fluoride (F⁻)** | Natural dissolution | Dental fluorosis (>1.5 mg/L), skeletal fluorosis (>4 mg/L) | **1.5 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Nitrate (NO₃⁻)** | Agricultural runoff | Methemoglobinemia ("Blue Baby Syndrome") in infants | **50 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | Industrial discharge, fish | Minamata disease—neurological damage | **0.006 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | Mining, batteries | Itai-Itai disease—kidney damage, bone demineralization | **0.003 mg/L** (WHO) |
| **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** | Tanneries, steel | Lung cancer, kidney damage | **0.05 mg/L** (WHO) |

| Pollutant | Primary Sources | Health Effects | WHO AQG (2021) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| **PM₂.₅** | Combustion, vehicles | Asthma, COPD, lung cancer, CVD | **15 µg/m³ (24-hr)** |
| **PM₁₀** | Dust, industry | Bronchitis, reduced lung function | **45 µg/m³ (24-hr)** |
| **SO₂** | Coal combustion | Bronchoconstriction, acid rain effects | **40 µg/m³ (24-hr)** |
| **NO₂** | Traffic, power plants | Respiratory infection, asthma exacerbation | **25 µg/m³ (24-hr)** |
| **O₃** | Photochemical smog | Chest pain, reduced lung function | **100 µg/m³ (8-hr)** |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion | Tissue hypoxia, headaches, death at high doses | **4 mg/m³ (24-hr)** |
| **Pb (airborne)** | Lead smelters, legacy gasoline | Cognitive deficits in children | **0.5 µg/m³ (annual)** |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Board Examinations:** Memorize these equations and their units. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in place of literal pipe characters to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $$\text{Dose} = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$$ | Average Daily Dose (ADD) — chronic non-cancer risk | mg/kg-day |
| $$\text{LADD} = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT_{lifetime}}$$ | Lifetime Average Daily Dose | mg/kg-day |
| $$\text{HQ} = \frac{ADD}{RfD}$$ | Hazard Quotient (non-carcinogenic) | dimensionless |
| $$\text{HI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} HQ_i$$ | Hazard Index (multiple chemicals) | dimensionless |
| $$\text{CR} = \text{LADD} \times \text{CSF}$$ | Cancer Risk (carcinogenic) | dimensionless (probability) |
| $$\text{AQI} = \frac{I_{hi} - I_{lo}}{C_{hi} - C_{lo}} \times (C - C_{lo}) + I_{lo}$$ | Linear AQI breakpoint interpolation | dimensionless index |
| $$\text{PL} = \frac{\text{Sub-chronic Dose}}{\text{Acute or Chronic RfD}}$$ | Pollutant Limit Margin | dimensionless |
| $$\text{BB} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(C_i \times t_i\right)$$ | Body Burden (cumulative dose) | mg |
| $$\text{BMF} = \frac{[C]_{predator}}{[C]_{prey}}$$ | Biomagnification Factor | dimensionless |
| $$\text{BCF} = \frac{[C]_{organism}}{[C]_{water}}$$ | Bioconcentration Factor | L/kg |

Where:
- $C$ = Contaminant concentration (mg/L water or µg/m³ air)
- $IR$ = Ingestion/Inhalation rate (L/day for water, m³/day for air)
- $EF$ = Exposure frequency (days/year)
- $ED$ = Exposure duration (years)
- $BW$ = Body weight (kg)
- $AT$ = Averaging time (days)
- $RfD$ = Reference Dose (mg/kg-day)
- $CSF$ = Cancer Slope Factor (mg/kg-day)⁻¹
- $I_{hi}, I_{lo}$ = AQI breakpoints (high, low)
- $C_{hi}, C_{lo}$ = Concentration breakpoints (high, low)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not merely academic. They are the **backbone of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports submitted to India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC). Every municipal water treatment plant design in Kerala uses ADD/HQ calculations to validate that treated water meets safety thresholds for lifetime consumption. Every ambient air quality monitoring station under India's **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** reports AQI values calculated via the breakpoint formula above.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Daily Dose (ADD) for a Water Contaminant

### Problem Statement
A 60 kg adult drinks 2 L/day of water containing **0.05 mg/L of lead**. Exposure occurs 350 days/year for 30 years. Calculate the Average Daily Dose (ADD) for chronic non-cancer risk assessment.

### Given Data
- Concentration $C = 0.05$ mg/L
- Ingestion Rate $IR = 2$ L/day
- Exposure Frequency $EF = 350$ days/year
- Exposure Duration $ED = 30$ years
- Body Weight $BW = 60$ kg
- Averaging Time $AT = ED \times 365 = 30 \times 365$ days (for non-cancer chronic)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the governing equation.

$$ADD = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator (total exposure).

The numerator represents the cumulative mass of contaminant ingested over the exposure period (in mg).

$$\text{Numerator} = C \times IR \times EF \times ED$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.05 \, \frac{mg}{L} \times 2 \, \frac{L}{day} \times 350 \, \frac{days}{yr} \times 30 \, yr$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intermediate product (mg).

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.05 \times 2 \times 350 \times 30 = 0.05 \times 21{,}000 = 1050 \, mg$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator (body weight × averaging time).

$$AT = 30 \, yr \times 365 \, \frac{days}{yr} = 10{,}950 \, days$$

$$\text{Denominator} = BW \times AT = 60 \, kg \times 10{,}950 \, days = 657{,}000 \, kg \cdot days$$

**Step 6:** Divide numerator by denominator.

$$ADD = \frac{1050 \, mg}{657{,}000 \, kg \cdot days}$$

$$\boxed{ADD = 1.598 \times 10^{-3} \, \frac{mg}{kg \cdot day} \approx 0.0016 \, \frac{mg}{kg \cdot day}}$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation against the Reference Dose (RfD) for lead.

The USEPA Reference Dose for lead is $RfD = 0.0035$ mg/kg-day (interim, oral).

$$HQ = \frac{ADD}{RfD} = \frac{0.0016}{0.0035} = 0.457$$

Since $HQ < 1$, the exposure is **below the threshold of concern**, but it is non-trivial (45.7% of the RfD), indicating that additional exposure pathways (food, dust) would push the risk higher.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** Always specify the units at every intermediate step. A common error leading to a 1-mark deduction is omitting the unit analysis for the final answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Quality Index (AQI) — PM₂.₅ Breakpoint Calculation

### Problem Statement
The 24-hour average PM₂.₅ concentration measured at a Kerala monitoring station is **75 µg/m³**. Calculate the AQI for this pollutant.

### Background
India's CPCB AQI uses linear interpolation between defined breakpoints. For PM₂.₅ (24-hr average):

| $C_{lo}$ (µg/m³) | $C_{hi}$ (µg/m³) | $I_{lo}$ | $I_{hi}$ |
|:----------------:|:----------------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| 0 | 30 | 0 | 100 |
| 31 | 60 | 101 | 200 |
| 61 | 90 | 201 | 300 |
| 91 | 120 | 301 | 400 |
| 121 | 250 | 401 | 500 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the concentration breakpoints that bound the measured value.

Measured $C = 75$ µg/m³ lies between 61 and 90 µg/m³.

Therefore: $C_{lo} = 61$, $C_{hi} = 90$, $I_{lo} = 201$, $I_{hi} = 300$.

**Step 2:** State the breakpoint interpolation formula.

$$I_p = \frac{I_{hi} - I_{lo}}{C_{hi} - C_{lo}} \times (C - C_{lo}) + I_{lo}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the AQI range (numerator).

$$I_{hi} - I_{lo} = 300 - 201 = 99$$

**Step 4:** Compute the concentration range (denominator).

$$C_{hi} - C_{lo} = 90 - 61 = 29$$

**Step 5:** Compute the deviation of measured concentration from the lower breakpoint.

$$C - C_{lo} = 75 - 61 = 14$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into the equation.

$$I_p = \frac{99}{29} \times 14 + 201$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the multiplication.

$$\frac{99}{29} = 3.4138$$

$$3.4138 \times 14 = 47.793$$

**Step 8:** Add the lower index value.

$$I_p = 47.793 + 201 = 248.793$$

$$\boxed{I_p \approx 249 \, (\text{"Poor"} \text{ category})}$$

**Step 9:** Health implication.

AQI 201–300 is categorized as **"Poor"** by CPCB, implying that everyone may begin to experience health effects, with sensitive groups (children, elderly, asthmatics) experiencing more serious impacts. This is the standard KTU-level interpretation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dose-Response Classification (Haber's Law & Beyond)

For the board exam, students must classify effects based on the relationship between dose $D$ and response $R$.

| Relationship | Equation | Description | Example |
|--------------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Linear Non-Threshold** | $R = a + bD$ | Any dose carries risk; used for carcinogens | Radionuclides, benzene |
| **Threshold (NOAEL/LOAEL)** | $R = 0$ if $D < D_0$ | No effect below threshold dose | Most non-carcinogens |
| **Hormesis** | $R = bD - cD^2$ | Low dose beneficial, high dose harmful | Some trace elements (Se, Cr³⁺) |
| **Haber's Law ($C \times t = k$)** | $C \cdot t = \text{constant}$ | Toxicity depends on product of concentration and time | War gases (mustard gas) |

**Haber's Law Derivation (Quick):** In the simplest single-compartment toxicokinetic model, the absorbed dose per unit body mass is the product of inhaled/ingested concentration and the time of exposure, divided by body weight:

$$\text{Absorbed Dose} \propto C \times t$$

For a fixed toxic effect (e.g., 50% mortality), $C \times t = k$ (a constant). Thus, doubling the exposure time at half the concentration yields the same biological outcome. This is foundational for **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** derivation in industrial hygiene.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Cancer Risk Calculation

### Problem
A 70 kg adult inhales 20 m³/day of air containing **1.5 µg/m³ of benzene** for 25 years (350 days/yr). The Cancer Slope Factor (CSF) for benzene via inhalation is **0.029 (mg/kg-day)⁻¹**. Calculate the lifetime cancer risk.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the Lifetime Average Daily Dose (LADD).

$$LADD = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT_{lifetime}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert units consistently. Convert µg to mg: $C = 1.5 \, \mu g/m^3 = 0.0015$ mg/m³.

$$LADD = \frac{0.0015 \times 20 \times 350 \times 25}{70 \times (70 \times 365)}$$

*Note:* For lifetime cancer risk, $AT = 70$ years (lifespan assumption) × 365 days/yr.

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$\text{Num} = 0.0015 \times 20 \times 350 \times 25 = 262.5 \, mg$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator.

$$AT_{lt} = 70 \times 365 = 25{,}550 \, days$$

$$\text{Den} = 70 \times 25{,}550 = 1{,}788{,}500 \, kg \cdot days$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$LADD = \frac{262.5}{1{,}788{,}500} = 1.468 \times 10^{-4} \, \frac{mg}{kg \cdot day}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the Cancer Risk equation.

$$CR = LADD \times CSF = 1.468 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.029 = 4.257 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 4.3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 7:** Interpret.

The USEPA's acceptable cancer risk range is $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ (i.e., 1 in a million to 1 in ten thousand). A risk of $4.3 \times 10^{-6}$ indicates approximately **4 additional cancer cases per million exposed individuals**—within the "de minimis" range but still significant at a population scale.

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Reading:**
This is the canonical **4-step risk assessment** mandated by the US National Research Council and adopted by India's MoEFCC. The decision node branches into either documentation (acceptable risk) or a hierarchy of controls—**source control > pathway control > receptor protection**—followed by iterative reassessment, mirroring the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle of quality management.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "Reference Dose (RfD)" used in environmental health risk assessment. Briefly explain its significance in evaluating non-carcinogenic health effects of a water pollutant.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reference Dose (RfD)** is defined by the US EPA as an estimate of a daily oral exposure to the human population (including sensitive subgroups) that is likely to be without an appreciable risk of deleterious effects during a lifetime. It is expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**.

**Significance:**
1. It serves as a benchmark dose below which adverse non-carcinogenic health effects are not expected over a lifetime of exposure.
2. The **Hazard Quotient (HQ)** is calculated as $HQ = ADD/RfD$. If $HQ < 1$, the exposure is considered safe; if $HQ \geq 1$, there may be a concern.
3. It is derived from NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) in animal or human studies, divided by uncertainty factors.
4. It accounts for intra-species variability and inter-species extrapolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the unit (mg/kg-day) and the comparison threshold ($HQ < 1$) for full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the three principal routes of human exposure to environmental pollutants.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ingestion:** Intake of contaminated water, food, or soil via the gastrointestinal tract. Example: Drinking arsenic-contaminated groundwater, consuming fish with bioaccumulated mercury.

2. **Inhalation:** Intake of contaminated air (gaseous or particulate) via the respiratory tract. Example: Breathing PM₂.₅-laden urban air, exposure to benzene vapors in industrial settings.

3. **Dermal Contact:** Absorption of pollutants through the skin. Example: Swimming in chlorinated pool water (chloroform uptake), agricultural pesticide exposure during spraying.

In some special cases, a fourth route—**trans-placental transfer**—is significant for fetuses of exposed mothers (e.g., methylmercury crossing the placental barrier causing Minamata disease in neonates).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the major waterborne diseases caused by bacterial, viral, and protozoan pathogens. For each category, list **two** specific diseases, their causative organisms, and the corresponding health effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community draws drinking water from a well containing **0.5 mg/L of fluoride**. Calculate the Average Daily Dose (ADD) for a 55 kg person who drinks 1.8 L/day for 340 days/year over 20 years. The RfD for fluoride is **0.06 mg/kg-day**. Comment on the health implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Bacterial Diseases (2 examples):**

1. **Cholera** — *Vibrio cholerae*
   - *Health effect:* Profuse watery "rice-water" diarrhea, severe dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, hypovolemic shock, and death within hours if untreated. Toxin (CT) activates adenylate cyclase in intestinal cells causing massive Cl⁻ and water efflux.

2. **Typhoid Fever** — *Salmonella typhi*
   - *Health effect:* Sustained high fever, abdominal pain, hepatosplenomegaly, rose spots, and intestinal hemorrhage/perforation. The bacterium invades Peyer's patches in the ileum.

**Viral Diseases (2 examples):**

1. **Infectious Hepatitis** — Hepatitis A or E virus
   - *Health effect:* Acute inflammation of the liver, jaundice, dark urine, fatigue. Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in pregnant women (up to 25% mortality in third trimester).

2. **Poliomyelitis** — Poliovirus
   - *Health effect:* Invasion of the central nervous system, motor neuron destruction, asymmetric flaccid paralysis (typically of the lower limbs), and in severe cases, respiratory failure.

**Protozoan Diseases (2 examples):**

1. **Giardiasis** — *Giardia lamblia*
   - *Health effect:* Persistent foul-smelling, greasy diarrhea (steatorrhea), malabsorption, weight loss, abdominal cramps. The trophozoite attaches to the duodenal epithelium.

2. **Amoebic Dysentery** — *Entamoeba histolytica*
   - *Health effect:* Bloody mucoid diarrhea, tenesmus, and extra-intestinal complications such as liver abscess (amoebic hepatitis) if trophozoites invade the portal circulation.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- $C = 0.5$ mg/L
- $IR = 1.8$ L/day
- $EF = 340$ days/yr
- $ED = 20$ years
- $BW = 55$ kg
- $AT = 20 \times 365 = 7300$ days
- $RfD = 0.06$ mg/kg-day

**Step 1:** Write the equation.

$$ADD = \frac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator.

$$\text{Num} = 0.5 \times 1.8 \times 340 \times 20 = 6120 \, mg$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$\text{Den} = 55 \times 7300 = 401{,}500 \, kg \cdot days$$

**Step 4:** Divide.

$$ADD = \frac{6120}{401{,}500} = 1.524 \times 10^{-2} = 0.01524 \, \frac{mg}{kg \cdot day}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Hazard Quotient.

$$HQ = \frac{ADD}{RfD} = \frac{0.01524}{0.06} = 0.254$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.

Since $HQ = 0.254 < 1$, the **non-carcinogenic risk is below the threshold of concern**. However, the concentration (0.5 mg/L) is well within the WHO permissible range (≤ 1.5 mg/L), and the actual exposure represents only ~25.4% of the RfD, providing a **substantial margin of safety**. Long-term exposure at this level is unlikely to cause skeletal or dental fluorosis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the **Averaging Time (AT)** for non-cancer and cancer assessments. For non-cancer chronic ADD, use $AT = ED \times 365$. For cancer LADD, use $AT = 70 \times 365$ (lifetime). Mixing these up is the **most common 1-mark deduction** in numerical questions on this topic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the major categories of air pollutants and their effects on human health, with emphasis on **particulate matter (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀)**, **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, and **ground-level ozone (O₃)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The 24-hour average PM₁₀ concentration in an industrial area is measured as **180 µg/m³**. Using the CPCB AQI breakpoints, calculate the corresponding AQI value. Also, identify the AQI category and its health implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀):**

- **Sources:** Combustion of fossil fuels (vehicles, power plants), industrial processes, biomass burning, road dust, construction.
- **PM₁₀** (aerodynamic diameter ≤ 10 µm): Trapped in the upper respiratory tract (nose, throat). Causes **chronic bronchitis**, reduced lung function, aggravated asthma, and irritation of mucous membranes.
- **PM₂.₅** (≤ 2.5 µm): Penetrates deep into the **alveolar region** of the lungs and can enter the bloodstream. Causes **chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)**, cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, and premature death. Linked to **systemic inflammation** and **atherosclerosis**.

**2. Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):**

- **Sources:** Combustion of sulphur-containing fossil fuels (coal, oil), smelting of ores, volcanic eruptions.
- **Health effects:** A **respiratory irritant** that causes **bronchoconstriction**, particularly in asthmatics. Reacts with moisture in the airways to form sulphurous acid (H₂SO₃), leading to inflammation. Long-term exposure contributes to chronic bronchitis and reduced pulmonary function. SO₂ is a precursor of acid rain, which indirectly affects drinking water and food crops.

**3. Ground-Level Ozone (O₃):**

- **Sources:** Not emitted directly; formed by photochemical reactions between NOₓ and VOCs in the presence of sunlight (photochemical smog).
- **Health effects:** Strong **oxidant** that damages lung tissue, causes chest pain, coughing, throat irritation, and inflames and scars the airways. Long-term exposure reduces lung function and increases susceptibility to respiratory infections. Children who play outdoors in summer are particularly vulnerable.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $C = 180$ µg/m³ (24-hr PM₁₀ average)

**CPCB AQI Breakpoints for PM₁₀ (24-hr):**

| $C_{lo}$ | $C_{hi}$ | $I_{lo}$ | $I_{hi}$ |
|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| 101 | 250 | 301 | 400 |

(Note: For PM₁₀ 24-hr, breakpoints of 51–100 correspond to AQI 101–200, and 101–250 to AQI 201–300 per revised CPCB. Some reference tables use 101–250 → 301–400. We adopt the most widely used CPCB 2014 scheme, where 101–250 maps to 301–400.)

**Step 1:** Identify breakpoints.

$C_{lo} = 101$, $C_{hi} = 250$, $I_{lo} = 301$, $I_{hi} = 400$.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$I_p = \frac{I_{hi} - I_{lo}}{C_{hi} - C_{lo}} \times (C - C_{lo}) + I_{lo}$$

$$I_p = \frac{400 - 301}{250 - 101} \times (180 - 101) + 301$$

**Step 3:** Compute the differences.

$$I_p = \frac{99}{149} \times 79 + 301$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$0.6644 \times 79 = 52.49$$

$$I_p = 52.49 + 301 = 353.49 \approx 353$$

$$\boxed{I_p \approx 353}$$

**Step 5:** AQI Category and Health Implications.

AQI 301–400 corresponds to the **"Very Poor"** category. Health implications:
- **General population:** Significant aggravation of heart and lung disease, reduced exercise capacity, premature mortality in people with cardiopulmonary disease.
- **Sensitive groups:** Should **avoid all outdoor exertion**. Healthy persons may experience respiratory symptoms on prolonged exposure.
- Engineering action: Trigger emergency industrial shutdowns, odd-even vehicle schemes, and construction dust controls (as practiced in NCR-Delhi under GRAP Stage III).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Two recurrent mistakes in AQI questions:
> 1. **Wrong breakpoint row** — Students often pick the row for PM₂.₅ when the question specifies PM₁₀. Always read the pollutant name twice.
> 2. **Forgetting to add $I_{lo}$** — A 1-mark error arises from omitting the trailing `+ I_{lo}` term, yielding a sub-100 value that makes no sense in context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the KTU Exam — Final 10-Minute Revision for "Effects on Human Health"**

- ☐ **Five-Stage Pathway:** Release → Transport → Exposure → Dose → Response. Memorize this exact sequence.
- ☐ **Waterborne Pathogens:** Remember the **B-V-P-H** mnemonic for *Bacteria, Viruses, Protozoa, Helminths*. Each class has at least 2 KTU-favorite examples.
- ☐ **"Itai-Itai" = Cadmium** (kidney + bone disease, Japan).
- ☐ **"Minamata" = Mercury** (neurological, Japan, 1950s).
- ☐ **"Blue Baby Syndrome" = Nitrate** (methemoglobinemia in infants).
- ☐ **"Itai" = "ouch" in Japanese = pain in bones** — links Cadmium to bone pain.
- ☐ **Fluoride > 1.5 mg/L = dental fluorosis; > 4 mg/L = skeletal fluorosis.**
- ☐ **Arsenic = WHO limit 0.01 mg/L** — causes skin lesions and cancers.
- ☐ **PM₂.₅ < PM₁₀ in size but more dangerous** (deeper lung penetration).
- ☐ **O₃ is a secondary pollutant** — not emitted directly, formed photochemically.
- ☐ **CO binds to Hb 200× more strongly than O₂** → tissue hypoxia.
- ☐ **AQI formula:** Always identify correct $C_{lo}, C_{hi}, I_{lo}, I_{hi}$ first, then plug in.
- ☐ **Hazard Quotient < 1 = acceptable non-cancer risk; ≥ 1 = concern.**
- ☐ **Cancer Risk acceptable range:** $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ☐ **AT for non-cancer = ED × 365; AT for cancer = 70 × 365.**
- ☐ **Body Burden = cumulative dose over time; Bioaccumulation Factor = ratio of concentration in organism to medium.**
- ☐ **Vulnerable populations:** Children, pregnant women, elderly, immunocompromised—always mention in answers for "engineering empathy" marks.
- ☐ **Units, units, units!** — A numerical answer without units will be marked down even if correct.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — "Effects on Human Health"**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vegetation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vegetation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vegetation**, in the context of Water and Air Quality Management, refers to the systematic use of plants, trees, shrubs, grasses, and associated microbial communities as a biological engineering tool to control, mitigate, monitor, and remediate environmental pollution in both atmospheric and aquatic systems. It is broadly classified into three engineering functions:

1. **Phytoremediation** – Use of plants to remove, transfer, stabilize, or destroy contaminants in soil, water, and air.
2. **Bioindication / Biomonitoring** – Use of vegetation as living sensors to detect and quantify pollution levels.
3. **Pollution Abatement Infrastructure** – Use of vegetation as engineered systems (green belts, vegetative buffers, constructed wetlands) to physically/chemically intercept pollutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** Vegetation is treated as a *passive green engineering technology* — it costs very little to operate, requires no fossil-fuel energy input, and provides simultaneous benefits: pollution control, carbon sequestration, biodiversity support, and aesthetic value.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy road filled with heavy trucks kicking up dust and emitting black smoke. Now imagine a *living wall* of tall trees and dense shrubs planted along that road. What happens?

- The tree canopy **physically blocks** the dust and smoke from reaching the other side (just like a curtain).
- The leaves' sticky surfaces and tiny pores (stomata) **absorb and trap** toxic gases like SO₂, NOₓ, and PM.
- The roots and soil microbes beneath **filter and biologically break down** pollutants that wash down with rain.
- Some sensitive plants **change color, get spots, or die** when pollution is high — they *signal* (indicate) the level of contamination.

In simple words, **vegetation acts as a natural, self-renewing, low-cost "green filter and alarm system"** for both air and water.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Parameters (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Dust capture by tree canopy | **0.1 to 0.5 kg/m²/yr** |
| Typical green-belt width | **10 m to 100 m** |
| Particulate removal efficiency (dense belt) | **up to 70–90 %** |
| SO₂ absorption by sensitive species | **5–20 µg/m³ of leaf flux** |
| Stomatal conductance (typical broadleaf) | **0.1 to 0.5 mol/m²/s** |
| Noise attenuation by 30 m dense belt | **5 to 10 dB(A)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Vegetation-based pollution control is **species-specific**, **density-dependent**, and **seasonally variable**. The choice of plant species is the single most important design decision.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Classification of Vegetation in Pollution Control

Vegetation contributes to air and water quality management through **five primary mechanisms**:

1. **Particulate Filtration (Mechanical Interception)**
   - Leaves, twigs, and bark act as physical filters.
   - Particles (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, dust) impact and adhere to leaf surfaces due to:
     - Inertial impaction
     - Brownian diffusion
     - Gravitational sedimentation
   - Effectiveness depends on **Leaf Area Index (LAI)**, **canopy density**, and **surface roughness**.

2. **Gaseous Pollutant Absorption (Stomatal Uptake)**
   - Gaseous pollutants (SO₂, NO₂, O₃, CO, HF, NH₃) diffuse through stomata.
   - Inside the leaf, they dissolve in the mesophyll cell moisture and undergo biochemical detoxification (e.g., SO₂ → sulfite → sulfate, stored in vacuoles).
   - Controlled by **stomatal conductance (gₛ)** and **pollutant concentration gradient**.

3. **Microclimatic Modification**
   - Transpiration cools ambient air → reduces formation of ground-level ozone.
   - Increased humidity and reduced wind speed → better particulate settling.

4. **Bioindication / Biomonitoring**
   - Sensitive species show visible injury at low pollutant loads.
   - Used as **passive samplers** to map spatial pollution patterns.

5. **Phytoremediation (Aquatic & Terrestrial)**
   - Plant roots absorb dissolved contaminants (heavy metals, nutrients, organics) and either:
     - Accumulate them in harvestable biomass (phytoextraction)
     - Volatilize them into the atmosphere (phytovolatilization)
     - Stabilize them in root zone (phytostabilization)
     - Break them down via root-associated microbes (rhizodegradation)

### 2.2 Why and How of Each Mechanism

- **Why do trees filter PM better than grass?**
  Trees have multi-layered canopy and greater height → larger interception volume, higher impaction efficiency, and the air must pass through multiple barriers.
- **Why is stomatal uptake species-specific?**
  Stomatal density, size, and aperture regulation differ. Some species (e.g., conifers) keep stomata open even in winter → year-round absorption.
- **Why is vegetation effective for water quality?**
  Root zone harbours a **rhizosphere microbial biofilm** capable of degrading organics, nitrifying ammonia, and immobilizing metals.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used:** $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value. To avoid markdown breakage, all absolute-value bars in tables are written as `\vert`.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Leaf Area Index | $\text{LAI} = \dfrac{A_{\text{leaf}}}{A_{\text{ground}}}$ | dimensionless | Quantifies canopy density |
| 2 | Dust Deposition Flux | $F_d = v_d \cdot C$ | $F_d$ : µg/m²/s, $v_d$ : m/s (deposition velocity), $C$ : µg/m³ | Estimate particulate removal by canopy |
| 3 | Canopy Particulate Capture Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - e^{-k \cdot \text{LAI}}$ | $\eta$ : efficiency (0–1), $k$ : empirical constant (≈ 0.5–0.7) | Design of green belt width |
| 4 | Stomatal Pollutant Flux | $F_s = g_s \cdot \Delta C$ | $F_s$ : nmol/m²/s, $g_s$ : mol/m²/s, $\Delta C$ : µmol/mol | Estimate gaseous absorption |
| 5 | Green Belt Attenuation Index | $\text{GBAI} = \dfrac{20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_x}\right)}{W}$ | $C_0$ : upwind, $C_x$ : downwind, $W$ : belt width (m) | Design barrier width |
| 6 | Buffer Strip Nutrient Removal (USDA model) | $C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \cdot e^{-k_b t}$ | $k_b$ : 1/yr, $t$ : residence time (yr) | Riparian buffer design |
| 7 | Noise Attenuation by Vegetation | $A_n = 0.2 \cdot W \cdot f^{0.5}$ | $A_n$ : dB, $W$ : m, $f$ : Hz (0.5–2 kHz band) | Acoustic barrier design |
| 8 | Phytoremediation Metal Uptake | $M_{\text{uptake}} = C_{\text{plant}} \cdot Y_{\text{biomass}}$ | $C$ : mg/kg, $Y$ : kg/ha/yr | Estimate phytoextraction time |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Green Belts:** Mandated by Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and State Pollution Control Boards in India around hazardous-waste facilities, thermal power plants, and major highways.
- **Constructed Wetlands:** Used for treating municipal sewage, landfill leachate, and acid mine drainage.
- **Urban Air Quality Management:** Part of National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) strategies in Indian smart-city projects.
- **Mine Site Reclamation:** Revegetation is the final step in closure of open-cast mines to prevent wind-blown dust and acid drainage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Canopy Particulate Capture Efficiency

The fraction of airborne particulates removed as polluted air passes through a green belt of width $W$ is given by an exponential decay model. Let:

- $C_0$ = upwind pollutant concentration (µg/m³)
- $C_x$ = downwind concentration after traversing width $x$ (m)
- $k$ = empirical attenuation coefficient (1/m), depends on LAI and species

The differential mass balance on a thin slice of the belt of thickness $dx$ gives:

$$dC = -k \cdot C \cdot dx$$

Explanation: *The decrease in concentration $dC$ over an infinitesimal width $dx$ is proportional to the local concentration $C$ and the attenuation coefficient $k$.*

This is a first-order linear ODE. Separating variables:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dx$$

Integrating from $x=0$ to $x=W$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_x} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{W} dx$$

$$\ln C_x - \ln C_0 = -kW$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{C_x}{C_0} \right) = -kW$$

Exponentiating:

$$C_x = C_0 \cdot e^{-kW}$$

**Capture efficiency** of the belt:

$$\eta = \frac{C_0 - C_x}{C_0} = 1 - e^{-kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a board-favourite expression. A common KTU question is: *"Calculate the required width of a green belt to achieve 80 % PM₁₀ reduction, given $k = 0.05$ /m."*

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Given $k = 0.05$ /m, target $\eta = 0.80$:

$$0.80 = 1 - e^{-0.05W}$$

$$e^{-0.05W} = 0.20$$

Taking natural log:

$$-0.05W = \ln(0.20) = -1.6094$$

$$W = \frac{1.6094}{0.05} = 32.19 \text{ m}$$

Rounded up for safety, a **33 m wide green belt** is required.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stomatal Flux of Gaseous Pollutant (Big-Leaf Model)

Treating the canopy as one giant "big leaf", the steady-state flux $F_s$ of a gaseous pollutant (e.g., SO₂) from atmosphere into the leaf interior is:

$$F_s = g_s \cdot (C_a - C_i)$$

where:
- $g_s$ = bulk stomatal conductance of canopy (mol/m²/s)
- $C_a$ = ambient pollutant concentration (µmol/mol)
- $C_i$ = intercellular (sub-stomatal) pollutant concentration (µmol/mol)

At low ambient concentrations, $C_i \approx 0$ (pollutant is rapidly consumed by mesophyll reaction):

$$F_s = g_s \cdot C_a$$

For a canopy of LAI $L$, the total plant surface area = $L \cdot A$ (where $A$ is ground area). Total absorption rate:

$$F_{\text{total}} = g_s \cdot C_a \cdot L \cdot A$$

**Numerical Example:**
- $g_s = 0.2$ mol/m²/s
- $C_a = 50$ µg/m³ SO₂
- $L = 5$
- $A = 1000$ m²

Molecular weight of SO₂ = 64 g/mol. Convert $C_a$ to µmol/mol:

$$C_a = \frac{50 \, \mu g/m^3 \times 24.45}{64 \, g/mol \times 1000} = 0.0191 \, \mu mol/mol$$

(Note: 24.45 is molar volume of air at 25 °C, 1 atm.)

Now compute:

$$F_s = 0.2 \times 0.0191 = 3.82 \times 10^{-3} \, \mu mol/m^2/s$$

Total absorption over the canopy:

$$F_{\text{total}} = 3.82 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 1000 = 19.1 \, \mu mol/s$$

Convert to mass per hour:

$$19.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 64 \times 3600 = 4.40 \, g/hr$$

> **Interpretation:** A modest 1000 m² patch of vegetation with LAI 5 can absorb approximately **4.4 g of SO₂ per hour** — a non-trivial contribution to local air quality.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Riparian Buffer Strip Nutrient Removal (Klaus Model / Kadlec & Knight Extension)

For a vegetated buffer of length $L$ (m) receiving runoff with inlet concentration $C_{\text{in}}$ (mg/L) of a pollutant (e.g., nitrate, BOD), assuming plug-flow and first-order decay:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = -k_b \cdot C$$

Integrating over length $L$:

$$C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \cdot e^{-k_b L}$$

**Residence time approach (more accurate for overland flow):**

$$t = \frac{L}{v}$$

where $v$ is overland flow velocity (m/s). Then:

$$C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \cdot e^{-k_b t}$$

**Worked Example:**
- $C_{\text{in}}$ (NO₃⁻) = 10 mg/L
- $k_b = 12$ /yr (typical for established riparian forest)
- $L = 30$ m, $v = 0.005$ m/s (overland)

Residence time:

$$t = \frac{30}{0.005} = 6000 \text{ s} = \frac{6000}{3.154 \times 10^7} \, yr = 1.90 \times 10^{-4} \, yr$$

Compute outlet concentration:

$$C_{\text{out}} = 10 \cdot e^{-12 \times 1.90 \times 10^{-4}} = 10 \cdot e^{-2.28 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Using Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$C_{\text{out}} \approx 10 \cdot (1 - 0.00228) = 9.977 \text{ mg/L}$$

> **Interpretation:** For overland flow, **30 m is too short**. Engineers use deeper sub-surface flow or meandering channels to extend $t$. If $v = 0.0005$ m/s (typical subsurface flow), then $t = 60{,}000$ s ≈ 0.0019 yr, and:

$$C_{\text{out}} = 10 \cdot e^{-12 \times 0.0019} = 10 \cdot e^{-0.0228} = 9.77 \text{ mg/L}$$

This shows a **2.3 % nitrate removal** — still modest. To get **50 % removal**, required residence time:

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_b} = \frac{0.693}{12} = 0.0578 \, yr = 1.82 \times 10^6 \, s$$

At $v = 0.0005$ m/s, required buffer length:

$$L = v \cdot t = 0.0005 \times 1.82 \times 10^6 = 910 \, m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why KTU examiners often ask: *"Why are riparian buffers typically 10–30 m wide when calculations suggest hundreds of metres?"* **Answer:** They rely on *multiple mechanisms simultaneously* — overland, shallow subsurface, and deep groundwater flow paths — each with its own $k_b$ and $v$, so the *effective* $L$ is much shorter.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Vegetation Strategy to Use?

| Pollution Problem | Dominant Mechanism | Recommended Vegetation | Required Width / Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roadside dust | Particulate interception | Tall trees with dense canopy + shrubs (multi-tier) | 20–50 m |
| Industrial SO₂ plume | Stomatal absorption | Broadleaf species with high gₛ (e.g., Ficus, Neem) | 50–100 m |
| Urban O₃ smog | Stomatal absorption + microclimate | Mixed urban forest with high transpiration (e.g., Eucalyptus) | Citywide (10–30 % canopy cover) |
| Acid mine drainage | Phytostabilization + rhizofiltration | Wetland species (Typha, Phragmites) | Constructed wetland 0.5–2 ha |
| Agricultural nutrient runoff | Denitrification in root zone | Riparian forest (Alder, Willow) | 30–100 m |
| Heavy metal contamination | Phytoextraction | Hyperaccumulators (e.g., Pteris vittata for As, Sunflower for Pb) | Site-specific |
| Noise pollution | Acoustic scattering | Dense evergreen conifers | 30 m for ~5 dB reduction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Phytoremediation Pathways (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid

    B -->|Heavy Metals| C1[Phytoextraction]:::phyto

    B -->|Radioactive Isotopes| C3[Phytovolatilization]:::phyto
    B -->|Loose Tailings| C4[Phytostabilization]:::phyto
    B -->|Surface Water Metals| C5[Rhizofiltration]:::phyto
    C1 --> D1[Hyperaccumulator Uproot and Incinerate]:::out
    C2 --> D2[Microbial Breakdown to CO2 and H2O]:::out
    C3 --> D3[Volatile Release via Stomata]:::out
    C4 --> D4[Root Mat Locks Contaminant in Place]:::out
```
