# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
> - Forgetting to **convert % purity** in commercial chemical doses (lime, alum) — losing 2 marks.
> - Mixing up **molecular weights** (anhydrous vs hydrated alum: 342 vs 594) — losing 1 mark.
> - Omitting units in $q_m$ (mg/g) and $K_L$ (L/mg) — losing 1 mark in derivations.
> - Using $C_0$ in Langmuir equation where the equation explicitly requires $C_e$ — instant zero for the isotherm evaluation step.
> - Skipping the **alkalinity check** in coagulation problems — guaranteed deduction.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the merits and demerits of Poly-Aluminum Chloride (PAC) over conventional alum as a coagulant in water treatment.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Merits** | **Demerits** |
|:---|:---|
| Wider effective pH range (5.0 – 9.0) | Higher unit cost (~1.5–2× alum) |
| Lower alkalinity consumption (saves lime) | Limited shelf-life of polymeric species |
| Produces denser, faster-settling floc | Slightly less effective for high-turbidity raw water |
| Reduced sludge volume (~30 % less) | — |
| Effective at lower temperatures | — |

(3 marks: 1 mark for ≥3 merits, 1 for ≥1 demerit, 1 for clarity/structure)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "adsorption isotherm" and distinguish between the Langmuir and Freundlich models.*

**Model Answer:**

An **adsorption isotherm** is the equilibrium relationship, at a constant temperature, between the mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent ($q_e$, mg/g) and the equilibrium concentration of solute remaining in the fluid phase ($C_e$, mg/L).

| **Feature** | **Langmuir** | **Freundlich** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Adsorption type | Monolayer | Multilayer |
| Surface | Homogeneous, energetically uniform | Heterogeneous |
| Saturation | Yes, finite $q_m$ | No saturation — $q_e \to \infty$ as $C_e \to \infty$ |
| Equation | $q_e = q_m K_L C_e / (1 + K_L C_e)$ | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ |
| Best fit region | Low to mid $C_e$ | Low $C_e$ |
| Parameters with meaning | $q_m$ (capacity), $K_L$ (affinity) | $K_F$ (capacity), $1/n$ (intensity) |

(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 differences)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** *Derive the linearized Langmuir isotherm and explain the physical significance of the constants $q_m$ and $K_L$. A batch adsorption study on 50 mL of dye solution (initial concentration 150 mg/L) with 0.025 g of activated carbon gave an equilibrium concentration of 25 mg/L. Calculate $q_e$ and the percentage removal.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A rapid sand filter is to be designed with sand of effective size $D_{10} = 0.55\ \text{mm}$ and uniformity coefficient $UC = 1.45$. The bed has a depth of 0.6 m and area 12 m². The filter is backwashed at 20 °C where the kinematic viscosity of water is $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the minimum fluidization velocity using the Ergun laminar form, assuming sphericity $\phi_s = 0.85$ and bed porosity $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$. Take $\rho_p = 2650\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (sand) and $\rho_f = 998\ \text{kg/m}^3$.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Derivation of Linearized Langmuir Isotherm (3 marks):**

The Langmuir equation:

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

Invert both sides:

$$\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1 + K_L C_e}{q_m K_L C_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L C_e} + \frac{1}{q_m}$$

Multiply throughout by $C_e$:

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m}$$

A plot of $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ (y-axis) versus $C_e$ (x-axis) yields:
- **slope** = $1/q_m$
- **intercept** = $1/(q_m K_L)$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
- $q_m$ (mg/g): the **maximum monolayer adsorption capacity** — the saturation loading of the surface once all sites are occupied.
- $K_L$ (L/mg): the **Langmuir affinity constant** — higher $K_L$ implies stronger binding between solute and adsorbent.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

$$q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(150 - 25) \times 0.050}{0.025} = \frac{125 \times 0.050}{0.025} = \frac{6.25}{0.025} = 250\ \text{mg/g}$$

$$\% \text{ Removal} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{125}{150} \times 100 = 83.3\ \text{%}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Convert diameter to SI:** $d_p = 0.55\ \text{mm} = 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the laminar Ergun correlation for $U_{mf}$:**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{(\phi_s d_p)^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{150\,\mu} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{mf}^{\,3}}{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic viscosity:**

$$\mu = \rho_f \nu = 998 \times 1.004 \times 10^{-6} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numbers:**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{(0.85 \times 5.5\times10^{-4})^2 (2650 - 998)(9.81)}{150 \times 1.002\times10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{(0.42)^3}{1 - 0.42}$$

Compute piecewise:

$$(\phi_s d_p)^2 = (4.675\times10^{-4})^2 = 2.186 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,g = 1652 \times 9.81 = 16{,}206\ \text{N/m}^3$$

Numerator first term:

$$2.186\times10^{-7} \times 16{,}206 = 3.543 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator first term:

$$150 \times 1.002\times10^{-3} = 0.1503$$

First factor:

$$\frac{3.543 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1503} = 0.02357\ \text{m/s}$$

Porosity factor:

$$\frac{0.42^3}{0.58} = \frac{0.0741}{0.58} = 0.1277$$

Multiply:

$$U_{mf} = 0.02357 \times 0.1277 = 3.01 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s} \approx 3.0\ \text{mm/s}$$

> **Engineering check:** Typical rapid-sand backwash velocities are 36 – 60 m/h = 10 – 17 mm/s. Since $U_{mf} = 3\ \text{mm/s}$, the design backwash velocity provides a safety factor of ~3–5× — acceptable.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** *Explain the chemistry of chlorination with reference to the formation of hypochlorous acid and hypochlorite ion. A water treatment plant treats 25,000 m³/day of raw water with chlorine. The chlorine dose required is 3.5 mg/L and the plant maintains a free chlorine residual of 0.6 mg/L. If the cost of chlorine is ₹35 per kg, calculate the daily cost of chlorination.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 100 MWe thermal power plant burns coal containing 1.4 % sulphur by mass. Coal consumption is 0.6 kg/kWh. The flue gas is treated in a wet limestone FGD system with 90 % $\text{SO}_2$ removal efficiency. Calculate:* **(7 marks)**
- *(i) The daily mass of $\text{SO}_2$ generated,*
- *(ii) The daily mass of limestone (CaCO₃, 95 % pure) required for complete capture of the removed $\text{SO}_2$.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Chemistry of Chlorination (3 marks):**

When chlorine gas is dissolved in water it undergoes disproportionation:

$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$

$$\text{HOCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^- \quad (\text{pKa} = 7.5)$$

- **HOCl (hypochlorous acid):** undissociated, electrically neutral, ~80× more effective as a biocide than $\text{OCl}^-$.
- **OCl⁻ (hypochlorite ion):** the dominant species above pH 7.5; less effective.

**Total chlorine dose = chlorine demand + chlorine residual** (1 mark):

$$D_{\text{total}} = 3.5\ \text{mg/L}\ \text{applied} = \text{demand} + 0.6\ \text{mg/L residual} \Rightarrow \text{demand} = 2.9\ \text{mg/L}$$

**Daily Cost (3 marks):**

$$\text{Daily chlorine mass} = Q \times D = 25{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 3.5\ \text{g/m}^3 = 87{,}500\ \text{g/day} = 87.5\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\text{Cost} = 87.5 \times ₹35 = ₹3{,}062.5 \approx ₹3{,}063/\text{day}$$

Annualized: $3{,}063 \times 365 = ₹11.18\ \text{lakh/yr}$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step (i) — Daily $\text{SO}_2$ generation (3 marks):**

Daily energy output:

$$E = 100 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \times 24\ \text{h} = 2.4 \times 10^9\ \text{Wh} = 2.4 \times 10^6\ \text{kWh}$$

Daily coal burnt:

$$m_{\text{coal}} = 2.4 \times 10^6\ \text{kWh} \times 0.6\ \text{kg/kWh} = 1.44 \times 10^6\ \text{kg/day}$$

Sulphur in coal:

$$m_S = 0.014 \times 1.44 \times 10^6 = 20{,}160\ \text{kg S/day}$$

Assuming all S is converted to $\text{SO}_2$ ($M_S = 32$, $M_{SO_2} = 64$):

$$m_{SO_2} = m_S \times \frac{64}{32} = 20{,}160 \times 2 = 40{,}320\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step (ii) — Limestone feed for capture (4 marks):**

$\text{SO}_2$ removed (90% efficiency):

$$m_{SO_2,\text{removed}} = 0.90 \times 40{,}320 = 36{,}288\ \text{kg/day}$$

FGD reaction: $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$

Stoichiometry: 1 mol $\text{SO}_2$ requires 1 mol $\text{CaCO}_3$. Molecular weights: $\text{SO}_2 = 64$, $\text{CaCO}_3 = 100$.

Theoretical limestone:

$$m_{CaCO_3,\text{theor}} = 36{,}288 \times \frac{100}{64} = 56{,}700\ \text{kg/day}$$

Applying 1.10 stoichiometric excess (typical):

$$m_{CaCO_3,\text{actual}} = 1.10 \times 56{,}700 = 62{,}370\ \text{kg/day pure CaCO}_3$$

Accounting for 95% purity:

$$m_{\text{limestone,commercial}} = \frac{62{,}370}{0.95} = 65{,}653\ \text{kg/day} \approx 65.7\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU follow-up:** Byproduct gypsum $\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ($M = 172$ g/mol):
> $$m_{\text{gypsum}} = 36{,}288 \times \frac{172}{64} = 97{,}524\ \text{kg/day} \approx 97.5\ \text{tonnes/day}$$
> — a saleable commodity for cement manufacturers (offsets FGD cost by ~₹800–1000/tonne in 2024 Kerala market).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Materials**

- **Coagulants:** Alum, PAC, ferric chloride, lime. Always check **alkalinity** (rule of thumb: 0.45 mg/L as CaCO₃ per 1 mg/L alum). Use Schulze–Hardy rule: trivalent ≫ divalent ≫ monovalent.
- **Flocculants:** Long-chain polyelectrolytes (anionic polyacrylamides) work via **bridging**; cationic variants neutralize negative colloids.
- **Adsorbents:** GAC with $S_{BET} = 500 - 1500\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$. Use **Freundlich** for low-concentration, multi-component; **Langmuir** for monolayer saturation analysis. Always validate with $0 < R_L < 1$.
- **Filtration Media:** $D_{10}$ (effective size) and $UC = D_{60}/D_{10}$. Multi-media uses anthracite (top, light) + sand (middle) + garnet (bottom, dense).
- **Membranes:** MF (50–500 nm) → UF (1–50 nm) → NF (0.5–2 nm) → RO (< 0.5 nm). TMP increases sharply through the spectrum (2 → 80 bar).
- **Ion Exchange:** SAC resins exchange $\text{H}^+$; SBA resins exchange $\text{OH}^-$. Regenerate with brine (cation) or NaOH (anion).
- **Disinfection:** $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$; pH controls HOCl/OCl⁻ ratio (pKa 7.5). CT concept for design.
- **Air-Quality Materials:** Lime slurry for $\text{SO}_2$ ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ : $\text{SO}_2$ = 74 : 64); $\text{V}_2\text{O}_5$/$\text{TiO}_2$ SCR catalyst for $\text{NO}_x$ at 300 – 400 °C; GAC for VOC off-gases.
- **Always** state units, purity corrections, and **stoichiometric excess** factors (typically 5 – 15 %).
- **Sanity check** every result: $q_m$ must be a positive finite number; $U_{mf}$ must be in mm/s; $R_L$ must be in (0, 1) for favorable isotherms.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics:** PAC vs alum comparison; GAC bed-life for micropollutants; lime vs limestone for FGD economics; SCR catalyst poisoning by arsenic in coal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Environmental Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Environmental Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Global Environmental Issues** are defined as trans-boundary, large-scale environmental problems whose origins, impacts, and consequences extend beyond a single nation or ecosystem, ultimately threatening the planetary life-support system. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET861), these issues encompass a cluster of interrelated phenomena—**Global Warming**, **Ozone Layer Depletion**, **Acid Rain**, **Climate Change**, **Loss of Biodiversity**, and **Sustainable Development Challenges**—that are driven predominantly by anthropogenic (human-induced) activities interacting with natural Earth-system processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET861 – Module 4):**
> The module emphasizes the atmospheric, hydrospheric, and lithospheric inter-linkages of environmental degradation. Students must be able to **identify, classify, and quantitatively assess** the major global environmental issues using standard parameters (GWP, pH, AQI, CO₂-equivalent concentrations, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Earth as a Living Body" Analogy**
> Imagine the Earth as a single, giant living organism floating in the cold vacuum of space. Its **atmosphere** is the "skin" that regulates temperature (like a sweater), the **oceans** are the "bloodstream" transporting heat and chemicals, and the **forests** are the "lungs" exchanging gases. When we burn fossil fuels, we are essentially "fevering" the planet (global warming); when we release CFCs, we are punching "holes" in the protective ozone "skin"; and when we emit SO₂ and NOₓ, we are "acidifying the bloodstream" (acid rain). Every action in one organ affects the entire body.

### 1.3 Classification of Major Global Environmental Issues

| Category | Issue | Primary Pollutant(s) | Core Metric |
|----------|-------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Atmospheric** | Global Warming | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs | **GWP** (Global Warming Potential) |
| **Atmospheric** | Ozone Depletion | CFCs, Halons, HCFCs | **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** |
| **Atmospheric/Hydrospheric** | Acid Rain | SO₂, NOₓ | **pH** (< 5.6 considered acidic) |
| **Climatic** | Climate Change | All GHGs | **°C rise** above pre-industrial baseline |
| **Ecological** | Biodiversity Loss | Habitat destruction, pollution | **Species extinction rate** (E/MSY) |
| **Cross-cutting** | Sustainable Development | Resource over-extraction | **Ecological Footprint** (gha) |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Essentials)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants and Standard References Students MUST memorize:**
> - **GWP of CO₂ = 1** (Reference gas)
> - **GWP of CH₄ = 28–34** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR5/AR6)
> - **GWP of N₂O = 265–298**
> - **ODP of CFC-11 = 1.0** (Reference substance)
> - **ODP of CFC-12 = 1.0**
> - **Natural pH of unpolluted rainwater = 5.6** (formed by dissolving CO₂)
> - **Pre-industrial CO₂ level ≈ 280 ppm** (year ~1750)
> - **Current (2024) CO₂ level ≈ 421 ppm** (Mauna Loa Observatory)
> - **Ambient air quality standard for SO₂ (India, CPCB 24-hr) = 80 µg/m³**
> - **Ambient air quality standard for NO₂ (India, CPCB 24-hr) = 80 µg/m³**
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Greenhouse Effect – Step-by-Step Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **The Greenhouse Effect is NOT inherently bad.** Without it, Earth's mean surface temperature would be approximately **−18 °C** instead of the current **+15 °C**. The problem arises from an **enhanced** greenhouse effect caused by human emissions.

The mechanism follows a strict radiative balance:

1. **Incoming Short-Wave Radiation (SW):** Solar radiation (λ ≈ 0.3–3 µm) penetrates the atmosphere, with about **51%** reaching the Earth's surface.
2. **Surface Absorption & Re-emission:** The Earth's surface absorbs SW and re-emits energy as **long-wave (infrared) radiation** (λ ≈ 3–100 µm) per the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**.
3. **Selective Absorption by GHGs:** Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O vapor, O₃, CFCs) possess vibrational-rotational energy states that absorb IR radiation at specific wavelengths.
4. **Re-radiation to Space and Back:** The GHGs re-emit IR radiation in **all directions**—some goes to space, but a significant portion returns to Earth, providing **+33 °C of warming**.
5. **Radiative Forcing Imbalance:** Anthropogenic emissions increase GHG concentrations, trapping more IR radiation and creating a net positive **radiative forcing (ΔF)**.

### 2.2 Global Warming Potential (GWP) – The Comparative Index

**GWP** is a relative index measuring the **time-integrated radiative forcing** of 1 kg of a given GHG relative to 1 kg of CO₂ over a specified time horizon (typically 100 years).

$$\text{GWP}_i = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_i \cdot [c_i(t)]\, dt}{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot [c_{CO_2}(t)]\, dt}$$

Where:
- $a_i$ = instantaneous radiative forcing per unit mass of gas $i$ (W m⁻² kg⁻¹)
- $c_i(t)$ = atmospheric decay function of gas $i$ at time $t$
- $TH$ = time horizon (20, 100, or 500 years)
- $c_{CO_2}(t)$ = background CO₂ decay function

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It contains 90% of the numerical questions asked in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Use | Standard Units |
|---|-------------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{GWP} = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_i c_i(t)\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} c_{CO_2}(t)\,dt}$ | Compare warming impact of GHGs | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{CO}_2\text{-eq} = m_i \times \text{GWP}_i$ | Convert mass of any GHG to CO₂ equivalent | kg CO₂-eq |
| 3 | $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (blackbody radiation) | W/m² |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's Displacement Law (peak emission wavelength) | m·K |
| 5 | $E = h c / \lambda$ | Photon energy (ozone photolysis) | J/photon |
| 6 | $\text{[H}^+\text{]} = 10^{-\text{pH}}$ | Hydrogen ion concentration from pH | mol/L |
| 7 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Definition of pH | dimensionless |
| 8 | $2 \text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ | Acid rain (sulfuric) formation | — |
| 9 | $4 \text{NO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3$ | Acid rain (nitric) formation | — |
| 10 | $\text{ODP} = \dfrac{\text{Steady-state ozone loss by gas }i}{\text{Steady-state ozone loss by CFC-11}}$ | Relative ozone depletion | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right)$ | Radiative forcing due to CO₂ change | W/m² |
| 12 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by a mass of water/surface | J |
| 13 | $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | Absorbance (Beer-Lambert) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Markdown Table Note:** All absolute value bars (`|x|`) and set notations have been replaced with `\vert` or `$\vert$` in the rows above to prevent breaking the KTU-rendered markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carbon Trading & Carbon Credits:** The CO₂-equivalent concept underpins the **EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS)**, **Article 6 of the Paris Agreement**, and **India's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme**. Every 1 tonne of CO₂-eq carries a market value (₹800–2500 in 2024 Indian markets).
- **Refrigerant Selection:** HVAC engineers use **GWP + ODP dual-criteria** to choose refrigerants—HFC-134a (GWP = 1430) is being phased out in favor of HFO-1234yf (GWP < 1) under the **Kigali Amendment**.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Mandatory in India under the **EIA Notification, 2006** (amended 2020) for projects exceeding ₹50 crore capital cost.
- **Urban Air Quality Management:** CPCB's **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP, 2019)** targets a 40% reduction in PM₂.₅/PM₁₀ concentrations by 2025–26 across 131 non-attainment cities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CO₂-Equivalent Mass Conversion (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A thermal power plant emits 1,500 tonnes of CH₄ and 800 tonnes of N₂O in one year. Calculate the total CO₂-equivalent emissions using GWP₁₀₀ values of 28 and 265 respectively.

**Step 1 — Recall the governing equation:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_i = m_i \times \text{GWP}_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CO₂-equivalent of CH₄ emissions:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{CH_4} = 1{,}500 \text{ tonnes} \times 28$$

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{CH_4} = 42{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CO₂-equivalent of N₂O emissions:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{N_2O} = 800 \text{ tonnes} \times 265$$

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{N_2O} = 212{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain total CO₂-equivalent emissions:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{\text{Total}} = 42{,}000 + 212{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{\text{Total}} = 254{,}000 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the mass of CH₄ (1500 t) is nearly twice that of N₂O (800 t), N₂O contributes **~5× more warming impact** because of its much higher GWP. This is why **mass-based emission control** is *insufficient*—**molecular potency** must be accounted for.

### 3.2 Derivation: Radiative Forcing from CO₂ Concentration Change

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from a pre-industrial concentration of 280 ppm to 421 ppm. Calculate the resulting radiative forcing (ΔF) at the tropopause.

**Step 1 — Apply the simplified Myhre et al. (1998) formula (used by IPCC):**

$$\Delta F = 5.35 \times \ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\Delta F = 5.35 \times \ln\left(\frac{421}{280}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(1.5036) = 0.4079$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the coefficient:**

$$\Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.4079$$

$$\boxed{\Delta F = 2.182 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The total anthropogenic radiative forcing from CO₂ alone is approximately **+2.18 W/m²**. When combined with other GHGs (CH₄, N₂O, halocarbons) and minus aerosol cooling, the **net** radiative forcing is approximately **+2.72 W/m²** (IPCC AR6, 2021).

### 3.3 Derivation: Acid Rain pH from Sulfuric Acid Concentration

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 1 L rainwater sample contains 5.0 mg of H₂SO₄ (assume complete dissociation). Calculate the pH of the rainwater and state whether it qualifies as acid rain.

**Step 1 — Molar mass of H₂SO₄:**

$$M_{H_2SO_4} = 2(1) + 32 + 4(16) = 98 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of H₂SO₄ in 1 L:**

$$n = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}}{98 \text{ g/mol}} = 5.102 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Complete dissociation releases 2 H⁺ per H₂SO₄ molecule:**

$$[\text{H}^+] = 2 \times 5.102 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0204 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pH:**

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}(1.0204 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\text{pH} = 4 - \log_{10}(1.0204)$$

$$\text{pH} = 4 - 0.0087$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = 3.99 \approx 4.0}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** Since **pH = 4.0 < 5.6**, this rainwater is definitively classified as **acid rain**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major Global Environmental Issues

| Parameter | Global Warming | Ozone Depletion | Acid Rain | Biodiversity Loss |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Primary Cause** | Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation | CFCs, halons, HCFCs | SO₂, NOₓ emissions | Habitat destruction, pollution |
| **Indicator Metric** | GWP, ppm CO₂-eq | ODP, Dobson Units (DU) | pH, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻ mg/L | E/MSY, Red List Index |
| **Worst Affected Region** | Arctic, Small Island States | Antarctica (spring), Australia | Northern Europe, NE USA, China | Tropical rainforests, coral reefs |
| **Time Scale of Impact** | Decades to centuries | Decades to ~100 years | Hours to years | Centuries to millennia |
| **Recovery Time** | ~1000+ years (CO₂) | ~50 years (after CFC ban) | ~Days to months (rainfall) | Extinction = irreversible |
| **Key International Treaty** | UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement | Montreal Protocol (1987) | UNECE LRTAP Convention | CBD (Rio 1992), CITES |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Greenhouse Effect Mechanism (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUN Emits Shortwave Radiation] --> B[50 Percent Reaches Earth Surface]
    A --> C[20 Percent Absorbed by Atmosphere]
    A --> D[30 Percent Reflected by Clouds and Surface]
    B --> E[Earth Surface Absorbs SW Energy]
    E --> F[Surface Emits Longwave IR Radiation]
    F --> G[Greenhouse Gases Absorb IR]
    G --> H[GHGs Reemit IR Toward Space]
    G --> I[GHGs Reemit IR Back to Earth]
    I --> J[Net Warming of Earth Surface]
    H --> K[Radiative Balance Maintained]
    L[Anthropogenic GHG Emissions] --> M[Increased GHG Concentration]
    M --> G
    M --> N[Enhanced Greenhouse Effect]
    N --> J
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Stratospheric Ozone Depletion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CFC 11 Released at Ground] --> B1[Rises to Stratosphere]
    B1 --> C1[UV Photolysis Breaks CFC Bond]
    C1 --> D1[Releases Chlorine Radical Cl]
    D1 --> E1[Cl Reacts with O3 to form ClO and O2]
    E1 --> F1[ClO Reacts with Free O Atom]
    F1 --> G1[Reforms Cl Radical Chain Carrier]
    G1 --> E1
    H1[Polar Stratospheric Clouds PSCs] --> I1[Provide Surface for Heterogeneous Reactions]
    I1 --> E1
    J1[Spring Sunlight Returns] --> K1[Massive Ozone Destruction Begins]
    K1 --> L1[Ozone Hole Forms over Antarctica]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Acid Rain Formation and Dispersal Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Fossil Fuel Combustion] --> B2[Emits SO2 and NOX]
    B2 --> C2[Atmospheric Transport by Winds]
    C2 --> D2[Oxidation to SO3 and NO2]
    D2 --> E2[Reaction with Atmospheric Water]
    E2 --> F2[Forms H2SO4 and HNO3 Droplets]
    F2 --> G2[Wet Deposition Acid Rain]
    F2 --> H2[Dry Deposition Acidic Particulates]
    G2 --> I2[Soil and Water Body Acidification]
    I2 --> J2[Leaching of Aluminum and Heavy Metals]
    J2 --> K2[Damage to Aquatic Life and Forests]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Block Architecture of Carbon Cycle and Global Warming Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Atmospheric CO2 421 ppm] --> B3[Photosynthesis by Plants]
    A3 --> C3[Ocean Absorption]
    A3 --> D3[Anthropogenic Emissions 36 Gt yr]
    D3 --> E3[Fossil Fuel Combustion]
    D3 --> F3[Deforestation and Land Use]
    B3 --> G3[Biomass Carbon Sink]
    C3 --> H3[Marine Carbon Sink]
    G3 --> I3[Respiration and Decomposition]
    H3 --> J3[Ocean Outgassing]
    I3 --> A3
    J3 --> A3
    K3[Rising Temperature] --> L3[Reduced Solubility of CO2 in Oceans]
    L3 --> M3[Positive Feedback Loop]
    M3 --> A3
    K3 --> N3[Permafrost Thaw Releases CH4]
    N3 --> A3
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5: Hierarchy of International Environmental Agreements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Stockholm 1972 UNCHE] --> B4[Rio Earth Summit 1992]
    A4 --> C4[Montreal Protocol 1987]
    A4 --> D4[Kyoto Protocol 1997]
    B4 --> E4[UNFCCC]
    B4 --> F4[Convention on Biological Diversity]
    B4 --> G4[Agenda 21]
    E4 --> H4[Paris Agreement 2015]
    E4 --> I4[Copenhagen Accord 2009]
    H4 --> J4[Nationally Determined Contributions NDCs]
    H4 --> K4[Article 6 Carbon Markets]
    C4 --> L4[Kigali Amendment 2016 HFC Phase Down]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `C3`), strictly avoiding reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels contain plain uppercase text without markdown formatting tags to ensure clean rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Global Warming Potential, (ii) Ozone Depletion Potential, (iii) Acid Rain.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Global Warming Potential (GWP):** GWP is a relative measure of how much heat a greenhouse gas traps in the atmosphere over a chosen time horizon (typically 100 years), compared to an equal mass of carbon dioxide. GWP of CO₂ = 1 (reference). Example: Methane (CH₄) has a GWP₁₀₀ of 28, meaning 1 kg of CH₄ traps 28 times more heat than 1 kg of CO₂.

**(ii) Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):** ODP is a measure of the destructive effect of a substance on the stratospheric ozone layer relative to CFC-11. ODP of CFC-11 = 1.0 (reference). Example: HCFC-22 has an ODP of 0.055, making it less harmful than CFC-11.

**(iii) Acid Rain:** Acid rain is any form of precipitation (rain, snow, fog) with a pH below 5.6, caused by atmospheric reactions of sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides emitted from fossil fuel combustion and vehicle exhausts. Example: Industrial regions like the Ruhr Valley (Germany) historically recorded rainwater pH as low as 2.5.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the major greenhouse gases. Explain the role of water vapor in the greenhouse effect.**

**Model Answer:**

The major greenhouse gases (in order of abundance and importance):

1. **Water Vapor (H₂O)** — most abundant, but largely a feedback, not a forcing agent
2. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — 421 ppm, primary anthropogenic driver
3. **Methane (CH₄)** — 1.9 ppm, GWP₁₀₀ = 28
4. **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)** — 0.33 ppm, GWP₁₀₀ = 265
5. **Ozone (O₃)** — tropospheric and stratospheric
6. **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** — synthetic, high GWP and ODP

**Role of Water Vapor:** Water vapor is the most abundant greenhouse gas and contributes approximately **60% of the natural greenhouse effect**. It absorbs strongly in the infrared region (6–12 µm). Importantly, water vapor acts as a **positive feedback amplifier**—warmer air holds more water vapor (Clausius-Clapeyron relation: ~7% more per °C), which traps more IR, causing further warming. However, water vapor has a **short atmospheric lifetime (~9 days)** and is not directly emitted by human activities in significant quantities; it is regulated by temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-level internal choice is mandatory. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two alternatives below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **greenhouse effect** and the phenomenon of **global warming**. Discuss the role of major greenhouse gases with their GWP values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal-fired power plant emits 4,500 tonnes of CO₂, 120 tonnes of CH₄, and 35 tonnes of N₂O annually. Calculate the total CO₂-equivalent emissions using GWP₁₀₀ values of 1, 28, and 265 respectively. Comment on which gas contributes most to the global warming impact despite having the lowest mass emission. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):** The greenhouse effect is the natural process by which greenhouse gases (GHGs) in the Earth's atmosphere absorb and re-emit infrared (longwave) radiation emitted by the Earth's surface, thereby warming the lower atmosphere and surface.

**2. Step-by-Step Mechanism (3 Marks):**
- **Step 1:** Solar shortwave (SW) radiation (~0.3–3 µm) passes through the atmosphere relatively unimpeded.
- **Step 2:** The Earth's surface absorbs SW and re-emits energy as longwave (LW) infrared radiation (~3–100 µm), governed by $E = \sigma T^4$.
- **Step 3:** GHGs selectively absorb LW radiation at specific IR bands (e.g., CO₂ at 15 µm, CH₄ at 7.6 µm, N₂O at 7.8 µm and 17 µm).
- **Step 4:** GHGs re-emit IR in all directions; the downward component warms the surface ("back radiation").
- **Step 5:** The net effect maintains Earth's mean surface temperature at ~15 °C (instead of −18 °C).

**3. Global Warming (1 Mark):** Anthropogenic emissions of GHGs (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs) have increased atmospheric concentrations, **enhancing** the natural greenhouse effect and causing a net positive radiative forcing of +2.72 W/m² (IPCC AR6).

**4. Major GHGs and GWP (2 Marks):**

| Gas | Pre-industrial | Current | GWP₁₀₀ |
|-----|----------------|---------|--------|
| CO₂ | 280 ppm | 421 ppm | **1** |
| CH₄ | 700 ppb | 1,900 ppb | **28** |
| N₂O | 270 ppb | 333 ppb | **265** |
| CFC-12 | 0 | 0.5 ppt | **10,900** |

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Given:** $m_{CO_2} = 4{,}500$ t, $m_{CH_4} = 120$ t, $m_{N_2O} = 35$ t; $\text{GWP}_{CO_2} = 1$, $\text{GWP}_{CH_4} = 28$, $\text{GWP}_{N_2O} = 265$.

**Step 1 — CO₂ contribution:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{CO_2} = 4{,}500 \times 1 = 4{,}500 \text{ t CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 2 — CH₄ contribution:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{CH_4} = 120 \times 28 = 3{,}360 \text{ t CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 3 — N₂O contribution:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}_{N_2O} = 35 \times 265 = 9{,}275 \text{ t CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 4 — Total CO₂-equivalent:**

$$\text{Total} = 4{,}500 + 3{,}360 + 9{,}275 = 17{,}135 \text{ t CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 Mark):** Although N₂O has the **lowest mass emission (35 t)**, it contributes the **largest share of warming (9,275 t CO₂-eq, or 54%)** because of its very high GWP. This demonstrates that **mass-based emission control alone is inadequate**; molecular potency must be accounted for.

$$\boxed{\text{Total CO}_2\text{-eq emissions} = 17{,}135 \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4 Choice 2]`

**(a)** Describe the **chemistry and mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion**. Explain the role of CFCs, nitrogen oxides, and polar stratospheric clouds. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rainwater sample has a pH of 4.2. Calculate the hydrogen ion concentration and the equivalent concentration of sulfuric acid (assuming H₂SO₄ is the sole acid). Comment on whether this constitutes acid rain. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**1. Importance of Ozone Layer (1 Mark):** The stratospheric ozone layer (15–35 km altitude) absorbs harmful UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-C (100–280 nm) radiation, protecting all living organisms.

**2. Chapman Cycle (Natural) (1 Mark):**
- $\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2\text{O}$
- $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$
- $\text{O}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}$
- $\text{O} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{O}_2$

**3. Catalytic Destruction by CFCs (2 Marks):**
- $\text{CFCl}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{CFCl}_2 + \text{Cl}$
- $\text{Cl} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO} + \text{O}_2$
- $\text{ClO} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl} + \text{O}_2$
- **Net:** $\text{O}_3 + \text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{O}_2$ (one Cl atom destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules)

**4. Role of Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs) (2 Marks):** During polar winter, PSCs form at −78 °C. They provide surfaces for heterogeneous reactions: $\text{ClONO}_2 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + \text{HNO}_3$. The Cl₂ is photolyzed by spring sunlight, releasing atomic chlorine that initiates the catalytic cycle.

**5. Role of NOₓ (1 Mark):** NO from N₂O or supersonic aircraft: $\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$, and $\text{NO}_2 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}_2$, similarly destroying ozone catalytically.

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Given:** pH = 4.2

**Step 1 — Hydrogen ion concentration:**

$$[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-pH} = 10^{-4.2}$$

$$[\text{H}^+] = 6.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to equivalent concentration of H₂SO₄ (each molecule yields 2 H⁺):**

$$[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4] = \frac{[\text{H}^+]}{2} = \frac{6.31 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 3.155 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mg/L:**

$$[\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4]_{mg/L} = 3.155 \times 10^{-5} \times 98 \times 10^3 = 3.09 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 Mark):** Since pH = 4.2 < 5.6, the rainwater is definitively **acid rain**. It is significantly more acidic than the natural background pH of 5.6 (caused by dissolved CO₂).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2:1 stoichiometry:** H₂SO₄ yields 2 H⁺ ions. Many students write [H₂SO₄] = [H⁺], losing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit errors:** Expressing final answer in g/L instead of mg/L. KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for wrong units.
> 3. **Skipping the comparison with pH 5.6:** A "Comment" question is mandatory for full marks. Always state the threshold and conclude.
> 4. **Ignoring significant figures:** Use at least 3 significant figures in calculations.
> 5. **In GWP problems,** students often forget to **state the reference time horizon** (100-year). Always mention "GWP₁₀₀."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-day revision list — must be memorized for KTU ESE Module 4:**

- [x] **Pre-industrial CO₂ = 280 ppm**; current ≈ **421 ppm**.
- [x] **GWP₁₀₀ values:** CO₂ = **1**, CH₄ = **28**, N₂O = **265**, CFC-12 = **10,900**, SF₆ = **23,500**.
- [x] **ODP values:** CFC-11 = CFC-12 = **1.0**, HCFC-22 = **0.055**, Halon-1211 = **3.0**.
- [x] **Natural rainwater pH = 5.6**; **acid rain threshold < 5.6**.
- [x] **Key formulas:** $\text{CO}_2\text{-eq} = m \times \text{GWP}$; $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0)$; $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-\text{pH}}$.
- [x] **Dissociation stoichiometry:** H₂SO₄ → 2 H⁺; HNO₃ → 1 H⁺; H₂CO₃ → 2 H⁺.
- [x] **Five key treaties:** UNFCCC (1992), Kyoto Protocol (1997), Montreal Protocol (1987), Paris Agreement (2015), Kigali Amendment (2016).
- [x] **CFC–Ozone cycle:** One Cl radical destroys **~100,000 O₃ molecules** before being sequestered.
- [x] **Antarctic ozone hole:** First reported by **Farman, Gardiner, Shanklin (1985)** in *Nature*.
- [x] **Heat-trapping mechanism:** Re-radiation by GHGs occurs at IR bands (e.g., CO₂ at 15 µm; H₂O at 6–12 µm).
- [x] **CPCB 24-hour Indian ambient standards:** SO₂ = 80 µg/m³, NO₂ = 80 µg/m³, PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³.
- [x] **Carbon footprint of common activities:** 1 L petrol ≈ **2.3 kg CO₂-eq**; 1 kWh electricity (coal-based India) ≈ **0.95 kg CO₂-eq**; one transatlantic flight ≈ **1.5 tonnes CO₂-eq**.
- [x] **Beer's Law:** $A = \log(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c l$ — used in spectrophotometric analysis of gaseous pollutants.
- [x] **Acid rain damages:** Leaching of Al³⁺ from soils, lowering lake pH (kills fish below pH 4.5), corroding marble (Taj Mahal effect), and damaging crops.
- [x] **UN SDGs linked:** SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 14 (Life Below Water), SDG 15 (Life on Land).

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-minute exam advice from a KTU Board Examiner:** In 14-mark questions, **always** provide a **schematic diagram** (even hand-drawn) and **at least 4–5 calculation steps** with units. The KTU valuation key awards partial marks for methodology even if the final numerical answer is incorrect. For 3-mark questions, **brevity + correct technical terms = full marks**. Avoid long paragraphs; use bullet points and equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Sampling and Pollution Measurement Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Sampling and Pollution Measurement Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Sampling and Pollution Measurement Methods** refer to the standardized, scientifically rigorous techniques employed to capture representative fractions of ambient air (or stationary source emissions) and to subsequently determine the type, concentration, and physical/chemical characteristics of the gaseous and particulate pollutants contained within them.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, this domain encompasses two distinct but interlinked engineering activities:

1. **Air Sampling** – The physical collection (capture) of an air volume using calibrated instruments such as High-Volume Samplers (HVS), Low-Volume Samplers (LVS), Cascade Impactors, Impingers, and Tedlar bags, under controlled flow, temperature, and pressure conditions.
2. **Pollution Measurement** – The qualitative identification and quantitative estimation of the captured pollutants using physico-chemical analytical techniques such as Gravimetry, Spectrophotometry, Gas Chromatography (GC), High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), Flame Ionization Detection (FID), Chemiluminescence, Non-Dispersive Infrared Analysis (NDIR), and Mass Spectrometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling ≠ Analysis.** Sampling is *acquiring* the air; analysis is *knowing what is in it.* Both must be done correctly; one without the other yields meaningless data.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine the atmosphere as a giant, invisible ocean of gases swirling around us. We cannot "see" pollution the way we see dirt on a surface, so we need a *fishing net* and a *laboratory test kit* to study it.

- **Sampling = The Fishing Net.** Just as a marine biologist dips a plankton net into seawater to collect a sample of marine life, an environmental engineer lowers an air-sampling probe into the atmosphere to "catch" a representative parcel of air. If the net holes are too big, big fish (large dust particles) escape; if the net is placed wrongly, it catches the wrong species. Hence, **isokinetic sampling**, **proper probe orientation**, and **calibrated flow rates** are critical.
- **Measurement = The Lab Test Kit.** Once the air sample is captured, a chemist runs it through instruments that act like a "blood test for the sky" — translating invisible chemical fingerprints into numbers we can act upon (e.g., PM₂.₅ = 62 μg/m³, SO₂ = 28 ppb).

The **sampling location**, **duration**, **frequency**, and **analytical method** must together satisfy representativeness, accuracy, precision, and comparability with National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Methods documented by IS 5182 (Parts I–XXIII), USEPA Compendium Methods (TO-1 through TO-17), and APHA's *Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater* (relevant for stack emissions washing) constitute the official procedural library.

---

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Key Categories at a Glance)

| Dimension | Categories |
|---|---|
| **By Target Pollutant** | Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, TSPM); Gaseous Pollutants (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, O₃, VOCs, H₂S) |
| **By Sampling Mode** | Grab (Instantaneous); Integrated (Time-averaged); Continuous (Real-time) |
| **By Source** | Ambient (Atmospheric); Stationary Source (Stack/Emission); Indoor; Personal/Workplace |
| **By Principle** | Active (Pump-driven); Passive (Diffusion-based) |
| **By Phase Captured** | Particulate-only; Gas-only; Simultaneous (e.g., Annular Denuder Systems) |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP) for air quality:** **25 °C (298.15 K)** and **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
> - **Molar Volume at STP:** **24.45 L/mol**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)**
> - **Reynolds Number threshold for laminar/turbulent flow in probes:** Re ≈ **2000 (laminar)**, > **4000 (turbulent)**
> - **CPCB NAAQS Reference Units:** μg/m³ (particulates, SO₂, NO₂); mg/m³ (CO); ppb/ppm (trace gases)
> - **Conversion identity:** 1 ppm = **1000 ppb** = **10⁻⁶ mol/mol**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of an Air Sampling Programme

A complete air-quality monitoring exercise executes the following structured sequence:

**Step 1 — Define the Objective.** Identify the pollutant(s) of interest, the regulatory standard to be compared against, and the required detection limit. For example, NAAQS for PM₂.₅ is **60 μg/m³ (24-hr average)**.

**Step 2 — Site Selection.** Apply CPCB's micro-meteorological siting criteria: the sampler inlet should be **at least 2 m above ground**, unobstructed, and representative of the breathing zone of the population under study.

**Step 3 — Selection of Sampling Principle.** Choose *active vs. passive*, *grab vs. integrated*, and *filter-based vs. absorbent-based* systems based on pollutant phase.

**Step 4 — Isokinetic Sampling (for stacks).** When sampling from a duct/stack carrying particulates, the velocity of air entering the probe must match the gas stream velocity. Mismatched velocity causes **anisokinetic error** — heavier particles are under-sampled (if probe velocity > stream) or over-sampled (if probe velocity < stream).

**Step 5 — Collection of Sample.** Operate the sampler for a defined duration (typically 8 hr, 24 hr, or 7 days depending on the standard).

**Step 6 — Preservation & Transport.** Maintain filter papers at **4 °C** in petri dishes sealed with parafilm to prevent volatilization losses and biological growth.

**Step 7 — Laboratory Analysis.** Weigh filters (gravimetric for PM); pass gases through absorbing solutions and analyze by spectrophotometry, GC, or HPLC.

**Step 8 — Data Reduction & Reporting.** Convert raw readings to standardized units (μg/m³, ppb) using the governing equations (see formula sheet).

---

### 2.2 Methods of Sampling Gaseous Pollutants

#### (A) Absorption / Impinger Method
- The gas stream is bubbled through a liquid absorbent that selectively dissolves the target gas.
- The absorbed species is then reacted with a color-developing reagent and analyzed by **UV-Vis Spectrophotometry**.
- *Example:* SO₂ is absorbed in **Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution to form dichlorosulfitomercurate, which reacts with **pararosaniline** and formaldehyde to give a magenta complex measured at **548 nm**.

#### (B) Adsorption Tube Method
- Target gases are trapped on solid sorbents like **activated charcoal**, **Tenax-TA**, **Carbopack-B**, or **molecular sieves**.
- Thermal Desorption (TD) or solvent elution releases the analytes for GC/FID/MS analysis.
- *Used for:* VOCs, semi-volatile organics.

#### (C) Canister / Tedlar Bag Sampling
- Passivated stainless-steel canisters or inert Tedlar bags capture whole-air samples.
- Analyzed by **GC-MS** or **GC-FID**.

#### (D) Passive (Diffusion) Samplers
- No pump. Pollutants diffuse through a calibrated membrane and accumulate on a sorbent.
- *Examples:* **Palmes tube** for NO₂, **badge-type** samplers for VOCs, **Ogawa** samplers.

#### (E) Continuous Real-Time Analyzers
- Multi-gas monitors employing **NDIR, Chemiluminescence, UV Fluorescence, FID, or Paramagnetic** detection.
- Provide 1-min, 5-min, hourly averages with automatic data logging.

---

### 2.3 Methods of Sampling Particulate Matter

#### (A) High-Volume Sampler (HVS) — *TSP / PM₁₀*
- Operates at **1.0 to 1.5 m³/min** flow rate.
- Air is drawn through a **20.3 × 25.4 cm** glass-fibre or quartz filter.
- Concentration = (Final weight − Initial weight) / Volume of air sampled.
- *Standard:* IS 5182 (Part XIV); USEPA RFPS-1287-063.

#### (B) Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS) — *PM₁₀*
- Uses a **cyclonic head** with 10 μm cut-point at **1.1 m³/min**.
- Two-filter design: PM₁₀ on front filter, PM₂.₅ on backup filter (when fitted with EPA-approved WINS impactor).

#### (C) Cascade Impactor
- Multi-stage inertial separator. Air accelerates through successive nozzles; particles of decreasing aerodynamic diameter are deposited on collection stages.
- Provides **size-fractionated** mass distribution.

#### (D) Dichotomous Sampler
- Virtual impactor splits the aerosol into **fine (<2.5 μm)** and **coarse (2.5–10 μm)** fractions on two separate filters.

#### (E) Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (BAM)
- Real-time PM monitor. Beta rays from a ¹⁴C source are attenuated proportionally to mass loading on a filter tape. **No gravimetric step required.**

#### (F) TEOM (Tapered Element Oscillating Microbalance)
- Filter mounted on a vibrating element. Frequency change correlates with accumulated mass. Real-time, hourly PM data.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the *core computational tools* required to solve KTU 2024 ESE numerical questions on this topic. Memorize and practise each.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{PM} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i)}{V}$ | Particulate concentration (gravimetric) | μg/m³ |
| 2 | $V = Q \times t$ | Sampling volume from flow × time | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{std} = V_{act} \times \dfrac{P_{act}}{P_{std}} \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{act}}$ | Actual-to-standard volume correction | m³ |
| 4 | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ | ppm to μg/m³ conversion at 25 °C, 1 atm | μg/m³ |
| 5 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{MW}$ | Reverse: μg/m³ to ppm | ppm |
| 6 | $C_{ppb} = C_{ppm} \times 10^3$ | ppm to ppb | ppb |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \times \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Combined Gas Law concentration scaling | — |
| 8 | $AQI_i = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Sub-index of AQI for pollutant *i* | dimensionless |
| 9 | $AQI = \max(AQI_i)$ | Overall AQI = maximum sub-index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{corrected} = C_{ideal} \times \left(\dfrac{298}{T}\right)^{0.5}$ | Slight temperature correction for diffusion tubes | — |
| 11 | $u_{probe} = u_{stack}$ | Isokinetic condition (velocity equality) | m/s |
| 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho u d}{\mu} = \dfrac{u d}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number for sampling probe | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **MW** = Molecular weight (g/mol); **W_f, W_i** = final and initial filter weights (μg); **BP** = Breakpoint concentration; **I** = AQI category index; **C** = measured concentration.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Industries must demonstrate stack emissions meet CPCB/SPCB standards; this requires accurate sampling & measurement.
- **Epidemiological Studies:** PM₂.₅ and O₃ are correlated with respiratory and cardiovascular mortality; public health action requires precise measurement.
- **Urban Planning:** AQI maps (e.g., CPCB's SAFAR) inform traffic regulation, construction bans, and school closures.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) use real-time analyzers for feedback control of scrubbers, ESPs, and SCR units.
- **Carbon Market & ESG Reporting:** Greenhouse gas quantification (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O) underpins carbon credit verification and Net-Zero pledges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Gravimetric Calculation of TSP Concentration

> **Problem:** A High-Volume Sampler (HVS) was operated at an average flow rate of **1.2 m³/min** for **24 hours** in an industrial zone. The initial weight of the glass-fibre filter was **4.2638 g**, and the final weight after sampling was **4.3012 g**. Compute the **Total Suspended Particulate (TSP)** concentration in μg/m³. State whether it complies with NAAQS (24-hr TSP standard = **230 μg/m³**).

#### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**

$$
Q = 1.2 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}, \quad t = 24 \ \text{hr}, \quad W_i = 4.2638 \ \text{g}, \quad W_f = 4.3012 \ \text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the net mass of particulates collected.**

$$
\Delta W = W_f - W_i = 4.3012 - 4.2638 = 0.0374 \ \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the mass to micrograms.**

$$
\Delta W = 0.0374 \ \text{g} \times 10^6 \ \dfrac{\mu g}{g} = 37400 \ \mu g
$$

**Step 4 — Convert sampling time to minutes.**

$$
t = 24 \ \text{hr} \times 60 \ \dfrac{\text{min}}{\text{hr}} = 1440 \ \text{min}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the total volume of air sampled.**

$$
V = Q \times t = 1.2 \ \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{min}} \times 1440 \ \text{min} = 1728 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the particulate concentration.**

$$
C_{TSP} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{V} = \dfrac{37400 \ \mu g}{1728 \ \text{m}^3}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TSP} & = \dfrac{37400}{1728} \\
& = 21.6435 \ \mu g/m^3 \\
& \approx 21.64 \ \mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with NAAQS.**

$$
21.64 \ \mu g/m^3 \ \ll \ 230 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad (\text{NAAQS 24-hr TSP})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The 24-hour TSP concentration is well **within the permissible CPCB NAAQS limit**. The ambient air in the monitored zone is compliant for total suspended particulates during the sampling period.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ppm ↔ μg/m³ Conversion

> **Problem:** An ambient air analyzer reports a 1-hour average **NO₂ concentration of 80 ppb** at **30 °C and 1 atm**. Convert this to **μg/m³** and check against NAAQS (1-hr NO₂ standard = **80 μg/m³**). Given: **MW of NO₂ = 46 g/mol**.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert ppb to ppm.**

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{80 \ \text{ppb}}{1000} = 0.080 \ \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — Correct the molar volume for the actual temperature.**

The standard molar volume **24.45 L/mol** is valid at **25 °C (298.15 K)**. At **30 °C (303.15 K)**, use the ideal gas correction:

$$
V_m = 24.45 \times \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{std}} = 24.45 \times \dfrac{303.15}{298.15}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m & = 24.45 \times 1.01677 \\
& = 24.860 \ \text{L/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the conversion formula.**

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m \ \text{(in L)}} \times 1000 \ \dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

More compactly:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times 1000}{V_m}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} & = 0.080 \times \dfrac{46 \times 1000}{24.860} \\
& = 0.080 \times 1850.36 \\
& = 148.03 \ \mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NAAQS.**

$$
148.03 \ \mu g/m^3 \ > \ 80 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad (\text{NAAQS 1-hr NO}_2)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The 1-hour NO₂ concentration **exceeds the NAAQS limit** by ~85%. Short-term mitigation (traffic restrictions, industrial shutdown advisories) is warranted.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — AQI Sub-Index Calculation

> **Problem:** The following 24-hour pollutant concentrations were measured at an urban monitoring station:
> - **PM₂.₅ = 75 μg/m³**
> - **PM₁₀ = 140 μg/m³**
> - **SO₂ = 65 μg/m³**
> - **NO₂ = 55 μg/m³**
>
> Compute the **AQI** using the standard Indian NAAQS breakpoints.

#### Solution (Selected Sub-Index: PM₂.₅)

**Step 1 — Reference the CPCB AQI Breakpoint Table for PM₂.₅ (24-hr).**

| AQI Range (I) | PM₂.₅ (C, μg/m³) |
|---|---|
| 0–50 | 0–30 |
| 51–100 | 31–60 |
| 101–200 | 61–90 |
| 201–300 | 91–120 |
| 301–400 | 121–250 |
| 401–500 | 251–380 |

**Step 2 — Locate C = 75 μg/m³ in the table.**

75 falls in the band **101–200**, so:
- $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 90$
- $BP_{Lo} = 60$

**Step 3 — Apply the AQI sub-index formula.**

$$
AQI_{PM_{2.5}} = \dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 60} \times (75 - 60) + 101
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AQI_{PM_{2.5}} & = \dfrac{99}{30} \times 15 + 101 \\
& = 3.3 \times 15 + 101 \\
& = 49.5 + 101 \\
& = 150.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Repeat similarly for PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂ and take the maximum.**

| Pollutant | Concentration | Sub-Index AQI |
|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 75 μg/m³ | **150.5** |
| PM₁₀ | 140 μg/m³ | ≈ 95 |
| SO₂ | 65 μg/m³ | ≈ 56 |
| NO₂ | 55 μg/m³ | ≈ 52 |

**Step 4 — Compute the overall AQI.**

$$
AQI = \max(150.5, \ 95, \ 56, \ 52) = 150.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Overall AQI ≈ 150 → "Unhealthy" category.** Health advisory: sensitive groups should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sampling Methods

| Method | Target | Principle | Sensitivity | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HVS | TSP/PM₁₀ | Filtration + Gravimetry | 5 μg/m³ | Regulatory monitoring |
| RDS | PM₁₀ | Cyclonic separation | 5 μg/m³ | NAAQS compliance |
| Cascade Impactor | Size-fractionated PM | Inertial impaction | 1 μg/m³ | Source apportionment |
| BAM | Real-time PM | Beta attenuation | 1 μg/m³ | Continuous monitoring |
| TEOM | Real-time PM | Microbalance | 0.5 μg/m³ | Research-grade monitoring |
| Impinger + UV-Vis | SO₂, NO₂, H₂S | Wet chemistry | ppb level | Manual monitoring |
| GC-FID | VOCs (hydrocarbons) | Flame ionization | ppt | VOC speciation |
| GC-MS | VOCs/SVOCs | Mass-selective detection | ppt | Toxicology, fingerprinting |
| NDIR Analyzer | CO, CO₂, CH₄ | IR absorption | ppm | Stack, CEMS |
| Chemiluminescence | NO/NO₂ | O₃ + NO → light | ppb | Real-time NOₓ |
| UV Fluorescence | SO₂ | UV excitation emission | ppb | Continuous SO₂ |
| Ogawa / Palmes | NO₂, O₃, BTEX | Passive diffusion | ppb–ppm | Personal exposure, screening |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three methods of sampling gaseous air pollutants. Briefly describe the principle of the **West-Gaeke method** for SO₂ measurement.*

#### Model Answer
The three principal methods of sampling gaseous air pollutants are:

1. **Absorption in liquid** (e.g., Impinger method) — gas dissolves in a selective absorbent.
2. **Adsorption on solid sorbents** (e.g., Activated charcoal, Tenax tubes) — gas adheres to a high-surface-area solid.
3. **Whole-air grab sampling** (e.g., Canister, Tedlar bag) — gas is captured in inert containers for later lab analysis.

**West-Gaeke Method for SO₂:** Ambient air is drawn at a controlled flow rate (typically 0.5–1.0 L/min for 24 hr) through an **impinger containing 0.04 M Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution. SO₂ dissolves to form **dichlorosulfitomercurate complex**, which is stable for several days. The complex is then reacted with **pararosaniline dye and formaldehyde** to produce a **magenta-colored compound** whose absorbance is measured spectrophotometrically at **548 nm**. The absorbance is proportional to SO₂ concentration. (Limit of detection ≈ 0.025 ppm for 24-hr sampling.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is **isokinetic sampling**? Why is it important in stack monitoring of particulate matter?*

#### Model Answer
**Isokinetic sampling** is the technique of drawing a gas sample from a stack/duct such that the **velocity of air entering the sampling probe is exactly equal to the velocity of the gas stream** in the duct (i.e., $u_{probe} = u_{stack}$).

**Importance:** When sampling particulates from a flowing gas stream, the inertia of particles causes them to deviate from gas streamlines as the flow bends into the probe. If $u_{probe} > u_{stack}$ (super-isokinetic), the gas bends sharply and **under-samples** large/heavy particles (they are pushed away by inertia). If $u_{probe} < u_{stack}$ (sub-isokinetic), the gas curves and **over-samples** coarse particles. Only isokinetic conditions yield a **truly representative** sample of the in-stack aerosol. This is the foundation of USEPA Method 5 and IS 5182 stack sampling protocols.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Particulate & Gravimetric Analysis Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Describe the construction, working principle, and operating procedure of a **High-Volume Sampler (HVS)** for measuring Total Suspended Particulates. Enumerate the necessary precautions to ensure representative sampling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A High-Volume Sampler was operated at a site near a cement plant. The flow rate during the 8-hour run was 1.1 m³/min. The initial filter weight was 3.9421 g and final weight 3.9890 g. Calculate the **24-hr equivalent TSP** concentration and comment on its compliance with the CPCB NAAQS standard of 230 μg/m³ (24-hr). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) High-Volume Sampler — Construction, Working & Procedure**

**Construction:** The HVS consists of a **shelter/housing** containing:
- A **brushless motor/blower** capable of drawing large volumes of air.
- A **flow measurement device** (rotameter or mass flow controller).
- An **elapsed-time indicator** (timer).
- A **filter holder** accommodating a **20.3 × 25.4 cm glass-fibre or quartz filter** with ≥ 99% collection efficiency for 0.3 μm particles.
- A **protective roof/hood** to shield from rain and direct deposition.

**Working Principle:** Ambient air is drawn through the filter at a calibrated high flow rate (**1.0 to 1.5 m³/min**). Particulates are deposited on the filter by **inertial impaction, interception, and diffusion**. The volumetric flow is integrated over the sampling period to obtain total sampled air volume.

**Operating Procedure:**
1. Condition the filter in a desiccator for 24 hours at controlled humidity (typically 30–40% RH) and weigh it on an analytical balance to **W_i**.
2. Install the filter in the holder with the gridded side up.
3. Set the timer and switch on the blower; verify the calibrated flow.
4. Record the initial flow, run for 8 or 24 hours, and record the average flow & elapsed time.
5. Remove the filter carefully, condition it again in the desiccator, and weigh to **W_f**.
6. Compute the concentration: $C = (W_f - W_i) / V$.

**Precautions:**
- **Calibrate** the rotameter periodically using a standard orifice or a Roots meter.
- Avoid finger contact with the filter — use tweezers and gloves.
- Maintain **constant flow**; recheck during the run.
- Protect the filter from **moisture, dust deposition from above, and contamination** during transport.
- Choose a **representative, unobstructed site** (breathing-zone height, away from local sources).

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
$$
Q = 1.1 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}, \quad t = 8 \ \text{hr}, \quad W_i = 3.9421 \ \text{g}, \quad W_f = 3.9890 \ \text{g}
$$

**Step 1 — Net mass collected.**

$$
\Delta W = 3.9890 - 3.9421 = 0.0469 \ \text{g} = 46900 \ \mu g
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of air sampled.**

$$
t = 8 \times 60 = 480 \ \text{min}
$$

$$
V = 1.1 \times 480 = 528 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — 8-hour TSP concentration.**

$$
C_{8hr} = \dfrac{46900}{528} = 88.83 \ \mu g/m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to 24-hour equivalent (assuming constant emission).**

Since the concentration is the same for any averaging period (only the standard changes), the 8-hr concentration **is** the measured TSP. For NAAQS comparison with 24-hr standard:

$$
C_{24hr} \approx 88.83 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad \text{(steady-state assumption)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with NAAQS.**

$$
88.83 \ \mu g/m^3 \ < \ 230 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad \Rightarrow \ \textbf{COMPLIANT}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** If a part of the question requires scaling (e.g., "extrapolate to 24 hr assuming the source remains constant"), the concentration remains unchanged but the *total mass collected* would triple over 24 hr. Always clarify the basis of averaging.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Gaseous Pollutants & Concentration Conversion Focus

**(a)** Explain the **Modified Jacob–Hochheiser (West-Gaeke) method** for ambient SO₂ monitoring in detail, including the chemistry, apparatus, sampling procedure, and spectrophotometric finish. State the wavelength of measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous ambient air monitor reports an **hourly O₃ concentration of 120 ppb at 35 °C and 1 atm**. Convert this to **μg/m³** and check against the NAAQS 1-hr standard of **180 μg/m³** for O₃. (MW of O₃ = 48 g/mol.) **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Modified Jacob–Hochheiser (West–Gaeke) Method for SO₂**

**Principle:** SO₂ from ambient air is absorbed in a dilute solution of **sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM, Na₂[HgCl₄])**, forming a stable **dichlorosulfitomercurate complex** that prevents oxidation of sulfite to sulfate. This complex is then reacted with **formaldehyde (HCHO)** and **pararosaniline dye** in acidic medium to produce a **reddish-purple/magenta para-rosaniline-methyl sulfonic acid** adduct whose absorbance is measured spectrophotometrically.

**Chemistry (Stepwise):**
$$
\text{SO}_2 + [\text{HgCl}_4]^{2-} \rightarrow [\text{HgCl}_2(\text{SO}_3)]^{2-} + 2\text{Cl}^-
$$

$$
[\text{HgCl}_2(\text{SO}_3)]^{2-} + \text{HCHO} + \text{Pararosaniline} \rightarrow \text{Magenta complex} \ (\lambda_{max} = 548 \ \text{nm})
$$

**Apparatus:** A calibrated **sampling train** comprising a particulate filter (to remove dust), a rotameter (0.5–1.0 L/min), a **fritted-glass impinger** containing 10 mL of 0.04 M TCM, and a vacuum pump.

**Sampling Procedure:**
1. Pour 10 mL of 0.04 M TCM into the impinger.
2. Connect the train and draw air at 0.5–1.0 L/min for a defined period (1 hr for short-term; 24 hr for 24-hr standard).
3. Record the sampled volume, temperature, and pressure.
4. In the lab, take an aliquot (typically 1 mL) and add **formaldehyde** and **pararosaniline** reagents.
5. Allow colour to develop for 20–30 minutes.
6. Measure absorbance at **548 nm** in a UV-Vis spectrophotometer.
7. Compute concentration from a calibration curve.

**Wavelength:** $\lambda_{max} = \mathbf{548 \ nm}$ (visible region, green light).

**Limit of detection:** ~25 ppb for 24-hr sampling.

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $C = 120$ ppb, $T = 35$ °C $= 308.15$ K, $P = 1$ atm, MW of O₃ = 48 g/mol.

**Step 1 — Convert ppb to ppm.**

$$
C_{ppm} = \dfrac{120}{1000} = 0.120 \ \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — Correct molar volume to 35 °C.**

$$
V_m = 24.45 \times \dfrac{308.15}{298.15}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m & = 24.45 \times 1.03354 \\
& = 25.270 \ \text{L/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conversion formula.**

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times 1000}{V_m}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\mu g/m^3} & = 0.120 \times \dfrac{48 \times 1000}{25.270} \\
& = 0.120 \times 1899.49 \\
& = 227.94 \ \mu g/m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with NAAQS.**

$$
227.94 \ \mu g/m^3 \ > \ 180 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad \Rightarrow \ \textbf{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to correct the molar volume for temperature** — the formula $C = \frac{ppm \times MW}{24.45}$ is valid ONLY at 25 °C. If the question specifies any other temperature, the student loses 2–3 marks by not applying the Charles's Law correction.
> 2. **Confusing ppb with ppm** — remember 1 ppm = 1000 ppb. A 120 ppb value is *not* 120 ppm.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in the denominator** — when using $V_m$ in litres, the conversion factor becomes $\times 1000$ to express per cubic metre; students forget this and get answers off by 1000.
> 4. **Not stating the NAAQS comparison explicitly** — KTU expects a clear "Compliant / Non-Compliant" statement with a quoted numerical standard. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air Sampling & Pollution Measurement Methods**

**Conceptual Anchors:**
- Air sampling ≠ analysis. Both must be done correctly.
- Three sampling modes: **Grab, Integrated, Continuous**.
- Two primary pollutant phases: **Particulate** and **Gaseous** — each needs different instruments.

**Particulate Methods:**
- **HVS (1.0–1.5 m³/min)** → TSP/PM₁₀ via gravimetry
- **RDS (1.1 m³/min, cyclonic head)** → PM₁₀
- **Cascade Impactor** → size-fractionated mass
- **BAM (Beta Attenuation)** and **TEOM** → real-time PM

**Gaseous Methods:**
- **West-Gaeke** → SO₂ via TCM + Pararosaniline at **548 nm**
- **Jacob–Hochheiser** is the older name; same method
- **NDIR** → CO, CO₂, CH₄
- **Chemiluminescence** → NOₓ
- **UV Fluorescence** → SO₂ (continuous)
- **GC-FID / GC-MS** → VOCs

**Key Equations to Memorize:**
- $C_{PM} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{Q \times t}$
- $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times 1000}{V_m}$ (where $V_m$ corrected for T)
- $V_m(T) = 24.45 \times \dfrac{T_{act}}{298.15}$
- $AQI_i = \dfrac{I_{Hi}-I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi}-BP_{Lo}} \times (C - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$
- $AQI = \max(\text{all sub-indices})$

**Definitions to Recall:**
- **Isokinetic sampling:** $u_{probe} = u_{stack}$ (mandatory for representative PM stack sampling).
- **TSP / PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅:** Total Suspended Particulates / < 10 μm / < 2.5 μm aerodynamic diameter.
- **Ambient vs. Source monitoring:** Outdoor air quality vs. industrial stack emissions.
- **Active vs. Passive sampling:** Pump-driven vs. diffusion-driven.

**Numerical Hot-Spots for KTU ESE:**
1. Gravimetric PM calculation (ΔW / V).
2. ppm ↔ μg/m³ conversions with **temperature correction**.
3. AQI sub-index calculation using **CPCB breakpoint tables**.
4. Isokinetic condition checks and Reynolds number for probe design.

**Syllabus-Aligned Standards (Recall-Only):**
- IS 5182 (Parts I–XXIII) — Indian Standard methods for ambient air quality.
- USEPA 40 CFR Part 50 — NAAQS and reference methods.
- CPCB NAAQS 2009 — Current Indian ambient air quality thresholds.
- APHA Standard Methods — Reference for laboratory analytical procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always quote the **method, instrument, and standard reference** (e.g., "as per IS 5182 Part II, by the West-Gaeke method") in descriptive answers. KTU examiners reward methodology citations generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
