<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Air Quality Monitoring — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Quality Monitoring** is the systematic, continuous, or periodic measurement of pollutants (gaseous and particulate) in ambient air using scientifically calibrated instruments, in order to evaluate compliance with National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) and to assess the impact of human activities on the atmospheric environment.

**Key Terms in KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:**

- **Ambient Air**: The outdoor air in the troposphere to which the general public has access, typically measured at breathing height (1.5 m to 15 m above ground).
- **Source Monitoring**: Direct measurement of pollutant concentrations at the point of emission (e.g., a stack).
- **Stack Sampling**: Sampling of flue gases from industrial chimneys using isokinetic sampling methods.
- **Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (AAQMS)**: A fixed installation housing multiple analyzers for round-the-clock surveillance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Air as a Doctor's Diagnostic Test"

Think of the atmosphere as a **patient's bloodstream** and air quality instruments as **medical diagnostic tools**:

- Just as a doctor measures a patient's **blood pressure, sugar, and oxygen** levels, an environmental engineer measures the **CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM₂.₅, PM₁₀** levels of the atmosphere.
- A **stethoscope** is to a doctor what a **High Volume Sampler (HVS)** is to an environmental engineer — both are non-invasive diagnostic instruments.
- A patient needs a **baseline (normal) value**; similarly, pollutants have **threshold limits (CPCB standards)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard reference for Indian air quality is the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009**, with 12 criteria pollutants: PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb, As, Ni, BaP, and Benzene.

## 1.3 Principles of Air Quality Measurement — The Three Pillars

Air quality measurement is governed by **three fundamental scientific principles**:

1. **Physical Principle (Gravimetric & Optical Methods)**
   - Used for **particulate matter (PM)** measurement.
   - Relies on **mass change** (gravimetric) or **light scattering/absorption** (optical).

2. **Chemical Principle (Wet Chemical & Absorption Methods)**
   - Pollutant-specific **absorbing reagents** form colored products.
   - Color intensity is measured photometrically (**West-Gaeke method for SO₂**, **Modified Jacob & Hochheiser for NO₂**).

3. **Spectroscopic Principle (IR, UV, Chemiluminescence)**
   - Molecules **absorb or emit** light at characteristic wavelengths.
   - **Beer-Lambert Law** governs absorption-based analyzers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Units**: µg/m³ (micrograms per cubic meter) for particulates, ppb (parts per billion) or µg/m³ for gases. **Ambient Temperature: 25°C**, **Standard Pressure: 101.325 kPa** (as per CPCB).

## 1.4 Classification of Monitoring Instruments

| Instrument Category | Physical Principle | Typical Pollutant | Example Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravimetric Sampler | Mass difference on filter | TSP, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | High Volume Sampler (HVS) |
| Optical Particle Counter | Light scattering | PM₂.₅, PM₁₀ real-time | Aerodynamic Particle Sizer (APS) |
| Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor | β-ray absorption | PM₂.₅, PM₁₀ | BAM-1020 |
| Non-Dispersive IR (NDIR) | IR absorption at specific λ | CO, CO₂, CH₄ | Ultramat 23 |
| UV Photometric | UV absorption | O₃ | Dasibi 1008 |
| Chemiluminescence | Light emission by NO + O₃ | NO, NO₂, NOₓ | Thermo 42i |
| Flame Ionization Detector (FID) | Ionization in H₂ flame | Hydrocarbons (HC), VOC | FID Analyzer |
| Fluorescence (UV) | SO₂ fluorescence | SO₂ | Thermo 43i |
| Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) | Element-specific light absorption | Pb, As, Ni, Cd | AAS Instrument |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Principle of High Volume Sampler (HVS) — The Workhorse of PM Monitoring

The **High Volume Sampler (HVS)** is the most widely used instrument for measuring **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)** and **PM₁₀** in India. It operates on the **gravimetric principle**.

### Operational Steps:
1. A **vacuum pump** draws ambient air through a **pre-weighed filter paper** (typically glass fiber or quartz microfiber) at a **high flow rate of 1.1 to 1.7 m³/min**.
2. Particulates are **deposited on the filter** by inertial impaction, diffusion, and electrostatic attraction.
3. After **24 hours of sampling**, the filter is **re-weighed** under controlled humidity and temperature.
4. The **mass difference** of the filter equals the mass of particulates collected.

### Mass Concentration Formula:

The fundamental equation used in all KTU problems:

$$C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{V_s}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Mass concentration of particulates (µg/m³)
- $W_f$ = Final weight of filter (grams)
- $W_i$ = Initial weight of filter (grams)
- $V_s$ = Total volume of air sampled (m³)
- $10^6$ = Conversion from grams to micrograms

### Volume of Air Sampled:

$$V_s = Q_{avg} \times t$$

Where:
- $Q_{avg}$ = Average flow rate (m³/min)
- $t$ = Total sampling duration (minutes)

### Isokinetic Sampling (For Stacks):

For stack monitoring, the sampling probe velocity must equal the gas stream velocity:

$$V_p = V_s = \frac{Q}{A_{stack}}$$

If $V_p \neq V_s$, sampling is **anisokinetic**, leading to errors in PM measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Flow Calibration**: The HVS must be calibrated with a **calibrated orifice** at multiple pressure points. The **blower motor voltage** is the primary calibration parameter.

## 2.2 Working Principle of Gas Analyzers

### 2.2.1 Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyzer

**Principle**: Hetero-atomic gas molecules (CO, CO₂, SO₂, NO, CH₄) absorb **infrared radiation** at specific characteristic wavelengths.

**Beer-Lambert Law (governing equation):**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot c \cdot l}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Transmitted light intensity
- $I_0$ = Incident light intensity
- $k$ = Absorption coefficient (pollutant-specific)
- $c$ = Concentration of absorbing gas
- $l$ = Path length (m)

**Linearized form** (for small concentrations):

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Where $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ is the **absorbance**, and $\varepsilon$ is the **molar absorptivity**.

### 2.2.2 Chemiluminescence Analyzer (For NOₓ)

**Principle**: NO reacts with O₃ to form NO₂* (excited), which emits light:

$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$$
$$NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu \text{ (600 to 875 nm)}$$

The light intensity is **proportional to NO concentration**.

**For NO₂ measurement**: NO₂ is first **converted to NO** using a **molybdenum converter** at ~325°C, then measured. NOₓ = NO + NO₂.

### 2.2.3 UV Photometric Ozone Analyzer

**Principle**: O₃ strongly absorbs UV light at **254 nm** (Hartley band). The absorption follows Beer-Lambert's law with a very high absorption cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ozone absorption cross-section at 254 nm** is $\sigma = 1.147 \times 10^{-17}$ cm²/molecule — a constant students must remember for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = (W_f - W_i) \times 10^6 / V_s$ | Particulate concentration (gravimetric) | µg/m³ |
| 2 | $V_s = Q_{avg} \times t$ | Sample volume from flow × time | m³ |
| 3 | $I = I_0 e^{-kcl}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (NDIR, UV) | Intensity ratio |
| 4 | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | Linearized absorbance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_p = V_s$ | Isokinetic sampling condition | m/s |
| 6 | $ppb = (C \times 24.45) / M$ | ppb to µg/m³ conversion (at 25°C, 1 atm) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{corrected} = C_{measured} \times \frac{T_{std}}{T_{actual}} \times \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{std}}$ | STP correction of gas concentration | µg/m³ |
| 8 | $R = \frac{[\text{NO}_2]}{[\text{NO}_x]}$ | NO₂/NOₓ ratio (photochemistry indicator) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $n = \frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times P \times V}{R \times T}$ | Number of gas molecules (Avogadro) | molecules |
| 10 | $AOD = \int \sigma_{ext}(z) dz$ | Aerosol Optical Depth (column measurement) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction in Tables**: All absolute values and ratios in the above table use plain text or LaTeX commands. The student should remember that the **vertical bar `|` cannot be used inside table rows** for KTU-formatted answer sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Smart City Air Quality Networks**: Real-time Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS) using BAM (Beta Attenuation) and gas analyzers, transmitting data to CPCB's **SAFAR** and **AQI** platforms.
2. **Industrial Compliance Monitoring**: Stack monitoring using **isokinetic sampling** as mandated by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).
3. **Occupational Health**: Workplace PM monitoring using **personal cascade impactors** for respirable dust fractions.
4. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**: Baseline air quality studies for new industrial projects, using both active (HVS) and passive (tube) sampling methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law for Pollutant Concentration

### 3.1.1 Starting from First Principles

Consider a beam of monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ passing through a gas cell of path length $l$, containing a gas of concentration $c$ that absorbs light at the chosen wavelength.

Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of the gas cell of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the source. Let the light intensity at this slice be $I(x)$.

The number of gas molecules in this slice is proportional to $c \, dx$. The fraction of photons absorbed in this slice is proportional to both the incident intensity and the number of molecules:

$$dI = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot I(x) \cdot dx$$

Where $\kappa$ is the absorption cross-section per molecule (m²/molecule) multiplied by Avogadro's number, giving units of m²/mol.

**Separating variables:**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot dx$$

**Integrating from 0 to l:**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot \int_0^{l} dx$$

**Evaluating the integral:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Exponentiating both sides:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\kappa c l}$$

**Defining the absorbance (base-10):**

$$A = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \frac{\kappa c l}{2.303} = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law**, the foundation of all absorption-based air quality analyzers (NDIR, UV photometric).

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $2.303$ comes from the change of base: $\ln(x) = 2.303 \times \log_{10}(x)$. The molar absorptivity $\varepsilon = \kappa / 2.303$ has units of L/(mol·cm).

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — HVS Calculation

### Problem:
A High Volume Sampler operated for **8 hours** at an average flow rate of **1.2 m³/min**. The initial weight of the filter was **4.2156 g** and the final weight was **4.2589 g**. Calculate the **concentration of Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)** in µg/m³.

### Step 1: Calculate the Mass of Particulates Collected

$$W_f - W_i = 4.2589 \text{ g} - 4.2156 \text{ g} = 0.0433 \text{ g}$$

Convert to micrograms:

$$\Delta W = 0.0433 \text{ g} \times 10^6 \text{ µg/g} = 43300 \text{ µg}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Total Volume of Air Sampled

$$V_s = Q_{avg} \times t = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \times (8 \times 60) \text{ min}$$

$$V_s = 1.2 \times 480 = 576 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Concentration

$$C = \frac{\Delta W}{V_s} = \frac{43300 \text{ µg}}{576 \text{ m}^3}$$

$$C = 75.17 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

### Step 4: Compare with CPCB Standard

The CPCB NAAQS limit for **PM₁₀ (24-hour average)** is **100 µg/m³** (Industrial, Residential, Rural areas). Our calculated value of **75.17 µg/m³** is **within the prescribed limit** — air quality is acceptable for this parameter.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Instrument for Pollutant

| Pollutant | Recommended Method | Why? | Detection Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅, PM₁₀ (manual) | Gravimetric (HVS) | CPCB reference method | 5 µg/m³ |
| PM₂.₅ (continuous) | Beta Attenuation | Real-time, EPA equivalent | 1 µg/m³ |
| SO₂ | UV Fluorescence | Highly selective, no interference | 0.5 ppb |
| NOₓ | Chemiluminescence | High sensitivity, low ppb | 0.4 ppb |
| O₃ | UV Photometric (254 nm) | Direct, absolute measurement | 0.5 ppb |
| CO | NDIR | Mature technology, stable | 0.1 ppm |
| Lead (Pb) | AAS/ICP-MS | Atomic specificity | 0.1 ng/m³ |
| VOCs (BTEX) | GC-FID/PID | Compound-specific | 0.1 ppb |
| NH₃ | Chemiluminescence (after conversion) | Real-time | 0.5 ppb |
| Hg | Cold Vapor AAS | Ultra-trace | 0.1 ng/m³ |

## 3.4 Calibration and Quality Assurance Equations

### 3.4.1 Multi-Point Calibration Linearity Check

For a linear analyzer response, the calibration equation is:

$$y = m \cdot x + b$$

Where:
- $y$ = Analyzer output signal (V, Hz, or display units)
- $x$ = Known gas concentration (ppb or µg/m³)
- $m$ = Slope (sensitivity)
- $b$ = Intercept (baseline)

**Coefficient of Determination** $R^2$ should be **> 0.999** for an EPA-grade analyzer.

### 3.4.2 Zero and Span Drift

$$\text{Drift}_{zero} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{S} \times 100 \text{ (\%)}$$

$$\text{Drift}_{span} = \frac{S_2 - S_1}{C_{span}} \times 100 \text{ (\%)}$$

Where:
- $Z_1, Z_2$ = Zero readings at time 1 and 2
- $S_1, S_2$ = Span readings at time 1 and 2
- $C_{span}$ = Span gas concentration

Acceptable limits per CPCB: **Zero drift < ±2%**, **Span drift < ±5%** over 24 hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a High Volume Sampler (HVS) with a neat sketch. Mention any three applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The High Volume Sampler operates on the **gravimetric principle**. Ambient air is drawn through a **pre-weighed glass fiber filter paper** at a flow rate of **1.1 to 1.7 m³/min** using a high-capacity vacuum blower for a sampling period of **24 hours**. Particulate matter (TSP or PM₁₀ depending on the inlet head used) is collected on the filter by inertial impaction, interception, and diffusion. After sampling, the filter is conditioned in a desiccator for 24 hours and **re-weighed** under controlled temperature (15–30°C) and humidity (40±5%) conditions.

**Concentration calculation:**

$$C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{Q_{avg} \times t}$$

**Three Applications:**
1. **Regulatory compliance** monitoring as per CPCB NAAQS for PM₁₀ and TSP.
2. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** baseline studies for industrial projects.
3. **Source apportionment** studies using the collected particulate samples for chemical analysis (metals, ions, carbon).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**State and explain Beer-Lambert's Law. How is it used in Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) gas analyzers?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement**: Beer-Lambert's Law states that the absorbance of light by an absorbing medium is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of the light through the medium.

**Mathematical form:**

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Where $A$ is the absorbance (dimensionless), $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹), $c$ is the molar concentration (mol/L), and $l$ is the path length (cm).

**Application in NDIR**: NDIR analyzers use an **infrared source** (often a heated filament), a **gas cell** of known path length, a **narrow-band optical filter** (wavelength selector) tuned to a characteristic absorption band of the target gas (e.g., 4.67 µm for CO, 4.26 µm for CO₂), and a **pyroelectric or semiconductor detector**. The reduction in light intensity reaching the detector, governed by Beer-Lambert's Law, is directly proportional to the gas concentration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Valuation Note)**: Students often write the **exponential form** $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ and the **logarithmic form** $A = \varepsilon c l$ as the same equation. They are **not equivalent** — the exponential form is exact, the logarithmic form is a linear approximation for low concentrations. Examiners deduct **½ mark** for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]: With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Chemiluminescence Analyzer for the measurement of NO and NOₓ in ambient air. Derive the relationship between NO concentration and detector signal.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle**: The Chemiluminescence NOₓ analyzer is based on the reaction of nitric oxide (NO) with ozone (O₃), which produces excited-state nitrogen dioxide (NO₂*), which relaxes to ground state by emitting a photon in the **600 to 875 nm** wavelength range.

**Chemical Reactions**:

$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$$

$$NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu$$

**Block Diagram** (Refer Section 4.3 above for the complete flow).

**Working Steps:**

1. Ambient air sample enters through a particulate filter to remove dust.
2. A three-way valve directs the sample through one of two paths:
   - **NO mode**: Sample directly enters the reaction chamber.
   - **NOₓ mode**: Sample passes through a **molybdenum converter** heated to **325 ± 5°C**, which reduces NO₂ to NO. Total (NO + converted NO₂) is measured.
3. **Ozone** is generated internally by an **ozonator** (UV lamp or corona discharge) from ambient air.
4. NO + O₃ reaction occurs in the **reaction chamber**, producing chemiluminescence.
5. The light is detected by a **photo-multiplier tube (PMT)** sensitive in the red-NIR region (typically cooled to -10°C to reduce thermal noise).
6. The PMT output is amplified and processed.

**Derivation of Signal vs. Concentration:**

The rate of photon emission per unit volume is proportional to the rate of the chemical reaction, which (for first-order kinetics with respect to NO when [O₃] >> [NO]) is:

$$\frac{d[h\nu]}{dt} = k \cdot [NO] \cdot [O_3]$$

Where $k$ is the rate constant. Since [O₃] is held in large excess and is constant, the photon flux $\Phi$ reaching the detector is:

$$\Phi = K \cdot [NO]$$

Where $K = k \cdot [O_3] \cdot \eta \cdot V$ (a constant involving quantum yield $\eta$ and reaction volume $V$). Thus, the **detector signal is linearly proportional to NO concentration**.

For NO₂: It is calculated by subtraction: $[NO_2] = [NO_x] - [NO]$.

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]: A NDIR analyzer is used to measure CO in ambient air. The cell path length is 10 cm. The molar absorptivity of CO at 4.67 µm is 0.55 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹. The transmittance (I/I₀) is measured as 0.82. Calculate (i) the absorbance, (ii) the concentration of CO in mol/L, and (iii) the concentration in ppm at 25°C and 1 atm.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Absorbance:**

$$A = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\log_{10}(0.82)$$

$$A = -(-0.0862) = 0.0862$$

**(ii) Concentration in mol/L:**

Using Beer-Lambert's Law: $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l} = \frac{0.0862}{0.55 \times 10}$$

$$c = 0.01567 \text{ mol/L}$$

**(iii) Concentration in ppm:**

Number of moles per liter of air at STP (using ideal gas law, 25°C = 298.15 K):

$$n = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{1 \text{ atm}}{0.0821 \text{ L atm mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1} \times 298.15 \text{ K}}$$

$$n = 0.04085 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Mixing ratio (mole fraction):**

$$x = \frac{0.01567}{0.04085} = 0.3836$$

**Convert to ppm:**

$$C_{ppm} = x \times 10^6 = 3.836 \times 10^5 \text{ ppm}$$

This is unrealistically high — likely indicating the problem is for a **high-concentration environment** (industrial stack) or that the path length should be higher for ambient air. For ambient air, CO is typically 1–10 ppm, requiring a longer path length (e.g., 50 m in folded-cell configuration).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Do not forget to convert natural log to log₁₀** (use 2.303 factor). This is a common 1-mark error.
> - **Units consistency**: $\varepsilon$ is in L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹, so $c$ comes out in mol/L, NOT mol/m³.
> - **Path length confusion**: Always check if $l$ is in cm or m; the units of $\varepsilon$ dictate the path length unit.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]: Explain with a block diagram the working of a UV Photometric Ozone Analyzer. State the principle of operation and the governing law.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle**: Ozone (O₃) absorbs UV light strongly at **254 nm** (Hartley band). The absorption cross-section at this wavelength is **σ = 1.147 × 10⁻¹⁷ cm²/molecule** — a fundamental constant for O₃.

**Beer-Lambert's Law (governing equation):**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-n \cdot \sigma \cdot l}$$

Where:
- $n$ = Number density of O₃ molecules (molecules/cm³)
- $\sigma$ = Absorption cross-section (cm²/molecule)
- $l$ = Cell path length (cm)

**Block Diagram (functional flow):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1[UV Lamp at 254 nm Low Pressure Mercury] --> nodeR2[Beam Splitter]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Reference Detector Photodiode A]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR4[Sample Cell with Gas Inlet Outlet]
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5[Sample Detector Photodiode B]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR6[Differential Amplifier]
    nodeR5 --> nodeR6
    nodeR6 --> nodeR7[Mini Computer for O3 Concentration]
    nodeR7 --> nodeR8[Display and Analog Output]
```

**Working**:
1. A low-pressure mercury vapor lamp emits UV light at 254 nm.
2. The beam is split: half goes through the sample cell, half goes to a reference detector.
3. The ratio $I/I_0$ is computed automatically and continuously.
4. The O₃ concentration is calculated from the absorption ratio using the formula:

$$[O_3] = \frac{-\ln(I/I_0)}{\sigma \cdot l}$$

5. **Scrubber check**: The sample is alternately passed through an O₃ scrubber to provide a true $I_0$ reading, eliminating errors due to lamp aging.

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]: A High Volume Sampler operates for 24 hours at an average flow rate of 1.4 m³/min. The initial filter weight is 3.7824 g and the final weight is 3.8551 g. Calculate (i) the total volume of air sampled, (ii) the mass of PM collected, and (iii) the PM concentration. Compare with CPCB standards.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Volume of Air Sampled:**

$$V_s = Q_{avg} \times t = 1.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \times (24 \times 60 \text{ min})$$

$$V_s = 1.4 \times 1440 = 2016 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Mass of PM Collected:**

$$\Delta W = W_f - W_i = 3.8551 - 3.7824 = 0.0727 \text{ g}$$

$$\Delta W = 0.0727 \times 10^6 = 72700 \text{ µg}$$

**(iii) PM Concentration:**

$$C = \frac{\Delta W}{V_s} = \frac{72700 \text{ µg}}{2016 \text{ m}^3} = 36.06 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Comparison with CPCB Standards**:
- **PM₁₀ (24-hour average)** = **100 µg/m³** (CPCB NAAQS, Industrial/Residential/Rural)
- **PM₂.₅ (24-hour average)** = **60 µg/m³** (CPCB NAAQS)
- Our value of **36.06 µg/m³** is **below both standards** — air quality is **within acceptable limits** for this parameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note**: A common error is to **forget the 10⁶ conversion factor**, leading to a PM concentration in g/m³, which is dimensionally wrong. Always recheck the unit conversion: grams → micrograms requires multiplication by $10^6$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (Principles and Instruments)**

- **Air Quality Monitoring (AQM)** = Systematic measurement of pollutants to assess compliance with NAAQS.
- **CPCB NAAQS 2009** lists 12 criteria pollutants; key ones: PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃.
- **High Volume Sampler (HVS)**: Gravimetric, flow 1.1–1.7 m³/min, filter weighed before/after, 24-hour sample.
- **Concentration Formula**: $C = (W_f - W_i) \times 10^6 / (Q_{avg} \times t)$ in **µg/m³**.
- **Beer-Lambert Law**: $A = \varepsilon c l$ (linearized) and $I = I_0 e^{-kcl}$ (exact) — apply to NDIR and UV analyzers.
- **NDIR** uses IR absorption (CO at 4.67 µm, CO₂ at 4.26 µm).
- **UV Photometric** for O₃ at 254 nm with $\sigma = 1.147 \times 10^{-17}$ cm²/molecule.
- **Chemiluminescence** for NOₓ: NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂ → NO₂ + hν; PMT detects photons.
- **UV Fluorescence** for SO₂: SO₂ + hν (190–230 nm) → SO₂* → SO₂ + hν (320 nm).
- **Isokinetic Sampling** ($V_p = V_s$): Essential for **stack monitoring** to avoid sampling errors.
- **Beta Attenuation** (BAM): Continuous PM monitor using β-ray absorption by deposited particulates.
- **Calibration Requirements**: Multi-point (zero + ≥3 span points), $R^2 > 0.999$, zero drift < ±2%, span drift < ±5%.
- **Isokinetic sampling condition**: Probe velocity = Stack gas velocity (avoids under/oversampling of particulates).
- **Standard Conditions**: 25°C, 101.325 kPa (CPCB) — corrections must be applied for actual field conditions.
- **Conversion**: 1 ppb of gas = $(M/24.45)$ µg/m³ at 25°C, where $M$ is molecular weight.

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU Board Exam**: Always draw the **block diagram** of the instrument first, then state the **principle**, then write the **relevant equation**, then **solve the numerical**. This 4-step structure is what examiners look for in the 7-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambient Air Quality and Emission Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ambient Air Quality and Emission Standards ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Ambient Air Quality (AAQ)** refers to the quality of the surrounding atmospheric air in its **outdoor, unconfined state**, assessed by measuring the concentration of specific **criteria pollutants** over a defined averaging period (1-hour, 8-hour, 24-hour, or annual). It is governed by the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** notified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** of India. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Emission Standards** are legally permissible **maximum allowable limits** of pollutants that may be **discharged from a specific source** (industrial stack, vehicular tailpipe, incinerator) per unit volume of effluent gas, expressed typically in **mg/Nm³** (milligrams per normal cubic meter) at a reference temperature (often 25 °C) and pressure (1 atm). The two standards are complementary but distinct: - **Ambient Standards** → measure **what is in the air we breathe** (receptor-end). - **Emission Standards** → control **what comes out of a source** (source-end). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Fish Tank Rule"** > Imagine a community fish tank (the atmosphere). Two sets of rules apply: > 1. **Ambient Standards** = *"How clean must the water in the tank be at all times for the fish to live?"* — This is checked by testing the water around the fish. > 2. **Emission Standards** = *"How much dirt can each person pour into the tank?"* — This is checked at the inlet pipe of every polluter. > > Just as you cannot allow the tank water to become toxic (AAQ) and cannot let one person dump waste freely (Emission), **both standards work together** to keep the air breathable. The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is like a daily "fish-tank health report card" broadcast to citizens. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Reference Conditions for Indian Air Quality Monitoring:** > - **Reference Temperature:** **25 °C (298.15 K)** > - **Reference Pressure:** **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** > - **Reference Oxygen (for combustion sources):** **6% O₂ for solid/liquid fuels; 11% O₂ for gaseous fuels** > - **Units:** **µg/m³** for particulates & gases; **mg/m³** for CO; **ppm/ppb** for gaseous concentration ### 1.4 Eight Criteria Pollutants (CPCB-India) > [!NOTE] > Under the **National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)**, India monitors **8 criteria pollutants**: > 1. Particulate Matter ≤ **2.5 µm (PM2.5)** > 2. Particulate Matter ≤ **10 µm (PM10)** > 3. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** > 4. **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** > 5. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** > 6. **Ozone (O₃)** > 7. **Lead (Pb)** > 8. **Ammonia (NH₃)** ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Two-Pillar Framework The Indian regulatory framework rests on two complementary pillars: **Pillar A — Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009):** - Applicable to the **ambient atmosphere** in industrial, residential, rural, and ecologically sensitive areas. - The CPCB classifies the country into **2 zones**: - **Industrial Area** - **Residential, Rural & Other Areas** - The **stricter (lower)** standard is applied to residential/rural zones. **Pillar B — Emission Standards:** - Prescribed by **CPCB** under Section 17 of the Air Act, 1981 and the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. - Issued sector-wise: thermal power plants, cement, iron & steel, petroleum refineries, vehicles (Bharat Stage norms), incinerators, etc. ### 2.2 Air Quality Index (AQI) — The Composite Health Metric > [!IMPORTANT] > The **AQI** is a single composite number that summarizes the cumulative impact of multiple pollutants on human health. In India, AQI is computed for **8 pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb)** using the **US-EPA linear interpolation method**, and the **final AQI is the maximum sub-index** of all pollutants. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** | |---|---|---| | Sub-Index for Pollutant *p* | $I_p = \left[\dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}}\right] \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) + I_{LO}$ | $I_p$ = Sub-index; $C_p$ = measured concentration | | AQI (Final) | $AQI = \max(I_1, I_2, I_3, \ldots, I_8)$ | Dimensionless; rounded to nearest integer | | Ideal Gas Law (Concentration) | $C = \dfrac{n}{V} = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $M$ in kg/mol | | Conversion µg/m³ ↔ ppb | $C_{ppb} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \cdot 24.45}{M}$ | at 25 °C and 1 atm; $M$ = molecular weight (g/mol) | | Normalization to NTP | $C_N = C \cdot \dfrac{P_{NTP}}{P_{actual}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{NTP}}$ | $T_{NTP} = 298.15$ K, $P_{NTP} = 101.325$ kPa | | Particulate Emission Factor | $E = Q_s \cdot C_s$ | $Q_s$ = stack gas flow (Nm³/s), $C_s$ = concentration (mg/Nm³) | ### 2.4 AQI Categorical Health Breakpoints (India — CPCB) | **AQI Range** | **Category** | **Health Impact** | **Color Code** | |---|---|---|---| | 0 – 50 | Good | Minimal impact | Green (🟢) | | 51 – 100 | Satisfactory | Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people | Yellow-Green (🟡) | | 101 – 200 | Moderate | Breathing discomfort to people with lung/heart disease | Yellow (🟠) | | 201 – 300 | Poor | Breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure | Orange (🟧) | | 301 – 400 | Very Poor | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure | Red (🔴) | | 401 – 500 | Severe | Affects healthy people; serious health impacts | Maroon (🟤) | ### 2.5 National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009 — India) | **Pollutant** | **Time-Weighted Average** | **Industrial Area** | **Residential/Rural/Ecological** | **Method of Measurement** | |---|---|---|---|---| | SO₂ | 24 hr / Annual | **80 / 50** µg/m³ | **80 / 50** µg/m³ | West-Gaeke | | NO₂ | 24 hr / Annual | **80 / 40** µg/m³ | **80 / 40** µg/m³ | Jacob-Hochheiser | | PM10 | 24 hr / Annual | **100 / 60** µg/m³ | **100 / 60** µg/m³ | Gravimetric | | PM2.5 | 24 hr / Annual | **60 / 40** µg/m³ | **60 / 40** µg/m³ | Gravimetric | | CO | 8 hr / 1 hr | **5.0 / 10.0** mg/m³ | **2.0 / 4.0** mg/m³ | Non-dispersive IR | | O₃ | 8 hr / 1 hr | **120 / 180** µg/m³ | **100 / 120** µg/m³ | UV Photometric | | Pb | 24 hr / Annual | **1.0 / 0.5** µg/m³ | **0.75 / 0.50** µg/m³ | AAS | | NH₃ | 24 hr / Annual | **400 / 100** µg/m³ | **400 / 100** µg/m³ | Indophenol Blue | | C₆H₆ (Benzene) | Annual | **5** µg/m³ | **5** µg/m³ | Gas Chromatography | | BaP (Benzo-a-Pyrene) | Annual | **1** ng/m³ | **1** ng/m³ | HPLC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These standards are the **legal benchmark** used by the **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** to issue **Consent to Operate (CTO)** and **Consent to Establish (CTE)** certificates to industries. Any industry that fails to comply is liable for prosecution, closure, or environmental compensation under the **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** Act. ### 2.6 Vehicular Emission Standards (Bharat Stage) | **Standard** | **Year** | **Equivalent** | **Key Feature** | |---|---|---|---| | India 2000 (BS-I) | 2000 | Euro-I | First norms for India | | BS-II | 2001 | Euro-II | Introduction of unleaded petrol | | BS-III | 2005 | Euro-III | Reduced sulphur (350 ppm → 50 ppm) | | BS-IV | 2017 | Euro-IV | Direct skip from BS-III | | **BS-VI** | **2020** | **Euro-VI** | Sulphur = 10 ppm; Real-driving emissions (RDE); On-board diagnostics mandatory | | **BS-VII** (proposed) | 2027+ | Beyond Euro-VI | Stricter NOx limits; discussion phase | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** A direct **BS-III → BS-VI** jump (skipping IV and V) was implemented in 2020, making India the **first major market** to leapfrog from Euro-IV to Euro-VI standards in a single regulatory step — a major milestone for vehicular air pollution control. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of the AQI Sub-Index Formula (US-EPA Linear Interpolation) **Step 1 — Definition of the Problem:** AQI is defined as a piecewise linear function of pollutant concentration. The CPCB defines a set of "breakpoints" $(B_{LO}, B_{HI})$ and corresponding index values $(I_{LO}, I_{HI})$ for each pollutant. Given a measured concentration $C_p$, we need to find the corresponding sub-index $I_p$. **Step 2 — Assumptions:** - The relationship between concentration and AQI is **linear within a breakpoint band**. - The function is **monotonically increasing** (higher concentration → higher AQI). **Step 3 — Setting up the Linear Equation:** We construct a straight line passing through the two known endpoints: - Point 1: $(B_{LO}, I_{LO})$ - Point 2: $(B_{HI}, I_{HI})$ **Step 4 — Slope of the Line:** $$ m = \dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}} $$ **Step 5 — Point-Slope Form of the Line:** $$ I_p - I_{LO} = m \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) $$ **Step 6 — Substituting and Rearranging:** $$ I_p = I_{LO} + m \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) $$ $$ I_p = I_{LO} + \left[\dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}}\right] \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) $$ **Step 7 — Final Compact Form (Boxed Result):** $$ \boxed{I_p = \left[\dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}}\right] \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) + I_{LO}} $$ **Step 8 — Final AQI Determination:** $$ \boxed{AQI = \max_{p=1}^{8}(I_p)} $$ The AQI reported to the public is the **highest** sub-index among all measured pollutants — a **conservative (health-protective)** approach ensuring that the **most severe pollutant dominates** the daily report. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — AQI Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** The following 24-hour average concentrations were measured at an urban air quality monitoring station: > - PM2.5 = 78 µg/m³ > - PM10 = 152 µg/m³ > - SO₂ = 65 µg/m³ > - NO₂ = 55 µg/m³ > > **Determine the AQI of the station.** **Step 1 — Reference the Indian AQI Breakpoint Table (24-hour averages):** | **Pollutant** | **$B_{LO}$** | **$I_{LO}$** | **$B_{HI}$** | **$I_{HI}$** | |---|---|---|---|---| | PM2.5 (0–30 / 30–60 / 60–90 / 90–120 / 120–250) | 60 | 101 | 90 | 200 | | PM10 (0–50 / 50–100 / 100–250 / 250–350 / 350–430) | 100 | 101 | 250 | 200 | | SO₂ (0–40 / 40–80 / 80–380 / 380–800 / 800–1600) | 40 | 51 | 80 | 100 | | NO₂ (0–40 / 40–80 / 80–180 / 180–280 / 280–400) | 40 | 51 | 80 | 100 | **Step 2 — Compute Sub-Index for PM2.5 ($C_p$ = 78 µg/m³):** $$ I_{PM2.5} = \left[\dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 60}\right] \cdot (78 - 60) + 101 $$ $$ I_{PM2.5} = \left[\dfrac{99}{30}\right] \cdot 18 + 101 $$ $$ I_{PM2.5} = 3.30 \cdot 18 + 101 = 59.40 + 101 = 160.4 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Sub-Index for PM10 ($C_p$ = 152 µg/m³):** $$ I_{PM10} = \left[\dfrac{200 - 101}{250 - 100}\right] \cdot (152 - 100) + 101 $$ $$ I_{PM10} = \left[\dfrac{99}{150}\right] \cdot 52 + 101 = 0.660 \cdot 52 + 101 $$ $$ I_{PM10} = 34.32 + 101 = 135.32 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Sub-Index for SO₂ ($C_p$ = 65 µg/m³):** $$ I_{SO_2} = \left[\dfrac{100 - 51}{80 - 40}\right] \cdot (65 - 40) + 51 $$ $$ I_{SO_2} = \left[\dfrac{49}{40}\right] \cdot 25 + 51 = 1.225 \cdot 25 + 51 $$ $$ I_{SO_2} = 30.625 + 51 = 81.625 $$ **Step 5 — Compute Sub-Index for NO₂ ($C_p$ = 55 µg/m³):** $$ I_{NO_2} = \left[\dfrac{100 - 51}{80 - 40}\right] \cdot (55 - 40) + 51 $$ $$ I_{NO_2} = 1.225 \cdot 15 + 51 = 18.375 + 51 = 69.375 $$ **Step 6 — Determine the Final AQI:** $$ AQI = \max(160.4,\ 135.32,\ 81.625,\ 69.375) = 160.4 \approx 160 $$ **Step 7 — Interpret the Result:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **AQI = 160 → "Moderate" Category (Yellow-Orange)** > *Breathing discomfort to people with lung or heart disease; mild effects on general public on prolonged exposure. The PM2.5 sub-index is the dominant pollutant.* --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Indian vs. WHO Air Quality Guidelines (2021) | **Pollutant** | **Averaging Period** | **NAAQS-India (2009)** | **WHO AQG (2021)** | **Gap Multiplier** | |---|---|---|---|---| | PM2.5 | 24 hr | 60 µg/m³ | **15 µg/m³** | 4× | | PM2.5 | Annual | 40 µg/m³ | **5 µg/m³** | 8× | | PM10 | 24 hr | 100 µg/m³ | **45 µg/m³** | 2.2× | | PM10 | Annual | 60 µg/m³ | **15 µg/m³** | 4× | | NO₂ | 24 hr | 80 µg/m³ | **25 µg/m³** | 3.2× | | SO₂ | 24 hr | 80 µg/m³ | **40 µg/m³** | 2× | | O₃ | 8 hr | 100 µg/m³ | **100 µg/m³** | ≈ 1× | | CO | 24 hr | 4 mg/m³ | **4 mg/m³** | ≈ 1× | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Insight for KTU Examination:** India's PM2.5 standards are **4 to 8 times weaker** than WHO guidelines. The **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)**, launched in 2019, aims to reduce PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations by **20–30% by 2024** and **40% by 2026** from 2017 baseline. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Problem — Mass Emission Rate from a Stack > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A cement plant stack emits PM at a concentration of **150 mg/Nm³**. The flue gas flow rate is **Q = 25 Nm³/s**. Calculate the **mass emission rate of particulate matter (g/s)**. **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:** $$ E = Q_s \cdot C_s $$ where $E$ = emission rate (mg/s), $Q_s$ = flow (Nm³/s), $C_s$ = concentration (mg/Nm³). **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$ E = 25\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{s} \times 150\ \text{mg/Nm}^3 = 3750\ \text{mg/s} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to grams:** $$ E = \dfrac{3750}{1000} = 3.75\ \text{g/s} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to kg/day for environmental load assessment:** $$ E_{day} = 3.75\ \text{g/s} \times 86400\ \text{s/day} = 324{,}000\ \text{g/day} = 324\ \text{kg/day} $$ **Step 5 — Verify against CPCB Particulate Emission Standard for Cement (Stack):** The CPCB standard for cement plant (rotary kiln, with cyclone preheater) is **30 mg/Nm³**. Our value of 150 mg/Nm³ is **5 times the limit** — the industry is **non-compliant** and corrective action (e.g., baghouse filter installation) is mandated. ## 4.
