KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). List any four criteria pollutants monitored under NAAQS along with their standard values (24-hour average) for residential areas.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **NAAQS Definition:** National Ambient Air Quality Standards are the **legally enforceable maximum permissible concentrations** of specific air pollutants in the **ambient atmosphere** to protect public health and the environment. In India, NAAQS were **notified in 2009** by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**. > **Four Criteria Pollutants (Residential Area, 24-hr average):** > 1. **PM2.5** → **60 µg/m³** > 2. **PM10** → **100 µg/m³** > 3. **SO₂** → **80 µg/m³** > 4. **NO₂** → **80 µg/m³** --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is an Air Quality Index (AQI)? Briefly explain the linear interpolation method used to compute the AQI sub-index.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **AQI Definition:** The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a **single composite numerical value** (0–500 scale) that summarizes the cumulative health impact of multiple air pollutants measured in a given period. It transforms raw pollutant concentrations (µg/m³) into a **standardized health-advisory scale**. > **Linear Interpolation Method:** > 1. For each pollutant *p*, the measured concentration $C_p$ is located within a defined **breakpoint band** $[B_{LO}, B_{HI}]$. > 2. The corresponding **index values** $I_{LO}$ and $I_{HI}$ are noted. > 3. The sub-index is calculated as: > $I_p = \left[\dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}}\right] \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) + I_{LO}$ > 4. The **final AQI** is the **maximum** of all sub-indices, ensuring the most critical pollutant governs the advisory. --- ### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]` **(a)** Explain the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) of India in detail. List the eight criteria pollutants with their standards (industrial and residential, 24-hour and annual). **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Calculate the AQI for a city from the following 24-hr pollutant concentrations. Use the Indian AQI breakpoint table provided. - PM2.5 = 55 µg/m³ - PM10 = 130 µg/m³ - SO₂ = 42 µg/m³ - NO₂ = 38 µg/m³ **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for (a):** > [!NOTE] > **NAAQS — Detailed Explanation:** > The **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** were notified in **2009** by the **CPCB** and remain the cornerstone of India's air quality regulation. The standards apply to **outdoor air** across the entire country and are categorized into **two zones** — **Industrial** and **Residential, Rural & Ecological** — with the **stricter (lower)** limits applied to the latter to protect vulnerable populations. > **The Eight Criteria Pollutants and Their Standards:** | **Pollutant** | **Industrial (24-hr / Annual)** | **Residential/Rural (24-hr / Annual)** | |---|---|---| | **PM2.5** | 60 / 40 µg/m³ | 60 / 40 µg/m³ | | **PM10** | 100 / 60 µg/m³ | 100 / 60 µg/m³ | | **SO₂** | 80 / 50 µg/m³ | 80 / 50 µg/m³ | | **NO₂** | 80 / 40 µg/m³ | 80 / 40 µg/m³ | | **CO** | 10.0 / 5.0 mg/m³ | 4.0 / 2.0 mg/m³ | | **O₃** | 180 / 120 µg/m³ | 120 / 100 µg/m³ | | **Pb** | 1.0 / 0.5 µg/m³ | 0.75 / 0.5 µg/m³ | | **NH₃** | 400 / 100 µg/m³ | 400 / 100 µg/m³ | > **Regulatory Authority & Legal Basis:** > - **Act:** Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. > - **Notifier:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), under Section 16. > - **Enforcement:** State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) through **Consent to Operate (CTO)**. > - **Monitoring Network:** National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (**NAMP**) — 804+ stations across India. ##### **Model Solution for (b):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Identify the appropriate breakpoint band for each pollutant:** > **For PM2.5 (Cp = 55 µg/m³):** > This lies in the band [30, 60] → $B_{LO} = 30$, $B_{HI} = 60$, $I_{LO} = 51$, $I_{HI} = 100$ > **For PM10 (Cp = 130 µg/m³):** > This lies in the band [100, 250] → $B_{LO} = 100$, $B_{HI} = 250$, $I_{LO} = 101$, $I_{HI} = 200$ > **For SO₂ (Cp = 42 µg/m³):** > This lies in the band [40, 80] → $B_{LO} = 40$, $B_{HI} = 80$, $I_{LO} = 51$, $I_{HI} = 100$ > **For NO₂ (Cp = 38 µg/m³):** > This lies in the band [0, 40] → $B_{LO} = 0$, $B_{HI} = 40$, $I_{LO} = 0$, $I_{HI} = 50$ > **Step 2 — Compute the Sub-Indices:** $$ I_{PM2.5} = \left[\dfrac{100 - 51}{60 - 30}\right] \cdot (55 - 30) + 51 = \left[\dfrac{49}{30}\right] \cdot 25 + 51 $$ $$ I_{PM2.5} = 1.633 \cdot 25 + 51 = 40.83 + 51 = 91.83 $$ $$ I_{PM10} = \left[\dfrac{200 - 101}{250 - 100}\right] \cdot (130 - 100) + 101 = \left[\dfrac{99}{150}\right] \cdot 30 + 101 $$ $$ I_{PM10} = 0.66 \cdot 30 + 101 = 19.80 + 101 = 120.80 $$ $$ I_{SO_2} = \left[\dfrac{100 - 51}{80 - 40}\right] \cdot (42 - 40) + 51 = \left[\dfrac{49}{40}\right] \cdot 2 + 51 $$ $$ I_{SO_2} = 1.225 \cdot 2 + 51 = 2.45 + 51 = 53.45 $$ $$ I_{NO_2} = \left[\dfrac{50 - 0}{40 - 0}\right] \cdot (38 - 0) + 0 = 1.25 \cdot 38 = 47.5 $$ > **Step 3 — Determine the Final AQI:** $$ AQI = \max(91.83,\ 120.80,\ 53.45,\ 47.50) = 120.80 \approx 121 $$ > **Step 4 — Interpretation:** > AQI = 121 falls in the **"Moderate"** band (101–200), color-coded **yellow-orange**. The **dominant pollutant is PM10**, indicating respirable dust pollution, likely due to construction activity or vehicular road dust resuspension. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between **Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS)** and **Emission Standards**. Explain the importance of BS-VI vehicular emission norms. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A thermal power plant stack emits NOₓ at a measured concentration of 250 mg/Nm³. The flue gas volume flow rate is 50 Nm³/s. The standard emission limit for NOₓ from coal-fired thermal power plants is 100 mg/Nm³. Calculate: (i) the actual NOₓ emission rate, (ii) the emission rate if the industry complies with the standard, and (iii) the excess NOₓ emitted per day. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for (a):** > [!NOTE] > **Differentiation between AAQS and Emission Standards:** | **Aspect** | **AAQS (Ambient Air Quality Standards)** | **Emission Standards** | |---|---|---| | **Point of Application** | Ambient atmosphere (receptor-end) | Discharge point of a specific source (source-end) | | **Objective** | Protect public health & environment in a region | Control pollution at the source | | **Unit** | µg/m³ or mg/m³ of ambient air | mg/Nm³ from stack; g/km from vehicles | | **Set By** | CPCB (national); WHO (international) | CPCB (sector-specific) | | **Examples** | NAAQS (2009), WHO AQG (2021) | BS-VI, CPCB stack standards for cement, TPP, etc. | | **Monitoring Frequency** | Continuous (CAAQMS) | Periodic + Continuous (CEMS) for large industries | > **BS-VI Norms — Importance:** > 1. **Sulphur Content Reduction:** From 50 ppm (BS-IV) to **10 ppm** in fuel. > 2. **NOₓ Reduction:** 25% reduction over BS-IV for diesel cars. > 3. **Particulate Reduction:** ~50% reduction in PM emissions. > 4. **RDE (Real Driving Emissions):** Tests under actual road conditions, not just lab cycles. > 5. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II):** Mandatory — continuous monitoring of engine performance. > 6. **Health Impact:** Estimated to prevent **~70,000 premature deaths** annually in India. ##### **Model Solution for (b):** > **Given:** > - $C_{actual} = 250$ mg/Nm³ > - $Q = 50$ Nm³/s > - $C_{standard} = 100$ mg/Nm³ > **Step (i) — Actual NOₓ Emission Rate:** $$ E_{actual} = Q \cdot C_{actual} = 50\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{s} \times 250\ \text{mg/Nm}^3 = 12{,}500\ \text{mg/s} = 12.5\ \text{g/s} $$ > **Step (ii) — Compliant NOₓ Emission Rate:** $$ E_{standard} = Q \cdot C_{standard} = 50 \times 100 = 5{,}000\ \text{mg/s} = 5.0\ \text{g/s} $$ > **Step (iii) — Excess NOₓ Emitted Per Day:** $$ E_{excess} = E_{actual} - E_{standard} = 12.5 - 5.0 = 7.5\ \text{g/s} $$ $$ E_{excess,day} = 7.5\ \text{g/s} \times 86{,}400\ \text{s/day} = 648{,}000\ \text{g/day} = 648\ \text{kg/day} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Conclusion:** The plant is emitting **648 kg/day of excess NOₓ** above the regulatory standard. Under **Section 25/26 of the Water Act and Section 31A of the Air Act**, the SPCB is empowered to issue a **show-cause notice, impose environmental compensation, or order closure** of the unit. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing AAQS with Emission Standards:** Always specify whether the standard applies to the **ambient atmosphere (µg/m³)** or to a **source (mg/Nm³)**. A 3-mark penalty is typical for mixing these up. > 2. **Forgetting the "Max" Rule for AQI:** Students often report an **average** of sub-indices. The correct AQI is the **maximum** sub-index — the most critical pollutant governs the advisory. > 3. **Wrong Breakpoint Bands:** Always cross-check the **correct averaging period** (1-hr, 8-hr, 24-hr, annual). Different averaging periods have different breakpoint tables. A wrong band selection gives wrong AQI → full marks lost. > 4. **Unit Mismatch in Stack Emissions:** Stack concentrations are at **NTP (25 °C, 1 atm)**, and flow is in **Nm³/s** (not m³/s). Forgetting the "N" prefix is a frequent marker-deduction point. > 5. **Omitting Units in Final Answer:** A numerical answer without units is **incomplete** — KTU evaluators typically deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** per question. > 6. **Missing Legal Framework:** Always cite the **specific Act** (Air Act 1981, EPA 1986) and the **notifying authority (CPCB)** while defining standards. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ambient Air Quality & Emission Standards** - [x] **AAQS** = air quality in the **ambient environment**; **Emission Standards** = permissible discharge from a **specific source**. - [x] **NAAQS (2009)** is notified by **CPCB** under the **Air Act, 1981**. - [x] **8 criteria pollutants:** PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃. - [x] **AQI range:** 0–500; categories: Good → Satisfactory → Moderate → Poor → Very Poor → Severe. - [x] **AQI formula:** $I_p = \left[\dfrac{I_{HI} - I_{LO}}{B_{HI} - B_{LO}}\right] \cdot (C_p - B_{LO}) + I_{LO}$; final AQI = **max** of all sub-indices. - [x] **WHO 2021** PM2.5 limit (24-hr) = **15 µg/m³** vs. Indian **60 µg/m³** — gap of 4×. - [x] **BS-VI** norms enforced in India from **April 2020** — leapfrogged from BS-IV. - [x] **NCAP (2019)** targets **40% reduction in PM2.5/PM10 by 2026** from 2017 baseline. - [x] **NGT** has powers to impose **environmental compensation** and order **industry closure**. - [x] **Stack concentration** is reported in **mg/Nm³** at **25 °C, 1 atm**. - [x] **CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring System) is mandatory for **17 categories of highly polluting industries**. - [x] **CAAQMS** = Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station — uses **Beta attenuation (PM)**, **UV fluorescence (SO₂)**, **Chemiluminescence (NO₂)**, **NDIR (CO)**, and **UV photometry (O₃)**. - [x] **Key Acts to Memorize:** Air Act 1981, Water Act 1974, Environment Protection Act 1986. - [x] **SPCBs** enforce standards; **CPCB** coordinates and sets national standards; **MoEFCC** is the apex policy body. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember 8 Criteria Pollutants: "P-S-N-C-O-L-N-A"** > **(P)M2.5, (S)O₂, (N)O₂, (C)O, (O)₃, (L)ead, (N)H₃, (A)** = "Please Stop Noxious Compounds, Our Lungs Need Air."

topics.title: Air Pollution Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a dimensionless, composite numerical index that transforms weighted ambient concentrations of multiple criteria air pollutants into a single, easy-to-interpret number representing the overall ambient air quality status of a location at a given time.

The index is computed from the **sub-indices** of individual pollutants using a standardized **piecewise linear interpolation** method prescribed by regulatory bodies. In India, the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** introduced the **National Air Quality Index (NAQI)** in **2014**, replacing the older **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)**.

The eight criteria pollutants monitored under the Indian NAQI are: **PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, and Pb**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of AQI as a **"thermometer for air pollution."** Just as a thermometer converts the kinetic energy of mercury atoms into a single readable number (°C/°F) so that anyone can understand "how hot it is," the AQI converts complex pollutant concentrations (μg/m³, mg/m³, ppb) into a single number on a **0–500 scale**, with a color tag and a one-line health advisory.

- **Raw pollutant data** = the raw molecular vibrations (hard to interpret).
- **AQI number** = the visible mercury column (instantly meaningful).
- **Color code + health message** = the verbal "it's hot outside" (actionable).

This **communicative compression** is the engineering purpose of pollution indices — they make invisible chemistry visible to citizens, planners, and emergency response agencies.

### 1.3 Why Air Pollution Indices are Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale for AQI:**
> 1. **Public Health Communication:** Non-technical stakeholders cannot interpret μg/m³ values of multiple gases.
> 2. **Decision Trigger:** AQI > 300 automatically triggers industrial action plans, school closures, and odd-even vehicle schemes in India.
> 3. **Regulatory Benchmarking:** Single-number compliance check across cities and over time.
> 4. **Health-Effects Mapping:** Direct linkage between index value and population-level health advisories.

### 1.4 Historical Evolution

| Index | Country | Year | Pollutants Covered | Scale |
|:------|:--------|:-----|:-------------------|:------|
| PSI (Pollutant Standards Index) | USA → India | 1976 → adopted in India | PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3 | 0–500 |
| AQI (Air Quality Index) | USA (EPA) | 1999 | PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3, Pb | 0–500 |
| NAQI (National AQI) | India (CPCB) | 2014 (operational Apr 2015) | PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb | 0–500 |

### 1.5 Indian AQI Categories & Color Codes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard AQI Categories (CPCB Notification, 2014):**

| AQI Range | Category | Color Code | Health Impact |
|:----------|:---------|:-----------|:--------------|
| 0 – 50 | Good | Blue (Deep) | Minimal impact |
| 51 – 100 | Satisfactory | Light Green | Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people |
| 101 – 200 | Moderately Polluted | Yellow | Breathing discomfort to people with lung/heart disease |
| 201 – 300 | Poor | Orange | Breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure |
| 301 – 400 | Very Poor | Red | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure |
| 401 – 500 | Severe | Maroon | Affects healthy people; serious health impacts for all |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation

The AQI is built on **three engineering principles**:

1. **Worst-Pollutant Dominance:** The reported AQI is the **maximum** of all pollutant sub-indices (a conservative, protective approach).
2. **Piecewise Linear Interpolation:** Within a defined concentration breakpoint range, the sub-index scales linearly with pollutant concentration.
3. **Saturating Capped Scale:** When concentrations exceed the maximum breakpoint, the index is reported as 500 with a "+" symbol; it is **not extrapolated**.

### 2.2 The Sub-Index Master Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPCB Linear Interpolation Formula (Master Equation for Sub-Index):**

$$
I_{p} \;=\; \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times \left(C_{p} - BP_{Lo}\right) + I_{Lo}
$$

Where every symbol has a precise meaning — see the cheat sheet below.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of AQI Determination

1. **Step 1 — Sample & Measure:** Acquire 24-hour (or 8-hour for CO/O3) average concentrations of all eight criteria pollutants at the monitoring station.
2. **Step 2 — Truncate at 24-hr/8-hr Standard:** AQI is reported only when data covers at least **75%** of the averaging period (16 of 24 hours, or 6 of 8 hours).
3. **Step 3 — Locate Breakpoints:** For each pollutant, identify the breakpoint interval $[BP_{Lo}, BP_{Hi}]$ that contains the measured $C_p$.
4. **Step 4 — Apply the Master Equation:** Compute the **sub-index** $I_p$ for every pollutant.
5. **Step 5 — Apply Saturation Cap:** If $C_p$ exceeds the highest breakpoint, $I_p = 500$ (or "AQI 500+").
6. **Step 6 — Take the Maximum:** The overall AQI is $\max(I_{PM2.5}, I_{PM10}, I_{NO2}, I_{SO2}, I_{CO}, I_{O3}, I_{NH3}, I_{Pb})$.
7. **Step 7 — Map to Category & Color:** Look up the dominant pollutant's category and assign color, health message, and advisory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Sheet for Examination:**

| Symbol | Definition | Units / Range |
|:-------|:-----------|:--------------|
| $I_p$ | Sub-index for pollutant $p$ | Dimensionless, 0–500 |
| $C_p$ | Measured (truncated) concentration of pollutant $p$ | μg/m³ or mg/m³ |
| $BP_{Lo}$ | Lower concentration breakpoint of the interval containing $C_p$ | μg/m³ or mg/m³ |
| $BP_{Hi}$ | Upper concentration breakpoint of the interval containing $C_p$ | μg/m³ or mg/m³ |
| $I_{Lo}$ | AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Lo}$ | 0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 |
| $I_{Hi}$ | AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Hi}$ | 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 |
| $\text{AQI}_{\text{overall}}$ | $\max(I_p)$ across all eight pollutants | 0–500 |

### 2.5 Pollutant-Specific Breakpoint Tables (CPCB NAQI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Breakpoints — Memorize the structure, not every number:**

| Pollutant | Averaging Time | $C_{0}$ | $C_{1}$ | $C_{2}$ | $C_{3}$ | $C_{4}$ | $C_{5}$ | Unit |
|:----------|:--------------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:----:|
| PM2.5 | 24 hr | 0–30 | 31–60 | 61–90 | 91–120 | 121–250 | 251–380 | μg/m³ |
| PM10 | 24 hr | 0–50 | 51–100 | 101–250 | 251–350 | 351–430 | 431–600 | μg/m³ |
| NO2 | 24 hr | 0–40 | 41–80 | 81–180 | 181–280 | 281–400 | 401–550 | μg/m³ |
| SO2 | 24 hr | 0–40 | 41–80 | 81–380 | 381–800 | 801–1600 | 1601–2400 | μg/m³ |
| CO | 8 hr | 0–1.0 | 1.1–2.0 | 2.1–10 | 10.1–17 | 17.1–34 | 34.1–50.4 | mg/m³ |
| O3 | 8 hr | 0–50 | 51–100 | 101–168 | 169–208 | 209–748 | 749–1004 | μg/m³ |
| NH3 | 24 hr | 0–200 | 201–400 | 401–800 | 801–1200 | 1201–1800 | 1801–2400 | μg/m³ |
| Pb | 24 hr | 0–0.5 | 0.5–1.0 | 1.1–2.0 | 2.1–3.0 | 3.1–3.5 | 3.6–5.4 | μg/m³ |

| AQI | 0–50 | 51–100 | 101–200 | 201–300 | 301–400 | 401–500 |
|:---:|:----:|:------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart City Dashboards:** Real-time AQI feeds from CPCB's **Sameer App** drive IoT-based public displays and traffic control signals.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** AQI is the **single metric** used to compare pre- and post-project ambient air quality in EIA reports.
- **Industrial Action Plans:** In NCR-Delhi, AQI crossing 300 triggers the **Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)** with specific measures per category.
- **International Benchmarking:** India's 0–500 NAQI scale matches the US EPA scale, allowing global comparison.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Sub-Index Formula

The breakpoint table gives a series of **ordered pairs** $(BP_k, I_k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$, e.g., for PM2.5:

$$(0, 0), \quad (30, 50), \quad (60, 100), \quad (90, 200), \quad (120, 300), \quad (250, 400), \quad (380, 500)$$

For a measured concentration $C_p$ that lies between $BP_{Lo}$ and $BP_{Hi}$, the sub-index is obtained from the **equation of a straight line** passing through the two known points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope } m &= \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \\[4pt]
\text{Line equation: } I_p - I_{Lo} &= m \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad I_p &= \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **CPCB master equation** — derived from elementary coordinate geometry. It is the only equation a student must memorize for AQI problems.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full AQI Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark question):**
> The following 24-hour average concentrations were recorded at an urban air quality monitoring station on 12 November 2024:
>
> - $C_{PM2.5} = 78 \; \mu g/m^3$
> - $C_{PM10} = 145 \; \mu g/m^3$
> - $C_{NO2} = 65 \; \mu g/m^3$
> - $C_{SO2} = 42 \; \mu g/m^3$
> - All other pollutants are below detection limit.
>
> **Determine the overall AQI and identify the health category with the dominant pollutant.**

**Solution — Sub-Index for PM2.5:**

Locate 78 in the PM2.5 breakpoint table: it lies in the interval **61–90**, hence:

- $BP_{Lo} = 60$, $BP_{Hi} = 90$
- $I_{Lo} = 100$, $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $C_p = 78 \; \mu g/m^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{PM2.5} &= \frac{200 - 100}{90 - 60} \times (78 - 60) + 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{30} \times 18 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 3.333 \times 18 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 60.0 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for PM10:**

Locate 145 in the PM10 table: it lies in the interval **101–250**, hence:

- $BP_{Lo} = 100$, $BP_{Hi} = 250$
- $I_{Lo} = 100$, $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $C_p = 145 \; \mu g/m^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{PM10} &= \frac{200 - 100}{250 - 100} \times (145 - 100) + 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{150} \times 45 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.6667 \times 45 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 30.0 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 130
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for NO2:**

Locate 65 in the NO2 table: it lies in the interval **41–80**, hence:

- $BP_{Lo} = 40$, $BP_{Hi} = 80$
- $I_{Lo} = 50$, $I_{Hi} = 100$
- $C_p = 65 \; \mu g/m^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{NO2} &= \frac{100 - 50}{80 - 40} \times (65 - 40) + 50 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50}{40} \times 25 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 1.25 \times 25 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 31.25 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 81.25 \;\approx\; 81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for SO2:**

Locate 42 in the SO2 table: it lies in the interval **41–80**, hence:

- $BP_{Lo} = 40$, $BP_{Hi} = 80$
- $I_{Lo} = 50$, $I_{Hi} = 100$
- $C_p = 42 \; \mu g/m^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SO2} &= \frac{100 - 50}{80 - 40} \times (42 - 40) + 50 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50}{40} \times 2 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 1.25 \times 2 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 52.5 \;\approx\; 53
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AQI}_{\text{overall}} &= \max(I_{PM2.5},\; I_{PM10},\; I_{NO2},\; I_{SO2}) \\[4pt]
&= \max(160,\; 130,\; 81,\; 53) \\[4pt]
&= 160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall AQI = 160, dominated by PM2.5, categorized as "Moderately Polluted" (Yellow).**
> Health advisory: People with lung/heart disease should reduce prolonged outdoor exertion; healthy population may experience minor breathing discomfort.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — PSI vs AQI

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield comparison table for 14-mark questions:**

| Parameter | PSI (Pre-2014 India) | AQI (Post-2014 India, NAQI) |
|:----------|:---------------------|:----------------------------|
| Year of adoption in India | 1984 (modified) | 2014 (CPCB Notification) |
| No. of pollutants | 5 | 8 |
| Pollutants | PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3 | PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb |
| Inclusion of PM2.5 | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Inclusion of NH3 | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Inclusion of Pb | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Averaging periods used | 24-hr (all), 8-hr (CO) | 24-hr (PM, gases, NH3, Pb), 8-hr (CO, O3) |
| Number of breakpoints | 5 (0, 100, 200, 300, 500) | 6 (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500) |
| Health category resolution | 5 categories | 6 categories |
| Methodology | Linear interpolation (same form) | Linear interpolation (same form) |
| Reporting aggregation | Worst sub-index | Worst sub-index |
| Reporting color code | Limited | Full (6-color code) |
| Health advisory specificity | Generic | Pollutant-specific and category-specific |
| Data validation rule | 75% data availability | 75% data availability (16 of 24 hr) |
| Authority | CPCB (legacy) | CPCB (current) |
| Public accessibility | Limited | Real-time via SAFAR, Sameer App, CPCB dashboard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AQI Calculation Flowchart (CPCB Methodology)

The diagram below maps the complete **functional architecture** of the National AQI pipeline — from raw monitoring data to public-facing color-coded advisory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([START AQI PIPELINE])
    nodeS1[STAGE 1: SAMPLE AIR FROM MONITORING STATION]
    nodeS2[STAGE 2: MEASURE 8 NAAQS POLLUTANTS]
    nodeP1[PM2.5]
    nodeP2[PM10]
    nodeP3[NO2]
    nodeP4[SO2]
    nodeP5[CO]
    nodeP6[O3]
    nodeP7[NH3]
    nodeP8[Pb]
    nodeS3[STAGE 3: TRUNCATE TO 24 HR OR 8 HR AVERAGE]
    nodeS4[STAGE 4: CHECK 75 PERCENT DATA AVAILABILITY]
    nodeS5[STAGE 5: LOCATE BREAKPOINT INTERVAL]
    nodeS6[STAGE 6: APPLY CPCB LINEAR INTERPOLATION FORMULA]
    nodeS7[STAGE 7: COMPUTE SUB INDEX I P]
    nodeS8[STAGE 8: CAP I P AT 500 IF C P EXCEEDS RANGE]
    nodeS9[STAGE 9: COMPUTE AQI OVERALL AS MAX I P]
    nodeS10[STAGE 10: IDENTIFY DOMINANT POLLUTANT]
    nodeS11[STAGE 11: MAP TO CATEGORY AND COLOR]
    nodeS12[STAGE 12: ISSUE HEALTH ADVISORY]
    nodeS13[STAGE 13: PUBLISH ON SAFAR SAMEER DASHBOARD]
    nodeEnd([END])
    nodeStart --> nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeP1
    nodeS2 --> nodeP2
    nodeS2 --> nodeP3
    nodeS2 --> nodeP4
    nodeS2 --> nodeP5
    nodeS2 --> nodeP6
    nodeS2 --> nodeP7
    nodeS2 --> nodeP8
    nodeP1 --> nodeS3
    nodeP2 --> nodeS3
    nodeP3 --> nodeS3
    nodeP4 --> nodeS3
    nodeP5 --> nodeS3
    nodeP6 --> nodeS3
    nodeP7 --> nodeS3
    nodeP8 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4 --> nodeS5 --> nodeS6 --> nodeS7 --> nodeS8 --> nodeS9 --> nodeS10 --> nodeS11 --> nodeS12 --> nodeS13 --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NAQI Reporting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FIELD MONITORING STATIONS NAAQS GRADE]
    nodeB[CENTRAL ANALYZER UNIT]
    nodeC[DATA LOGGER AND VALIDATION MODULE]
    nodeD[CPCB SERVER DATABASE]
    nodeE[AQI COMPUTATION ENGINE]
    nodeF[SUBSCRIPT FORMULA APPLIED PER POLLUTANT]
    nodeG[MAXIMUM OPERATOR SELECTS WORST CASE]
    nodeH[CATEGORY AND COLOR LOOKUP TABLE]
    nodeI[PUBLIC FACING DASHBOARD SAFAR SAMEER]
    nodeJ[HEALTH ADVISORY DISPATCH MODULE]
    nodeK[GRAP ACTION TRIGGER WHEN AQI GREATER THAN 300]
    nodeL[ARCHIVE HISTORICAL TRENDS]
    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

### 4.3 AQI Category Mapping (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart2[INPUT OVERALL AQI VALUE]
    nodeAQI1[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 50]
    nodeAQI2[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 100]
    nodeAQI3[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 200]
    nodeAQI4[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 300]
    nodeAQI5[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 400]
    nodeAQI6[IS AQI LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 500]
    nodeC1[CATEGORY GOOD COLOR BLUE]
    nodeC2[CATEGORY SATISFACTORY COLOR LIGHT GREEN]
    nodeC3[CATEGORY MODERATELY POLLUTED COLOR YELLOW]
    nodeC4[CATEGORY POOR COLOR ORANGE]
    nodeC5[CATEGORY VERY POOR COLOR RED]
    nodeC6[CATEGORY SEVERE COLOR MAROON]
    nodeStart2 --> nodeAQI1
    nodeAQI1 -- YES --> nodeC1
    nodeAQI1 -- NO --> nodeAQI2
    nodeAQI2 -- YES --> nodeC2
    nodeAQI2 -- NO --> nodeAQI3
    nodeAQI3 -- YES --> nodeC3
    nodeAQI3 -- NO --> nodeAQI4
    nodeAQI4 -- YES --> nodeC4
    nodeAQI4 -- NO --> nodeAQI5
    nodeAQI5 -- YES --> nodeC5
    nodeAQI5 -- NO --> nodeAQI6
    nodeAQI6 -- YES --> nodeC6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**Define Air Quality Index (AQI). List the eight criteria pollutants monitored under the National Air Quality Index of India.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a composite, dimensionless numerical scale (0–500) that condenses ambient concentrations of multiple criteria air pollutants into a single number, color, and health advisory, enabling easy public communication of air quality status.

The eight criteria pollutants monitored under the CPCB National AQI (2014) are:

1. **Particulate Matter PM2.5** (particles $\leq$ 2.5 μm, 24-hr average, μg/m³)
2. **Particulate Matter PM10** (particles $\leq$ 10 μm, 24-hr average, μg/m³)
3. **Nitrogen Dioxide NO2** (24-hr average, μg/m³)
4. **Sulphur Dioxide SO2** (24-hr average, μg/m³)
5. **Carbon Monoxide CO** (8-hr average, mg/m³)
6. **Ozone O3** (8-hr average, μg/m³)
7. **Ammonia NH3** (24-hr average, μg/m³)
8. **Lead Pb** (24-hr average, μg/m³)

**(3 marks — full marks for correct definition + complete list of 8 pollutants)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**

**What is the Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)? How does it differ from the AQI in terms of pollutants considered and health category resolution?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)** was the first-generation air pollution index adopted in India in 1984 (modified from the US EPA 1976 model). It reported ambient air quality as a single number on a 0–500 scale computed from sub-indices of five pollutants.

**Key differences from AQI:**

| Aspect | PSI | AQI (NAQI) |
|:-------|:----|:-----------|
| Number of pollutants | 5 | 8 |
| Pollutants covered | PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3 | PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb |
| Health categories | 5 (broad) | 6 (finer resolution) |
| Breakpoints | 5 intervals | 6 intervals |
| PM2.5 monitoring | Absent (only PM10) | Present |

The AQI was introduced in 2014 by the CPCB to **overcome the limitations of PSI** — particularly the absence of PM2.5 (the most health-critical pollutant) and finer health category resolution.

**(3 marks — full marks for definition + at least three valid differences)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — Internal Choice Pair]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the methodology adopted by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** for computing the National Air Quality Index (NAQI) in India. Include the master sub-index formula and the procedure for identifying the dominant pollutant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following pollutant concentrations were measured at an industrial area air quality station on a winter morning:

- $C_{PM2.5} = 145 \; \mu g/m^3$
- $C_{PM10} = 280 \; \mu g/m^3$
- $C_{NO2} = 95 \; \mu g/m^3$
- $C_{SO2} = 55 \; \mu g/m^3$

Compute the **overall AQI**, identify the **dominant pollutant**, and state the **health category** with the color code. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — CPCB AQI Methodology (7 marks)

The CPCB's National AQI computation follows a **standardized, linear-segment-based procedure** applicable to all eight criteria pollutants.

**Step 1: Pollutants and Averaging Periods**

The eight pollutants are PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, and Pb. A 24-hour averaging period is used for PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, NH3, and Pb; an 8-hour averaging period is used for CO and O3.

**Step 2: Data Validation**

AQI is reported only if valid data is available for at least **75% of the averaging period** (i.e., 18 hours out of 24, or 6 hours out of 8).

**Step 3: Breakpoint Identification**

The CPCB has published **six concentration breakpoint intervals** for each pollutant, corresponding to AQI values 0–50, 51–100, 101–200, 201–300, 301–400, and 401–500. For the measured concentration $C_p$, the interval $[BP_{Lo}, BP_{Hi}]$ that contains $C_p$ is identified.

**Step 4: Sub-Index Calculation (Master Equation)**

$$
I_p \;=\; \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}
$$

This linear interpolation transforms the measured concentration into a unitless sub-index on the 0–500 scale. If $C_p$ exceeds the highest breakpoint, the sub-index is capped at **500** (reported as "AQI 500+").

**Step 5: Overall AQI Determination**

$$
\text{AQI}_{\text{overall}} = \max(I_{PM2.5},\; I_{PM10},\; I_{NO2},\; I_{SO2},\; I_{CO},\; I_{O3},\; I_{NH3},\; I_{Pb})
$$

**Step 6: Dominant Pollutant Identification**

The pollutant whose sub-index equals the overall AQI is called the **dominant (or critical) pollutant**. It is reported alongside the AQI value (e.g., "AQI 178 — Dominant: PM2.5").

**Step 7: Category, Color, and Advisory Mapping**

The overall AQI is mapped to one of six categories (Good, Satisfactory, Moderately Polluted, Poor, Very Poor, Severe) with corresponding color codes and health advisories.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Sub-Index for PM2.5:**

$C_{PM2.5} = 145 \; \mu g/m^3$ lies in the PM2.5 interval **121–250**:

- $BP_{Lo} = 120$, $BP_{Hi} = 250$, $I_{Lo} = 300$, $I_{Hi} = 400$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{PM2.5} &= \frac{400 - 300}{250 - 120} \times (145 - 120) + 300 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{130} \times 25 + 300 \\[4pt]
&= 0.7692 \times 25 + 300 \\[4pt]
&= 19.23 + 300 \\[4pt]
&= 319.23 \;\approx\; 319
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for PM10:**

$C_{PM10} = 280 \; \mu g/m^3$ lies in the PM10 interval **251–350**:

- $BP_{Lo} = 250$, $BP_{Hi} = 350$, $I_{Lo} = 200$, $I_{Hi} = 300$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{PM10} &= \frac{300 - 200}{350 - 250} \times (280 - 250) + 200 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{100} \times 30 + 200 \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 \times 30 + 200 \\[4pt]
&= 30 + 200 \\[4pt]
&= 230
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for NO2:**

$C_{NO2} = 95 \; \mu g/m^3$ lies in the NO2 interval **81–180**:

- $BP_{Lo} = 80$, $BP_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Lo} = 100$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{NO2} &= \frac{200 - 100}{180 - 80} \times (95 - 80) + 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{100} \times 15 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 \times 15 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 15 + 100 \\[4pt]
&= 115
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-Index for SO2:**

$C_{SO2} = 55 \; \mu g/m^3$ lies in the SO2 interval **41–80**:

- $BP_{Lo} = 40$, $BP_{Hi} = 80$, $I_{Lo} = 50$, $I_{Hi} = 100$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SO2} &= \frac{100 - 50}{80 - 40} \times (55 - 40) + 50 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50}{40} \times 15 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 1.25 \times 15 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 18.75 + 50 \\[4pt]
&= 68.75 \;\approx\; 69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregation and Final Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AQI}_{\text{overall}} &= \max(319,\; 230,\; 115,\; 69) \\[4pt]
&= 319
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: Overall AQI = 319, Dominant Pollutant = PM2.5, Health Category = "Very Poor" (Red Color Code).**
> Health advisory: Respiratory illness likely on prolonged exposure; people with existing heart/lung disease must avoid outdoor activity; general public should limit exertion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 — Internal Choice Pair]**

**(a)** Compare the **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)** and the **Air Quality Index (AQI)** with respect to the pollutants considered, methodology, breakpoint structure, health category resolution, and reporting aggregation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the **six AQI categories** prescribed by CPCB with their respective **color codes**, **numerical ranges**, and **associated health impacts**. Explain why AQI crossing **300** triggers the **Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)** in NCR-Delhi. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — PSI vs AQI Comparison (7 marks)

A tabular comparison is the standard KTU answer format for this question. The detailed comparison is provided below.

**1. Pollutants Considered:**

The PSI considered only **five pollutants** — PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, and O3. The AQI expanded this to **eight pollutants** by adding PM2.5, NH3, and Pb. The most significant addition is **PM2.5**, recognized by modern medical research as the most health-damaging particulate metric.

**2. Methodology:**

Both indices use the **same linear interpolation sub-index formula**:

$$
I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}
$$

The aggregation rule is also identical: the **overall index is the maximum of all sub-indices**, and the index is **capped at 500** with no extrapolation.

**3. Breakpoint Structure:**

- **PSI:** 5 breakpoints (0, 100, 200, 300, 500), creating 4 intervals and 5 categories.
- **AQI:** 6 breakpoints (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500), creating 5 intervals and **6 categories** with finer health resolution.

**4. Health Category Resolution:**

The AQI's "Satisfactory" (51–100) and "Moderately Polluted" (101–200) categories allow the public to distinguish between "acceptable with minor sensitivity" and "active breathing discomfort for at-risk groups" — a distinction that PSI's coarser 5-category scale could not capture.

**5. Reporting Aggregation:**

Both indices report the **worst sub-index as the overall value** and identify the **dominant pollutant**. The AQI additionally mandates a **75% data-availability rule** for valid reporting and a **pollutant-specific health advisory message**.

**Summary Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PSI | AQI |
|:----------|:----|:----|
| Pollutants | 5 | 8 |
| PM2.5 included | No | Yes |
| NH3, Pb included | No | Yes |
| Breakpoints | 5 | 6 |
| Health categories | 5 | 6 |
| Color-coded reporting | Limited | Full |
| Data validation rule | None | 75% data availability |

---

#### Part (b) — AQI Categories, Colors, and GRAP Trigger (7 marks)

**The six AQI categories prescribed by CPCB (2014) are:**

| AQI Range | Category | Color Code | Health Impact |
|:----------|:---------|:-----------|:--------------|
| 0 – 50 | Good | Blue | No health impact; air quality is satisfactory. |
| 51 – 100 | Satisfactory | Light Green | Minor breathing discomfort to highly sensitive people. |
| 101 – 200 | Moderately Polluted | Yellow | Breathing discomfort to people with lung/heart disease, children, and elderly. |
| 201 – 300 | Poor | Orange | Breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure; aggravated heart/lung disease. |
| 301 – 400 | Very Poor | Red | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure; reduced lung function in healthy people. |
| 401 – 500 | Severe | Maroon | Affects healthy people; serious health impacts for all populations. |

**Why GRAP Triggers at AQI > 300:**

The **Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)** was notified by the **Environment Pollution (Prevention and Control) Authority (EPCA)** for the National Capital Region (NCR) of Delhi. The plan operates on a **four-stage escalation framework** keyed to specific AQI thresholds:

- **AQI 201–300 (Poor):** Stage I — Mechanized sweeping, water sprinkling, enforcement of pollution norms for industries.
- **AQI 301–400 (Very Poor):** Stage II — Ban on diesel generator sets (except emergency), parking fee hikes to discourage private vehicles, enhanced public transport.
- **AQI 401–450 (Severe):** Stage III — Stoppage of construction and demolition activities, brick kilns, stone crushers; odd-even vehicle scheme.
- **AQI > 450 (Severe+):** Stage IV — Emergency measures including closure of schools, entry of trucks banned, and possible odd-even extension.

The threshold of **AQI 300** is chosen because, beyond this level, the **entire population (not just sensitive groups) begins to show measurable respiratory symptoms** on prolonged exposure. The "Very Poor" category is the inflection point where the index is no longer a "sensitive group warning" but a "general public health emergency trigger." Therefore, the regulatory system is pre-programmed to escalate to Stage II (and potentially Stage III) when AQI crosses 300.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to identify the dominant pollutant:** Many students compute the AQI correctly but forget to state the **critical/dominant pollutant** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this omission.
> 2. **Using the wrong breakpoint interval:** Always re-verify the interval from the standard CPCB table; using the wrong $BP_{Lo}/BP_{Hi}$ is the most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Confusing averaging periods:** CO and O3 use **8-hr averages**, while PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, NH3, and Pb use **24-hr averages**. Mixing these is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting the 75% data validity rule:** In theory questions, mentioning the 75% data availability requirement carries bonus marks.
> 5. **Exceeding 500:** The sub-index is **capped at 500** — it must not be extrapolated beyond the highest breakpoint. Students who extrapolate linearly lose marks.
> 6. **PSI vs AQI confusion:** The AQI includes PM2.5; PSI does not. This is a guaranteed mark question and a frequent error.
> 7. **Omitting color codes:** AQI categories MUST be reported with their **color codes** (Blue, Light Green, Yellow, Orange, Red, Maroon).
> 8. **Not writing units in the final answer:** Always state the units (μg/m³, mg/m³) of the input concentrations; examiners check for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ AQI is a **0–500 dimensionless composite index** that compresses multi-pollutant data into a single number for public communication.
- ✅ **Eight criteria pollutants** in Indian NAQI: PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb.
- ✅ **Averaging periods:** 24-hr for PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, NH3, Pb; 8-hr for CO and O3.
- ✅ **Master Sub-Index Formula:** $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$.
- ✅ **Aggregation Rule:** $\text{AQI}_{\text{overall}} = \max(I_p)$ across all pollutants.
- ✅ **Cap Rule:** Sub-index is **capped at 500** (no extrapolation beyond highest breakpoint).
- ✅ **Data Validity Rule:** At least **75% data availability** required for valid reporting.
- ✅ **Six AQI Categories:** Good, Satisfactory, Moderately Polluted, Poor, Very Poor, Severe.
- ✅ **Six Color Codes:** Blue, Light Green, Yellow, Orange, Red, Maroon.
- ✅ **Dominant Pollutant:** The pollutant whose sub-index equals the overall AQI.
- ✅ **PSI vs AQI:** PSI had only 5 pollutants (no PM2.5, no NH3, no Pb); AQI has 8 with finer health resolution.
- ✅ **GRAP Trigger:** Activated when AQI > 300 in NCR-Delhi under EPCA notification.
- ✅ **CPCB introduced NAQI in 2014** to replace the legacy PSI framework.
- ✅ **The breakpoint interval $[BP_{Lo}, BP_{Hi}]$ must be identified FIRST** before applying the master equation.
- ✅ AQI is a **piecewise-linear function** of concentration — not a true continuous function.
- ✅ AQI is a **conservative (worst-case) indicator** — it is always the maximum sub-index.
- ✅ AQI is **not a mass or volume** — it is a unitless reporting scale.
- ✅ **SAFAR (System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting And Research)** and the **Sameer App** are the real-time public dissemination platforms for NAQI in India.
- ✅ AQI is a **regulatory and public health tool**, not a direct physical measurement; it is a derivative index.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Act
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET861 - WATER AND AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Act

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Air Act – Formal Definition

The **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** is an **Act of the Parliament of India** enacted to provide for the **prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution**, and for the establishment of **Boards at the Central and State levels** with a view to carrying out the aforesaid purposes. The Act was assented to by the President of India on **29 March 1981** and came into force on **16 May 1981**.

It is administered by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC)**, Government of India. The Act is complemented by the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**, and in many Sections, the Air Act borrows the structural framework of the Water Act, designating the same Boards (where they exist) to also function as State Air Pollution Control Boards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The Air Act is a **statutory, regulatory, and command-and-control** environmental law — not a technical engineering code. Examination questions will test your ability to recall *Sections, powers, penalties, and the institutional framework (CPCB/SPCB)* — not numerical air dispersion modelling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Amendment:** The Air Act was amended in **1987** to bring **noise pollution** within its scope by inserting *“noise”* into the definition of **“air pollutant”** under **Section 2(a)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "How Does the Act Work?"

Imagine a **municipal traffic warden system** for the atmosphere:

| Real-World Analogy | Air Act Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A city's traffic police | **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** – national traffic police for air |
| State-wise traffic police | **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** – state-level air wardens |
| Speed limits on roads | **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** – pollutant limits in air |
| Declaring a no-parking zone | **Declaration of Air Pollution Control Areas (APCA)** under Section 19 |
| Getting a "licence" to drive a heavy vehicle | **Consent to Operate** under Sections 21/22 |
| Challan / Penalty for breaking rules | **Penalties under Section 26** – imprisonment up to **6 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh (now ₹10 lakh with CPI-linked revision)** |
| Higher court's appeal against a challan | **Appeals to the Appellate Authority under Section 25 / National Green Tribunal (NGT)** |

**In simple English:** The Air Act tells us *who* is in charge of cleaning the air (CPCB + SPCBs), *what* levels of pollutants are tolerable (Standards), *where* the rules apply strictly (Pollution Control Areas), and *what* happens if industries break the rules (Consent cancellation + Jail + Fine).

---

## 1.3 Objectives of the Air Act (Section-wise Reading)

The Act rests on **Five Pillars** (a high-yield KTU exam framework):

1. **Pillar 1 – Institutional Framework:** Constitution of CPCB and SPCBs.
2. **Pillar 2 – Standards Setting:** Power to lay down ambient air quality standards and emission standards.
3. **Pillar 3 – Area Declaration:** Power to declare any area as an *Air Pollution Control Area* (APCA).
4. **Pillar 4 – Consent Mechanism:** No person shall establish or operate any industrial plant without prior **Consent of the SPCB**.
5. **Pillar 5 – Enforcement & Penalties:** Penalties for contravention, powers of entry/inspection, and appeal mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salient Features:**
> - The Act applies to the **whole of India** (Section 1(2)).
> - It defines **air pollution** comprehensively to include the *presence in the atmosphere of any air pollutant* (Section 2(b)).
> - It empowers the **Central Government** to make rules (Section 53) and the **State Government** to make rules for State Boards (Section 54).
> - The Act has **53 Sections** in total across **7 Chapters**.

---

## 1.4 Key Definitions (Section 2 – Definitions)

| Term | Section | Definition (Simplified) |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Pollutant** | 2(a) | Any solid, liquid, or gaseous substance (including **noise**) present in the atmosphere in such concentration as may be or tend to be injurious to human beings, other living organisms, property, or the environment. |
| **Air Pollution** | 2(b) | The presence in the atmosphere of any air pollutant. |
| **Approved Fuel** | 2(c) | Any fuel approved by the State Government for the purposes of the Act. |
| **Industrial Plant** | 2(d) | Any plant used for industrial or trade purposes and emitting air pollutants. |
| **Member** | 2(e) | A member of a Board (CPCB/SPCB). |
| **Occupier** | 2(f) | The person who has control over the affairs of an industrial plant. |

---

## 1.5 Important Constitutional & Statutory Position

The Air Act operates under **Entry 24 of List I (Union List)** of the **Seventh Schedule** of the Indian Constitution, which gives the **Central Government** the primary responsibility for **mines and oilfields**, but environmental protection falls under **Entry 6 of List II (State List)** and **Article 48A (DPSP)**. Hence the Act is implemented in a **cooperative federalism** mode, with **CPCB** as the national apex body and **SPCBs** as field-level enforcement arms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Air Act – Chapter-by-Chapter Architecture

The Act is organized into **7 Chapters** and **53 Sections**. For KTU 2024 ESE, the student must remember the chapter framework:

| Chapter | Sections | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Chapter I – Preliminary** | Sections 1–2 | Short title, extent, commencement, definitions |
| **Chapter II – Central & State Boards** | Sections 3–15 | Constitution, composition, functions, terms of office, vacancies |
| **Chapter III – Powers & Functions** | Sections 16–18 | General functions of CPCB and SPCBs |
| **Chapter IV – Air Pollution Control Areas & Standards** | Sections 19–20 | Declaration of APCA; power to prescribe standards |
| **Chapter V – Control of Air Pollution** | Sections 21–25 | Consent mechanism, application to court, directions, appeal |
| **Chapter VI – Penalties & Procedure** | Sections 26–38 | Offenses, penalties, offences by companies, cognizance of offences |
| **Chapter VII – Miscellaneous** | Sections 39–53 | Power of Central Govt to make rules, protection of action taken in good faith, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The most frequently asked sections in the KTU ESE are **Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, 16, 17, 19, 20, 21, 22, 25, and 26**.

---

## 2.2 The Air Act Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

The Air Act is a **legal-regulatory** subject and hence does not contain engineering formulas. However, the following is a **high-yield structural formula sheet** for KTU examination answers:

| Provision | Section No. | Key Power / Concept | Sanction / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definitions | **2(a)–(h)** | Air pollutant includes noise (post-1987) | — |
| Constitution of CPCB | **3** | Central apex body | Max **15 members** |
| Constitution of SPCB | **4** | State-level body | Max **15 members** |
| Joint Water + Air Board | **5** | Single State Board for both Acts | At least **5 members** full-time |
| Functions of CPCB | **16** | Coordinate, advise, plan, collect data | National level |
| Functions of SPCB | **17** | Inspect, advise, plan, enforce | State level |
| Declaration of APCA | **19** | State Govt declares APCA on SPCB recommendation | By notification in **Official Gazette** |
| Standards for emissions | **20** | SPCB prescribes standards for industry/stack | State-specific |
| **Consent to Establish** | **21** | No industrial plant without consent | Mandatory |
| **Consent to Operate** | **22** | No operation without consent | Mandatory |
| Power to apply to court | **23** | SPCB can approach court to restrain pollution | Inquiring magistrate's court |
| Power to give directions | **24** | SPCB can direct closure/regulation | 15 days notice |
| Appeals | **25** | To **Appellate Authority** within **30 days** | Tribunal / court |
| Penalties | **26** | Failure to comply with Section 21/22 directions | Imprisonment up to **1.5 years** + fine |
| Penalty for continuing offence | **27** | Continuing failure to comply | Additional **₹100/day** fine |
| Offences by companies | **28** | Director/Manager liable | Fine up to **₹1 lakh** + jail up to **6 years** |
| Cognizance of offences | **30** | No court shall take cognizance except on SPCB complaint | After **60 days** notice |
| Rule-making by Central Govt | **53** | Power to frame rules | — |
| Rule-making by State Govt | **54** | Power to frame rules | — |

---

## 2.3 Operational Logic – "How the Air Act Works Step-by-Step"

Let us break down the operational logic of the Act in **engineering-style** flow steps:

**Step 1: Identification of Pollutants**
The CPCB under **Section 16(2)(h)** identifies the major air pollutants: **PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Lead, Benzene, BaP, As, Ni**.

**Step 2: Setting of Standards**
- **Ambient Standards** – NAAQS prescribed by CPCB for ambient air (notification **16 November 2009**, last revised).
- **Emission Standards** – Stack emission standards prescribed by CPCB for specific industries under **Section 17(1)(g)**.

**Step 3: Declaration of APCA (Air Pollution Control Area)**
The State Government, on the recommendation of the SPCB, declares any area as an APCA under **Section 19**. Within an APCA:
- The use of any fuel other than **approved fuel** is restricted (Section 23 of Rules).
- Industry cannot be set up without consent.

**Step 4: Consent Mechanism (Sections 21–22)**
Any industry in an APCA must apply for:
- **Consent to Establish (CTE)** – before construction
- **Consent to Operate (CTO)** – before commissioning

**Step 5: Inspection & Monitoring**
SPCB under **Section 24** can:
- Enter and inspect any premises
- Take samples
- Examine records
- Direct the closure, prohibition, or regulation of any industrial plant

**Step 6: Enforcement & Penalties**
- Failure to comply → **Penalty under Section 26** (imprisonment up to 1.5 years, or fine up to ₹1 lakh, or both).
- Continuing failure → **Section 27** (additional fine of ₹100/day; may extend to **₹5,000** for repeated violation).
- Offence by company → **Section 28** (every person in charge is liable).
- Offence by government department → **Section 29** (Head of Department liable).
- Cognizance by court → **Section 30** (only on SPCB complaint, with **60 days** prior notice).

**Step 7: Appeals**
Any person aggrieved by an order of the SPCB may appeal to the **Appellate Authority** under **Section 25** within **30 days**. Post-2010, the **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** under the **NGT Act, 2010** has appellate jurisdiction for substantial environmental questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Linkage:** The **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010** and the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** operate *parallel* to the Air Act. The NGT can hear *all* environmental cases involving the Air Act, Water Act, EPA, Forest Conservation Act, and Public Liability Insurance Act.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The Air Act is the **primary legal instrument** that:

- Empowers the **CPCB** to publish the **National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)**.
- Forms the legal basis for the **National Air Quality Index (AQI)** introduced in **2014** by CPCB.
- Supports the **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** launched in **January 2019** to achieve a **20–30% reduction in PM2.5 and PM10 by 2024** (later extended to 2026).
- Provides the legal foundation for city-level **Clean Air Action Plans (CAAPs)** in **131 non-attainment cities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** As a B.Tech (Civil / Environmental) graduate, you will routinely interact with the Air Act when designing:
> - **Emission stacks** (height as per CPCB guidelines)
> - **Air Quality Monitoring Stations (AQMS)**
> - **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** clearances
> - **Consent applications** (CTE/CTO) for your plant designs

---

## 2.5 Penalties Matrix (Quick Recall Table)

| Offence | Section | Punishment |
|---|---|---|
| Failure to obtain consent (Sec 21/22) | **26** | Imprisonment up to **1.5 years** OR fine up to **₹10,000**, or both |
| Continuing failure | **27** | Additional fine of **₹5,000/day** (post-amendment) |
| Contravention of Sec 22 directions | **26(2)** | Imprisonment **6 months – 2 years** + fine |
| Offence by company | **28** | Director/Manager liable; fine up to **₹1 lakh** + imprisonment up to **2 years** |
| Offence by govt department | **29** | Head of Department deemed guilty |
| Cognizance of offence | **30** | Only on SPCB complaint, after 60 days notice |
| False statement in consent application | **26(1)(c)** | Imprisonment up to **3 months** OR fine up to **₹10,000**, or both |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the penalty durations. Memorize: *Section 26(2) is the toughest — 6 months to 2 years jail. Section 28 (company offence) — 2 years jail + ₹1 lakh fine.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Air Act's Enforcement Chain

This section provides a **rigorous legal-logical derivation** of how the Air Act is enforced — written in a way that maps directly onto a KTU 14-mark answer.

### Step 1: Trigger Event — Existence of an Industrial Plant

Any **person** (individual, company, partnership, LLP, govt department) wishing to set up an industrial plant that emits air pollutants (or is located in an Air Pollution Control Area) must trigger the consent procedure.

> **The Trigger:** $\text{Industrial Plant Establishment/Operation} \implies \text{Statutory Obligation to Apply for Consent}$

### Step 2: Statutory Source — Section 21 of the Air Act

> **Section 21(1):** *"No person shall, without the previous consent of the State Board, establish or take any step to establish any industrial plant in an air pollution control area."*

The **antecedent** (A) is: *Plant in APCA*. The **consequent** (B) is: *SPCB consent mandatory*. The **logical form** is:

$$
A \rightarrow B \quad \text{where} \quad A = \text{Plant establishment in APCA}, \quad B = \text{Prior Consent of SPCB}
$$

### Step 3: Application Procedure (Section 22)

The applicant submits an application in the prescribed form to the SPCB, accompanied by:
- **Process flowchart** of the industry
- **Stack emission details** (height, diameter, temperature, velocity)
- **Effluent/waste management plan**
- **Consent fees** (as per state rules)

The SPCB has the power to:
- **Grant** consent unconditionally
- **Grant** consent with conditions
- **Refuse** consent for valid reasons (Section 21(2))

### Step 4: Inquiry & Site Inspection (Section 24)

If the SPCB has *reason to believe* that an offence is being committed, it can:
- **Enter** any place at *any reasonable time*
- **Inspect** the premises, plant, equipment
- **Examine** any record, register, document
- **Take samples** of air, water, raw material, product

The Board officer is treated as a **public servant** under **Section 30** for legal protection.

### Step 5: Decision Tree – Powers of SPCB upon Non-Compliance

$$
\text{Non-Compliance Detected} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Section 23: Application to Court} \\ \text{Section 24: Issue Directions} \\ \text{Section 25: Appeal Mechanism} \end{cases}
$$

#### Sub-step 5a: Section 23 — Application to Court
If the SPCB is of the opinion that a person is contravening the Act, it can **apply to a court** (not below the rank of a Metropolitan Magistrate or First Class Judicial Magistrate) for an order to **restrain** such person from causing air pollution.

#### Sub-step 5b: Section 24 — Directions
The SPCB can, by order, direct the occupier to:
- **Close down** the plant
- **Stop** the use of any fuel
- **Install** specified control equipment
- **Take** such other measures as the Board may direct

The occupier must be given a **reasonable opportunity** of being heard, and the direction must specify the **time period** for compliance.

#### Sub-step 5c: Section 25 — Appeals
Any person aggrieved by the order/direction of the SPCB may appeal to the **Appellate Authority** constituted under **Section 28 of the Water Act** (which applies to the Air Act through Section 25) within **30 days** of the order.

> **Time Computation:** 
> $$\text{Limitation Period} = 30 \text{ days from date of order}$$
> 
> Beyond 30 days, the appeal may be condoned only if sufficient cause is shown.

### Step 6: Penalty Computation

Let us formulate the penalty computation **algorithmically** (in plain math, not code):

$$
\text{Total Penalty} = \text{Base Fine} + (\text{Fine per Day} \times \text{Days of Continuing Offence})
$$

Where:
- $\text{Base Fine (Sec 26)} = \text{Up to } \mathbf{₹10,000}$
- $\text{Fine per Day (Sec 27)} = \mathbf{₹5,000/day}$ (after 1987 amendment)
- $\text{Days of Continuing Offence} = $ Count of calendar days from date of direction to date of compliance

> **Example Calculation (KTU Style):**
> 
> An industry in an APCA continues to operate without SPCB consent for **45 days** after a closure order. Compute the maximum penalty.
> 
> $$\text{Base Fine (Section 26)} = ₹10,000$$
> $$\text{Continuing Fine (Section 27)} = ₹5,000 \times 45 = ₹2,25,000$$
> $$\text{Total Penalty} = ₹10,000 + ₹2,25,000 = ₹2,35,000$$
> 
> Additionally, the occupier faces imprisonment up to **1.5 years** under Section 26.

### Step 7: Cognizance & Trial Procedure (Section 30)

No court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under the Act, **except on a complaint made by the SPCB**, and no such complaint shall be made **except after giving the accused a notice of not less than 60 days** of the offence alleged and the intention to prosecute.

> **Notice Period Formula:**
> 
> $$\text{Minimum Notice Period} = 60 \text{ days (calendar days)}$$
> 
> The notice must specify: (a) the offence alleged, (b) the time within which the accused should remedy, and (c) the intention to file complaint.

### Step 8: Final Logical Closure

The full enforcement chain can be represented as a **state machine**:

$$
\text{Plant Setup} \rightarrow \text{Consent Application} \rightarrow \text{Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Directions} \rightarrow \text{Appeal} \rightarrow \text{Penalty/Jail}
$$

And in case of appeal:
$$
\text{Directions} \rightarrow \text{Appellate Authority (30 days)} \rightarrow \text{NGT (Substantial Question of Environment)} \rightarrow \text{Supreme Court (Article 136)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** When the examiner asks *"Explain the enforcement mechanism under the Air Act"* (a common 7-mark or 14-mark question), your answer MUST trace the above chain with **section numbers** at each step. This is the *valuation key*.

---

## 3.2 Comparison Matrix — Air Act vs Water Act vs EPA 1986

| Feature | Water Act, 1974 | **Air Act, 1981** | EPA, 1986 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year of Enactment | 1974 | **1981** | 1986 |
| Pollution Medium | Water | **Air (incl. noise)** | Environment (Umbrella) |
| Apex Body | CPCB | **CPCB** | MoEF&CC |
| State Body | SPCB | **SPCB** | — |
| Consent Mechanism | Sections 25/26 | **Sections 21/22** | — (no consent mechanism) |
| APCA Declaration | Section 19 (water pollution control area) | **Section 19 (APCA)** | — |
| Penalties | Up to 6 years jail + fine | **Up to 6 years jail + fine** | Up to 5 years jail + fine up to ₹1 lakh |
| Noise Pollution | Not covered | **Covered (post-1987)** | Covered |
| Penalties for first offence | 3 months – 1 year jail | **1.5 years jail** | 5 years jail |
| Hazardous Waste | Not specifically | **Not specifically** | Specifically |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "air pollutant" as per the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. State any two air pollutants.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **Section 2(a)** of the Air Act, 1981, **"air pollutant"** means any solid, liquid or gaseous substance (including **noise**) present in the atmosphere in such concentration as may be or tend to be injurious to human beings, other living creatures, plants, property, or the environment.

Two examples of air pollutants:
1. **Particulate Matter (PM10 and PM2.5)** – solid/liquid particles suspended in air.
2. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** – gaseous pollutant from combustion of fossil fuels.

> *(Note: Noise was added as an air pollutant by the Air (Amendment) Act, 1987.)*

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the functions of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Air Act.**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 16** of the Air Act, 1981, the functions of the CPCB include:
1. **Advise** the Central Government on matters relating to air pollution prevention, control, and abatement.
2. **Coordinate** the activities of State Pollution Control Boards and resolve disputes.
3. **Lay down standards** for the quality of air (**NAAQS**).
4. **Collect and disseminate** information relating to air pollution.
5. **Plan and execute** nationwide programmes for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain in detail the composition, functions, and powers of the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) under the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Composition of the SPCB (7 Marks)

As per **Section 4** of the Air Act, 1981, the State Government shall, by notification in the Official Gazette, constitute a State Pollution Control Board for the purpose of the Act. The composition is as follows:

- A **Chairman** to be nominated by the State Government.
- A **Member-Secretary** to be appointed by the State Government.
- **Up to 14 other members** to be nominated by the State Government, representing the following categories:
  1. Officials of the State Government (representing Health, Industries, Environment departments).
  2. Persons representing local authorities, municipal bodies, or corporations.
  3. Persons representing persons engaged in agriculture, fishery, or industry.
  4. Persons representing registered medical practitioners.
  5. Persons with special knowledge in environmental science, engineering, or law.

**Total members: Maximum 15.**

Under **Section 5**, a **single State Board** can function for both the Water Act and the Air Act, provided it has at least **5 full-time members**.

The **term of office**, **terms and conditions of service**, and **vacation of office** of the Chairman and members are governed by **Sections 6 to 10**.

---

#### (b) Functions and Powers of the SPCB (7 Marks)

The **functions of the SPCB** are enumerated in **Section 17** of the Act:

**Functions (Section 17):**
1. Plan and execute a comprehensive programme for prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution in the State.
2. Advise the State Government on matters relating to air pollution.
3. Collect and disseminate information relating to air pollution.
4. Collaborate with CPCB in executing the National Programme.
5. Inspect, at all reasonable times, any control equipment, industrial plant, or manufacturing process.
6. Lay down standards for emissions from industrial plants and automobiles.
7. Advise industries on the use of pollution control equipment.

**Powers of the SPCB:**
- **Section 23:** Apply to a court for an order to restrain persons from causing air pollution.
- **Section 24:** Issue directions for closure, prohibition, or regulation of industrial plants.
- **Section 26:** Prosecute offenders.

**Conclusion:** The SPCB is the **field-level enforcement arm** of the Air Act, with quasi-judicial and quasi-executive powers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Discuss the salient features and salient provisions of the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981, with particular emphasis on Consent Mechanism and Penalties.**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Salient Features of the Air Act (7 Marks)

The Air Act, 1981, has the following salient features:

1. **Comprehensive Scope:** The Act covers air pollutants including smoke, dust, fumes, gases, and **noise** (added in 1987).
2. **Institutional Framework:** Establishes CPCB and SPCBs to implement the Act.
3. **Standards-Based:** Lays down NAAQS for ambient air quality and emission standards for industries.
4. **Area-Based Control:** Empowers the State Government to declare any area as an **Air Pollution Control Area (APCA)**.
5. **Consent Mechanism:** No person can establish or operate an industrial plant in an APCA without SPCB consent.
6. **Penalty Provisions:** Imprisonment up to 6 years and fines up to ₹1 lakh.
7. **Citizen Empowerment:** Any person can file a complaint after 60 days' notice to the SPCB.

---

#### (b) Consent Mechanism and Penalties (7 Marks)

**Consent Mechanism (Sections 21 & 22):**
- **Section 21:** No person shall, without the previous consent of the SPCB, establish or take any step to establish any industrial plant in an APCA.
- **Section 22:** Application for consent must be made in the prescribed form, accompanied by details of the plant, control equipment, and consent fees.
- **Types of Consent:**
  - **Consent to Establish (CTE):** Required before construction.
  - **Consent to Operate (CTO):** Required before commissioning.
- **Conditions:** The SPCB may grant consent subject to conditions regarding stack height, control equipment, monitoring, and reporting.
- **Validity:** Usually 5 years, renewable.

**Penalties (Sections 26, 27, 28):**
- **Section 26:** Failure to comply with Sections 21/22 — imprisonment up to **1.5 years** OR fine up to **₹10,000**, or both.
- **Section 27:** Continuing offence — additional fine of **₹5,000/day**.
- **Section 28:** Offence by company — every person in charge (Director, Manager) is liable; imprisonment up to **2 years** + fine up to **₹1 lakh**.
- **Section 30:** Cognizance of offence — only on SPCB complaint, after 60 days' notice.

**Conclusion:** The Air Act combines **preventive** (consent), **deterrent** (penalties), and **curative** (directions) mechanisms to ensure clean air.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Section Number Confusion:** Students often confuse Sections 21 and 22. Remember: **Section 21 = Establish (CTE), Section 22 = Operate (CTO)**. Get this wrong, and you lose 2 marks instantly.
> 
> 2. **Penalty Magnitude:** Memorize **6 years** as the **maximum** imprisonment (for repeat/aggravated offences under Section 28). The general first-offence limit is **1.5 years**.
> 
> 3. **Noise Inclusion:** Many students forget that **noise** is an air pollutant under the amended Air Act, 1987. This is a favourite one-mark question.
> 
> 4. **CPCB vs SPCB:** Do not mix up the **16 functions of CPCB** and **17 functions of SPCB**. CPCB = National advisory; SPCB = State-level enforcement.
> 
> 5. **60-Day Notice Rule (Section 30):** Many students miss the **60 days prior notice** requirement before a court can take cognizance. This is a common 2-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Appellate Authority:** Do not say "NGT" directly. Say: "Appellate Authority under Section 25, and subsequently NGT for substantial environmental questions."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Year:** Air Act enacted in **1981**, came into force on **16 May 1981**.
- [ ] **Sections:** Total **53 Sections** across **7 Chapters**.
- [ ] **Amendment:** **1987** — added **noise** as an air pollutant.
- [ ] **Apex Body:** **CPCB** under Section 3.
- [ ] **State Body:** **SPCB** under Section 4 (or joint Water + Air Board under Section 5).
- [ ] **Definitions:** Section 2(a) – air pollutant, 2(b) – air pollution.
- [ ] **Functions of CPCB:** Section 16.
- [ ] **Functions of SPCB:** Section 17.
- [ ] **APCA Declaration:** Section 19.
- [ ] **Standards:** Section 20 (SPCB) and Section 16(2)(h) (CPCB for NAAQS).
- [ ] **Consent to Establish:** Section 21.
- [ ] **Consent to Operate:** Section 22.
- [ ] **Application to Court:** Section 23.
- [ ] **Directions:** Section 24 (15 days' notice).
- [ ] **Appeal:** Section 25 (30 days' limitation).
- [ ] **Penalty:** Section 26 (1.5 years jail + ₹10,000 fine).
- [ ] **Continuing Offence:** Section 27 (₹5,000/day).
- [ ] **Offence by Company:** Section 28 (2 years jail + ₹1 lakh fine).
- [ ] **Offence by Govt Dept:** Section 29.
- [ ] **Cognizance:** Section 30 (60 days' prior notice).
- [ ] **Rule-making:** Section 53 (Central Govt), Section 54 (State Govt).
- [ ] **NAAQS:** Issued by CPCB, last revised **16 November 2009**; covers **12 pollutants** (PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb, As, Ni, BaP, Benzene).
- [ ] **AQI:** Launched in **2014** with 8 parameters.
- [ ] **NCAP:** Launched **January 2019** for **131 non-attainment cities**.
- [ ] **NGT Act, 2010:** Provides parallel appellate jurisdiction.

### **Key Numerical & Limit Values to Memorize**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Maximum imprisonment (general) | 1.5 years |
| Maximum imprisonment (Section 26(2)) | 2 years |
| Maximum imprisonment (Section 28 – company) | 2 years |
| Maximum imprisonment (aggravated/repeat) | 6 years |
| Maximum fine (Section 26) | ₹10,000 |
| Maximum fine (Section 28) | ₹1 lakh |
| Daily fine (Section 27) | ₹5,000/day |
| Appeal period (Section 25) | 30 days |
| Cognizance notice (Section 30) | 60 days |
| Direction notice (Section 24) | 15 days |
| Maximum members (CPCB/SPCB) | 15 |
| Minimum full-time members (Joint Board) | 5 |
| Number of NAAQS pollutants | 12 |
| Number of non-attainment cities under NCAP | 131 |

### **5 Most Important Sections (KTU Favourite)**

1. **Section 2(a) and 2(b)** – Definitions of air pollutant and air pollution.
2. **Section 19** – Declaration of Air Pollution Control Area.
3. **Sections 21 & 22** – Consent mechanism.
4. **Section 26** – Penalties.
5. **Section 30** – Cognizance of offence (60 days rule).
