KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define aerodynamic diameter. Why is it preferred over geometric diameter in air quality monitoring?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **aerodynamic equivalent diameter** ($d_a$) of a particle is the diameter of a **unit-density sphere** ($\rho_0 = 1000$ kg/m³) that has the same **terminal settling velocity** as the particle in question. It is given by: $$d_a = d_p \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0} \cdot C_c}$$ (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explanation) It is preferred because: (i) it accounts for **shape, density, and slip effects**; (ii) it correlates directly with **lung deposition probability**; and (iii) it is the **regulatory metric** used in NAAQS (PM10, PM2.5) standards. ### Question 2 `[KTU Series Test 2 - PECET861]` **Q: List the five main mechanisms of particle collection in air pollution control devices.** **Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per mechanism with one-line description):** 1. **Gravitational settling** — dominant in chambers, Stk $\to 0$ (1/2) 2. **Inertial impaction** — dominant in cyclones and baghouses, Stk $\gg 1$ (1/2) 3. **Direct interception** — fibers in HEPA filters (1/2) 4. **Brownian diffusion** — submicron particles, $d_p < 0.1 \, \mu m$ (1/2) 5. **Electrostatic attraction** — ESPs, charged particles drift to grounded plate (1/2) (Add ½ mark for the **electrostatic / diffusiophoretic / thermophoretic** if mentioned for 3/3) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **fabric filter (baghouse)** used for particulate emission control. Derive the **cake-filtration pressure drop equation**. **(b) [7 Marks]** An ESP handles $Q = 200$ m³/s of flue gas at 150°C. The drift velocity is $w = 0.12$ m/s. Calculate the **plate area required** for 99.5% collection efficiency. If the gas volume flow drops to 150 m³/s (same $w$), what is the new efficiency with the same plate area? Comment on the result. **Model Solution (a):** A baghouse consists of **hundreds to thousands of fabric bags** (cylindrical, 0.1–0.3 m diameter, 2–10 m long) suspended in a housing. **Dirty gas** enters from the side/bottom; **clean gas** exits through the bag interiors via a centrifugal fan. (2 marks for description + schematic) - **Initial stage:** particles lodge in the fabric interstices (depth filtration). - **Cake stage:** a **dust cake** builds up — this is the *primary filter medium*. - **Cleaning:** bags are pulsed with **reverse-jet air** periodically; cake falls into the hopper. (2 marks for cake-formation mechanism) **Derivation of cake-filtration $\Delta P$:** For a dust cake of mass per unit area $W$ (kg/m²) and filter velocity $v$ (m/s): $$\Delta P = K_1 v + K_2 v W$$ where $K_1$ = clean-fabric resistance (Pa·s/m), $K_2$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg). (2 marks for derivation) Real-world design: $\Delta P_{max} \approx 1500$ Pa triggers cleaning pulse. (1 mark for design comment) **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Deutsch-Anderson equation:** $$\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$$ $$0.995 = 1 - e^{-0.12 \cdot A / 200}$$ $$e^{-0.12 A / 200} = 0.005$$ $$-\frac{0.12 A}{200} = \ln(0.005) = -5.298$$ $$A = \frac{5.298 \times 200}{0.12} = 8830 \, m^2$$ (3 marks for first part calculation) **Step 2 — New efficiency at $Q = 150$ m³/s, same $A$:** $$\eta = 1 - e^{-0.12 \times 8830 / 150} = 1 - e^{-7.064} = 1 - 0.000854 = 0.9991$$ (2 marks for second part) **Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):** > The efficiency **rises from 99.5% → 99.91%** when flow drops by 25% (at constant $w$). This is because the **residence time** of gas in the ESP increases, giving particles more time to migrate to the plates. **However**, in real ESPs, $w$ is also a function of gas velocity (turbulence decreases $w$ at very low flow), so this is an idealized calculation. --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTu University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **Stairmand high-efficiency cyclone separator** with a labeled diagram. Derive the **cut diameter** using the Lapple equation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A thermal power plant emits 250,000 m³/hr of flue gas containing 1500 mg/Nm³ of fly ash. Two control options are being considered: * Option 1: ESP with $\eta_1 = 99.5\%$ * Option 2: Baghouse with $\eta_2 = 99.9\%$ Calculate: (i) emission concentration from each option in mg/Nm³; (ii) total fly ash collected per day (kg/day) by each option; (iii) annual revenue from selling collected fly ash at ₹800/tonne (assume 350 operating days/year). **Model Solution (a):** **Diagram (2 marks):** Show tangential inlet, outer barrel, conical section, vortex finder, dust hopper, tangential outlet. **Working principle (2 marks):** Dirty gas enters tangentially, spins downward as an outer vortex, then reverses direction and exits via the inner vortex through the top. Centrifugal force throws particles to the wall; they slide down the cone into the hopper. **Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2: $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$$ with $N_e \approx 5$ to 8 effective turns. Higher $v_i$ → smaller $d_{pc}$ → better efficiency, but higher $\Delta P$. **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Convert gas flow:** $$Q = \frac{250000}{3600} = 69.44 \, m^3/s$$ (½ mark) **Step 2 — Inlet mass flow of fly ash:** $$\dot{m}_{in} = 1500 \times 10^{-6} \times 69.44 = 0.1042 \, kg/s$$ $$= 0.1042 \times 86400 = 9000 \, kg/day$$ (1 mark) **Step 3 — Outlet emission concentration:** | Option | $\eta$ | Outlet concentration (mg/Nm³) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | ESP | 99.5% | $1500 \times (1-0.995) = 7.5$ | | Baghouse | 99.9% | $1500 \times (1-0.999) = 1.5$ | (1 mark) **Step 4 — Fly ash collected per day:** | Option | Collected (kg/day) | | :--- | :---: | | ESP | $9000 \times 0.995 = 8955$ | | Baghouse | $9000 \times 0.999 = 8991$ | (1 mark) **Step 5 — Annual revenue (350 operating days):** | Option | Collected (tonnes/yr) | Revenue (₹) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | ESP | $8955 \times 350 / 1000 = 3134.25$ | $3134.25 \times 800 = $ ₹25,07,400 | | Baghouse | $8991 \times 350 / 1000 = 3146.85$ | $3146.85 \times 800 = $ ₹25,17,480 | (2 marks — 1 for each table) **Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Although the **baghouse** gives 30× lower emission (1.5 vs 7.5 mg/Nm³), the revenue difference is only ₹10,080/year — meaning **compliance with NAAQS** (CPCB limit: 50 mg/Nm³ for PM) is the deciding factor, and the baghouse is preferred for power plants near urban areas. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always state `mg/Nm³`, `µm`, `m/s`, or `kg/day` explicitly. 1 mark is reserved for **dimensional consistency**. > 2. **Mixing up aerodynamic vs geometric diameter** in PM10/PM2.5 questions. Always mention $C_c$ for $d_p < 1 \, \mu m$. > 3. **Applying Stokes' law outside its validity range.** State the condition $Re_p < 0.1$ explicitly; otherwise the derivation is invalid. > 4. **Writing the Deutsch-Anderson equation as $\eta = e^{-wA/Q}$** — this is the **penetration** $P$, not efficiency! $\eta = 1 - P$. > 5. **Neglecting buoyancy** in the gravitational settling derivation. The term $(\rho_p - \rho)$ is **NOT** $\rho_p$ alone (except for $\rho \ll \rho_p$). > 6. **Wrong temperature correction.** Gas viscosity $\mu$ depends on $T$; use Sutherland's law for $\mu$ at non-25°C. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECET861 Module 4** - [x] **PM classification:** TSP (< 100 µm), PM10 (< 10 µm), PM2.5 (< 2.5 µm), Ultrafine (< 0.1 µm) - [x] **NAAQS limits (India):** PM10 = 100 µg/m³ (24-hr), PM2.5 = 60 µg/m³ (24-hr) - [x] **Stokes' Law** applies only for $Re_p < 0.1$ (Laminar) - [x] **Slip correction** $C_c$ **mandatory** for $d_p < 1 \, \mu m$ - [x] **Cyclone cut diameter** $d_{pc}$ given by **Lapple's equation** — decreases with $\sqrt{v_i}$ - [x] **Cyclone pressure drop** $\Delta P = \xi \rho v_i^2 / 2$, with $\xi = 4$ to 8 (Stairmand) - [x] **ESP efficiency** follows **Deutsch-Anderson**: $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ - [x] **ESP drift velocity** $w = 0.06$ to 0.20 m/s (industrial range) - [x] **Baghouses** give highest efficiency (> 99.9%) but high $\Delta P$ - [x] **Venturi scrubbers** work on **impaction + diffusion** mechanisms - [x] **Selection logic** (KTU standard answer): - **Hot, dry gas + large volume** → ESP - **Hot, moist gas + corrosive** → Wet scrubber - **Strict PM limit + low temp** → Baghouse - **Pre-cleaning + low cost** → Cyclone - **Very coarse dust** → Settling chamber - [x] **Mean free path of air** $\lambda = 0.066 \, \mu m$ at STP (use in $C_c$ formula) - [x] **Stokes Number** $Stk = \rho_p d_p^2 v / (18 \mu D_c)$ — high Stk ⇒ good impaction efficiency - [x] **CPC standards** for industry stack emissions: **< 50 mg/Nm³** (general) - [x] **Aerodynamic diameter** is the **regulatory metric** (not geometric diameter) - [x] **Key dimensionless number** to remember: $Re$, $Stk$, $C_c$, $Pe$ (Peclet for diffusion) > **Final 30-Second Mnemonic:** *"**S**mall **P**articles need **S**lip correction; **C**yclones use **C**entrifugal force; **E**SPs use **E**lectrostatic fields; **B**aghouses use **B**locked fabric; **S**crubbers use **S**prayed water."*
